topics.title: Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fuel Cell # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Official Definition)** > A **fuel cell** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (most commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water and heat as the primary by-products**. Unlike a battery, a fuel cell does not undergo internal chemical consumption; it continues to produce electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are continuously supplied, making it an *open* thermodynamic system. The reaction at the heart of a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) hydrogen fuel cell is: $$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant vs. The Lunchbox" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Imagine a Battery is a Lunchbox and a Fuel Cell is a Restaurant.** > - A **battery** stores its reactants (food) inside. Once the lunchbox is empty, you must stop to recharge or replace it. > - A **fuel cell** is like a restaurant table — fuel (food) is *delivered continuously* from outside, oxidant (oxygen) comes from the air, and electrical power (satisfaction) flows out continuously. As long as supply lines are open, electricity is generated. ## 1.3 Why This Matters in Engineering > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** Fuel cells are **NOT** heat engines. They bypass the **Carnot cycle** and convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy via an electrochemical pathway, achieving theoretical efficiencies of **83%** for H₂/O₂ systems compared to **~40%** for thermal power plants. ## 1.4 Salient Features of Fuel Cells (High-Yield KTU Points) | Feature | Specification | |---|---| | Standard cell potential (H₂/O₂) | **E° = 1.229 V** | | Operating temperature | 60 °C – 1000 °C (cell-type dependent) | | Primary by-products | **Water (H₂O)** and **Heat** | | Moving parts | **Zero** (silent operation) | | Emissions (H₂ fuel) | **Near-zero** CO₂, NOₓ, SOₓ | | Theoretical efficiency | **83 %** (η = ΔG/ΔH) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A fuel cell comprises **three core sandwiched layers**: 1. **Anode (Fuel Electrode)** — Receives hydrogen; catalysis splits H₂ into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻). 2. **Electrolyte** — Permits ion flow (e.g., H⁺ in PEMFC, O²⁻ in SOFC) but **blocks electrons**, forcing them through an external circuit. 3. **Cathode (Oxidant Electrode)** — Receives oxygen; combines O₂ with ions and returned electrons to form water. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of the electrolyte's selectivity:** The electrolyte is the *gatekeeper*. By allowing only ions to migrate internally, it forces electrons to take the **external path**, where they perform **useful electrical work** (I × V). ## 2.2 Half-Cell and Overall Reactions (PEMFC Example) | Electrode | Half-Reaction | Standard Potential (E°) | |---|---|---| | **Anode** (Oxidation) | $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ | 0.000 V | | **Cathode** (Reduction) | $\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ | +1.229 V | | **Overall** | $\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ | **+1.229 V** | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Gibbs–Helmholtz Relation** | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | ΔG: J/mol, n: e⁻ transferred, F = **96,485 C/mol**, E: V | Links free energy to cell voltage | | 2 | **Standard Cell Potential** | $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ | All in Volts | Determines open-circuit voltage | | 3 | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | R = **8.314 J/mol·K**, T: K, Q: reaction quotient | Predicts voltage under load | | 4 | **Thermodynamic Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°}$ | Dimensionless | Max theoretical efficiency | | 5 | **Voltage Efficiency** | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{E_{operating}}{E_{reversible}}$ | Ratio | Real-world voltage losses | | 6 | **Faraday's Law** | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ | m: mass (g), M: molar mass (g/mol), I: A, t: s | Fuel consumption rate | | 7 | **Power Output** | $P = V \cdot I$ | W | Useful electrical work | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Application Map (KTU Board Favourite):** > - **Automotive:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO — PEMFCs at **65–95 kW**. > - **Stationary Power:** Bloom Energy SOFC servers for data centres — **>60%** electrical efficiency. > - **Space:** NASA Apollo & Space Shuttle missions used **AFC (Alkaline Fuel Cells)** for onboard power and drinking water. > - **Portable:** Soldier-portable methanol fuel cells for field operations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Linking Gibbs Free Energy to Cell Voltage (Gibbs–Helmholtz) **Starting thermodynamic premise:** The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the **decrease in Gibbs free energy** of that reaction at constant temperature and pressure. $$W_{max,elec} = -\Delta G$$ For a reaction transferring **n** moles of electrons through a potential difference **E**, the electrical work done is: $$W_{elec} = Q \cdot V = (nF) \cdot E$$ where: - $Q$ = total charge transferred (C) - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - $n$ = moles of electrons per mole of reaction **Equating the two expressions:** $$-\Delta G = nFE$$ **Solving for E:** $$\boxed{E = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}}$$ **Sign convention:** Since ΔG is **negative** for a spontaneous reaction, E becomes **positive**, indicating a feasible galvanic cell. **Numerical check (H₂/O₂ at 25 °C):** $$E° = \frac{-(-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$ $$E° = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$ $$\boxed{E° = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation II — Derivation of the Nernst Equation **Starting point — Van't Hoff isotherm (chemical equilibrium):** $$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. **Substitute the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation** $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G° = -nFE°$: $$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$ **Divide both sides by $-nF$:** $$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ $$\boxed{E = E° - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q}$$ **At standard temperature (T = 298 K):** Substitute R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96{,}485} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V}$$ **Simplified standard Nernst form:** $$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$ ## 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Thermodynamic Efficiency **Premise:** The HHV (Higher Heating Value) of hydrogen combustion is: $$\Delta H° = -285{,}830 \text{ J/mol (liquid water product)}$$ **Free energy change for the same reaction:** $$\Delta G° = -237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°}$$ **Substitution:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{-237{,}200}{-285{,}830}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 0.83 = 83\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Compare this with a Carnot engine operating between 800 K (hot) and 300 K (cold): $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{800} = 62.5\%$. This is precisely **why fuel cells are NOT heat engines** — they sidestep Carnot's limit by avoiding thermal conversion. ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Fuel Consumption Rate from Faraday's Law **Given:** - Current demand: $I$ = 50 A - Time: $t$ = 1 hour = 3600 s - Reaction: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ → therefore $n = 2$ - Molar mass of H₂: $M = 2.016$ g/mol **Step 1 — Total charge transferred:** $$Q = I \times t = 50 \times 3600 = 180{,}000 \text{ C}$$ **Step 2 — Moles of electrons:** $$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{180{,}000}{96{,}485} = 1.866 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of H₂ consumed (2 e⁻ per H₂):** $$n_{H_2} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{2} = 0.933 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of H₂ consumed:** $$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M = 0.933 \times 2.016$$ $$\boxed{m_{H_2} \approx 1.88 \text{ grams of H}_2 \text{ per hour}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — PEM Fuel Cell Operation The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal flow architecture** of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC), illustrating how fuel, oxidant, ions, electrons, and by-products interact across the three primary layers. ```mermaid flowchart LR H2IN[("Hydrogen Fuel In")]:::input --> ANODE[("ANODE
Catalyst Layer
Pt/C")]:::anode AIRIN[("Air In
Oxygen Supply")]:::input --> CATHODE[("CATHODE
Catalyst Layer
Pt/C")]:::cathode ANODE -- "H2 splits into
2H+ and 2e-" --> MEM[("PROTON EXCHANGE
MEMBRANE
Nafion Electrolyte")]:::membrane MEM -- "H+ ions migrate
through membrane" --> CATHODE ANODE -- "e- blocked
forced externally" --> EXT[("EXTERNAL CIRCUIT
Load: P = VI")]:::load EXT -- "e- return to cathode" --> CATHODE CATHODE -- "1/2 O2 + 2H+ + 2e-" --> WATER[("WATER + HEAT
By-products Out")]:::output ANODE -. "Heat loss" .-> HEAT[("Thermal
Management")]:::output classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef anode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cathode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef membrane fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Component-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FC["FUEL CELL STACK SYSTEM"] direction TB subgraph BOP["BALANCE OF PLANT - BOP"] HUM["Humidifier"]:::bop CPUMP["Coolant Pump"]:::bop COMP["Air Compressor"]:::bop HX["Heat Exchanger"]:::bop end subgraph CORE["CORE ELECTROCHEMICAL CORE"] A1["Anode Flow Field"]:::core E1["Membrane Electrode Assembly - MEA"]:::core C1["Cathode Flow Field"]:::core end subgraph COND["POWER CONDITIONING"] DC["DC-DC Converter"]:::cond INV["Inverter DC to AC"]:::cond end end HUM --> A1 COMP --> C1 A1 --> E1 --> C1 C1 --> HX --> CPUMP E1 --> DC --> INV INV --> LOAD[("Load / Grid")]:::out classDef bop fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef core fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef cond fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Performance Map of Fuel Cell Types ```mermaid flowchart LR START[("Fuel Cell Family Tree")]:::root --> PEMFC["PEMFC
60-100 C
H+ conductor"]:::type1 START --> AFC["AFC
60-220 C
OH- conductor"]:::type2 START --> PAFC["PAFC
150-220 C
H+ conductor"]:::type3 START --> MCFC["MCFC
600-700 C
CO3 2- conductor"]:::type4 START --> SOFC["SOFC
700-1000 C
O2- conductor"]:::type5 PEMFC --> APP1[("Automotive
Toshiba Toyota")]:::app AFC --> APP2[("Space Missions
NASA Apollo")]:::app PAFC --> APP3[("Stationary
200 kW Plants")]:::app MCFC --> APP4[("Industrial
Large Scale Power")]:::app SOFC --> APP5[("Distributed
Stationary Power")]:::app classDef root fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef type1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef type2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef type3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef type4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 classDef type5 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 classDef app fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define a fuel cell. How is it fundamentally different from a primary battery?** **Model Answer:** A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel (H₂) and oxidant (O₂) directly into electrical energy, producing water and heat as by-products. | Property | Fuel Cell | Primary Battery | |---|---|---| | Energy Source | External (continuous feed) | Internal (finite) | | Recharging | Not required | Must be replaced | | Lifetime | Indefinite (with supply) | Limited by stored reactants | | Electrode Consumption | None (catalysts stay intact) | Active material depletes | *(3 marks: definition 1, table comparison 2)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **State the Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ fuel cell and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** $$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ - $E$ = Actual cell potential (V) - $E°$ = Standard cell potential (**1.229 V** for H₂/O₂) - $R$ = Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**) - $T$ = Operating temperature (K) - $n$ = Number of electrons transferred (= 2 for H₂) - $F$ = Faraday constant (**96,485 C/mol**) - $Q$ = Reaction quotient of partial pressures *Significance:* Allows prediction of cell voltage under non-standard operating pressures and temperatures. *(3 marks: equation 1, term definitions 1.5, significance 0.5)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **(i)** Derive the relationship between Gibbs free energy and the cell EMF of a fuel cell. **(ii)** For a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell at standard conditions, calculate the theoretical EMF given $\Delta G° = -237.2$ kJ/mol and $n = 2$. **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** The maximum non-expansion (electrical) work obtainable from a chemical reaction at constant T and P is given by: $$W_{max,elec} = -\Delta G \quad \text{...(1)}$$ For a reaction transferring $n$ moles of electrons across a potential $E$, the charge moved is $nF$ (where $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol). Hence: $$W_{elec} = Q \times E = nFE \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Equating (1) and (2): $$-\Delta G = nFE$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** $$E° = \frac{-\Delta G°}{nF} = \frac{-(-237{,}200)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$ $$E° = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$ $$\boxed{E° = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ **(7 marks: derivation 4, substitution 1.5, final answer with units 1.5)** **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **(i)** Define thermodynamic efficiency of a fuel cell. **(ii)** Compute the maximum thermodynamic efficiency of a H₂/O₂ fuel cell producing **liquid water** as product. Given: $\Delta H° = -285.83$ kJ/mol, $\Delta G° = -237.2$ kJ/mol. **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency of a fuel cell is defined as the ratio of the useful electrical work obtainable (ΔG°) to the total chemical energy released (ΔH°) by the reaction: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°}$$ **Calculation:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{-237{,}200}{-285{,}830} = \frac{237{,}200}{285{,}830}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.8299 \approx 83\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparison:** A Carnot heat engine operating between T_hot = 1000 K and T_cold = 300 K has η_Carnot = 70%. Therefore, fuel cells **exceed Carnot efficiency** because they bypass the thermal intermediate step entirely — a key conceptual distinction frequently tested. **(7 marks: definition 2, formula 1, substitution 1.5, numerical answer 1, Carnot comparison 1.5)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — *Alternative Choice* **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). Write the half-cell reactions and overall cell reaction.** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle:** A PEMFC operates at low temperatures (60–100 °C) with a proton-conducting polymer membrane (typically Nafion) as the electrolyte. Hydrogen fuel is fed to the anode, and oxygen (from air) to the cathode. **Step 1:** At the **anode**, the platinum catalyst dissociates H₂ into protons (H⁺) and electrons: $$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode: Oxidation)}$$ **Step 2:** Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane toward the cathode. Electrons are **blocked** by the membrane and forced through the external circuit, producing useful electrical work. **Step 3:** At the **cathode**, oxygen combines with the arriving protons and electrons: $$\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Cathode: Reduction)}$$ **Overall Reaction:** $$\boxed{\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}}$$ **Block Diagram (textual reference):** ``` H₂ in → [Anode] → e⁻ → External Load → e⁻ → [Cathode] ← O₂ in ↘ H⁺ → [PEM Membrane] → H⁺ ↗ ↘ H₂O out ``` **(7 marks: working explanation 3, anode reaction 1, cathode reaction 1, overall reaction 1, block diagram 1)** **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **(i)** State Faraday's laws of electrolysis as applied to fuel cells. **(ii)** A fuel cell stack delivers a constant current of **80 A** for **5 hours**. Calculate the mass of hydrogen consumed. (M_H₂ = 2 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol) **Model Solution:** **Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance consumed/produced at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of charge passed. $$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$$ **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of charge is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights. **Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Total charge:** $$Q = I \times t = 80 \times (5 \times 3600) = 80 \times 18{,}000 = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ C}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law:** $$m_{H_2} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 18{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$m_{H_2} = \frac{2{,}880{,}000}{192{,}970}$$ $$\boxed{m_{H_2} \approx 14.92 \text{ grams}}$$ **(7 marks: Faraday's laws 2, formula 1, substitution 2, final numerical answer with units 2)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Fuel Cell Questions:** > 1. **Sign Confusion:** ΔG is **negative** for spontaneous reactions. Forgetting the negative sign in $E = -\Delta G / nF$ leads to wrong sign on E. > 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing ΔG in kJ/mol (e.g., -237.2) with F in C/mol gives an E that is off by a factor of 1000. **Always convert ΔG to J/mol** before substitution. > 3. **Reaction Quotient Q:** Students forget that for the H₂/O₂ cell, $Q = 1/(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2})$, and partial pressures must be in **atmospheres**. > 4. **HHV vs LHV:** When $\Delta H$ is given, the question may use HHV (liquid water, -285.83 kJ/mol) or LHV (steam, -241.82 kJ/mol). State clearly which assumption is used. > 5. **Confusing Fuel Cell with Battery:** Fuel cells are *open* systems; batteries are *closed*. Examiners routinely deduct marks for this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell (Module 1)** > - **Definition:** Electrochemical device converting fuel + oxidant directly into electricity; **not** a heat engine. > - **Core Reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$; **E° = 1.229 V** at 25 °C. > - **Key Constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K; $n = 2$ for H₂. > - **Master Equations:** > - $E = -\Delta G / nF$ > - $E = E° - (RT/nF) \ln Q$ (Nernst) > - $\eta_{th} = \Delta G° / \Delta H° \approx 83\%$ > - $m = M \cdot I \cdot t / nF$ (Faraday) > - **Fuel Cell vs Battery:** Fuel cell = open, continuous feed, indefinite life. Battery = closed, finite reactants. > - **PEMFC Specifics:** Low T (60–100 °C), H⁺ conductor (Nafion), Pt catalyst, automotive applications. > - **Why Fuel Cells Beat Carnot:** No thermal intermediate — direct chemical → electrical conversion. > - **Bypass Traps:** Sign of ΔG, unit consistency (kJ vs J), HHV vs LHV, partial pressure units in Nernst.

topics.title: Brief History of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Brief History of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fuel Cell

A **fuel cell** is an *electrochemical energy conversion device* that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into electrical energy, water, and heat, **without combustion**. Unlike a battery, a fuel cell is supplied with reactants from external sources and can produce electricity continuously as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard**
> According to the KTU PECHT634 syllabus, a fuel cell is defined as a **galvanic cell** in which the free energy of a chemical reaction is converted directly into electrical energy. It consists of two porous electrodes (anode and cathode) separated by an electrolyte, with continuous supply of reactants and removal of products.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electrochemical Furnace"

Imagine a conventional car engine that burns gasoline. Fuel and air enter, spark ignites the mixture, hot gases expand, and pistons convert that expansion into rotation. Roughly **60–70 %** of the chemical energy is lost as heat and noise.

Now picture a **silent, flameless teapot**:
- You pour hydrogen (the "tea leaves") on one side.
- You pour oxygen (the "water") on the other side.
- The two streams meet across a **special membrane** (the "tea bag") that only lets ions pass.
- Instead of flame, an electron current is forced to flow through an external wire — that is your electricity.
- The "used tea" (water) drips out warm but clean.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fuel cell is **NOT a heat engine**, so it is **NOT bound by the Carnot efficiency limit** ($\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$). This single fact is the most important motivation behind 200 years of fuel cell research.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are mandatory for KTU numerical problems:

- **Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$** (often rounded to **96,500 C mol⁻¹**)
- **Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$**
- **Standard temperature: $T^\circ = 298.15 \text{ K}$**
- **Standard pressure: $P^\circ = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**
- **Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$**
- **Gibbs free energy of H₂O(l) formation: $\Delta G_f^\circ = -237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$**
- **Enthalpy of H₂O(l) formation: $\Delta H_f^\circ = -285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel cell is an *open* system (mass crosses the boundary) — therefore, classical thermodynamic potentials ($\Delta G$, $\Delta H$) apply, NOT internal energy ($U$) balance, which assumes closed systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chronological Logic of Fuel Cell Evolution

The history of fuel cells is best understood as **four overlapping waves**, each driven by a unique technical bottleneck:

### Wave 1 — Discovery Era (1838–1900)
- Driven by **scientific curiosity**, not practical demand.
- Key bottleneck: electrode polarization and slow kinetics.

### Wave 2 — Inter-War Vacuum (1900–1950)
- Work halted largely because the **internal combustion engine** and **grid electricity** dominated.
- Bacon's pioneering work in alkaline systems during WWII.

### Wave 3 — Space Race (1950–1970)
- NASA needed **silent, lightweight, high-energy-density** power for Gemini and Apollo missions.
- Practical engineering + cryogenic H₂/O₂ supply chain solved the practical demand.

### Wave 4 — Modern Commercialization (1970–Present)
- Driven by **oil crises**, **climate change**, and **hydrogen economy** concepts.
- Focus shifted to cost reduction, durability, and platinum-group metal (PGM) reduction.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Equations Driving the History

The performance of a fuel cell at any historical point in time was constrained by these equations:

- **Cell EMF (Reversible Voltage):**
  $$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

- **Theoretical Thermal Efficiency:**
  $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$$

- **Actual Cell Voltage (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**
  $$E_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

- **Nernst Equation (Non-Standard Conditions):**
  $$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

- **Energy Density (Wh/kg) — Why Fuel Cells Beat Batteries:**
  $$\rho_E = \frac{n F E^\circ}{M_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** The theoretical efficiency of a H₂/O₂ fuel cell is **83 %** at 25 °C, while a Carnot engine between the same temperatures is limited to ~93 %. Both are theoretical maxima — the fuel cell achieves its maximum **directly**, without heat transfer losses.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Notes for H₂/O₂ Cell** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reversible Voltage | $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ \vert nF$ | Volts (V) | $1.229$ V at 25 °C |
| Thermodynamic Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \Delta G^\circ / \Delta H^\circ$ | Dimensionless | $0.83$ (or 83 %) |
| Nernst Voltage | $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$ | V | Use $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| Heat Generated | $q = T \Delta S$ | J/mol | $48.7$ kJ/mol for H₂/O₂ |
| Faradaic Current | $I = nF \dot{n}_{fuel}$ | A | $\dot{n}_{fuel}$ in mol/s |
| Power Output | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | W (J/s) | Multiplied by $n_{cells}$ for stack |
| Energy Density | $\rho_E = nFE^\circ / M_{fuel}$ | Wh/kg | $\approx 32{,}800$ Wh/kg theoretical |

> **Engineering Note:** The historical challenge was that **E_actual was far below E_rev** due to overpotentials ($\eta_{act}$, $\eta_{ohmic}$, $\eta_{conc}$). Each era in history tackled a different overpotential.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reversible Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

For the overall hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell reaction:

$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the change in Gibbs free energy at standard conditions.**
At $T = 298.15$ K and $P = 1$ atm, the standard Gibbs free energy change is tabulated as:

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta G_f^\circ(H_2O, l) - \left[\Delta G_f^\circ(H_2, g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta G_f^\circ(O_2, g)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute standard values.**
By definition, $\Delta G_f^\circ$ of elements in their standard state is zero. Thus:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} - [0 + 0]$$

$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the number of electrons transferred.**
From the half-cell reactions:
- Anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$

Therefore, $n = 2$ electrons per mole of hydrogen.

**Step 4 — Apply the relationship between $\Delta G$ and cell EMF.**
The maximum useful electrical work obtainable equals $-\Delta G$:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE^\circ_{cell}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the reversible standard cell voltage.**

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{(-237{,}130 \text{ J mol}^{-1})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1})}$$

$$E^\circ_{cell} = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

This is the **famous "1.23 V"** of the hydrogen fuel cell, derived from first principles.

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermodynamic Efficiency

The theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency of an energy conversion device is defined as the ratio of useful energy output to total energy input. For a fuel cell:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Useful Electrical Energy}}{\text{Total Chemical Energy Input}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify useful output.**
For a fuel cell, the useful output is the Gibbs free energy change:

$$W_{useful} = -\Delta G^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Identify total input.**
The total chemical energy released when hydrogen combusts (in any form) is the enthalpy of formation of liquid water:

$$Q_{input} = -\Delta H^\circ = 285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{-\Delta H^\circ} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13}{285.83} = 0.8296$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 0.83 \text{ or } 83\%}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
The remaining 17 % is the entropic heat $T\Delta S$ that *must* be released as heat, regardless of device design, because of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. This is **intrinsic** to the chemistry and is the *upper bound* of fuel cell efficiency.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Equation (Non-Standard Conditions)

When the reactants or products are not at standard conditions, the cell potential shifts. We begin with the general electrochemical free energy relation:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. For the H₂/O₂ cell:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot (a_{O_2})^{1/2}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $-nF$:**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Apply for the H₂/O₂ cell with $n=2$, $T=298.15$ K, partial pressures $p_{H_2}$ and $p_{O_2}$:**

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{2(96{,}485)}\ln\left[\frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot (p_{O_2})^{1/2}}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical coefficient:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01284 \text{ V} = 12.84 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Final form at 25 °C:**

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.01284 \ln\left[p_{H_2} \cdot (p_{O_2})^{1/2}\right] \text{ V}}$$

This explains why **modern fuel cell vehicles** pressurize H₂ to **700 bar** — it raises the EMF by approximately 30–40 mV, which directly translates to higher efficiency.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: The Four Eras of Fuel Cell History

| **Era** | **Period** | **Driving Force** | **Key Scientist(s)** | **Major Breakthrough** | **Limitation Tackled** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discovery | 1838–1889 | Scientific curiosity | Schönbein, Grove, Mond, Langer | First "gas battery" (Grove, 1839) | Concept proof |
| Inter-War | 1889–1939 | WWI/WWII power needs | Ostwald, Baur, Bacon | Alkaline electrolyte (Bacon) | Electrolyte purity |
| Space Race | 1959–1972 | NASA Apollo program | Bacon, Grubb, Niedrach | PEM membrane (DuPont) | Operating temperature, lifetime |
| Modern | 1973–Present | Oil crisis, climate change | Numerous | SOFC, DMFC, PGM reduction | Cost & durability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Generic Fuel Cell (Context for Historical Discussion)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["FUEL INLET H2 TANK OR REFORMER"]
    A2["ANODE ELECTRODE POROUS CARBON WITH CATALYST"]

```

> **Why this matters for history:** Grove's 1839 cell had primitive versions of **all** these components. What changed over 185 years was the *material* of A3 (electrolyte) — from sulfuric acid (Grove) → KOH (Bacon) → Nafion (Grubb) → YSZ (SOFC).

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — Voltage Achievement Over Time

```mermaid

```

> **Note:** Voltage per cell **decreased** in modern stacks because designers trade cell voltage for higher current density and power density ($\text{W/cm}^2$), which is the real engineering metric for vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Who is credited with the invention of the first fuel cell, and what was the original name given to it?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sir William Robert Grove, a Welsh judge and physicist, is credited with the invention of the first fuel cell in 1839. He called it the **"gas battery"** (also referred to as a *gaseous voltaic battery*). The device was constructed by connecting several platinum electrodes in series, with hydrogen supplied to one set and oxygen to the other, separated by a sulfuric acid electrolyte. Grove demonstrated that reversing the electrolysis of water produced an electric current, thereby experimentally confirming the fuel cell principle first noted by Christian Friedrich Schönbein in 1838.

> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark for year (1839); 1 mark for name of inventor (Grove); 1 mark for the term "gas battery" with correct context.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State the contribution of Ludwig Mond and Charles Langer in the development of fuel cells."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In 1889, Ludwig Mond and his assistant Charles Langer improved Grove's gas battery by using a **diaphragm (a porous non-conductor)** soaked in a sulfuric acid electrolyte, together with **platinum black** as a catalyst and perforated platinum or gold electrodes. They achieved a current density of about **2–3 mA/cm²**, which was a major improvement. Most importantly, they coined the term **"fuel cell"** for the first time, recognizing that the device was continuously supplied with fuel (coal-derived hydrogen) and oxidant, analogous to a heat engine being supplied with coal and air. Their work was a stepping stone to the modern concept of the fuel cell as a practical energy device.

> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark for diaphragm innovation; 1 mark for platinum black catalyst; 1 mark for coining the term "fuel cell."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) "Explain in detail the historical milestones in the development of fuel cells, highlighting the contributions of Grove, Mond, Bacon, and the NASA space program."** (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Grove's Gas Battery (1839) — 2 marks**
Sir William Grove constructed the first practical fuel cell by serially combining several platinum electrodes. Hydrogen was bubbled over one electrode and oxygen over the other, with dilute sulfuric acid as the electrolyte. He observed that the cell produced a current and water as a byproduct — the reverse of water electrolysis. This experimentally proved the fuel cell principle.

**2. Mond and Langer's Improvements (1889) — 2 marks**
Ludwig Mond and Charles Langer refined Grove's design by introducing a **porous diaphragm** impregnated with sulfuric acid, replacing the bulky liquid acid. They used **platinum black catalysts** to enhance reaction kinetics, and reached current densities of 2–3 mA/cm². They coined the term **"fuel cell"**, recognizing its continuous-supply character.

**3. Bacon's Alkaline Fuel Cell (1932–1959) — 2 marks**
Francis Thomas Bacon developed the **alkaline fuel cell (AFC)**, replacing the corrosive sulfuric acid with **potassium hydroxide (KOH)**. He used nickel electrodes (avoiding expensive platinum) and operated at higher temperatures (200–240 °C) and pressures (40–55 bar) to overcome slow kinetics. By 1959, Bacon demonstrated a 5 kW stack — the first practically useful fuel cell.

**4. NASA Space Program (1960s) — 1 mark**
NASA adopted the AFC for the **Apollo missions (1965–1972)**, providing 1.5 kW of continuous power plus drinkable water for the astronauts. This was the **first commercial success** of fuel cells. General Electric (with Grubb and Niedrach) developed the **proton exchange membrane (PEM)** fuel cell for the Gemini program.

**(b) "Derive the theoretical open-circuit voltage of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell at standard conditions."** (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the overall cell reaction.
$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work.
$$-\Delta G^\circ = nFE^\circ_{cell}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify $\Delta G^\circ$ for the reaction.
$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} = -237{,}130 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify $n$ and $F$.
$$n = 2, \quad F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Substitute and solve.

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = -\frac{(-237{,}130)}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$E^\circ_{cell} = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the result and physical interpretation.
The reversible OCV of the H₂/O₂ fuel cell is **1.229 V at 25 °C and 1 atm**. This represents the *maximum* voltage obtainable from the cell at standard conditions and is the **historical benchmark** that fuel cell researchers have been trying to approach in practice for nearly two centuries.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) "Discuss the role of Sir William Grove, Christian Friedrich Schönbein, and Wilhelm Ostwald in the foundational understanding of fuel cells."** (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Schönbein — Discovery of the Effect (1838) — 2 marks**
Christian Friedrich Schönbein, a German-Swiss chemist, was the **first** to observe and publish (in 1838) the fuel cell effect during experiments on electrolysis. He corresponded with Grove and predicted the principle, but never built a working cell. Schönbein's contribution is the **theoretical discovery** that electrolysis can be reversed to produce electricity.

**2. Grove — First Working Cell (1839) — 3 marks**
Sir William Grove took Schönbein's theoretical idea and constructed the **"gas battery"** — a series of 50 cells with platinum electrodes in sulfuric acid, using H₂ and O₂. He demonstrated that:
- Current flowed continuously as long as gas was supplied.
- Water was produced at the cathode.
- Voltage was proportional to cell count.
Grove's **"gas battery"** is the **proto-type of every modern fuel cell**. He is rightly called the **"Father of the Fuel Cell."**

**3. Ostwald — Theoretical Foundation (1893) — 2 marks**
Wilhelm Ostwald, in 1893, provided the **theoretical explanation** of the fuel cell. He proved that the direct conversion of chemical energy to electrical energy was not in violation of the Second Law of Thermodynamics, and showed that fuel cells — being **non-heat-engine devices** — were not subject to the Carnot limit. He also identified the source of voltage losses: electrode polarization and slow ion kinetics — the very same issues we still address today.

**(b) "Calculate the theoretical efficiency of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell operating at 25 °C and 1 atm. Given: $\Delta H^\circ = -285.83$ kJ/mol, $\Delta G^\circ = -237.13$ kJ/mol."** (7 marks)

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency.
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Useful electrical energy output}}{\text{Total chemical energy input}} = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{-\Delta H^\circ}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute given values.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}{285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the ratio.
$$\eta_{th} = 0.8296$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Express as a percentage.
$$\eta_{th} \approx 83\%$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate the wasted energy as heat ($T\Delta S$).
$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ}{T} = \frac{285.83 - 237.13}{298.15} = 0.1634 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$q_{lost} = T\Delta S^\circ = 298.15 \times 0.1634 = 48.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Cross-check.
$$237.13 + 48.7 = 285.83 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude.
The H₂/O₂ fuel cell has a **theoretical maximum efficiency of ~83 %** at 25 °C, significantly higher than any practical heat engine. The 17 % loss is **intrinsic** to the chemistry, due to entropy production $T\Delta S$, and cannot be eliminated by engineering improvements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing $\Delta G$ with $\Delta H$** in efficiency calculations → lose 2 marks instantly. *Remember: fuel cell output is $\Delta G$, not $\Delta H$.*
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** for $E^\circ$. Students often write $E^\circ = \Delta G / nF$ without the negative sign, giving a negative voltage. The correct formula is $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$.
> 3. **Forgetting units of $F$** — must be C/mol, not C. A common KTU mistake costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing "Bacon's nickel electrodes"** in history questions — examiner specifically tests this as a cost-reduction milestone.
> 5. **Forgetting the year of the term "fuel cell"** (1889, Mond and Langer) — frequently asked as a 1-mark specific.
> 6. **Confusing PAFC (Phosphoric Acid) and PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane)** — both have "P" but are different. PEMFC = Nafion-based, PAFC = liquid H₃PO₄ at 200 °C.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **1838:** Schönbein discovers the fuel cell *effect* (concept).
- **1839:** Grove builds the first *gas battery* (practical proof) → **Father of the Fuel Cell**.
- **1889:** Mond & Langer coin the term **"fuel cell"** + use platinum black catalyst.
- **1893:** Ostwald provides thermodynamic foundation (non-Carnot device).
- **1932–1959:** Bacon develops the **alkaline fuel cell (AFC)** with nickel electrodes.
- **1965–1972:** NASA Apollo program uses **AFC** for spacecraft power + drinking water.
- **Gemini program (1962):** First **PEMFC** by Grubb and Niedrach (GE).
- **1970s onward:** **PAFC, MCFC, SOFC, DMFC** developed for stationary and portable use.
- **1990s onward:** PEMFC commercialization for **transportation** (Ballard, Toyota Mirai).
- **Standard OCV of H₂/O₂ cell: $E^\circ = 1.229$ V** at 25 °C.
- **Theoretical efficiency: $\eta_{th} = \Delta G^\circ / \Delta H^\circ = 83\%$.**
- **Reversible heat: $T\Delta S = 48.7$ kJ/mol** at 25 °C.
- **Fuel cells are NOT heat engines** → not bound by Carnot limit.
- **Nernst equation: $E = E^\circ - (RT/nF)\ln Q$** — explains why pressurization helps.
- **Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol** — memorize the value.

> **Final Tip:** When asked "What is the significance of fuel cell history?" in KTU exams, frame your answer around **three E's** — **Efficiency** (83 % vs. ~40 % for ICE), **Emissions** (only water at point of use), and **Energy security** (hydrogen from diverse sources).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Cell (FC)** is a galvanic (electrochemical) energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, by means of a pair of redox reactions separated by an ion-conducting electrolyte. The classification of fuel cells into distinct "types" is fundamentally based on the **electrolyte material** employed, which dictates the operating temperature range, charge carrier ion, catalyst requirements, and suitable applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of fuel cells is a *Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme* learning outcome. The student is expected to differentiate the major fuel cell types based on electrolyte, ion conductor, operating temperature, efficiency, and applications. This forms the foundation for all subsequent modules on stack design, materials, and system integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Battery" Intuition

Imagine a **leaf on a tree** performing photosynthesis. A leaf takes in raw chemical ingredients (water, carbon dioxide), uses a *special internal membrane* (the thylakoid), and produces sugar (energy) without any combustion or moving parts. A fuel cell works in essentially the same way — it is a **"man-made leaf"**. The electrolyte acts like the thylakoid membrane: it strictly controls *which* charged particles (ions) can pass through. Electrons, being blocked by the membrane, are forced to take the "long way around" through an external wire — and that detour is what we call **electricity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Unlike a battery, a fuel cell does not run down or require recharging. As long as fuel and oxidant are supplied, it produces electricity continuously — making it a true *open system* energy converter.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are universally used in fuel cell analysis and must be memorized:

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \ \text{K}$, $P^{\circ} = 1 \ \text{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Standard Cell Potential of H₂/O₂ Cell:** $E^{\circ}_{cell} = \mathbf{1.229 \ V}$ (theoretical maximum at 25 °C)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f} = -237.13 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ at 25 °C
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f} = -285.83 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ at 25 °C
- **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency (LHV basis):** $\eta_{max} = \Delta G / \Delta H \approx \mathbf{83\%}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter:** The student will repeatedly use $F$, $R$, and $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ when deriving the **Nernst equation** and computing cell efficiencies — these are guaranteed marks in any KTU numerical question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Criteria for Fuel Cells

Fuel cells are classified along **five primary axes** (KTU examiners frequently test these discriminators):

1. **Electrolyte Type** — the dominant classification parameter.
2. **Operating Temperature** — low (≤100 °C), intermediate (100–250 °C), or high (>500 °C).
3. **Mobile Ion / Charge Carrier** — $\text{H}^+$, $\text{OH}^-$, $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, or $\text{O}^{2-}$.
4. **Fuel Type** — hydrogen, methanol, hydrocarbons, or bio-fuels.
5. **Application Domain** — automotive, stationary, portable, or space.

### 2.2 The "Big Five" Fuel Cell Technologies (Detailed Logic Steps)

#### 2.2.1 Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Solid polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion™ — perfluorosulfonic acid).
- **Mobile ion:** $\text{H}^+$ (proton).
- **Operating temperature:** 60–80 °C (low temperature).
- **Charge half-reactions:**
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Catalyst:** Platinum (expensive — major limitation).
- **Why this design?** Low temperature ⇒ fast start-up, suitable for **automotive (cars, buses)** applications.

#### 2.2.2 Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous KOH solution (typically 30–50 wt%).
- **Mobile ion:** $\text{OH}^-$.
- **Operating temperature:** 65–220 °C.
- **Charge half-reactions:**
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^$
- **Why?** Alkaline environment permits use of **non-precious metal catalysts** (Ag, Ni) — cheaper than PEMFC. Used by **NASA Apollo missions** (historic first space application).

#### 2.2.3 Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Concentrated liquid $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (≈100%) held in a SiC matrix.
- **Mobile ion:** $\text{H}^+$.
- **Operating temperature:** 150–200 °C.
- **Catalyst:** Pt (high loading tolerated by acid).
- **Why?** Higher temperature ⇒ improved CO tolerance compared to PEMFC. Mature technology used in **stationary CHP** (combined heat & power) units up to 400 kW.

#### 2.2.4 Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Molten alkali carbonate mixture (Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃) in a LiAlO₂ matrix.
- **Mobile ion:** $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$.
- **Operating temperature:** 600–700 °C (high temperature).
- **Charge half-reactions:**
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$
- **Why?** High temperature ⇒ **internal reforming** of natural gas possible; **no noble metal catalyst** required. Used for **large-scale stationary power** (MW class).

#### 2.2.5 Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)
- **Electrolyte:** Solid ceramic — typically Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ).
- **Mobile ion:** $\text{O}^{2-}$.
- **Operating temperature:** 800–1000 °C.
- **Charge half-reactions:**
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{O}^{2-}$
- **Why?** Highest electrical efficiency among all FCs (up to 60% LHV); tolerates sulfur and hydrocarbon fuels. Used in **utility-scale power plants**.

#### 2.2.6 Emerging / Specialized Variants (KTU Short-Answer Favourites)
- **Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC):** Liquid methanol fed directly; uses PEM-type electrolyte; portable electronics.
- **Solid Acid Fuel Cell (SAFC):** $\text{CsH}_2\text{PO}_4$ electrolyte; intermediate temp (200–300 °C).
- **Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC):** Bacteria catalyze oxidation of organic waste; produces electricity + treats wastewater.
- **Reversible / Regenerative Fuel Cell (RFC):** Operates in reverse (electrolysis mode) to regenerate H₂ from water.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting note:** All table cells use $\vert$ instead of literal `|` to prevent markdown table breaking. Memorize this entire table — it is a 14-mark KTU favorite.

| Parameter | PEMFC | AFC | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | KOH (aq) | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (liq) | Molten $(\text{Li/K})_2\text{CO}_3$ | YSZ ceramic |
| Mobile ion | $\text{H}^+$ | $\text{OH}^-$ | $\text{H}^+$ | $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ | $\text{O}^{2-}$ |
| Operating T (°C) | 60–80 | 65–220 | 150–200 | 600–700 | 800–1000 |
| Catalyst | Pt (high cost) | Ni / Ag (cheap) | Pt | Ni (no precious metal) | Perovskite (no precious metal) |
| Fuel (typical) | Pure $\text{H}_2$ | Pure $\text{H}_2$ | $\text{H}_2}$ / reformate | $\text{H}_2}$ / $\text{CH}_4}$ (internal reforming) | $\text{H}_2}$ / $\text{CH}_4}$ / CO |
| Electrical efficiency (LHV) | 50–60% | 60–70% | 36–42% | 45–55% | 50–60% |
| CO tolerance (ppm) | < 10 | < 10 | < 1000 | Fuel-side tolerant | Fully tolerant |
| Cogeneration use | Low-grade heat | Limited | Yes (200 °C heat) | Yes (400–600 °C) | Yes (high-grade) |
| Primary application | Automobiles, portable | Space, defense | Stationary CHP | Large stationary | Utility power |
| Key disadvantage | Expensive Pt; sensitive to CO | $\text{CO}_2$ poisoning (KOH reacts) | Acidic corrosion | Slow start-up; electrolyte corrosion | Slow start-up; thermal cycling |
| Power density ($\text{W cm}^{-2}$) | 0.5–1.0 | 0.1–0.3 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.1–0.2 | 0.2–0.4 |
| Start-up time | Seconds | Minutes | Minutes–hours | Hours | Hours |
| Cell voltage (typical) | 0.6–0.7 V | 0.7–0.9 V | 0.6–0.8 V | 0.7–0.9 V | 0.8–1.0 V |

### 2.4 Nernst Equation (The Master Equation of Fuel Cell Analysis)

The reversible cell voltage as a function of operating conditions is given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right)
$$

For a **standard hydrogen-air fuel cell at 1 atm and 25 °C**, with $n = 2$ electrons transferred per mole of $\text{H}_2$:

$$
E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{2(96485)} \ln \left( \frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing pressure (e.g., 3 atm operation in PEMFC vehicles) raises $E_{cell}$ logarithmically — this is why automotive FCs use **compressed hydrogen tanks at 350–700 bar**. Higher temperature, by contrast, *decreases* $E_{cell}$ (exothermic reaction favors lower T per Le Chatelier).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Transportation:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity — all PEMFC-powered passenger vehicles.
- **Stationary power:** Bloom Energy (SOFC) units deployed at eBay, Google, Equinix data centers.
- **Space:** Apollo, Space Shuttle Orbiter — AFC technology (100 kW class).
- **Portable electronics:** DMFC power packs for laptops, military field radios.
- **Waste-to-energy:** MFC technology in wastewater treatment plants (simultaneously generating electricity while purifying water).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for the H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

**Starting point — the overall cell reaction:**

$$
\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)
$$

**Step 1 — Free energy change of the cell reaction.**

The general thermodynamic relationship between Gibbs free energy change and cell potential is:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}
$$

At standard conditions:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell}
$$

**Step 2 — Incorporate the reaction quotient $Q$.**

For a general reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the relationship between $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

where the reaction quotient $Q$ for the H₂/O₂ reaction is:

$$
Q = \frac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{a_{\text{H}_2} \cdot a_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}
$$

For ideal gases, activities equal partial pressures (in atm). For liquid water, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 1$ if pure. Therefore:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}} \quad \text{(when product water is liquid)}
$$

If water leaves as vapor, include $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ in the denominator.

**Step 3 — Combine and eliminate $\Delta G$.**

Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell}$:

$$
-nFE_{cell} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $-nF$:**

$$
E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the explicit form of $Q$ and apply the sign rule for ln of inverse.**

Since $Q = 1 / (P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$, then $-\ln Q = \ln(P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$, giving:

$$
E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (Nernst Equation for H₂/O₂ cell):**

$$
E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right) \quad \text{(general, includes vapor)}
$$

**Step 7 — Insert numerical values for the standard case (25 °C, 1 atm, liquid water product, pure H₂ and O₂):**

With $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$, $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1$ atm:

$$
E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{2(96485)} \ln(1 \cdot 1^{1/2}) = 1.229 + 0 \ = \mathbf{1.229 \ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** Always show the value of $\frac{RT}{nF}$ at the temperature you use. At 25 °C, $\frac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V; at 80 °C (typical PEMFC), $\frac{RT}{F} = 0.02856$ V. Examiners award partial credit for this numerical substitution.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Nernst Calculation at Elevated Pressure and Temperature

**Problem:** A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 3$ atm, and liquid product water. Compute the open-circuit voltage (OCV).

**Step 1 — Convert temperature to Kelvin:**

$$
T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Nernst equation:**

$$
E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pre-factor:**

$$
\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2(96485)} = \frac{2936.41}{192970} = 0.01522 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm argument:**

$$
P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} = 3 \cdot 3^{1/2} = 3 \cdot 1.7321 = 5.1962
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the natural log:**

$$
\ln(5.1962) = 1.6477
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the voltage correction term:**

$$
\Delta E = 0.01522 \times 1.6477 = 0.02508 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 7 — Final open-circuit voltage:**

$$
E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.02508 = \mathbf{1.2541 \ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure effect decoded:** Operating at 3 atm raises the OCV by **≈ 25 mV** over the standard 1.229 V. This is the thermodynamic basis for *pressurized* fuel cell stacks in automotive applications. However, parasitic power for the compressor partially offsets this gain — a classic KTU "design tradeoff" question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cell Efficiency at Different Conditions

The **electrical efficiency** of a fuel cell (ideal case) is given by:

$$
\eta_{ideal} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{-nFE_{cell}}{\Delta H}
$$

Using the HHV and LHV values for hydrogen oxidation:

$$
\eta_{LHV} = \frac{237.13}{285.83} = 82.97\% \quad \text{(theoretical max at 25 °C)}
$$

$$
\eta_{HHV} = \frac{237.13}{286.02} = 82.92\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam trap:** The *maximum* theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell is **not 100%** — it is bounded by the second law of thermodynamics. About 17% of the chemical energy is unavoidably released as *heat* (entropy of reaction), not as electricity. This is why real FC systems achieve 50–60% electrical efficiency — not because they are poorly designed, but because of fundamental thermodynamic limits.

### 3.4 Derivations Summary Table (KTU 14-Mark Ready)

| Equation | Use case | KTU marks weightage |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Link free energy and voltage | 2 marks |
| Nernst: $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Variable P, T conditions | 4 marks |
| $\eta_{max} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ | Maximum efficiency | 2 marks |
| $V_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack design | 1 mark |
| $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I$ | Power output | 1 mark |
| Faraday's law: $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ | Fuel consumption rate | 2 marks |
| Activation overpotential: $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha nF} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Voltage losses | 2 marks |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **"Differentiate between a fuel cell and a battery. List any two distinguishing features." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Fuel supply:** A fuel cell is an *open* energy system that continuously consumes an externally supplied fuel (e.g., H₂) and oxidant, producing electricity as long as the reactants are fed. A battery is a *closed* energy storage system with a finite amount of active material sealed inside; it stops delivering current when that material is exhausted.
2. **Recharging:** A battery needs recharging (reverse current) to restore its chemical state. A fuel cell does not require recharging — only refueling.
3. **Operating temperature and life:** Fuel cells typically operate at steady temperatures for thousands of hours with continuous replacement of reactants; batteries exhibit gradual voltage decay over charge-discharge cycles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **"State the electrolyte, mobile ion, and one application each for PEMFC and SOFC." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | PEMFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Solid polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion) | Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) ceramic |
| Mobile ion | $\text{H}^+$ (proton) | $\text{O}^{2-}$ (oxide ion) |
| Application | Automotive vehicles (cars, buses) | Utility-scale stationary power plants |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Classify the major types of fuel cells based on electrolyte and operating temperature. For each, state the electrolyte, mobile ion, operating temperature, and one characteristic application. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operates at 80 °C with partial pressures $P_{\text{H}_2} = 2.5$ atm and $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21$ atm (air at 1 atm partial pressure, with cathode pressurized to 1.2 atm total). The product water is in liquid form. Calculate the open-circuit voltage using the Nernst equation. Take $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Mobile Ion | Operating T | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PEMFC | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | $\text{H}^+$ | 60–80 °C | Automobiles (cars, buses) |
| AFC | KOH (aq) | $\text{OH}^-$ | 65–220 °C | Spacecraft (Apollo) |
| PAFC | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (liquid) | $\text{H}^+$ | 150–200 °C | Stationary CHP units |
| MCFC | Molten (Li/K)₂CO₃ | $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ | 600–700 °C | Large MW power plants |
| SOFC | YSZ (ceramic) | $\text{O}^{2-}$ | 800–1000 °C | Utility-scale generation |

*(1 mark each for correctly identifying electrolyte + ion + T + app per row; 5 rows = 5 marks; remaining 2 marks for introductory classification criterion statement and correct grouping into low/intermediate/high T bands.)*

#### Part (b) — Nernst Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $T = 80 °C = 353.15$ K; $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V; $P_{\text{H}_2} = 2.5$ atm; $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21 \times 1.2 = 0.252$ atm (since cathode air pressurized to 1.2 atm total, partial pressure scales linearly).

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation:**

$$
E_{cell} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pre-factor $RT / (nF)$:**

$$
\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2(96485)} = \frac{2936.41}{192970} = 0.01522 \text{ V}
$$

*(1 mark for the pre-factor)*

**Step 3 — Compute the argument of ln:**

$$
P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} = 2.5 \cdot (0.252)^{1/2} = 2.5 \cdot 0.5020 = 1.2550
$$

*(1 mark for the argument)*

**Step 4 — Compute ln:**

$$
\ln(1.2550) = 0.2271
$$

*(1 mark for ln value)*

**Step 5 — Compute the voltage correction:**

$$
\Delta E = 0.01522 \times 0.2271 = 0.003457 \text{ V}
$$

*(1 mark for multiplication)*

**Step 6 — Final open-circuit voltage:**

$$
E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.003457 = \mathbf{1.2325 \ V}
$$

*(2 marks for final answer with proper units and correct rounding)*

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and half-reactions of a **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)**. State two advantages and two disadvantages. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The Gibbs free energy change for the hydrogen oxidation reaction at 25 °C is $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13$ kJ/mol. Calculate the standard cell potential $E^{\circ}_{cell}$. If 2 moles of electrons are transferred per mole of hydrogen, what is the standard enthalpy change $\Delta H^{\circ}$ corresponding to an efficiency of 78%? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — PAFC Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Two porous electrodes (anode and cathode) made of carbon-supported Pt catalyst.
- Concentrated phosphoric acid (≈100%) held in a porous SiC matrix as the electrolyte.
- Operates at 150–200 °C (intermediate temperature).
- Bipolar plates distribute reactant gases and collect current.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Hydrogen is fed to the anode, air to the cathode. H₂ is catalytically oxidized to produce protons and electrons. Protons migrate through the phosphoric acid electrolyte; electrons are forced through the external circuit. At the cathode, O₂ combines with protons and electrons to form water and heat.

**Half-reactions (1 mark):**
- Anode: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$

**Advantages (0.5 each, 1 mark total):**
1. Higher CO tolerance than PEMFC (up to ~1%).
2. Mature, commercialized technology with long operational lifetimes (>40,000 hours demonstrated).

**Disadvantages (0.5 each, 1 mark total):**
1. Phosphoric acid is corrosive; requires acid-resistant stack materials.
2. Lower electrical efficiency (~40%) compared to MCFC/SOFC; large and heavy.

#### Part (b) — Thermodynamic Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Use the Gibbs free energy relation:**

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell}
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = \frac{237130 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}
$$

*(2 marks for equation setup and substitution)*

**Step 2 — Compute the standard cell potential:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = \frac{237130}{192970} = \mathbf{1.229 \ V}
$$

*(1 mark for numerical answer)*

**Step 3 — Use the efficiency relation to find $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

$$
\eta = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} \Rightarrow \Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\eta}
$$

*(2 marks for relation)*

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

$$
\Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{-237.13}{0.78} = \mathbf{-304.01 \ kJ/mol}
$$

*(2 marks for final answer with proper sign and units)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is *negative* for a spontaneous reaction; ensure the negative sign is correctly carried through. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing kJ and J inside the same equation (e.g., writing 237.13 instead of 237130). Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Forgetting $n = 2$:** The H₂/O₂ cell transfers 2 electrons per mole of hydrogen — students often use $n = 1$ mistakenly. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 4–5 significant figures until the final step to avoid cumulative rounding errors. Marks deducted: 0.5.
> 5. **Confusing LHV/HHV:** In efficiency questions, state explicitly which heating value is being assumed. Marks deducted: 1.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Five major FC types:** PEMFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC — distinguished primarily by **electrolyte** and **mobile ion**.
- ✅ **Mobile ion mnemonic:** **"PAPA drinks Cold Orange Soda"** → **P**EMFC: $\text{H}^+$, **A**FC: $\text{OH}^-$, **P**AFC: $\text{H}^+$, **M**CFC: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, **S**OFC: $\text{O}^{2-}$.
- ✅ **Operating temperature order (lowest to highest):** PEMFC < AFC < PAFC < MCFC < SOFC.
- ✅ **Catalyst cost order (cheapest to most expensive):** SOFC/MCFC (no precious metal) < AFC (Ni/Ag) < PAFC/PEMFC (Pt required).
- ✅ **$E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229$ V** is THE number for the H₂/O₂ cell at standard conditions — non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Nernst equation** — pressure increases voltage, temperature decreases voltage.
- ✅ **Maximum theoretical efficiency** of an H₂/O₂ cell ≈ **83%** (LHV basis).
- ✅ **AFC** — first fuel cell used in space (Apollo). KOH is highly sensitive to CO₂ (forms K₂CO₃, blocking pores).
- ✅ **MCFC and SOFC** — only types capable of **internal reforming** of hydrocarbons due to high T.
- ✅ **PEMFC** — used in **all major automotive FC vehicles** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity).
- ✅ **Mobile ion direction:** In PEMFC, $\text{H}^+$ moves anode → cathode. In AFC, $\text{OH}^-$ moves cathode → anode. (Opposite directions — a common MCQ trap.)
- ✅ **Three standard constants to commit:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V.
- ✅ **For the "type" question on KTU:** Always state **electrolyte + mobile ion + operating T + application** in that order — this is the examiner's marking template.
- ✅ **Voltage loss sources** (preview for Module 2): activation, ohmic, concentration — total cell voltage $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$.
- ✅ **Faraday's law for fuel consumption:** $m_{fuel} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ — used to compute hydrogen consumption rate (g/s) for a given current.

> **End of Notes — PECHT634 / Module 1 / Types of Fuel Cells**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education standards. Aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels 1–4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
