> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fuel Cell and Conventional Processes - Comparison # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fuel Cell (FC)** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into electrical energy, with **water and heat as the only by-products**, as long as fuel is supplied. Unlike batteries, fuel cells do not require recharging — they generate electricity continuously provided the reactants are fed. A **Conventional Process** (for electricity generation or propulsion) refers to the traditional thermomechanical route used in **Internal Combustion Engines (ICE)**, **Gas Turbines**, **Steam Turbines**, and **Rankine/Brayton cycles**, where chemical energy is first converted to **thermal energy** (via combustion), then to **mechanical energy** (via expansion of gases), and finally to **electrical energy** (via a generator). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The core distinction examined by the Board is the **direct (single-step)** electrochemical conversion in fuel cells versus the **indirect (multi-step)** thermomechanical conversion in conventional processes. This single distinction cascades into differences in efficiency, emissions, noise, and modularity. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Imagine two ways to boil water for your morning tea:** - **Conventional Process (ICE/Turbine):** You light a match → flame heats a coil → coil heats water → steam pushes a piston → piston turns a shaft → shaft turns a generator → electricity. **Six intermediate steps**, and you lose energy as **hot exhaust gases**, friction, and noise at every step. - **Fuel Cell:** You simply pour hydrogen and oxygen on either side of a special membrane → electricity flows directly out of the terminals, with water dripping out the side. **No flame. No piston. No roaring turbine.** The fuel and oxidant "shake hands" through chemistry, and out pops electricity. > [!NOTE] > **Key Take-home Analogy:** A fuel cell is to an engine what a **lightning bug (bioluminescence)** is to a **bonfire**. Both produce light/energy from chemistry, but one does it *cool, quiet, and direct* — the other burns hot, loud, and wasteful. ## 1.3 Foundational Physical Metrics The following constants and metrics are referenced throughout this comparison: - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$ - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - **Standard Hydrogen Enthalpy of Formation:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f,H_2O(l)} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (HHV) - **Standard Hydrogen Gibbs Free Energy:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f,H_2O(l)} = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$ - **Carnot (Ideal Thermal) Efficiency Upper Bound:** $\eta_{Carnot} \le 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$ > [!NOTE] > The numerical gap between $\Delta H = 285.8$ and $\Delta G = 237.13$ kJ/mol is the **theoretical maximum efficiency** of a hydrogen fuel cell at standard conditions — approximately **83%** if based on HHV. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Energy Conversion Pathway: Why It Matters ### Conventional Process (Multi-Step Path) $$\text{Chemical} \xrightarrow{\text{Combustion}} \text{Thermal} \xrightarrow{\text{Expansion}} \text{Mechanical} \xrightarrow{\text{Generator}} \text{Electrical}$$ - **Step 1 — Combustion:** Chemical → Thermal. *Why inefficient?* Second Law limits how much heat can be turned into work. - **Step 2 — Heat-to-Work:** Thermal → Mechanical. *Capped by Carnot efficiency.* - **Step 3 — Generator:** Mechanical → Electrical. *~95–98% efficient (relatively small loss).* > **The bottleneck is Step 2.** Even if Step 1 and Step 3 were 100% efficient, Step 2 is bounded by the **Carnot limit**, which for typical engines ($T_h = 800\text{–}1000$ K, $T_c = 300$ K) gives $\eta \le 50\text{–}70\%$. ### Fuel Cell (Single-Step Path) $$\text{Chemical} \xrightarrow{\text{Electrochemistry}} \text{Electrical (Direct)}$$ - **Why higher efficiency?** No intermediate thermal stage → **Carnot limit does not apply**. - The only theoretical limit is the ratio of **Gibbs free energy to enthalpy** of the overall reaction. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Application / Limit** | |-------------|-----------------|--------------------------| | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Carnot (ideal heat engine) efficiency | Upper bound for **conventional** thermal processes | | $\eta_{FC,HHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}}$ | Fuel cell efficiency based on HHV | Theoretical max for H₂/O₂ cell; $\approx 83\%$ at 25 °C | | $\eta_{FC,LHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{LHV}}$ | Fuel cell efficiency based on LHV | Used when product water is vapor (high-T cells); $\approx 94\%$ at 25 °C | | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz relation | Defines the electrical work extractable from a reaction | | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Standard cell potential | Voltage produced per mole of electrons ($n = 2$ for H₂) | | $E_{Nernst} = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{p_{H_2}\, p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}}$ | Nernst equation | Cell voltage variation with $T$, $p$, and partial pressures | | $P_{el} = n \cdot F \cdot I \cdot V_{cell}$ | Electrical power output | Used in performance calculations | | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{FC} \cdot \eta_{BOP} \cdot \eta_{inverter}$ | System-level efficiency | BOP = balance-of-plant; inverter = DC–AC conversion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare" the efficiencies, always write BOTH the Carnot expression and the fuel cell expression side by side, then plug in numbers. Marks are awarded for the **derivation**, not just the final percentage. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stationary Power:** Fuel cells in combined heat and power (CHP) mode achieve **80–90% total efficiency**, while grid electricity from coal plants sits around **33–40%**. - **Automotive:** Toyota Mirai FCEV uses a fuel cell powertrain with system efficiency **~50–60%** vs. gasoline ICE at **~25–35%** (well-to-wheel). - **Space:** NASA has used alkaline fuel cells since the 1960s (Apollo, Space Shuttle) — the only practical way to generate both electricity and drinking water in orbit. - **Military Submarines:** Siemens PEMFC systems enable air-independent propulsion (AIP) — impossible with conventional diesel engines underwater. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Efficiency of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell **Given Reaction (Overall):** $$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(l)$$ **Step 1 — Identify the thermodynamic state functions at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm).** We need the **enthalpy of reaction** $\Delta H$ and the **Gibbs free energy of reaction** $\Delta G$ for the formation of liquid water. From standard thermodynamic tables: $$\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad \text{(Higher Heating Value, HHV)}$$ $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > **Logic:** The negative sign indicates an **exothermic** reaction; energy is *released* to the surroundings. The absolute value is the energy content we can extract. **Step 2 — Express the maximum reversible electrical work.** By the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a galvanic cell**, the maximum non-PV (pressure–volume) electrical work obtainable from a constant-$T$, constant-$P$ electrochemical process equals the change in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{el,max} = -\Delta G$$ This is the **fundamental reason** fuel cells bypass the Carnot limit. The Gibbs free energy is, by definition, the *isothermal, isobaric* maximum work — there is no temperature gradient involved. **Step 3 — Define the fuel cell thermodynamic efficiency.** The efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical work extracted to the total chemical energy released (enthalpy of combustion): $$\eta_{FC} = \frac{W_{el,max}}{\vert \Delta H \vert} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$ > **Logic:** We take magnitudes (absolute values) because both $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are negative, but efficiency is a positive ratio. **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** $$\eta_{FC,HHV} = \frac{237.13}{285.8}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{FC,HHV} \approx 0.8297 \text{ or } 82.97\%}$$ > **Interpretation:** At standard conditions, a hydrogen fuel cell can theoretically convert **~83%** of the chemical energy in H₂ into electricity — *in a single step, with no moving parts*. **Step 5 — Alternative LHV-based expression (water as vapor).** If the product water leaves as **steam** (relevant for high-temperature fuel cells like SOFC), the enthalpy of reaction uses the Lower Heating Value: $$\Delta H_{LHV} = \Delta H_{HHV} - \Delta H_{vap,H_2O} = 285.8 - 44.0 = 241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Then: $$\eta_{FC,LHV} = \frac{237.13}{241.8}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{FC,LHV} \approx 0.9807 \text{ or } 98.07\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does LHV-based efficiency exceed 100%?** It is *not* perpetual motion. The "missing" 2% comes from **entropy** ($T\Delta S$ term), which represents heat that must be rejected to the surroundings to keep the process reversible. The cell is producing both electricity AND useful heat. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot Efficiency of a Conventional ICE (Comparison Benchmark) **Given:** A spark-ignition engine with combustion temperature $T_h = 1000$ K and exhaust temperature $T_c = 350$ K. **Step 1 — Write the Carnot expression.** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{350}{1000} = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 65\%}$$ **Step 3 — Apply real-world derating.** In practice, real engines operate at **30–40% of the Carnot limit** due to friction, incomplete combustion, heat losses, and finite-rate processes. A typical gasoline ICE achieves: $$\eta_{ICE,real} \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.35 \quad (25\text{–}35\%)$$ A combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT) reaches $\approx 60\%$ in CHP mode. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Fuel Cell (PEMFC, SOFC)** | **Conventional (ICE / Turbine)** | **Winner** | |---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|------------| | **Conversion Steps** | 1 (chemical → electrical) | 3+ (chemical → thermal → mechanical → electrical) | Fuel Cell | | **Theoretical Efficiency** | 83% (H₂/O₂, HHV) | 65% (Carnot, 1000 K/350 K) | Fuel Cell | | **Practical Efficiency** | 50–60% (system), 80–90% (CHP) | 25–35% (ICE), 35–40% (CCGT) | Fuel Cell | | **Carnot-Limited?** | No | Yes | Fuel Cell | | **CO₂ Emissions (Tailpipe)** | Zero (pure H₂) | High (CO₂, CO, HC, NOx) | Fuel Cell | | **Noise (dB)** | ~55 dB (quiet) | 75–95 dB (loud) | Fuel Cell | | **Moving Parts** | None (static device) | Hundreds (pistons, valves, crankshaft) | Fuel Cell | | **Modularity / Scalability** | Excellent (stack design) | Poor (single large unit scales best) | Fuel Cell | | **Cold Start Time** | Slow (PEMFC: 1–5 min) | Fast (seconds) | Conventional | | **Power Density (W/kg)** | 500–1000 (PEMFC stack) | 500–1500 (ICE) | Comparable | | **Operating Temperature** | 60–1000 °C (by type) | 800–1000 °C (combustion) | Comparable | | **Fuel Flexibility** | H₂ (pure); reformed HC, alcohols | Gasoline, diesel, NG, biofuels | Conventional | | **Capital Cost ($/kW)** | $50–150 (PEMFC, 2024) | $30–80 (ICE, mature) | Conventional | | **Lifetime / Durability** | 5000–20000 h (transport) | 5000–10000 h (transport) | Comparable | | **Maintenance** | Low (no lubrication) | High (oil, filters, spark plugs) | Fuel Cell | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Standard:** When asked "compare fuel cells and conventional processes," examiners expect **at least 6 comparison points** with quantitative support, not just qualitative statements. Use the matrix structure above as a template. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Conversion Pathway Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **fundamental structural difference** between the two energy conversion paradigms. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Chemical Energy
H2 + O2]:::input --> B1[Electrochemical
Reaction]:::fcbox B1 --> C1[Electrical Energy]:::fcout B1 --> D1[Heat
byproduct]:::fcheat B1 --> E1[Water
byproduct]:::fcwater A2[Chemical Energy
Fuel + Air]:::input --> B2[Combustion]:::convbox B2 --> C2[Thermal Energy]:::convheat C2 --> D2[Mechanical Energy
Piston or Turbine]:::convmech D2 --> E2[Electrical Energy
Generator]:::convout C2 --> F2[Exhaust Heat Loss]:::convloss D2 --> G2[Friction Loss]:::convloss classDef input fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000 classDef fcbox fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 classDef fcout fill:#86efac,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef fcheat fill:#fde68a,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#000 classDef fcwater fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0284c7,color:#000 classDef convbox fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000 classDef convheat fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef convmech fill:#f87171,stroke:#991b1b,color:#fff classDef convout fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#fff classDef convloss fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The upper path (green) shows the **single-step** fuel cell pathway producing 3 outputs. The lower path (red) shows the **multi-step** conventional pathway producing electrical energy as the *final* of 3 conversions, with two intermediate loss streams (exhaust heat, friction). ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: System-Level Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FC["FUEL CELL SYSTEM"] direction TB F1[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> F2[Humidifier] F2 --> F3[FC Stack
Electrochemical Core] F3 --> F4[DC-DC Converter] F4 --> F5[Inverter DC to AC] F5 --> F6[Load AC Load] F3 --> F7[Thermal Management
Cooling Loop] F3 --> F8[Water Management
Separator] end subgraph CONV["CONVENTIONAL SYSTEM"] direction TB C1[Fuel Tank] --> C2[Feed Pump] C2 --> C3[Engine or Turbine
Combustion Chamber] C3 --> C4[Pistons or Blades
Mechanical Train] C4 --> C5[Alternator or Generator] C5 --> F6 C3 --> C6[Exhaust and Emissions] C4 --> C7[Lubrication and Cooling] end classDef fcNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 classDef convNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000 classDef sharedNode fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000 class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8 fcNode class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7 convNode class F6 sharedNode ``` > **Key architectural insight:** Both systems converge on the **load** (F6), but the fuel cell path requires **electrochemical conditioning** (humidification, DC-DC, inverter) while the conventional path requires **mechanical conditioning** (pistons, alternators, lubrication). The fuel cell has *more electronics but no rotating machinery*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Why is the efficiency of a fuel cell not limited by the Carnot cycle? Justify with the relevant thermodynamic relationship."** **Model Answer:** The efficiency of a conventional heat engine is limited by the **Carnot efficiency** because the conversion path is: $$\text{Chemical} \rightarrow \text{Thermal} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical} \rightarrow \text{Electrical}$$ This intermediate thermal step requires a **temperature gradient** ($\Delta T = T_h - T_c$), and the Second Law of Thermodynamics bounds the work extractable to: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$$ A fuel cell, however, converts chemical energy **directly** to electrical energy via an **isothermal, isobaric electrochemical reaction**. The maximum electrical work extractable is given by the **change in Gibbs free energy**: $$W_{el,max} = -\Delta G = -(\Delta H - T\Delta S)$$ Since no temperature gradient is involved, the Carnot limit does not apply. The theoretical efficiency is: $$\eta_{FC} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$ For the H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions: $\eta_{FC,HHV} = 237.13/285.8 \approx 83\%$, which **exceeds the Carnot limit** for any practical $T_h/T_c$ ratio achievable in engines. ✓ **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"List any six points of comparison between fuel cells and conventional IC engines."** **Model Answer (tabular form expected):** | **S. No.** | **Parameter** | **Fuel Cell** | **IC Engine** | |------------|----------------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | Energy conversion | Single-step (direct) | Multi-step (via heat) | | 2 | Efficiency (practical) | 50–60% (system) | 25–35% | | 3 | Emissions | Water only (pure H₂) | CO₂, CO, NOx, HC | | 4 | Moving parts | None | Many (pistons, valves) | | 5 | Noise level | Low (~55 dB) | High (75–95 dB) | | 6 | Maintenance | Low | High (oil, filters, spark plugs) | ✓ **(3 marks for 6 valid comparison points)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., the student answers EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B"). Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical efficiency of a hydrogen fuel cell based on the Higher Heating Value (HHV). Calculate the efficiency at 25 °C. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare fuel cells and conventional power plants with respect to (i) efficiency, (ii) emissions, (iii) noise, (iv) modularity, and (v) fuel flexibility. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of HHV-based fuel cell efficiency (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the overall cell reaction:** $$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$ **Step 2 — State the thermodynamic data at 25 °C, 1 atm:** $$\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol (HHV)}$$ $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Express the maximum reversible electrical work:** By definition of Gibbs free energy at constant $T$ and $P$: $$W_{el,max} = -\Delta G^{\circ} = 237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 4 — Define and compute the fuel cell efficiency:** $$\eta_{FC,HHV} = \frac{W_{el,max}}{\vert \Delta H \vert} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$ Substituting: $$\eta_{FC,HHV} = \frac{237.13}{285.8} = 0.8297 \approx 82.97\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{FC,HHV} \approx 83\%}$$ ✓ **Marks allocation:** Reaction (1) + Data (1) + Work expression (2) + Efficiency formula + calculation (3) = **7 marks** **(b) Five-point comparison (7 marks):** | **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Fuel Cell** | **Conventional** | |-----------|----------------|----------------|------------------| | (i) | **Efficiency** | 50–60% (system), 80–90% (CHP) | 25–35% (ICE), 35–40% (CCGT) | | (ii) | **Emissions** | Only H₂O (zero CO₂ if pure H₂) | CO₂, CO, NOx, SOx, unburnt HC, PM | | (iii) | **Noise** | ~55 dB (silent operation) | 75–95 dB (loud combustion + mechanical) | | (iv) | **Modularity** | Excellent — add cells to stack | Poor — economy of scale, single large unit optimal | | (v) | **Fuel Flexibility** | H₂ (best); reformate, methanol, NG with reformer | Gasoline, diesel, NG, biofuels, LPG (high flexibility) | ✓ **Marks allocation:** 5 parameters × ~1.4 marks each = **7 marks** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** A fuel cell operates with hydrogen and oxygen. Given $\Delta G = -237 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta H = -286 \text{ kJ/mol}$, calculate (i) the standard cell potential, and (ii) the theoretical efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the limitations of conventional energy conversion processes. How does the fuel cell overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Numerical calculation (7 marks):** **(i) Standard cell potential:** For the H₂/O₂ reaction, the number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂ is $n = 2$ (each H atom gives up 1 electron; 2 H atoms per H₂ molecule). Using the relation: $$E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$ Substituting: $$E^{\circ} = -\frac{-237 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$ $$E^{\circ} = \frac{237000}{192970}$$ $$\boxed{E^{\circ} \approx 1.23 \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) Theoretical efficiency:** $$\eta_{FC,HHV} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{237}{286}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{FC,HHV} \approx 0.8287 \text{ or } 82.87\%}$$ ✓ **Marks allocation:** Formula (1) + Substitution (2) + Answer $E°$ (1) = **4 marks** for (i); Formula (1) + Calculation (2) = **3 marks** for (ii) **(b) Limitations of conventional processes & how fuel cells overcome them (7 marks):** **Limitations of Conventional Processes:** 1. **Carnot efficiency ceiling:** Thermal stage caps efficiency at $\eta \le 1 - T_c/T_h$, requiring very high $T_h$ for any meaningful efficiency. **(2 marks)** 2. **Multi-step losses:** Each conversion step (combustion → heat → mechanical → electrical) introduces entropy production, friction, and heat losses. Typical real efficiencies are **25–40%**, far below Carnot. **(2 marks)** 3. **High emissions & pollution:** Combustion produces CO₂ (greenhouse), NOx (smog), CO (toxic), unburnt hydrocarbons, and particulates. **(1 mark)** 4. **Noise, vibration, wear:** Moving mechanical parts (pistons, valves, bearings) generate noise, vibration, and require regular maintenance. **(1 mark)** 5. **Poor part-load efficiency:** Engines and turbines are most efficient at design load; efficiency drops significantly at partial load. **(1 mark)** **How Fuel Cells Overcome These:** | **Limitation** | **Fuel Cell Solution** | |----------------|--------------------------| | Carnot limit | Direct electrochemical conversion — no thermal stage | | Multi-step losses | Single-step conversion → fewer entropy losses | | Emissions | Zero CO₂, NOx, CO (with pure H₂); only H₂O and heat | | Noise/mechanical wear | Static device, no moving parts in the stack | | Part-load efficiency | Better part-load performance (no throttle losses) | ✓ **Marks allocation:** 5 limitations (~5 marks) + 5 solutions (~2 marks) = **7 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the sign of $\Delta G$.** Always write $\Delta G = -237.13$ kJ/mol; the negative sign indicates spontaneity. A common error is writing $\eta = \Delta H / \Delta G$ (inverted!). > 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV.** Use HHV when product water is *liquid* (low-T cells, e.g., PEMFC at 80 °C); use LHV when product water is *vapor* (high-T cells, e.g., SOFC at 800 °C). Board expects you to state which one. > 3. **Writing Carnot efficiency for fuel cells.** This is a fatal conceptual error. The Carnot limit applies ONLY when there is a temperature gradient converting heat to work. > 4. **Missing the $n = 2$ factor** in $E° = -\Delta G / nF$ when computing cell potential. > 5. **Comparing only efficiency, ignoring emissions, noise, modularity, and fuel flexibility.** Board expects a *holistic* comparison (at least 5–6 points). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Fuel Cell vs Conventional Processes** - ☐ **Core distinction:** Fuel cell = single-step electrochemical; Conventional = multi-step thermomechanical. - ☐ **Carnot limit applies ONLY to conventional** (heat-engine) processes, not to fuel cells. - ☐ **Maximum fuel cell efficiency** is $\eta_{FC,HHV} = \Delta G / \Delta H \approx 83\%$ at 25 °C for H₂/O₂. - ☐ **LHV-based efficiency** $\approx 94\text{–}98\%$ — used when product water is vapor. - ☐ **Standard cell potential** for H₂/O₂ cell: $E^{\circ} = 1.23$ V (from $\Delta G = -237.13$ kJ/mol, $n = 2$). - ☐ **Real-world efficiency:** Fuel cell 50–60% (system) > ICE 25–35%. - ☐ **Emissions:** Fuel cell → H₂O only (pure H₂); ICE → CO₂, CO, NOx, HC, PM. - ☐ **Noise:** Fuel cell ~55 dB; ICE 75–95 dB. - ☐ **No moving parts** in fuel cell stack (no friction, low maintenance). - ☐ **Modularity:** Fuel cell scales linearly by adding cells to stack; conventional scales by economy of size. - ☐ **Fuel flexibility:** Conventional (gasoline, diesel, NG) > Fuel cell (H₂ best; others need reformer). - ☐ **Cold start:** Conventional (seconds) > Fuel cell (1–5 min for PEMFC). - ☐ **Capital cost:** Conventional ($30–80/kW) < Fuel cell ($50–150/kW) — fuel cells still cost more. - ☐ **Formula to memorize cold:** $\eta_{FC} = \Delta G / \Delta H$, $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G / (nF)$, $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_c/T_h$. - ☐ **Remember the limits:** Fuel cells do NOT violate the Second Law — they simply bypass the thermal stage where the Carnot bound is most restrictive. - ☐ **Key application areas:** Stationary CHP, FCEV (transport), space (NASA), submarines (AIP), portable electronics.

topics.title: Energy and Power Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Power Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermodynamic Energy Relations in Fuel Cell Context

The **Energy and Power Relations** form the foundational thermodynamic framework upon which the entire operation, performance evaluation, and efficiency analysis of fuel cells are built. Every electrochemical reaction inside a fuel cell—whether it is the Hydrogen-Oxygen reaction in a PEMFC or the Methanol oxidation in a DMFC—is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy conservation) and the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (entropy and directionality).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Energy and Power Relations in fuel cell technology refers to the quantitative interrelationship between the **thermodynamic state functions** (Internal Energy $U$, Enthalpy $H$, Gibbs Free Energy $G$, Helmholtz Free Energy $A$, and Entropy $S$) and the **path functions** (Heat $Q$ and Work $W$), expressed through the change in these functions during an electrochemical reaction, ultimately determining the **maximum reversible electrical work** extractable from a fuel cell.

### 1.2 The Four Primary Energy Forms Encountered

| Energy Form | Mathematical Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Internal Energy | $U$ | Total microscopic kinetic + potential energy of molecules | **J (Joule)** |
| Enthalpy | $H$ | Energy content at constant pressure (heat content) | **J** |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $G$ | Maximum non-expansion (electrical) work at constant $T,P$ | **J** |
| Heat (transferred) | $Q$ | Energy transfer due to temperature gradient | **J** |
| Work (transferred) | $W$ | Energy transfer due to force/displacement or electrical | **J** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Waterfall of Energy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Energy as a Mountain Reservoir**
> Imagine a mountain reservoir holding water at a high elevation. This is your **chemical energy** stored in the fuel (e.g., H₂). The water flows down through a hydroelectric turbine (the **fuel cell**), and depending on the design:
> - The *gross head* of water = **Enthalpy ($\Delta H$)** — the total energy content released.
> - The *useful electrical energy harvested* = **Gibbs Free Energy ($\Delta G$)** — the part that can do non-expansion (electrical) work.
> - The *turbulent splashes and friction losses* = **$T\Delta S$ (irreversible losses)** — the energy dissipated as heat that cannot be converted to electricity.
> - The water reaching the downstream lake = **Unrecoverable energy**.
>
> The **efficiency** of the fuel cell is therefore the ratio of the useful electrical output to the gross chemical energy input — analogous to the ratio of electricity generated to total water-head energy available.

### 1.4 Power vs. Energy — A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy** ($E$) is a *state quantity* measured in **Joules (J)** or **Watt-hours (Wh)**.
> **Power** ($P$) is the *rate* of energy transfer measured in **Watts (W) = J/s**.
> A fuel cell's *capacity* is measured in energy (kWh), but its *performance* is measured in power density (W/cm²).

$$P = \frac{dE}{dt} = V_{cell} \times I$$

The standard metric for fuel cell power density is **mW/cm²** or **kW/m²** at a specified current density.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 First Law of Thermodynamics for Fuel Cell Systems

The First Law for a closed reacting system in a fuel cell is expressed through the change in internal energy, incorporating heat and all forms of work:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

For an **open flow system** (the practical reality of fuel cells, where reactants flow in and products flow out continuously), the **First Law is reformulated using Enthalpy**:

$$\Delta H = Q - W_{non-PV}$$

This is the most critical form for fuel cell analysis because:
- Fuel cells operate at **approximately constant pressure** (1 atm typical).
- The only useful work is **electrical (non-PV) work**.
- Hence $W = W_{electrical}$ and $W_{PV} = P\Delta V$ is not the desired output.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Enthalpy-Internal Energy Relationship

Starting from the definition of enthalpy:

$$H = U + PV$$

For a process at constant pressure, the change is:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + P\Delta V$$

Substituting $\Delta U = Q - W$ and recognizing that at constant pressure, $P\Delta V$ is the pressure-volume work:

$$\Delta H = Q_P - W_{non-PV}$$

This shows that at constant pressure, the heat absorbed equals the change in enthalpy minus non-PV work. For a fuel cell with only electrical work, this becomes the **energy balance of the cell**.

### 2.3 The Four Fundamental Thermodynamic Relations (Maxwell's Framework)

These are the **Gibbs fundamental equations** that interrelate $U, H, G, A$:

$$dU = TdS - PdV$$
$$dH = TdS + VdP$$
$$dG = -SdT + VdP$$
$$dA = -SdT - PdV$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter for fuel cells:**
> - $dG = -SdT + VdP$ shows that at **constant $T$ and $P$** (fuel cell conditions), the **change in Gibbs Free Energy equals the maximum non-expansion (electrical) work**:
> $$W_{max,elec} = -\Delta G$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam (PECHT634 — Module 1)**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law (closed system) | J or kJ |
| 2 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition | J or kJ |
| 3 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$ | Enthalpy change | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$ | Max electrical work | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs-Helmholtz relation | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $W_{elec} = n \cdot F \cdot E_{rev}$ | Electrical work from $n$ moles electrons | J (with $F = 96485$ C/mol) |
| 8 | $E_{rev} = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Reversible (Nernst) cell voltage | V |
| 9 | $E_{thermo,HHV} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage (HHV basis) | V |
| 10 | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Power output of fuel cell | W |
| 11 | $P_{density} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{electrode}}$ | Power density (per unit area) | W/cm² |
| 12 | $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + n \cdot h_{fg} \cdot M_{water}$ | Relation between HHV and LHV | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$ | Voltage efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{Faraday}$ | Overall fuel cell efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Values for Memorization:**
> - Faraday's Constant $F = 96485$ C/mol $\approx 96500$ C/mol
> - Universal Gas Constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - Standard Temperature $T = 298.15$ K (25 °C)
> - Standard Pressure $P = 1$ atm $= 101.325$ kPa

### 2.5 Heating Values: HHV vs LHV

The **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** assumes the water product is in *liquid* form (latent heat recovered). The **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** assumes water is in *vapor* form (latent heat lost to exhaust).

For the hydrogen-oxygen reaction:
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H_{HHV} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)} \quad \Delta H_{LHV} = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The difference is the enthalpy of vaporization of water ($h_{fg} = 44$ kJ/mol at 25°C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a numerical is given, check whether $\Delta G$, $\Delta H_{HHV}$, or $\Delta H_{LHV}$ is specified. The choice determines whether you compute $E_{rev}$, $E_{thermo,HHV}$, or $E_{thermo,LHV}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical fuel cell stack design, these relations enable engineers to:
1. **Predict maximum open-circuit voltage** from $\Delta G$ of the reaction.
2. **Calculate the thermodynamic (Carnot-equivalent) ceiling** of fuel cell efficiency (which can exceed 90% for H₂-O₂, far surpassing heat engines limited by Carnot cycle).
3. **Compare fuel cell efficiency** with conventional combustion engines.
4. **Quantify heat management requirements** through $Q = \Delta H - W_{elec}$.
5. **Size fuel cell stacks** for given power demands using $P = V \cdot I$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From First Law to Maximum Electrical Work

We now rigorously derive the expression $W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$ for an isothermal, isobaric fuel cell reaction.

**Step 1: Start with the First Law for an open system at constant $T$ and $P$.**

From the open system energy balance, the rate of change of enthalpy equals the net heat added minus the shaft (electrical) work output:

$$\Delta H = Q - W_{elec}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Second Law in the form of the Clausius inequality.**

For any real (irreversible) process:

$$\Delta S \geq \frac{Q}{T}$$

For a **reversible** (ideal, maximum work) process, equality holds:

$$\Delta S = \frac{Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{rev} = T\Delta S$$

**Step 3: Substitute $Q_{rev}$ into the First Law for a reversible process.**

$$\Delta H = T\Delta S - W_{elec,rev}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to isolate the work term.**

$$W_{elec,rev} = T\Delta S - \Delta H$$

**Step 5: Recognize the right-hand side as the negative of Gibbs Free Energy change.**

By definition, $G = H - TS$, so at constant $T$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

Therefore:

$$W_{elec,rev} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 6: State the final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G_{rxn}}$$

This is the **single most important result** in fuel cell thermodynamics. It states that the *maximum electrical work* obtainable from an electrochemical reaction at constant $T$ and $P$ equals the *negative of the change in Gibbs Free Energy* of the reaction.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reversible Cell Voltage (Nernst Potential)

**Step 1: Express electrical work in terms of charge and potential difference.**

For $n$ moles of electrons transferred through a potential difference $E$:

$$W_{elec} = n \cdot F \cdot E$$

where $F$ is Faraday's constant (charge per mole of electrons) and $E$ is the cell voltage.

**Step 2: Equate with the maximum work expression.**

$$nFE_{rev} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 3: Solve for the reversible cell voltage.**

$$\boxed{E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A hydrogen-oxygen PEM fuel cell operates at standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm). Calculate:
(a) The maximum electrical work obtainable per mole of $H_2$ consumed.
(b) The reversible cell voltage.
(c) The thermoneutral voltage.
(d) The ideal (thermodynamic) efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta G° = -237.2$ kJ/mol for $H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$
- $\Delta H° = -285.8$ kJ/mol (HHV basis)
- $n = 2$ electrons per mole of $H_2$
- $F = 96485$ C/mol

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum Electrical Work:**

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G = -(-237.2) = 237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(b) Reversible Cell Voltage:**

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-237.2 \times 10^3)}{2 \times 96485}$$

Calculating the denominator:

$$2 \times 96485 = 192970 \text{ C/mol}$$

Calculating the voltage:

$$E_{rev} = \frac{237200}{192970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

**(c) Thermoneutral Voltage (HHV basis):**

$$E_{th} = -\frac{\Delta H}{nF} = -\frac{(-285.8 \times 10^3)}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{285800}{192970} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

**(d) Ideal Thermodynamic Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An ideal hydrogen fuel cell converts **83%** of the chemical energy into electrical work — a value unattainable by any heat engine (Carnot-limited).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Efficiency Bases

| Efficiency Basis | Formula | Numerical Value (H₂-O₂) | Physical Meaning |
|------------------|---------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| Thermodynamic (HHV) | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}}$ | 83.0% | Max efficiency if water condenses |
| Thermodynamic (LHV) | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{LHV}}$ | 94.5% | Max efficiency if water leaves as vapor |
| Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_V = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$ | depends on load | Loss due to polarization |
| Faradaic Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{useful}}{I_{total}}$ | depends on fuel crossover | Loss due to parasitic reactions |
| Overall Practical | $\eta = \eta_{th} \times \eta_V \times \eta_F$ | 40–60% | Real-world net efficiency |

### 3.5 Derivation: Power Output in Terms of Current Density

**Step 1: Define current density.**

Current density $i$ is the current per unit electrode area:

$$i = \frac{I}{A_{electrode}} \quad \text{(A/cm²)}$$

**Step 2: Express total current from current density.**

$$I = i \cdot A$$

**Step 3: Substitute into power equation.**

$$P = V_{cell} \cdot I = V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot A$$

**Step 4: Define power density.**

$$P_{density} = \frac{P}{A} = V_{cell} \cdot i \quad \text{(W/cm²)}$$

**Step 5: Express in terms of overpotential.**

Since $V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$:

$$P_{density} = (E_{rev} - \eta_{tot}) \cdot i$$

This polarization-curve based relation is the **fundamental design equation** for fuel cell stacks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Energy Transformation in a Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel H2 Inlet]:::input --> B[Anode Electrode]
    C[Oxidant O2 Inlet]:::input --> D[Cathode Electrode]
    B --> E[Electrochemical Reaction Zone]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Electron Flow Through External Circuit]
    E --> G[Proton Migration Through Electrolyte]
    F --> H[Electrical Work Output We]
    G --> I[Heat Rejection Q]
    E --> J[Water Product Out]
    H --> K[Power Density W per cm2]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    classDef input fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Caption:** The figure illustrates the energy partitioning in a fuel cell. Chemical energy ($\Delta H$) entering the reaction zone is split into useful electrical work ($W_{elec} = -\Delta G$), heat rejection ($Q = T\Delta S$), and product enthalpy. The performance metric *Power Density* emerges at the system output node.

### 4.2 Energy Conversion Flowchart (Enthalpy Partitioning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Enthalpy Change deltaH] --> A[Gibbs Free Energy deltaG]
    H --> B[Entropy Term T deltaS]
    A --> C[Useful Electrical Work W elec]
    A --> D[Reversible Cell Voltage E rev]
    B --> E[Irreversible Heat Loss]
    C --> F[Power Output P]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    style H fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#239b56,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b,stroke-width:2px
```

**Caption:** Block diagram showing how the total enthalpy change of a fuel cell reaction is partitioned. The Gibbs free energy portion ($\Delta G$) is converted to useful electrical work, while the entropy term ($T\Delta S$) is inevitably lost as heat. This is the thermodynamic *energy balance* that constrains fuel cell efficiency.

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of Overall Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Overall Fuel Cell Efficiency eta overall] --> T[Thermodynamic Efficiency eta th]
    O --> V[Voltage Efficiency eta V]
    O --> F[Faradaic Efficiency eta F]
    T --> T1[deltaG / deltaH]
    V --> V1[Vcell / Erev]
    F --> F1[I useful / I total]
    T1 --> R[Result: 83 percent HHV basis]
    V1 --> R
    F1 --> R

    style O fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
```

**Caption:** Hierarchical block diagram showing how the three multiplicative efficiency components combine to give the overall fuel cell efficiency. Each block represents a distinct loss mechanism: thermodynamic irreversibility, electrochemical polarization, and fuel crossover.

### 4.4 Energy and Power Relations — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[First Law dU Q minus W] --> P2[Enthalpy H U plus PV]
    P2 --> P3[Constant P dH Q minus W elec]
    P3 --> P4[Second Law dS Q by T]
    P4 --> P5[Gibbs Free Energy G H minus TS]
    P5 --> P6[Max Electrical Work minus deltaG]
    P6 --> P7[Reversible Voltage minus deltaG by nF]
    P7 --> P8[Power Output P V times I]
    P8 --> P9[Power Density W per cm2]

    style P1 fill:#fff5e6
    style P5 fill:#e8f8f5
    style P7 fill:#fdedec
    style P9 fill:#f4ecf7
```

**Caption:** Concept map showing the logical chain from First Law principles to the final design metric of power density. Each block is a *derivation step* in the thermodynamic theory of fuel cells, illustrating the connectivity between energy, voltage, and power relations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Gibbs Free Energy. Explain its significance in determining the maximum electrical work obtainable from a fuel cell reaction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Gibbs Free Energy ($G$) is a thermodynamic state function defined as $G = H - TS$, where $H$ is enthalpy, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $S$ is entropy. It represents the maximum non-expansion (non-PV) work that can be extracted from a closed system at constant temperature and pressure.

**Significance in Fuel Cells (1.5 marks):** For an electrochemical reaction occurring in a fuel cell (constant $T$, constant $P$), the maximum reversible electrical work obtainable is given by:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$$

This relation is the *cornerstone of fuel cell thermodynamics* because it directly links the chemical energy of the fuel to the electrical energy output, and also provides the theoretical upper limit for the reversible cell voltage through $E_{rev} = -\Delta G / nF$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a fuel. Why is LHV preferred in fuel cell calculations at high temperatures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Difference (1.5 marks):**
- **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Total heat released when fuel is combusted and the water product is *fully condensed* to liquid. Includes the latent heat of condensation of water.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released when fuel is combusted and the water product remains in *vapor* form. Excludes the latent heat of vaporization.
- Relation: $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + n_{water} \cdot h_{fg}$ where $h_{fg} \approx 44$ kJ/mol at 25°C.

**Preference at High Temperature (1.5 marks):** At elevated operating temperatures (e.g., SOFC at 600–1000°C, PAFC at 200°C), the water product exits as steam, and the latent heat cannot be recovered as useful work. Therefore, **LHV is the more realistic energy reference** for fuel cell efficiency calculations, while HHV is reserved for low-temperature systems (PEMFC at 80°C) where liquid water forms.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, derive the expression for the maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a fuel cell reaction at constant temperature and pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operating at standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm), the following data are given:

$$\Delta G° = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}, \quad \Delta H° = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}, \quad n = 2, \quad F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$$

Calculate: (i) the reversible cell voltage, (ii) the thermoneutral voltage, and (iii) the ideal thermodynamic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the First Law for an open system at constant pressure. For a fuel cell, the only useful work is electrical:

$$\Delta H = Q - W_{elec}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the Second Law (Clausius inequality) for a reversible process:

$$\Delta S = \frac{Q_{rev}}{T}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Solve for reversible heat:

$$Q_{rev} = T \Delta S$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute into the First Law expression:

$$\Delta H = T \Delta S - W_{elec,rev}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearrange:

$$W_{elec,rev} = T\Delta S - \Delta H$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Identify the RHS as $-\Delta G$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{elec,rev} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude with the boxed result:

$$\boxed{W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G_{rxn}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Reversible Cell Voltage (2.5 marks):**

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-237.2 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol})}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E_{rev} = \frac{237200}{192970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Thermoneutral Voltage (2.5 marks):**

$$E_{th} = -\frac{\Delta H}{nF} = -\frac{(-285.8 \times 10^3)}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{285800}{192970} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Thermodynamic Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the relationship between Enthalpy ($H$), Internal Energy ($U$), Gibbs Free Energy ($G$), and Entropy ($S$). Derive the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation and discuss its importance in fuel cell thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) operates with the reaction:
$$CH_3OH_{(l)} + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O_{(l)}$$

Given: $\Delta G° = -702.5$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H° = -726.6$ kJ/mol, $n = 6$ electrons per mole of methanol, $F = 96485$ C/mol. Calculate the reversible cell voltage, the thermoneutral voltage, and the ideal efficiency. Comment on why the DMFC has lower efficiency than a PEMFC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Relationships (3 marks):**
- $H = U + PV$ (Enthalpy = Internal Energy + Flow work)
- $G = H - TS$ (Gibbs Free Energy = Enthalpy − Temperature × Entropy)
- $A = U - TS$ (Helmholtz Free Energy)
- $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ at constant $T$

**Derivation of Gibbs-Helmholtz (2 marks):**
Starting from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ and differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$$

This is the **Gibbs-Helmholtz equation**, useful for evaluating $\Delta G$ at temperatures other than the reference state.

**Importance in Fuel Cells (2 marks):** The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation allows prediction of $E_{rev}$ at any operating temperature, which is critical for high-temperature fuel cells (SOFC, MCFC) where standard 25°C data must be corrected.

**(b) Numerical Solution for DMFC (7 marks):**

**(i) Reversible Cell Voltage (2.5 marks):**

$$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{(-702.5 \times 10^3)}{6 \times 96485}$$

$$E_{rev} = \frac{702500}{578910} = 1.213 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Thermoneutral Voltage (2.5 marks):**

$$E_{th} = -\frac{\Delta H}{nF} = -\frac{(-726.6 \times 10^3)}{6 \times 96485} = \frac{726600}{578910} = 1.255 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Ideal Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{702.5}{726.6} = 0.967 = 96.7\%$$

**Comment on Lower Efficiency (1 mark):** Although the *thermodynamic* efficiency of DMFC (96.7%) is higher than PEMFC (83%), the *practical* efficiency is much lower due to: (1) methanol crossover through the membrane, (2) slow anode kinetics, (3) mixed potential at the cathode. These irreversibilities reduce the operating voltage drastically to ~0.4 V in practice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Energy and Power Relations" Questions:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often forget that $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are *negative* for spontaneous fuel cell reactions, leading to sign errors in $E_{rev} = -\Delta G / nF$. Always write the negative sign explicitly.
> 2. **HHV vs. LHV Confusion:** Using $\Delta H_{HHV}$ when the problem states high-temperature operation (LHV applicable), or vice versa, costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are usually in kJ/mol, but $F$ is in C/mol. **MUST convert kJ to J** (multiply by $10^3$) before computing voltage.
> 4. **Missing Number of Electrons ($n$):** Forgetting the stoichiometric factor $n$ (e.g., $n=2$ for H₂, $n=4$ for O₂ reduction, $n=6$ for methanol) is a common 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Incomplete Derivation:** Skipping the "starting point" of the derivation (e.g., First Law statement) loses the first 1–2 marks. Always begin with the fundamental principle before any algebra.
> 6. **Mixing $W_{PV}$ and $W_{elec}$:** In fuel cells, the only *useful* work is electrical. The $P\Delta V$ work is "wasted" expansion work and should not be confused with electrical output.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy and Power Relations**

- **First Law (open system):** $\Delta H = Q - W_{elec}$ — *the fundamental energy balance of a fuel cell.*
- **Maximum electrical work:** $W_{max} = -\Delta G$ — *most important result of the topic.*
- **Reversible voltage:** $E_{rev} = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ — *the open-circuit voltage of an ideal cell.*
- **Thermoneutral voltage:** $E_{th} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ — *the voltage corresponding to total energy release.*
- **Thermodynamic efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ — *the ideal ceiling of fuel cell efficiency (83% for H₂-O₂ on HHV basis, 94.5% on LHV basis).*
- **Power output:** $P = V \cdot I$ and **Power density:** $P/A = V \cdot i$ — *the key performance metric.*
- **HHV vs LHV:** HHV includes latent heat of water condensation; LHV does not. PEMFC → HHV; SOFC/PAFC → LHV.
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485$ C/mol — *convert kJ to J by multiplying by $10^3$ before voltage calculation.*
- **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{Faraday}$ — *multiplicative loss model.*
- **Standard conditions:** $T = 298.15$ K, $P = 1$ atm — *remember to state when using $\Delta G°$ or $\Delta H°$ values.*
- **Spontaneity criterion:** A reaction is spontaneous in a fuel cell if $\Delta G < 0$ (and correspondingly $E_{rev} > 0$).
- **Irreversibility:** $T\Delta S$ represents the minimum unavoidable heat rejection — a Second Law constraint.
- **Polarization losses:** Activation ($\eta_{act}$), Ohmic ($\eta_{ohmic}$), and Concentration ($\eta_{conc}$) reduce the actual cell voltage from $E_{rev}$ to $V_{cell}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of "Units" in Fuel Cell Technology

In the context of **PECHT634 – Fuel Cell Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term *Units* refers to the **standardized system of physical measurement** used to quantify the **electrochemical, thermodynamic, electrical, and mass-transport performance** of fuel cells. Every measurable quantity — be it the cell voltage, current density, power output, fuel consumption rate, or thermodynamic potential — is expressed in well-defined units derived from the **International System of Units (SI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1 – Units):**
> *Units* in fuel cell engineering denote the standardized physical quantities and their SI-compatible expressions used to characterize the performance, efficiency, and thermodynamic behaviour of electrochemical energy conversion devices. Mastery of these units is a **prerequisite** for every subsequent module in the syllabus (Module 2: Fuel Cell Components, Module 3: Types of Fuel Cells, etc.).

### 1.2 The Seven SI Base Units Relevant to Fuel Cells

| Sl. No. | Physical Quantity | SI Base Unit | Symbol | Fuel Cell Context |
|:-------:|:------------------|:-------------|:------:|:------------------|
| 1 | Length | metre | m | Electrode thickness, membrane thickness |
| 2 | Mass | kilogram | kg | Mass of catalyst, mass of fuel stored |
| 3 | Time | second | s | Reaction kinetics, current measurement |
| 4 | Electric Current | ampere | A | Cell current output |
| 5 | Temperature | kelvin | K | Operating temperature of the cell |
| 6 | Amount of Substance | mole | mol | Moles of electrons transferred, fuel moles |
| 7 | Luminous Intensity | candela | cd | (Not used in fuel cell analysis) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Language of Fuel Cell Engineering":**
> Imagine a team of engineers from five different countries collaborating on a fuel cell prototype. The Japanese engineer reports voltage, the German engineer reports current density, the American engineer reports power density in W/cm², the Indian engineer quotes efficiency in %, and the Korean engineer quotes specific energy in Wh/kg. **Without a common, standardized system of units, the data is meaningless.** SI units act as the **universal translator** — a single, unambiguous numerical language that allows every fuel cell parameter to be compared, validated, and reproduced globally. A "1.2 V open-circuit voltage" means the same thing in Tokyo, Berlin, Boston, Kerala, and Seoul.

### 1.4 Why Units Are Critical in Module 1

- **Every KTU examination question** in this course expects answers in **specific units with correct symbols**.
- **Performance benchmarking** between fuel cell technologies (PEMFC, SOFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC) is only meaningful when units are consistent.
- **Energy efficiency, voltage efficiency, and Faradaic efficiency** are dimensionless ratios (%), but their *numerators and denominators* must be in compatible units.
- **The Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}$ acts as the **unit-bridge** between electrical (coulombs) and chemical (moles of electrons) worlds.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Units Used in Fuel Cell Technology

Units in this course can be logically classified into **four tiers**:

1. **Tier 1 – SI Base Units** (metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole)
2. **Tier 2 – SI Derived Units** (volt, watt, joule, pascal, coulomb)
3. **Tier 3 – Electrochemical Constants** (Faraday's constant, gas constant, Avogadro's number)
4. **Tier 4 – Fuel-Cell-Specific Performance Units** (current density, power density, specific energy, round-trip efficiency)

### 2.2 Why Each Unit Exists — The "How" Behind the Symbol

- **Volt (V = J/C):** Measures the *energy per unit charge* delivered by the cell. A PEMFC operating at **0.7 V** means each coulomb of charge carries 0.7 joules of useful electrical energy.
- **Ampere (A = C/s):** Measures the *rate of electron flow*. A fuel cell producing **50 A** is moving 50 coulombs of charge through the external circuit every second.
- **Watt (W = J/s = V·A):** Measures the *rate of energy delivery*. Power is the product of voltage and current.
- **Coulomb (C = A·s):** Measures *electric charge*. Fuel cell stoichiometry links coulombs to moles of fuel via Faraday's law.
- **Joule (J = N·m):** Measures *energy*. The Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G$ and enthalpy change $\Delta H$ of the fuel oxidation reaction are reported in **J/mol** or **kJ/mol**.

> **The "Big 10" Conversions every KTU student must memorize:**

1. $1 \ \text{A·h} = 3600 \ \text{C}$
2. $1 \ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \ \text{J} = 3.6 \ \text{MJ}$
3. $1 \ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{J}$
4. $1 \ \text{atm} = 101{,}325 \ \text{Pa} = 1.01325 \ \text{bar}$

10. $1 \ \text{mole} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ entities (Avogadro's number $N_A$)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive fuel cell stacks** (e.g., Toyota Mirai) are rated in **kW of net power** and **W/cm² of power density**.
- **Hydrogen storage tanks** are characterized by **gravimetric energy density (Wh/kg)** and **volumetric energy density (Wh/L)**.
- **NASA space missions** specify fuel cell performance in **W/kg specific power** and **Wh/kg specific energy**.
- **Stationary SOFC power plants** (Bloom Energy, Siemens) report output in **MW** with **electrical efficiency in % LHV (Lower Heating Value)**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Current to Moles of Electrons (Faraday's Law Bridge)

This is the **single most important unit-conversion derivation** in fuel cell engineering. It links the **electrical world (coulombs)** with the **chemical world (moles of electrons)**.

**Given:** A fuel cell delivers a constant current $I$ for a time $t$.

**Step 1 — Define charge:**
The electric charge $Q$ that flows through the external circuit is the product of current and time.

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

Here, $I$ is in **amperes (A)** and $t$ is in **seconds (s)**, so $Q$ comes out in **coulombs (C)**, because $1 \ \text{A} \times 1 \ \text{s} = 1 \ \text{C}$.

**Step 2 — Define moles of electrons:**
By definition, **one mole of electrons** carries a charge equal to **Faraday's constant**, $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}$. Therefore, the number of moles of electrons $n_{e^-}$ that have flowed is the total charge divided by the charge per mole.

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Unit check:**
$$\frac{[\text{C}]}{[\text{C/mol}]} = [\text{mol}] \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{n_{e^-} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \quad \text{with units of mol}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: From Moles of Electrons to Moles of Fuel

**Given:** A fuel cell reaction transfers $z$ electrons per molecule of fuel (e.g., $z = 2$ for $\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$).

**Step 1 — Establish stoichiometric ratio:**
For every $z$ electrons flowing through the external circuit, exactly **one molecule (and hence one mole)** of fuel is consumed in the electrochemical reaction.

$$n_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{n_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F} \quad \text{with units of mol}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: From Moles of Fuel to Mass of Fuel

**Step 1 — Use molar mass:**
The mass $m_{\text{fuel}}$ of fuel consumed is simply the number of moles multiplied by the molar mass $M$ (in **kg/mol** or **g/mol**).

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = n_{\text{fuel}} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F} \quad \text{with units of kg (or g)}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Theoretical Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic first principles:**
The maximum electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure equals the negative of the Gibbs free energy change.

$$W_{\text{elec,max}} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 2 — Relate work to charge and voltage:**
Electrical work is charge multiplied by voltage.

$$W_{\text{elec,max}} = Q \cdot E^{\circ} = n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}$$

Here, $n$ is the number of moles of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, and $F$ is Faraday's constant.

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $E^{\circ}$:**

$$-\Delta G = n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G}{n \cdot F} \quad \text{with units of V (volt)}}$$

**Step 4 — Unit check:**

$$\frac{[\text{J/mol}]}{[\text{mol}] \cdot [\text{C/mol}]} = \frac{[\text{J/mol}]}{[\text{C}]} = \frac{[\text{J}]}{[\text{C}]} = [\text{V}] \quad \checkmark$$

> This is a beautifully consistent result: **$1 \ \text{V} = 1 \ \text{J/C}$**, and the derivation lands exactly on that.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Thermodynamic (Energy) Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

**Step 1 — Define efficiency:**
Efficiency is the ratio of useful electrical energy output to the total chemical energy input from the fuel.

$$\eta_{\text{therm}} = \frac{\text{Electrical Energy Out}}{\text{Chemical Energy In}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\eta_{\text{therm}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 3 — Note on units:**
Both $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ are reported in **J/mol** (or **kJ/mol**), so the ratio is **dimensionless**. The result is typically expressed as a **percentage (%).

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{therm}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \times 100\% \quad \text{(dimensionless, in \%)}}$$

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Voltage Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Voltage efficiency is the ratio of the **actual operating cell voltage** $V_{\text{cell}}$ to the **theoretical (reversible) voltage** $E^{\circ}$.

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}}$$

**Step 2 — Note on activation, ohmic, and concentration losses:**
The actual cell voltage is lower than the theoretical voltage due to three irreversibilities:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

So the voltage efficiency can also be written as:

$$\eta_V = 1 - \frac{\eta_{\text{act}} + \eta_{\text{ohm}} + \eta_{\text{conc}}}{E^{\circ}}$$

**Step 3 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_V = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(dimensionless, in \%)}}$$

### 3.7 Derivation 7: Faradaic (Coulombic) Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Faradaic efficiency is the ratio of the **actual charge delivered** to the **theoretical charge expected** from complete fuel consumption.

$$\eta_F = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of moles:**

$$\eta_F = \frac{n_{e^-,\text{actual}}}{n_{e^-,\text{theoretical}}}$$

**Step 3 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_F = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(dimensionless, in \%)}}$$

### 3.8 Derivation 8: Total Fuel Cell Efficiency (Combined Form)

The **overall fuel cell efficiency** is the product of the three component efficiencies:

$$\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{therm}} \times \eta_V \times \eta_F$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this multiplication valid?** Because each efficiency is a **dimensionless ratio between 0 and 1**, their product is also dimensionless and represents the cumulative effect of all irreversibilities.

### 3.9 Derivation 9: Specific Energy of a Fuel

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Specific energy is the electrical energy obtainable per unit mass of fuel.

$$E_{\text{sp}} = \frac{W_{\text{elec}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$$

**Step 2 — Express electrical work via charge and voltage:**

$$W_{\text{elec}} = n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ} \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}$$

(per mole of fuel, where $\eta_{\text{total}}$ captures all real-world losses)

**Step 3 — Express mass of one mole of fuel:**

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = 1 \ \text{mol} \cdot M = M \quad \text{(in kg if $M$ is in kg/mol)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$E_{\text{sp}} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ} \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}}{M}$$

**Step 5 — Final form (per kg of fuel):**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{sp}} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ} \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}}{M} \quad \text{with units of J/kg (or Wh/kg)}}$$

### 3.10 Numerical Worked Example — Putting It All Together

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern):**
> A hydrogen fuel cell operates at $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.7 \ \text{V}$ with hydrogen consumption of $m = 1 \ \text{kg}$. Given: $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \ \text{V}$, $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}$, $M_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/mol}$, $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ molecule, $\eta_{\text{therm}} = 83\%$.

**Step 1 — Voltage efficiency:**

$$\eta_V = \frac{0.7}{1.229} = 0.5696 = 56.96\%$$

**Step 2 — Moles of H₂ in 1 kg:**

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{1 \ \text{kg}}{2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/mol}} = 500 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical electrical energy from 1 kg H₂:**

$$W_{\text{theor}} = n_{\text{H}_2} \cdot n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ} = 500 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 1.229$$

$$W_{\text{theor}} = 1.186 \times 10^{8} \ \text{J} = 118.6 \ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Actual energy at 0.7 V (use voltage efficiency):**

$$W_{\text{actual}} = W_{\text{theor}} \times \eta_V = 118.6 \times 0.5696 = 67.55 \ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh:**

$$W_{\text{actual}} = \frac{67.55 \times 10^{6}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 18.76 \ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Specific energy (per kg of H₂):**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{sp}} = 18.76 \ \text{kWh/kg}}$$

> **Physical insight:** This is roughly **50× higher** than a modern lithium-ion battery (~0.25 kWh/kg), which is why hydrogen fuel cells are attractive for heavy transport and aviation.

### 3.11 Analytical Matrix — Unit Dependencies

| If you know... | And you know... | You can compute... | Using... |
|:--------------|:----------------|:-------------------|:---------|
| $I$ (A), $t$ (s) | $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | $n_{e^-}$ (mol) | $n_{e^-} = It/F$ |
| $I$ (A), $t$ (s), $z$ | $M$ (kg/mol), $F$ | $m_{\text{fuel}}$ (kg) | $m = ItM/(zF)$ |
| $\Delta G$ (J/mol), $n$, $F$ | — | $E^{\circ}$ (V) | $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G/(nF)$ |
| $V_{\text{cell}}$ (V), $E^{\circ}$ (V) | — | $\eta_V$ (%) | $\eta_V = V_{\text{cell}}/E^{\circ}$ |
| $I$ (A), $V_{\text{cell}}$ (V) | $A_{\text{cell}}$ (cm²) | $P_d$ (W/cm²) | $P_d = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I / A_{\text{cell}}$ |
| $W$ (J), $m_{\text{fuel}}$ (kg) | — | $E_{\text{sp}}$ (J/kg) | $E_{\text{sp}} = W/m_{\text{fuel}}$ |
| $\Delta G$ (J/mol), $\Delta H$ (J/mol) | — | $\eta_{\text{therm}}$ (%) | $\eta_{\text{therm}} = \Delta G/\Delta H$ |
