interconnects"| LT ``` ### 4.4 SOFC Stack & Balance of Plant (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stack["SOFC Stack Assembly"] direction TB IC1["Interconnect Plate 1
LaCrO3 or SS"] R1["Repeating Unit Cell
Anode | Electrolyte | Cathode"] IC2["Interconnect Plate 2"] R2["Repeating Unit Cell"] IC3["Interconnect Plate 3"] IC1 --- R1 --- IC2 --- R2 --- IC3 end subgraph BoP["Balance of Plant"] FH["Fuel Preheater"] AR["Air Preheater"] HX["Heat Exchanger CHP"] INV["DC to AC Inverter"] end FH --> Stack AR --> Stack Stack --> HX Stack --> INV HX -->|"Recovered heat"| District["District Heating / Process Steam"] INV -->|"AC Power"| Grid["Grid / Load"] ``` ### 4.5 Material-Component Mapping Matrix | Component | HT-SOFC (800–1000°C) | IT-SOFC (600–800°C) | LT-SOFC (<600°C) | |---|---|---|---| | **Electrolyte** | 8YSZ (8 mol% Y₂O₃-ZrO₂) | CGO (Gd-doped CeO₂), LSGM | Protonic ceramics (BCZY), Barium cerates | | **Cathode** | LSM (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃) | LSCF (La₁₋ₓSrₓCo₁₋ᵧFeᵧO₃) | BSCF, LSCF composites | | **Anode** | Ni-YSZ cermet | Ni-CGO cermet | Ni-BCZY, perovskite anodes | | **Interconnect** | Lanthanum chromite (LaCrO₃) | Ferritic stainless steel (Crofer 22 APU) | Ferritic SS / Coated SS | | **Sealant** | Glass-ceramic (BaO-CaO-SiO₂) | Mica-based compressive seals | Glass-ceramic / Metallic brazes | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **List the major cell components of a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) and state the function of the electrolyte.** > **Model Answer:** > The major components of an SOFC are: > 1. **Cathode** (porous perovskite like LSM/LSCF) — site of oxygen reduction > 2. **Electrolyte** (dense YSZ ceramic) — conducts O²⁻ ions from cathode to anode > 3. **Anode** (porous Ni-YSZ cermet) — site of fuel oxidation > 4. **Interconnect** (LaCrO₃ or stainless steel) — electrically connects adjacent cells, separates gas streams > 5. **Sealant** (glass-ceramic) — prevents fuel/oxidant mixing > > **Function of electrolyte:** The electrolyte is a **dense, gas-tight solid ceramic** that conducts **oxygen ions (O²⁻)** from the cathode to the anode while blocking the passage of electrons and gases. In YSZ, O²⁻ conduction occurs via **vacancy hopping** in the fluorite lattice, and the ionic conductivity follows an Arrhenius relation: $\sigma = \sigma_0 e^{-E_a/k_BT}$. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **Why does an SOFC operate at high temperature (600–1000°C)? State any two reasons.** > **Model Answer:** > 1. **Ionic conductivity requirement:** The YSZ electrolyte exhibits significant O²⁻ ionic conductivity only above ~700°C. At lower temperatures, the resistance to ion hopping becomes prohibitively high, causing excessive ohmic losses. > 2. **Electrochemical kinetics:** The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and fuel oxidation reactions have high activation energies. High temperature provides the thermal activation needed for fast kinetics, eliminating the need for expensive Pt-group catalysts. > 3. **Fuel flexibility:** High temperature enables **internal reforming** of hydrocarbons (e.g., CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂) directly within the fuel cell, eliminating the need for an external reformer. > **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC). Write the electrochemical half-cell and overall reactions.** > **Model Solution:** > > **Construction:** > An SOFC consists of a planar (or tubular) layered structure: a **porous cathode** (LSM) on top, a **dense ceramic electrolyte** (YSZ, ~10 μm) in the middle, and a **porous anode** (Ni-YSZ cermet) at the bottom. Adjacent cells are connected by **interconnect plates** (LaCrO₃). The entire assembly is sealed with glass-ceramic seals to prevent gas crossover. > > **Working Principle:** > - Air is supplied to the cathode side; fuel (H₂ or hydrocarbon) to the anode side. > - At the **cathode**, O₂ from air adsorbs on the porous electrode, accepts electrons from the external circuit, and is reduced to O²⁻ ions: > $$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) + 2e^- \longrightarrow O^{2-} $$ > - The O²⁻ ions **migrate through the YSZ electrolyte** via vacancy hopping (driven by concentration and potential gradients). > - At the **anode**, O²⁻ ions react with fuel (e.g., H₂): > $$ H_2 + O^{2-} \longrightarrow H_2O + 2e^- $$ > - Electrons released travel through the external circuit to the cathode, producing **direct current electricity**. > > **Overall reaction:** > $$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat} $$ > > **(Schematic expected — refer to Section 4 diagram)** > **(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for reactions, 2 for explanation)** --- **(b)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Discuss the major cell components of an SOFC with emphasis on the material properties of the electrolyte (YSZ) and anode (Ni-YSZ cermet).** > **Model Solution:** > > **1. Electrolyte — YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia):** > - **Composition:** ZrO₂ doped with 8 mol% Y₂O₃. > - **Structure:** Fluorite-type cubic crystal lattice with oxygen vacancies created by Y³⁺ substituting Zr⁴⁺ (charge compensation: $Y_2O_3 \xrightarrow{ZrO_2} 2Y'_{Zr} + V^{\bullet\bullet}_O + 3O^x_O$). > - **Property requirements:** > - High **ionic conductivity** for O²⁻ (≥ 0.1 S/cm at 1000°C, ~0.02 S/cm at 800°C). > - **Zero electronic conductivity** (must block electrons to force them through external circuit). > - **Dense, gas-tight** structure (no porosity). > - Chemical stability in both oxidizing (cathode side) and reducing (anode side) atmospheres. > - Thermal expansion match with adjacent electrodes (~10.5 × 10⁻⁶ /K for 8YSZ). > > **2. Anode — Ni-YSZ Cermet:** > - **Composition:** A composite of **nickel metal** (~40 vol%) and **YSZ** (~60 vol%), forming a dual-phase cermet. > - **Role of Ni:** Provides **catalytic activity** for H₂ oxidation and **electronic conductivity** (Ni metal is a good conductor). > - **Role of YSZ:** Provides **structural support**, inhibits Ni sintering at high T, and **matches thermal expansion** with the electrolyte. > - **Porosity requirement:** ~30–40% to allow gas transport to the **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)** where Ni, YSZ, and gas phase meet. > - **Failure modes:** Ni sintering, carbon deposition (in hydrocarbon fuels), sulfur poisoning. > > **(7 marks — 3 for electrolyte, 3 for anode, 1 for summary)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style] **Explain the operating conditions of an SOFC. Differentiate between HT-SOFC, IT-SOFC, and LT-SOFC with respect to temperature, materials, and challenges.** > **Model Solution:** > > **Operating Conditions of SOFC:** > - **Temperature range:** 600°C to 1000°C (most common 800°C). > - **Pressure:** Typically atmospheric, though pressurized operation up to 5 atm is used for hybrid SOFC-GT systems. > - **Fuel:** H₂, natural gas (CH₄), syngas (CO + H₂), liquid hydrocarbons (with external reforming). > - **Oxidant:** Air or pure O₂. > - **Fuel utilization:** 70–85% (high utilization gives better efficiency but lower voltage). > - **Current density:** 0.2 to 0.5 A/cm² typical; up to 1 A/cm² in advanced designs. > > **HT-SOFC (800–1000°C):** > - **Electrolyte:** 8YSZ; **Cathode:** LSM; **Interconnect:** LaCrO₃. > - **Advantage:** High power density, fast kinetics, internal reforming possible. > - **Challenge:** Expensive materials, thermal stress, slow startup. > > **IT-SOFC (600–800°C):** > - **Electrolyte:** CGO (Gd-doped ceria) or LSGM; **Cathode:** LSCF; **Interconnect:** Ferritic stainless steel (Crofer 22 APU). > - **Advantage:** Cheaper materials, faster startup, longer lifetime. > - **Challenge:** Lower ionic conductivity, requires thinner electrolytes or better cathode catalysts. > > **LT-SOFC (<600°C):** > - **Electrolyte:** Proton-conducting perovskites (BCZY); Cathode: BSCF or composite. > - **Advantage:** Very low-cost materials, rapid startup. > - **Challenge:** Poor kinetics, very low power density; mostly at research stage. > > **(7 marks — 2 for operating conditions, 5 for regime comparison)** --- **(b)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style] **Discuss the major applications of SOFC systems. Explain the SOFC-CHP concept and its significance.** > **Model Solution:** > > **Major Applications of SOFC:** > > 1. **Stationary Power Generation (1 kW – 10 MW):** > - Residential and commercial primary power systems. > - Grid-connected or grid-independent distributed generation. > - Example: Bloom Energy's 200 kW – 1 MW units. > > 2. **Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** > - Captures waste heat from high-temperature exhaust for hot water, space heating, or process steam. > - Total efficiency reaches **80–85%**. > > 3. **Auxiliary Power Units (APU) for Vehicles:** > - Diesel reformate → SOFC → onboard electricity for trucks, ships, aircraft. > - High efficiency reduces engine idling emissions. > > 4. **Hybrid SOFC-Gas Turbine Systems:** > - SOFC + microturbine combined cycle for **large-scale (>1 MW) power plants**. > - Electrical efficiency > **65%**. > > 5. **Industrial Process Power:** > - Refineries, chemical plants — uses byproduct fuels (syngas, off-gases). > > **SOFC-CHP Concept and Significance:** > - SOFCs reject high-grade heat (400–700°C) suitable for **steam generation** or **absorption chilling**. > - This makes them ideal for **cogeneration** — electricity + heat simultaneously. > - **Significance:** > - Maximizes **total energy utilization** from a single fuel input. > - Reduces **CO₂ emissions per kWh** delivered (useful energy basis). > - Provides **energy security** for hospitals, data centers, and remote sites. > - Compatible with **hydrogen economy** transition when fuel is green H₂. > > **(7 marks — 4 for applications, 3 for CHP explanation)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SOFC Questions:** > > 1. **Writing the wrong ion migration direction:** SOFC transports **O²⁻ from cathode → anode** (NOT the reverse). Many students confuse this. > 2. **Conflating electrolyte with electrode porosity:** Electrolyte must be **100% dense** (gas-tight); electrodes must be **porous** (~30–40% porosity). Reversing this loses marks. > 3. **Forgetting the Nernst equation dependency on T:** OCV **increases with temperature** for H₂/O₂ SOFC because $E^0$ rises; do not assume constant E°. > 4. **Wrong material identification:** LSM is a **cathode** material; Ni-YSZ is an **anode**. These are very commonly swapped in answer scripts. > 5. **Missing the internal reforming advantage of HT-SOFC** in operating-condition questions — examiners specifically look for fuel-flexibility discussion. > 6. **In Nernst numerical problems, forgetting to use T in Kelvin**, not Celsius. ($T(K) = T(°C) + 273$) --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — SOFC Module 1** - ☐ SOFC = **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell**; electrolyte is **solid ceramic**; mobile ion is **O²⁻**. - ☐ Operating temperature: **600 – 1000°C** (HT: 800–1000°C, IT: 600–800°C, LT: <600°C). - ☐ **Standard electrolyte:** 8 mol% YSZ (Y₂O₃-doped ZrO₂); fluorite structure with O²⁻ vacancies. - ☐ **Standard anode:** Ni-YSZ cermet (~40 vol% Ni for electronic percolation). - ☐ **Standard cathode:** LSM (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃) for HT-SOFC; LSCF for IT-SOFC. - ☐ **Interconnects:** LaCrO₃ (HT) → Ferritic SS (IT) — material choice is temperature-driven. - ☐ **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \longrightarrow O^{2-}$ - ☐ **Anode reaction:** $H_2 + O^{2-} \longrightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$ - ☐ **Overall reaction:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$ - ☐ **E° = 1.18 V at 800°C** for H₂/O₂; **Nernst equation** governs OCV under load conditions. - ☐ **Three overpotentials:** Activation (kinetics), Ohmic (ASR), Concentration (mass transport). - ☐ **Ohmic loss dominates in HT-SOFC**; **Activation loss dominates in LT-SOFC**. - ☐ **Arrhenius conductivity:** $\sigma = \sigma_0 e^{-E_a/k_BT}$ → explains T-dependent electrolyte behavior. - ☐ **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB):** The critical region where Ni (or LSM), YSZ, and gas meet — site of electrochemical reaction. - ☐ **Key advantage:** Internal reforming of hydrocarbons (no external reformer needed). - ☐ **Key advantage:** Highest electrical efficiency among fuel cells (~50–60% standalone; ~85% with CHP). - ☐ **Key disadvantage:** Slow startup (thermal inertia); expensive high-T materials. - ☐ **Main applications:** Stationary power (1 kW – 10 MW), CHP, APU, hybrid SOFC-GT plants. - ☐ **CHP efficiency** can reach **80–85%** due to high-grade waste heat.

topics.title: General Thermodynamics: Enthalpy - Heat Potential of Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** General Thermodynamics: Enthalpy - Heat Potential of Fuel ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Enthalpy **Enthalpy (H)** is a thermodynamic state function of a system, defined as the sum of the internal energy ($U$) of the system plus the product of its pressure ($P$) and volume ($V$). $$H \equiv U + PV$$ The change in enthalpy at constant pressure is exactly equal to the heat transferred from (or to) the surroundings: $$\Delta H = Q_p \quad \text{(at constant pressure)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In fuel cell technology, we are interested in the **change in enthalpy** ($\Delta H$) of a reaction, not the absolute value of $H$. The magnitude of $\Delta H$ represents the **total chemical energy released (or absorbed)** when reactants are converted to products. ### 1.2 Heat of Combustion — The "Heat Potential" of a Fuel The **Heat of Combustion** (also called the **Calorific Value** or **Heat Potential**) is the amount of heat liberated when **one mole (or one kilogram) of a fuel is completely burned in oxygen** under standard conditions (typically 25 °C and 1 atm). > [!NOTE] > **Two Critical Variants of Heat of Combustion:** > - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** Heat released assuming the water product is condensed to **liquid** form. > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV):** Heat released assuming the water product remains as **vapor** (steam) at the end of combustion. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Wallet" 🪙 Imagine the **enthalpy of a fuel** as the **money in a digital wallet** that the fuel carries with it. When the fuel is "spent" (combusted) in a chemical reaction: - The **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** is the *full* balance — every last rupee is counted, including the latent heat "locked" inside the water vapor that condenses to liquid. - The **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** is the balance *after* the fuel "tips" the vaporization cost of water — the heat needed to keep water as steam is deducted, much like a transaction fee. For **fuel cells**, the electrochemical reaction produces water (typically as vapor at the operating temperature), so the **LHV is the practically useful value**, while the **HHV is the thermodynamically maximum** heat content. ### 1.4 Standard Reference States - **Pressure:** **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** - **Temperature:** **25 °C (298.15 K)** - **Reference form of elements:** The most stable form of an element at 1 atm and 25 °C (e.g., O₂ as gas, C as graphite, H₂ as gas). The standard enthalpy of formation for any element in its reference state is defined as **0 kJ/mol**. ### 1.5 Why Enthalpy Matters in Fuel Cells A fuel cell is **not a heat engine** — it converts chemical energy directly into electrical work. However, the **enthalpy change** sets the *upper limit* on the electrical work that can be obtained. The actual electrical work output is governed by the **Gibbs Free Energy** ($\Delta G$), and the difference $|\Delta H - \Delta G| = T\Delta S$ is the unavoidable waste heat. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** Knowing $\Delta H$ (heat potential) tells the engineer the **total chemical energy budget**; knowing $\Delta G$ tells the engineer the **electrically recoverable** portion. The ratio $\eta_{thermodynamic} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ is the *thermodynamic efficiency ceiling* of the fuel cell. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Thermodynamic Roadmap for Combustion When a fuel is burned, we trace the following energy hierarchy: **Step 1 — Bond Breaking (Reactants):** Energy is *absorbed* to break chemical bonds in the fuel and oxygen. This is an **endothermic** stage. **Step 2 — Bond Forming (Products):** Energy is *released* as new, stronger bonds form in the products (CO₂, H₂O). This is an **exothermic** stage. **Step 3 — Net Energy Released:** The difference between (energy released in bond formation) and (energy absorbed in bond breaking) is the **enthalpy of combustion**, $\Delta H_c$. > **Why $\Delta H$ and not $\Delta U$ in practice?** > Most laboratory and industrial combustions (and fuel cell reactions) occur at **constant pressure** (open to atmosphere). Since $Q_p = \Delta H$, enthalpy is the natural quantity to use. The internal energy change $\Delta U$ is reserved for constant-volume processes (e.g., bomb calorimeter). ### 2.2 Derivation of the Constant-Pressure Relation Starting from the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: $$dU = \delta Q - \delta W \quad ; \quad \delta W = P\,dV \quad \text{(for PV work only)}$$ $$dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$$ At constant pressure: $$dU = \delta Q_p - P\,dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_p = dU + P\,dV = d(U + PV) = dH$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\delta Q_p = dH}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units | |---|--------------------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of enthalpy | kJ | | 2 | $\Delta H = Q_p$ | Heat at constant pressure | kJ | | 3 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ | For ideal-gas reactions | kJ | | 4 | $\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \nu_p \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_r \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | Standard heat of combustion (Hess's Law) | kJ/mol | | 5 | $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot \Delta H_{vap}$ | Relating HHV and LHV | kJ/mol | | 6 | $\Delta H_{vap}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, 25^\circ\text{C}) = 44.01$ kJ/mol | Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C | kJ/mol | | 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency of fuel cell | dimensionless | | 8 | $\Delta H = -(\text{HHV or LHV})$ | Sign convention (combustion is exothermic) | kJ/mol | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Mastery:** Combustion reactions are **exothermic**, meaning heat is *released* and $\Delta H < 0$. The "calorific value" is therefore conventionally reported as a **positive number** equal to $-\Delta H_c^\circ$. Be careful: the *reaction* value is negative; the *calorific value* is positive. ### 2.4 HHV vs LHV — The Water Question When 1 mole of H₂ burns: $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H^\circ = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol (HHV)}$$ $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta H^\circ = -241.82 \text{ kJ/mol (LHV)}$$ Difference: $285.83 - 241.82 = 44.01$ kJ/mol — exactly the enthalpy of vaporization of water at 25 °C. This is the **latent heat of condensation** that is "lost" if the product water escapes as steam. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PEM Fuel Cells:** Operate around 60–80 °C; product water is liquid. The HHV is the appropriate value for low-temperature cells. - **SOFC / MCFC:** Operate at 600–1000 °C; product water is vapor. The LHV is the appropriate value. - **Hydrogen Storage & Delivery:** Engineers use LHV when computing the energy density (kWh/kg) of compressed/liquid hydrogen because the exhaust water is vapor. - **Combustion Engineers:** Use HHV for boiler efficiency calculations (because flue gas often condenses in economizers). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From $H = U + PV$ to the Constant-Pressure Heat Relation **Starting point:** The combined statement of the First Law and the mechanical work done by pressure. $$\begin{aligned} dU &= \delta Q - \delta W \\ \delta W &= P\,dV \quad \text{(reversible expansion against constant external pressure)} \\ \therefore \quad dU &= \delta Q - P\,dV \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1 — Group the terms into a state function:** $$\begin{aligned} \delta Q &= dU + P\,dV \\ \delta Q &= dU + d(PV) - V\,dP \quad \text{(by product rule: } d(PV) = P\,dV + V\,dP\text{)} \\ \delta Q &= d(U + PV) - V\,dP \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the constant-pressure constraint ($dP = 0$):** $$\begin{aligned} \delta Q_p &= d(U + PV) - V(0) \\ \delta Q_p &= d(U + PV) \\ \boxed{\delta Q_p &= dH} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The heat absorbed by a system in a constant-pressure process equals the change in enthalpy. This is the foundational result that allows us to equate "heat of combustion" with "enthalpy of combustion." --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta U$ for an Ideal-Gas Reaction For a reaction involving only ideal gases at constant $T$ and $P$: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H &= \Delta U + \Delta(PV) \\ \Delta(PV) &= \Delta(nRT) \quad \text{(ideal-gas law)} \\ \Delta(PV) &= RT\,\Delta n \quad \text{(since } T \text{ is constant)} \\ \therefore \quad \Delta H &= \Delta U + RT\,\Delta n_g \end{aligned}$$ where $\Delta n_g = \sum \nu_p(\text{gaseous products}) - \sum \nu_r(\text{gaseous reactants})$. > **Physical Interpretation:** $\Delta n_g$ molecules of gas have been "added" or "removed." Each mole of gas contributes $RT$ to the $PV$ energy at constant $P$, $T$. For combustion reactions, $\Delta n_g < 0$ (fewer gas moles on product side), so $\Delta U$ is *more negative* than $\Delta H$ by the amount $RT\Delta n_g$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: HHV and LHV of Methane **Problem:** Calculate the HHV and LHV of methane ($\text{CH}_4$) at 25 °C and 1 atm using standard enthalpies of formation. **Given Standard Enthalpies of Formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$ at 298 K):** | Substance | Phase | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) | |-----------|-------|----------------------------| | CH₄ | g | $-74.85$ | | O₂ | g | $0$ (reference) | | CO₂ | g | $-393.52$ | | H₂O | l | $-285.83$ | | H₂O | g | $-241.82$ | **Combustion Reaction (Balanced):** $$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l \text{ or } g)$$ **Step 1 — Apply Hess's Law for HHV (water as liquid):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{c,\text{HHV}}^\circ &= \big[\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O},l)\big] - \big[\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_4) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)\big] \\ &= \big[(-393.52) + 2(-285.83)\big] - \big[(-74.85) + 2(0)\big] \\ &= \big[-393.52 - 571.66\big] - \big[-74.85\big] \\ &= -965.18 - (-74.85) \\ &= -965.18 + 74.85 \\ &= -890.33 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** HHV = $\mathbf{890.33}$ kJ/mol (positive calorific value, exothermic reaction). **Step 2 — Apply Hess's Law for LHV (water as vapor):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{c,\text{LHV}}^\circ &= \big[(-393.52) + 2(-241.82)\big] - \big[(-74.85) + 2(0)\big] \\ &= \big[-393.52 - 483.64\big] - \big[-74.85\big] \\ &= -877.16 + 74.85 \\ &= -802.31 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** LHV = $\mathbf{802.31}$ kJ/mol. **Step 3 — Verification via the HHV–LHV Relation:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{HHV} - \text{LHV} &= 890.33 - 802.31 = 88.02 \text{ kJ/mol} \\ n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot \Delta H_{vap} &= 2 \times 44.01 = 88.02 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ The difference matches exactly with the condensation of 2 moles of water vapor — confirming the calculation. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Reaction Stoichiometry on $\Delta n_g$ For the reaction $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$: $$\Delta n_g = (1 + 2) - (1 + 2) = 0$$ Therefore $\Delta H = \Delta U$ (for methane combustion with vapor water). For liquid water, we treat it as a condensed phase not in the gas count, so $\Delta n_g$ is still effectively defined relative to gaseous species. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Energy Accounting in a Fuel The diagram below traces how the **total heat potential (HHV)** of a fuel is partitioned between the *latent heat locked in product water* and the *practically recoverable lower heating value*: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HHV - Higher Heating Value
Total chemical energy released
Water product = liquid] B[Latent Heat of Condensation
n_H2O x 44.01 kJ/mol
Recovered only if water condenses] C[LHV - Lower Heating Value
Net heat at stack temperature
Water product = vapor] D[Gibbs Free Energy Delta G
Converted to electrical work] E[Waste Heat T Delta S
Irreversible losses] A --> B A --> C C --> D C --> E D --> F[Electrical Output of Fuel Cell] E --> G[Thermal Management System] F --> H[Net System Efficiency] G --> H ``` > **How to read the diagram:** The HHV is the *gross* energy. Subtracting the latent-heat portion (condensation energy) gives the LHV. The LHV then splits into useful electrical work ($\Delta G$) and unavoidable waste heat ($T\Delta S$). ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermodynamic Pathway of a Combustion Reaction The following block diagram illustrates the energy pathway from reactants (high-enthalpy fuel + oxidant) to products (low-enthalpy CO₂ + H₂O) via the **Hess's Law construction**: ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Reactants
CH4 + 2 O2
Sum of Delta Hf equals -74.85 kJ] P1[Products
CO2 + 2 H2O l
Sum of Delta Hf equals -965.18 kJ] E1[Elements in Standard State
C graphite + 2 H2 + 2 O2
Reference enthalpy equals 0 kJ] R1 -->|Decomposition
Delta H equals +74.85 kJ| E1 E1 -->|Formation of Products
Delta H equals -965.18 kJ| P1 R1 -.Direct Combustion.-> P1 P1 -.Delta Hc equals -890.33 kJ.-> P1 ``` > **How to read the diagram:** The **two-step path** (Reactants → Elements → Products) gives the *same* $\Delta H$ as the **direct path** (Reactants → Products). This is Hess's Law, and it is the conceptual engine that allows us to compute heats of combustion from tabulated enthalpies of formation. ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Role of Enthalpy in a Fuel Cell Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph FuelCell[Fuel Cell System] FS[Fuel Inlet
H2 or CH4 or CH3OH] OX[Oxidant Inlet
O2 from air] SA[Stack Anode
Electrochemical Oxidation] SC[Stack Cathode
Electrochemical Reduction] EP[Electrical Power Terminals
Delta G converted to W] WH[Waste Heat Exchanger
T Delta S removed] EX[Exhaust
H2O vapor and CO2] end FS --> SA OX --> SC SA --> EP SC --> EP SA --> WH SC --> WH SA --> EX SC --> EX EP --> U[Useful Work Output] WH --> Q[Recoverable Thermal Energy] ``` > **Engineering note:** The $\Delta H$ entering the stack is the *design constraint*; the $\Delta G$ is the *useful work*; the $T\Delta S$ is the *heat to be removed* to keep the stack at operating temperature. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define enthalpy of combustion. Distinguish between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a fuel.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **enthalpy of combustion** ($\Delta H_c^\circ$) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is completely burnt in oxygen at standard conditions (1 atm, 25 °C). For exothermic combustion, $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is negative, and the **calorific value** is reported as the positive magnitude $-\Delta H_c^\circ$. **HHV vs LHV (1.5 marks):** - **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** The heat released when the product water is in the **liquid** state. It includes the latent heat of condensation of water. - **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** The heat released when the product water remains in the **vapor** state. The latent heat is *not* recovered. - **Relation:** $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times 44.01$ kJ/mol. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** *State Hess's Law. How is it used to calculate the enthalpy of combustion of a fuel from standard enthalpies of formation?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement of Hess's Law (1 mark):** Hess's Law states that the **total enthalpy change** in a chemical reaction is **independent of the pathway** taken; it depends only on the initial and final states. **Application to combustion (2 marks):** For a combustion reaction: $$\text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Products}$$ the standard enthalpy of combustion is calculated as: $$\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \nu_p \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_r \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$$ This is valid because the reaction can be conceptually decomposed into: (1) decomposition of reactants into constituent elements in their standard states, and (2) re-formation of products from those elements. The net enthalpy change is the same as the direct reaction, allowing the use of tabulated $\Delta H_f^\circ$ values. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the relation $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ for an ideal-gas reaction at constant temperature and pressure. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The standard enthalpies of formation are: - $\text{CO}_2(g) = -393.52$ kJ/mol - $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) = -285.83$ kJ/mol - $\text{CH}_4(g) = -74.85$ kJ/mol - $\text{O}_2(g) = 0$ kJ/mol Calculate the **HHV** and **LHV** of methane. State which value is more appropriate for an SOFC operating at 800 °C and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from the definition of enthalpy: $$H = U + PV$$ For a reaction, the change in enthalpy is: $$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$ For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$. At constant temperature $T$: $$\Delta(PV) = \Delta(nRT) = RT \cdot \Delta n_g$$ where $\Delta n_g$ is the change in the number of moles of gas: $$\Delta n_g = \sum \nu_p(\text{gaseous products}) - \sum \nu_r(\text{gaseous reactants})$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\Delta H = \Delta U + RT \cdot \Delta n_g}$$ **Physical Significance of Each Term (2 marks within the 7):** - $\Delta U$: Change in the **internal (chemical) energy** of the system — represents bond-energy reorganization. - $RT \cdot \Delta n_g$: The **boundary work** done by the system at constant $P, T$ due to the change in the number of gas molecules. If $\Delta n_g < 0$ (typical of combustion), this term is negative, meaning the surroundings do work on the system. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** Balanced combustion reaction: $$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l \text{ or } g)$$ **Step 1: HHV (water as liquid):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{c,\text{HHV}}^\circ &= [(-393.52) + 2(-285.83)] - [(-74.85) + 2(0)] \\ &= [-393.52 - 571.66] - [-74.85] \\ &= -965.18 + 74.85 \\ &= -890.33 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ $$\therefore \text{HHV} = 890.33 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ **Step 2: LHV (water as vapor, using $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) = -241.82$ kJ/mol):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta H_{c,\text{LHV}}^\circ &= [(-393.52) + 2(-241.82)] - [(-74.85) + 2(0)] \\ &= [-393.52 - 483.64] - [-74.85] \\ &= -877.16 + 74.85 \\ &= -802.31 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ $$\therefore \text{LHV} = 802.31 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ **Step 3: Choice for SOFC (3 marks):** At 800 °C (1073 K), the operating temperature of an SOFC is far above the boiling point of water (100 °C). The product water exits the cell as **vapor**, and there is no condensation in the stack. The latent heat of condensation **cannot** be recovered. Hence, the **LHV = 802.31 kJ/mol** is the appropriate heat potential for an SOFC. Using HHV would overestimate the recoverable energy by approximately 88 kJ/mol. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Define **enthalpy** as a thermodynamic state function. Show that the heat transferred in a constant-pressure process equals the change in enthalpy of the system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the hydrogen oxidation reaction: $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l), \quad \Delta H^\circ = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g), \quad \Delta H^\circ = -241.82 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Calculate (i) the latent heat of vaporization of water, and (ii) the energy difference (in kJ/mol and in kWh/kg) between HHV and LHV of hydrogen. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Enthalpy is a state function defined as: $$H = U + PV$$ where $U$ is internal energy, $P$ is pressure, and $V$ is volume. It is a state function because it depends only on the state variables $U, P, V$. **Derivation (5 marks):** From the First Law: $$dU = \delta Q - P\,dV \quad \text{(reversible process)}$$ At constant $P$: $$\begin{aligned} \delta Q_p &= dU + P\,dV \\ &= dU + d(PV) \quad \text{(since } dP = 0\text{)} \\ &= d(U + PV) \\ &= dH \end{aligned}$$ Therefore, for a finite change: $$Q_p = \Delta H$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Latent Heat of Vaporization (3 marks):** The two reactions differ only in the product water phase. Subtracting: $$\begin{aligned} \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \\ \Delta H_{vap}^\circ &= \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) - \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O},l) \\ &= -241.82 - (-285.83) \\ &= 44.01 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Energy Difference Between HHV and LHV (4 marks):** **Per mole of H₂:** $$\Delta E = 285.83 - 241.82 = 44.01 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Per kilogram of H₂** (molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol): $$\begin{aligned} \Delta E \text{ per kg} &= \frac{44.01 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} \\ &= 21{,}830 \text{ kJ/kg} \\ &= \frac{21{,}830}{3600} \text{ kWh/kg} \\ &\approx 6.06 \text{ kWh/kg} \end{aligned}$$ > **Engineering insight:** This ~6 kWh/kg is a *significant* fraction of hydrogen's total energy density (~39.4 kWh/kg on LHV basis). It illustrates why *water management* (condensing vs. venting) is a critical design choice in low-temperature fuel cell systems. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Enthalpy & Heat-of-Combustion Questions:** > 1. **Sign Error:** Students often forget that combustion is *exothermic* and write $\Delta H = +890$ kJ/mol. The reaction value is **negative**; the calorific value is reported as a **positive** number. > 2. **HHV vs LHV Confusion:** In problems where the question specifies "water is produced as vapor," students frequently use $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.83$ instead of the gaseous value. Read the problem statement carefully! > 3. **Missing Stoichiometric Multipliers:** The enthalpy of formation of water must be multiplied by the **stoichiometric coefficient** (e.g., 2 for the two moles of H₂O in methane combustion). Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Forgetting the Standard States of Elements:** $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of O₂(g), N₂(g), and graphite C(s) are **zero** by convention. Students sometimes hunt for them in tables and end up with incorrect values. > 5. **Confusing $Q_p$ and $Q_v$:** Bomb-calorimeter experiments give $\Delta U$, not $\Delta H$. The $\Delta n_g RT$ correction is mandatory to convert. > 6. **Units:** Always report $\Delta H$ in kJ/mol *and* explicitly state the physical state of water (l or g) in the products. The valuation key deducts marks for ambiguous states. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Enthalpy definition:** $H = U + PV$ — a state function whose differential equals the heat absorbed at constant pressure. - **First-law foundation:** $\delta Q_p = dH$ — this is the single most important identity for the entire module. - **Calorific value = $-\Delta H_c^\circ$** (combustion is exothermic). - **HHV** assumes product water is **liquid**; **LHV** assumes product water is **vapor**. - **HHV − LHV = $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times 44.01$ kJ/mol** (latent heat of vaporization at 25 °C). - **Hess's Law:** $\Delta H_c^\circ = \sum \nu_p \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_r \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$. - **Standard states** are 25 °C, 1 atm; elements in their reference form have $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$. - **For ideal gases:** $\Delta H = \Delta U + RT\Delta n_g$ — relevant when converting bomb-calorimeter data to $\Delta H$. - **Fuel cell efficiency ceiling:** $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ — the difference $\Delta H - \Delta G = T\Delta S$ is the minimum heat rejected. - **Low-T cells (PEMFC, AFC):** Use **HHV**; product water is liquid. - **High-T cells (SOFC, MCFC):** Use **LHV**; product water is vapor. - **Methane benchmark values to memorize:** - HHV = **890.33 kJ/mol** (or ~55.5 MJ/kg) - LHV = **802.31 kJ/mol** (or ~50.0 MJ/kg) - **Hydrogen benchmark values:** - HHV = **285.83 kJ/mol** (~39.4 kWh/kg LHV) - LHV = **241.82 kJ/mol** (~33.3 kWh/kg HHV) - **Units in exam:** Always use kJ/mol for molar quantities; convert to MJ/kg using molar mass for gravimetric energy density. - **Common units:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$; $1 \text{ eV/particle} = 96.485 \text{ kJ/mol}$. ---

topics.title: Gibbs Free Energy - Work Potential of Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gibbs Free Energy - Work Potential of Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Gibbs Free Energy (G)** is a thermodynamic state function that quantifies the maximum amount of **non-expansion (useful) work** obtainable from a closed system at constant temperature (T) and constant pressure (P). For an electrochemical device such as a **fuel cell**, the change in Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$) represents the **theoretical maximum electrical work** deliverable per mole of fuel consumed, which is the true measure of the fuel's *electrochemical work potential*.

Mathematically, Gibbs free energy is defined as:

$$G \;=\; H \;-\; T \cdot S$$

where $H$ is the **enthalpy** (total heat content), $T$ is the **absolute temperature** in Kelvin, and $S$ is the **entropy** of the system. The change in Gibbs free energy between products and reactants is given by:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta H \;-\; T \cdot \Delta S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *The change in Gibbs free energy of a reaction at standard conditions ($\Delta G^\circ$) represents the maximum useful electrical work that can be obtained from a fuel cell operating reversibly at constant temperature and pressure.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel of Energy

Imagine a stream of water flowing downhill from a mountain reservoir. The **total drop in height** is the **enthalpy ($\Delta H$)** — the *gross* energy released as the water falls. However, a small portion of that drop is always lost as turbulence, splashing, and heat (entropy dissipation, $T\Delta S$).

Now imagine you install a **perfectly engineered water wheel** at the bottom. The energy actually delivered as **rotational (useful) work** by the wheel is *not* the full drop in height — it is the drop *minus* the chaotic, unusable turbulence losses.

| Energy Concept | Water Wheel Analogy | Meaning |
|----------------|---------------------|---------|
| Enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) | Total height drop | Gross heat released by combustion |
| Entropy loss ($T\Delta S$) | Turbulence & splashing | Energy dissipated as disorder/heat |
| **Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$)** | **Useful rotation of the wheel** | **Maximum useful electrical work** |
| Heat loss ($T\Delta S$) | Friction in the bearings | Irrecoverable thermal energy |

> [!NOTE]
> A fuel cell is essentially an "electrochemical water wheel" — it captures the **useful work fraction ($\Delta G$)** of a chemical reaction, bypassing the combustion step entirely. This is why fuel cells can in principle exceed the efficiency of any heat engine (which is capped by the **Carnot limit**).

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard values are used throughout KTU board problems and must be memorised:

- **Faraday's constant:** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485 \; C \cdot mol^{-1}}$ (often rounded to $96{,}500 \; C/mol$)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Standard temperature:** $T = \mathbf{298.15 \; K}$ (often rounded to $25^\circ C$)
- **Standard pressure:** $P = \mathbf{1 \; atm} = \mathbf{101.325 \; kPa}$
- **Standard states:** All reactants/products at unit activity, gases at 1 atm fugacity, liquids/solids in pure form.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU problems, when the problem states "standard conditions" without further qualification, always assume **$T = 298 \; K$** and **$P = 1 \; atm$** unless explicitly given otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Why $\Delta G$, not $\Delta H$, Governs Fuel Cell Work

In a **heat engine** (e.g., internal combustion engine), fuel is burned and the resulting *heat* ($q = \Delta H$) is partially converted to work, subject to the **Carnot limit** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_c/T_h$. This means a large fraction of the chemical energy is *unavoidably* dumped into the cold reservoir.

In a **fuel cell**, the chemical reaction is split into two half-reactions at separate electrodes, and electrons are forced through an external circuit. There is **no high-temperature flame** and **no Carnot ceiling**. The maximum electrical work is therefore the **full Gibbs free energy change**:

$$W_{elec,\,max} \;=\; -\,\Delta G$$

The **negative sign** is a sign convention: a *spontaneous* reaction (fuel cell discharging) has $\Delta G < 0$, so the work done *by* the system is positive.

### Step 2 — The Electrochemical Bridge: $\Delta G = -nFE$

When a fuel cell reaction transfers **$n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction** at a cell potential of $E$ volts, the electrical work per mole is:

$$W_{elec} \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E$$

Equating the thermodynamic maximum to the electrochemical expression:

$$-\,\Delta G \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\;\Delta G \;=\; -\,n\,F\,E\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important equation in fuel cell thermodynamics.** The cell EMF ($E$) is directly proportional to $-\Delta G$. Any voltage loss (activation, ohmic, concentration) reduces the *actual* work output below $-\Delta G$.

### Step 3 — Temperature and Pressure Dependence of $\Delta G$

#### 3a. **Temperature Dependence — The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation**

$$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

This tells us that as temperature rises, $\Delta G$ generally becomes *more negative* (more spontaneous) for **exothermic** reactions like hydrogen oxidation, but the *rate* of change is governed by $\Delta H$.

#### 3b. **Pressure Dependence**

For an ideal-gas reaction involving a change of $\Delta n$ moles of gas, the **Nernst-type correction** for non-standard pressures is:

$$\Delta G(T,P) \;=\; \Delta G^{\circ}(T) \;+\; R\,T\,\ln\!\left(\frac{a_{products}}{a_{reactants}}\right)$$

In terms of partial pressures for gases:

$$\Delta G(T,P) \;=\; \Delta G^{\circ}(T) \;+\; R\,T\,\sum_{i}\nu_{i}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{i}}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

where $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient (negative for reactants, positive for products).

### Step 4 — Enthalpy vs. Gibbs Free Energy: Calorific Value vs. Work Potential

For the hydrogen–oxygen reaction:

$$\mathrm{H_2(g) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O(l)}$$

| Quantity | Value (kJ/mol) | Meaning |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** = $-\Delta H^\circ$ | **$-285.83$** | Heat released if water condenses to liquid |
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** = $-\Delta H^\circ$ (water as vapour) | **$-241.82$** | Heat released if water leaves as steam |
| **Gibbs free energy change ($-\Delta G^\circ_{298}$)** | **$-237.13$** | Maximum reversible electrical work at 25 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency of a fuel cell:**
> $$\eta_{rev} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \;=\; \frac{237.13}{285.83} \;\approx\; 0.830 \;=\; 83.0\%$$
> This **83 %** figure is the theoretical upper limit for a $\mathrm{H_2/O_2}$ fuel cell at 25 °C producing liquid water. Practical fuel cells achieve **40–60 %** due to irreversibilities.

### Step 5 — Why $T\Delta S$ Is "Lost" Even in a Reversible Fuel Cell

The difference $\Delta H - \Delta G = T\Delta S$ represents heat that **must** be rejected to the surroundings even in a perfectly reversible (lossless) fuel cell. It is **not** a loss to inefficiency — it is an intrinsic thermodynamic requirement of the second law.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the *high-yield* formula set the KTU 2024 scheme examiner expects you to reproduce from memory under examination conditions.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $G = H - TS$ | Definition of Gibbs free energy | kJ or J |
| 2 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Change in Gibbs free energy at constant T, P | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$ | Standard Gibbs free energy from formation data | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | **Bridge between thermodynamics & electrochemistry** | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Standard cell potential from $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\Delta H = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$ | Reaction enthalpy from standard heats of formation | kJ/mol |
| 7 | $\Delta S = \sum \nu_i S^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i S^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$ | Reaction entropy | J/(mol·K) |
| 8 | $\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Reversible thermodynamic efficiency of fuel cell | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $W_{elec,\,max} = -\Delta G = nFE$ | Maximum electrical work per mole of fuel | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $\Delta G(T) = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ (assumes $\Delta H, \Delta S$ approx. T-independent) | $\Delta G$ at any T (small T range) | kJ/mol |
| 11 | $\Delta G(T,P) = \Delta G^{\circ}(T) + RT\ln Q$ | Non-standard conditions (Nernst form) | kJ/mol |
| 12 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz equation | — |
| 13 | $F = 96{,}485 \; C/mol$ | Faraday's constant | C/mol |
| 14 | $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$ | Universal gas constant | J/(mol·K) |
| 15 | $\Delta n = n_{gas,\,prod} - n_{gas,\,react}$ | Change in gaseous moles (for entropy estimate) | mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap in KTU papers:** When $\Delta G$ is given in **kJ/mol**, you **must** convert to **J/mol** (multiply by 1000) before plugging into $\Delta G = -nFE$, because $F$ is in **C/mol** and $E$ in **V**, and $1\,J = 1\,C \cdot V$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fuel Cell Sizing:** Engineers use $-\Delta G^\circ$ to predict the **maximum open-circuit voltage** ($E^0$) of any fuel cell chemistry (PEMFC, SOFC, MCFC, PAFC).
2. **Efficiency Benchmarking:** The ratio $\Delta G / \Delta H$ is the *theoretical ceiling* against which practical fuel cell efficiency is reported in datasheets and academic literature.
3. **Reactor Design:** Knowing $T\Delta S$ heat output lets thermal engineers design the **cooling system** for a fuel cell stack — the reversible heat load is *unavoidable* even at 100 % electrical efficiency.
4. **Alternative Fuel Screening:** The magnitude of $-\Delta G^\circ$ per unit mass (or per electron) is the key metric for ranking fuels like $\mathrm{H_2}$, methanol, ethanol, methane, and ammonia.
5. **High-Temperature Fuel Cells (SOFC):** At 800 °C, $\Delta G$ is more negative (more electrical work), but $\Delta H$ is nearly unchanged — the *ratio* $\Delta G/\Delta H$ actually *decreases*, so high-T cells can have a *lower* theoretical electrical efficiency but produce more *useful heat* for combined heat & power (CHP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: From First Principles — Why $\Delta G$ Equals Maximum Useful Work

**Starting point (First Law of Thermodynamics):**
For a closed system undergoing a process that exchanges only heat $q$ and $PV$-work $w_{PV}$ with the surroundings:

$$\Delta U \;=\; q \;+\; w_{total}$$

where $w_{total} = w_{PV} + w_{useful}$ (the useful non-expansion work, e.g., electrical work from a fuel cell).

**Step 1:** Substitute $w_{total}$ and rearrange:
$$\Delta U \;=\; q \;+\; w_{PV} \;+\; w_{useful}$$

**Step 2:** Enthalpy is defined as $H = U + PV$, so at constant pressure:
$$\Delta H \;=\; \Delta U \;+\; P\Delta V \;=\; q_P \;+\; w_{PV} \;+\; P\Delta V$$

**Step 3:** The $PV$-work at constant pressure is $w_{PV} = -P\Delta V$. Substituting:
$$\Delta H \;=\; q_P \;+\; (-P\Delta V) \;+\; P\Delta V \;=\; q_P$$

So the heat absorbed at constant pressure is simply $q_P = \Delta H$.

**Step 4:** From Step 1, the useful work is:
$$w_{useful} \;=\; \Delta U - q - w_{PV}$$

**Step 5:** For a **reversible** isothermal process, the second law gives $q_{rev} = T\Delta S$ (heat absorbed from a thermal reservoir at temperature $T$). Combining with Step 1:
$$w_{useful,\,rev} \;=\; \Delta U - T\Delta S - w_{PV} \;=\; (U_2 - U_1) - T(S_2 - S_1) + P(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 6:** Recognise the bracketed combination as $\Delta(U + PV - TS) = \Delta(H - TS) = \Delta G$:
$$w_{useful,\,rev} \;=\; \Delta U + P\Delta V - T\Delta S \;=\; \Delta H - T\Delta S \;=\; \Delta G$$

**Final boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\;w_{useful,\,max} \;=\; \Delta G\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention recap:** When the system *does* useful work on the surroundings (fuel cell discharging), $\Delta G < 0$ and $w_{useful} = \Delta G < 0$ in the *thermodynamic* sign convention, or equivalently $W_{max} = -\Delta G > 0$ in the *engineering* sign convention used throughout KTU fuel cell problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Connecting $\Delta G$ to Cell Voltage ($\Delta G = -nFE$)

**Step 1:** Consider a fuel cell reaction that transfers $n$ moles of electrons through the external circuit per mole of reaction. The total charge moved is:
$$Q \;=\; n \cdot F \quad [\text{coulombs per mole of reaction}]$$

**Step 2:** The electrical work done by the cell at a terminal voltage $E$ (volts) is:
$$W_{elec} \;=\; Q \cdot E \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E$$

**Step 3:** For a **reversible** fuel cell, this work equals the maximum possible useful work:
$$W_{elec,\,max} \;=\; -\Delta G$$

**Step 4:** Equating the two expressions:
$$n \cdot F \cdot E \;=\; -\Delta G$$

**Step 5:** Solving for either variable:
$$\boxed{\;\Delta G \;=\; -\,n\,F\,E\;} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \boxed{\;E \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta G}{n\,F}\;}$$

**Step 6 (Substitution check for the H₂/O₂ cell):**
- Reaction: $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)}$
- $n = 2$ electrons per mole of $\mathrm{H_2}$
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{298} = -237.13 \; kJ/mol = -237{,}130 \; J/mol$
- $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/(nF) = -(-237{,}130)/(2 \times 96{,}485) = 237{,}130 / 192{,}970$
- $\boxed{\;E^{\circ} \approx 1.229 \; V\;}$ — the famous **theoretical open-circuit voltage of a PEMFC** at 25 °C.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation (Temperature Dependence of $\Delta G$)

**Step 1:** Start with the definition $G = H - TS$. At constant pressure:
$$dG \;=\; dH - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**Step 2:** From the first law applied at constant pressure, $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$. At constant $P$, $dH = T\,dS$, so:
$$dG \;=\; T\,dS - T\,dS - S\,dT \;=\; -\,S\,dT$$

This gives the **Maxwell-type identity**:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,S$$

**Step 3:** For a reaction, the change between products and reactants is:
$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\Delta S$$

**Step 4:** To express $\Delta S$ in terms of $\Delta H$, start from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, so $\Delta S = (\Delta H - \Delta G)/T$.

**Step 5:** Substitute into Step 3:
$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H - \Delta G}{T} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T} \;+\; \frac{\Delta G}{T}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:
$$\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T} - \frac{\Delta G}{T} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T}$$

The left-hand side equals $T \cdot \partial(\Delta G/T)/\partial T$, since:
$$T \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) \;=\; T \cdot \frac{(\partial \Delta G/\partial T) \cdot T - \Delta G}{T^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T} - \frac{\Delta G}{T}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Since $\Delta H$ for a fuel cell reaction is *negative* (exothermic), the right side is *positive*. This means $(\Delta G/T)$ *increases* with $T$, but $\Delta G$ itself (which is already negative) generally *becomes less negative* (i.e., the available work decreases) as $T$ rises. In other words, **higher temperature ⇒ lower cell voltage** for the same reaction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** *For the methane fuel cell reaction $\mathrm{CH_4(g) + 2\,O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2\,H_2O(l)}$, calculate (a) the standard Gibbs free energy change at 25 °C, (b) the theoretical cell EMF, and (c) the reversible efficiency. Given:*  
> $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4,\,g}) = -74.85 \; kJ/mol$,  
> $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CO_2,\,g}) = -393.52 \; kJ/mol$,  
> $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O,\,l}) = -285.83 \; kJ/mol$  
> $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4,\,g}) = -50.50 \; kJ/mol$,  
> $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CO_2,\,g}) = -394.36 \; kJ/mol$,  
> $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O,\,l}) = -237.13 \; kJ/mol$  
> $n = 8$ electrons per mole of CH₄.

### Part (a): Standard Enthalpy Change
$$\Delta H^{\circ} \;=\; \left[\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CO_2}) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O})\right] - \left[\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4}) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2})\right]$$

Substitute numerical values (note: $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2,\,g}) = 0$ for an element in its standard state):
$$\Delta H^{\circ} \;=\; \left[(-393.52) + 2 \cdot (-285.83)\right] - \left[(-74.85) + 2 \cdot (0)\right]$$
$$\Delta H^{\circ} \;=\; \left[-393.52 - 571.66\right] - \left[-74.85\right]$$
$$\Delta H^{\circ} \;=\; -965.18 + 74.85 \;=\; \mathbf{-890.33 \; kJ/mol}$$

### Part (b): Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; \left[\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CO_2}) + 2 \cdot \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O})\right] - \left[\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4}) + 2 \cdot \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2})\right]$$

Substitute (again $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2}) = 0$):
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; \left[(-394.36) + 2 \cdot (-237.13)\right] - \left[(-50.50) + 0\right]$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; \left[-394.36 - 474.26\right] + 50.50$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -868.62 + 50.50 \;=\; \mathbf{-818.12 \; kJ/mol}$$

### Part (c): Theoretical Cell EMF
Using the bridge equation $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/(nF)$:
$$E^{\circ} \;=\; -\,\frac{-818.12 \times 10^{3} \; J/mol}{8 \times 96{,}485 \; C/mol}$$
$$E^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{818{,}120}{771{,}880} \;=\; \mathbf{1.060 \; V}$$

### Part (d): Reversible Efficiency
$$\eta_{rev} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} \;=\; \frac{-818.12}{-890.33} \;=\; \mathbf{0.919 \;=\; 91.9\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** $\eta_{rev}$ must always lie between 0 and 1 (0 % to 100 %). If your answer exceeds 100 %, you have likely swapped a sign or used the wrong sign of $\Delta H$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: $\Delta G$ vs. $\Delta H$ for Common Fuels (at 25 °C, 1 atm)

| Fuel | Half/Full Reaction | $-\Delta H^{\circ}$ (kJ/mol) | $-\Delta G^{\circ}$ (kJ/mol) | $\eta_{rev}$ (%) | $E^{\circ}$ (V) | n |
|------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|----------------|----|
| Hydrogen (PEMFC) | $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O(l)}$ | 285.83 | 237.13 | 83.0 | 1.229 | 2 |
| Methane (SOFC) | $\mathrm{CH_4 + 2O_2 \to CO_2 + 2H_2O(l)}$ | 890.33 | 818.12 | 91.9 | 1.060 | 8 |
| Methanol (DMFC) | $\mathrm{CH_3OH + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \to CO_2 + 2H_2O(l)}$ | 726.5 | 702.5 | 96.7 | 1.213 | 6 |
| Carbon (coal cell) | $\mathrm{C + O_2 \to CO_2}$ | 393.52 | 394.36 | 100.2* | 1.020 | 4 |
| Ammonia | $\mathrm{NH_3 + \tfrac{3}{4}O_2 \to \tfrac{1}{2}N_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2O(l)}$ | 382.6 | 338.2 | 88.4 | 1.17 | 3 |

*Coal cell exceeds 100 % because $\Delta S$ is *positive* for $\mathrm{C + O_2 \to CO_2}$ (more moles of gas produced), so $T\Delta S$ becomes a *credit*, not a debit. This is the *only* common fuel reaction with $\eta_{rev} > 100\%$.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Flow in a Fuel Cell from $\Delta H$ to Electrical Work

The following Mermaid diagram traces the **logical flow of energy** from the chemical bond energy of the fuel, through the partitioning by Gibbs free energy, to the final electrical output of a fuel cell. This is a **functional/architectural** rendering, not a physical drawing of cell components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel H2 plus Reactant O2 enter cell] --> B[Chemical Reaction H2 plus half O2 gives H2O liquid]
    B --> C[Total Energy Released: Enthalpy change delta H negative]
    C --> D[Energy Partitioning by 2nd Law of Thermodynamics]
    D --> E[Useful Work Fraction: Gibbs Free Energy delta G negative]
    D --> F[Unavoidable Heat Fraction: T times delta S positive]
    E --> G[Bridge Equation: delta G equals minus n F E]
    G --> H[Cell Terminal Voltage E volts]
    H --> I[Electrical Power Output P equals n F E dot n fuel]
    F --> J[Cooling System Heat Rejection at Temperature T cell]
    I --> K[Reversible Efficiency eta rev equals delta G over delta H]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Net Useful Energy Delivered to Load]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#7d0000,color:#000
    style G fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a5e,color:#000
    style H fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a5e,color:#000
    style I fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#002b80,color:#000
    style J fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000
    style K fill:#c2c2f0,stroke:#1a1a4d,color:#000
    style L fill:#9bbb59,stroke:#1a3d00,color:#000
```

### Reading Guide for the Diagram

| Node | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| **A → B** | The half-reactions at anode and cathode combine into the full cell reaction. |
| **B → C** | The enthalpy released per mole of fuel is $-\Delta H$, the gross calorific value. |
| **C → D** | The second law *partitions* $-\Delta H$ into a useful work share and an unavoidable heat share. |
| **D → E** | The useful work share is $-\Delta G$ — the theoretical maximum electrical output. |
| **D → F** | The unavoidable heat $T\Delta S$ must be rejected, even by a *perfect* (reversible) fuel cell. |
| **E → G → H** | The bridge $\Delta G = -nFE$ translates $-\Delta G$ into the open-circuit voltage $E$. |
| **H → I** | The actual electrical power is $P = nFE \cdot \dot{n}_{fuel}$ (reversible case). |
| **F → J** | The cooling system removes $T\Delta S$ per mole of reaction. |
| **K** | The ratio $\eta_{rev} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ is the *thermodynamic ceiling* of the fuel cell. |
| **L** | Final net useful energy delivered to the external electrical load. |

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Gibbs free energy. Why is the change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) the appropriate measure of a fuel's work potential, rather than the change in enthalpy (ΔH)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Gibbs free energy $G = H - TS$ is a state function whose change at constant $T$ and $P$ equals the maximum useful (non-expansion) work obtainable from a process.
- **(1 mark) ΔG vs ΔH:** $\Delta H$ gives the *total* heat released by a reaction, but part of this ($T\Delta S$) is unavoidably dissipated as heat and cannot be converted to electrical work, even by a *reversible* (lossless) device.
- **(1 mark) Fuel cell connection:** In a fuel cell, electrons are routed through an external circuit; the *electrical* work delivered equals $-\Delta G$, not $-\Delta H$. Thus, $\Delta G$ correctly represents the **maximum reversible electrical work per mole of fuel**, which is the true "work potential" of the fuel.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Write the relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change and the standard cell EMF of a fuel cell. State the significance of Faraday's constant in this relation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Equation:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$, where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, $F$ is Faraday's constant, and $E^{\circ}$ is the standard cell potential.
- **(1 mark) Significance of $F$:** $F = 96{,}485 \; C/mol$ is the charge carried by one mole of electrons; it acts as the *conversion factor* between the *thermodynamic* energy scale (joules per mole) and the *electrochemical* energy scale (coulomb-volts per mole).
- **(1 mark) Application:** This equation allows the open-circuit voltage of any fuel cell chemistry to be predicted directly from tabulated thermodynamic data ($\Delta G_f^{\circ}$ values) without performing the experiment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B (this is the standard KTU ESE module choice pattern).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a) Derive the relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^{\circ}$) and the standard cell EMF ($E^{\circ}$) of a fuel cell. Starting from first principles, show clearly each logical step. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a fuel cell reaction in which $n$ moles of electrons are transferred per mole of overall reaction. The total charge that moves through the external circuit per mole of fuel is $Q = nF$ coulombs, where $F$ is Faraday's constant ($96{,}485 \; C/mol$).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** When this charge moves through a potential difference of $E$ volts, the electrical work done by the cell is:
$$W_{elec} \;=\; Q \cdot E \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E \quad [\text{joules per mole}]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For a *reversible* (lossless) fuel cell, the second law of thermodynamics states that the maximum useful work obtainable at constant $T$ and $P$ equals the change in Gibbs free energy:
$$W_{max} \;=\; -\,\Delta G$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equating the electrical and thermodynamic expressions for maximum work:
$$n \cdot F \cdot E \;=\; -\,\Delta G$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** At standard conditions, $E = E^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ}$:
$$n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ} \;=\; -\,\Delta G^{\circ}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Solving for either variable, the final boxed results are:
$$\boxed{\;\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -\,n\,F\,E^{\circ}\;} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \boxed{\;E^{\circ} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n\,F}\;}$$

The sign convention is such that a *spontaneous* fuel cell reaction has $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$, giving a *positive* $E^{\circ}$ (a galvanic cell).

---

**Q: (b) For the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell reaction $\mathrm{H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)}$ at 25 °C and 1 atm, calculate the theoretical cell EMF ($E^{\circ}$) and the reversible thermodynamic efficiency. Given: $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) = -285.83 \; kJ/mol$, $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) = -237.13 \; kJ/mol$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \; C/mol$. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the standard free energy of formation for each species. By convention:
- $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2, g}) = 0$ (element in standard state)
- $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2, g}) = 0$ (element in standard state)
- $\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) = -237.13 \; kJ/mol$ (given)

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}$ for the cell reaction:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) - \left[\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2, g}) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{O_2, g})\right]$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -237.13 - [0 + 0] \;=\; \mathbf{-237.13 \; kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Convert to J/mol and apply the bridge equation:
$$E^{\circ} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} \;=\; -\,\frac{(-237.13 \times 10^{3}) \; J/mol}{2 \times 96{,}485 \; C/mol}$$
$$E^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} \;=\; \mathbf{1.229 \; V}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction:
$$\Delta H^{\circ} \;=\; -285.83 - [0 + 0] \;=\; \mathbf{-285.83 \; kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute the reversible (thermodynamic) efficiency:
$$\eta_{rev} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} \;=\; \frac{-237.13}{-285.83} \;=\; \mathbf{0.830 \;=\; 83.0\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students forget to convert kJ → J before using $E = -\Delta G/(nF)$, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000. Always write units explicitly in every line.
> 2. **Stoichiometry for n:** For the *overall* reaction $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O}$, exactly **$n = 2$** electrons are transferred per mole of $\mathrm{H_2}$. Do *not* use $n = 1$ (which would correspond to writing the half-reaction $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{H_2 \to H^+} + e^-$).
> 3. **Sign of $\eta_{rev}$:** Since *both* $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $\Delta H^{\circ}$ are negative, the negative signs cancel, giving a *positive* efficiency. A negative efficiency means a sign error has been made.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: (a) Explain the significance of the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation in the context of fuel cell operation. Show the derivation clearly. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Start from the definition of Gibbs free energy:
$$G \;=\; H \;-\; T \cdot S \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,S$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** For a chemical reaction, the change in Gibbs free energy between products and reactants is:
$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\Delta S$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Substitute $\Delta S = (\Delta H - \Delta G)/T$ from the relation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:
$$\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H - \Delta G}{T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G}{T} \;-\; \frac{\Delta H}{T}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Rearrange to recognise the exact derivative of $\Delta G/T$:
$$T \cdot \frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \;=\; \frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T} - \frac{\Delta G}{T} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T}$$

Dividing both sides by $T$:
$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Significance in fuel cells:** Since $\Delta H$ is negative (exothermic) for a fuel cell reaction, $(\Delta G/T)$ *increases* with $T$, meaning $\Delta G$ becomes *less negative* at higher temperatures. Consequently, the cell EMF $E = -\Delta G/(nF)$ **decreases** with rising temperature. This is the reason why **low-temperature fuel cells (e.g., PEMFC at 80 °C) have higher open-circuit voltages than high-temperature fuel cells (e.g., SOFC at 800 °C)** — even though the SOFC can use the rejected heat for cogeneration.

---

**Q: (b) The standard enthalpy of formation and standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water at 25 °C are $-285.83 \; kJ/mol$ and $-237.13 \; kJ/mol$ respectively. For the $\mathrm{H_2/O_2}$ fuel cell, calculate (i) the standard entropy change $\Delta S^{\circ}$ of the reaction, and (ii) the cell EMF at 80 °C, assuming $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are independent of temperature over this range. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** For the reaction $\mathrm{H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \to H_2O(l)}$:
- $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) = -285.83 \; kJ/mol$
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{298} = \Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{H_2O, l}) = -237.13 \; kJ/mol$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Calculate the standard entropy change at 25 °C (298 K):
$$\Delta S^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} \;=\; \frac{(-285.83) - (-237.13)}{298 \; K}$$
$$\Delta S^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{-48.70 \; kJ/mol}{298 \; K} \;=\; -0.1634 \; kJ/(mol \cdot K) \;=\; \mathbf{-163.4 \; J/(mol \cdot K)}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction produces a *more ordered* system (3 moles of gas → 1 mole of liquid), as expected from the loss of translational freedom.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at 80 °C ($T = 353 \; K$), assuming $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are temperature-independent:
$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{353} \;=\; \Delta H^{\circ} - T \cdot \Delta S^{\circ}$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{353} \;=\; -285.83 \; kJ/mol - 353 \; K \times (-0.1634 \; kJ/(mol \cdot K))$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{353} \;=\; -285.83 + 57.68 \;=\; \mathbf{-228.15 \; kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Calculate the cell EMF at 80 °C:
$$E^{\circ}_{353} \;=\; -\,\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{353}}{nF} \;=\; -\,\frac{(-228.15 \times 10^{3}) \; J/mol}{2 \times 96{,}485 \; C/mol}$$
$$E^{\circ}_{353} \;=\; \frac{228{,}150}{192{,}970} \;=\; \mathbf{1.182 \; V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Comparison with 25 °C value:** $E^{\circ}$ drops from 1.229 V (at 25 °C) to 1.182 V (at 80 °C) — a decrease of about 47 mV. This is consistent with the Gibbs-Helmholtz prediction that the EMF of an exothermic fuel cell reaction **decreases monotonically** with temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B, Part b):**
> 1. **Temperature in Kelvin:** Students frequently plug in $T = 80$ instead of $T = 353$ K. Always convert °C → K **before** substituting.
> 2. **Mixed units in $\Delta S$ calculation:** $\Delta H$ and $\Delta G$ are in kJ, $T$ is in K — keep everything in kJ when first computing $\Delta S$, then convert to J at the end if needed.
> 3. **Constant-$\Delta H$/$\Delta S$ assumption:** The problem explicitly states this assumption; do *not* introduce Kirchhoff's equation or temperature-dependent heat capacities unless asked. KTU examiners want to test the *basic* equation first.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Global Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfall List for Gibbs Free Energy Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Sign convention chaos:** Always state explicitly whether you are using the *thermodynamic* sign convention ($\Delta G < 0$ for spontaneous, $W_{max} = \Delta G$) or the *engineering* sign convention ($W_{max} = -\Delta G$). Mixing them costs 1–2 marks silently.
> 2. **kJ vs J unit conversion:** A missing "× 10³" or "÷ 1000" in $\Delta G = -nFE$ yields a 1000× wrong answer, which is *always* caught and *always* zeroed.
> 3. **Standard states of elements:** $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G_f^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{H_2, O_2, N_2,}$ etc. are *zero* by definition. Forgetting this gives a wrong algebraic sign in $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$.
> 4. **Identifying $n$ (electrons transferred):** Read the *full* balanced cell reaction, not the half-reaction. For $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O}$, $n = 2$, not 1 and not 4.
> 5. **HHV vs LHV trap:** A KTU question may give the *lower* heating value (water as vapour) and ask for cell EMF at 25 °C where water is *liquid*. Always match the *thermodynamic state* of water in $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ to the problem's stated product.
> 6. **Reversibility assumption:** All $\Delta G = -nFE$ formulas assume a *reversible* (no-loss) cell. Practical cell voltages are *lower* due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials — the *difference* is the *irreversibility loss*, not a thermodynamic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Print/Revise Before Exam**

- [ ] **Definition:** $G = H - TS$; $\Delta G$ is the **maximum non-expansion (useful) work** at constant $T$, $P$.
- [ ] **Key bridge equation (THE most important formula):** $\Delta G = -nFE$ — links thermodynamics to electrochemistry.
- [ ] **Solve for cell voltage:** $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ} / (nF)$ — gives the open-circuit voltage of any fuel cell.
- [ ] **Reversible efficiency:** $\eta_{rev} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ — theoretical upper bound on electrical efficiency.
- [ ] **H₂/O₂ reference values (must memorise):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \; kJ/mol$; $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83 \; kJ/mol$; $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \; V$; $\eta_{rev} = 83\%$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Spontaneous reaction ⇒ $\Delta G < 0$ ⇒ $E > 0$ (galvanic cell delivers power).
- [ ] **Standard free energy of formation of elements = 0** (H₂, O₂, N₂ in their standard states).
- [ ] **n (electrons transferred) is counted from the OVERALL cell reaction**, not half-reaction.
- [ ] **Unit trap:** Convert kJ → J before using $\Delta G = -nFE$ (multiply by 10³).
- [ ] **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:** $\partial(\Delta G/T)/\partial T = -\Delta H/T^2$ — predicts that **EMF decreases** with increasing $T$ for exothermic fuel cell reactions.
- [ ] **$T\Delta S$ is unavoidable heat** — not a defect, but a fundamental 2nd-law requirement.
- [ ] **HHV vs LHV:** HHV assumes liquid water product; LHV assumes vapour product. Match $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ to the problem's stated product phase.
- [ ] **Practical cells** achieve 40–60 % electrical efficiency (vs the 83 % H₂/O₂ theoretical limit) due to irreversibilities.
- [ ] **$\Delta G$** changes sign and magnitude with temperature and pressure; use Nernst form $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ for non-standard conditions.
- [ ] **Carbon/coal cell is unique:** $\mathrm{C + O_2 \to CO_2}$ has $\eta_{rev} > 100\%$ because $\Delta S > 0$ (more gas moles produced) — the only common fuel where this occurs.
- [ ] **Faraday's constant value:** $F = 96{,}485 \; C/mol$ (sometimes written as $96{,}500$) — do not confuse with the Faraday *unit* (older electrical unit of charge).
- [ ] **Pressure correction** uses partial-pressure ratio $P_i/P^{\circ}$ with $P^{\circ} = 1 \; atm$ in the Nernst term.
- [ ] **Exam mnemonic:** "**G**ood **H**orses **T**ravel **S**lowly" → $G = H - TS$ (subtract, not add, $TS$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversible Voltage - Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversible Voltage - Nernst Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 Reversible Voltage & The Nernst Equation — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversible Voltage (E_rev)**, also called the **Nernst Voltage (E_Nernst)**, is defined as the **theoretical maximum electromotive force (EMF)** that a galvanic (electrochemical) cell can deliver under **infinitesimally near-equilibrium** (lossless / thermodynamically reversible) operating conditions, evaluated at specified reactant partial pressures, product states, and cell temperature.

Mathematically, for any electrochemical cell running the half-cell redox reaction:

$$aA + bB \;\rightleftharpoons\; cC + dD + n\,e^{-}$$

the **Nernst Equation** prescribes the reversible cell potential as:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q$$

where every variable aligns with the KTU board-approved symbols used in the **PECHT634 Module 1** syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> The reversible voltage represents the *ideal* ceiling of fuel cell performance. **No real fuel cell can exceed E_rev** — it is the thermodynamic target that real cells (PEMFC, SOFC, PAFC, AFC, MCFC) chase but never reach due to irreversibilities (activation, ohmic, concentration losses).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Water Dam Model

Imagine a reservoir of water held back by a tall dam. The **maximum possible energy** you could extract from that water by letting it fall through a perfect (frictionless) turbine equals the **height of the dam multiplied by gravity**. That is your "reversible" or theoretical energy. 

In a fuel cell:
- The **dam height** = the **Gibbs Free Energy of reaction (ΔG)**
- The **frictionless turbine** = the ideal electrochemical cell
- The **actual output** = always less than E_rev because real turbines have friction (just as real fuel cells have activation losses, ohmic losses, and concentration losses)

The Nernst equation simply *converts* the chemical energy stored in hydrogen and oxygen into a voltage, just as the dam height converts stored water mass into gravitational potential energy.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **R** | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| **F** | Faraday's constant | **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** |
| **T** | Absolute temperature | **298.15 K** (standard) |
| **n** | Electrons transferred per mole of fuel | **2** (for H₂/H⁺ reaction) |
| **E°_rev** | Standard reversible EMF (H₂/O₂, liquid H₂O) | **1.229 V** at 25 °C |
| **ΔG°** | Std. Gibbs free energy of reaction | **−237.2 kJ/mol** (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l)) |

> [!NOTE]
> The number **1.229 V** is the **flagship benchmark** of the entire fuel cell course. Memorize this value — it appears in nearly every KTU board question on Module 1 thermodynamics.

---

## 1.4 Scope of the Nernst Equation in Module 1

The Nernst equation in Module 1 covers the following operational sub-topics that examiners love to test:
1. Derivation from the **Gibbs–Helmholtz** framework.
2. Application to the **Hydrogen–Oxygen (H₂/O₂) fuel cell**.
3. Effect of **temperature** on E_rev.
4. Effect of **reactant partial pressures** (P_H₂, P_O₂).
5. Treatment of product water as **liquid vs. vapour**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Logic Chain (Step-by-Step)
