The Nernst equation is **not** empirical — it is derived rigorously from the second law of thermodynamics. KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who can present the *logic chain*, not merely the final formula.

### Step 1 — Maximum Work from a Chemical Reaction
The **maximum non-PV (electrical) work** obtainable from any spontaneous reaction equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:

$$\Delta G \;=\; W_{elec,\,max} \;=\; -n\,F\,E_{cell}$$

- The negative sign indicates that energy is *released* (ΔG < 0) when E_cell > 0.
- Under standard-state conditions: $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}_{rev}$

### Step 2 — Gibbs Free Energy at Arbitrary Conditions
Real fuel cells rarely operate at 1 atm and 25 °C. Using the fundamental thermodynamic relation:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta G^{0} \;+\; R\,T\,\ln Q$$

where **Q** is the **reaction quotient** (product activities raised to stoichiometric coefficients divided by reactant activities).

### Step 3 — Equate and Isolate E_rev
Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$-n\,F\,E_{rev} \;=\; -n\,F\,E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; R\,T\,\ln Q$$

Dividing throughout by −nF:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** in its natural logarithmic form. For engineering convenience it is converted to base-10:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{2.303\,R\,T}{n\,F}\,\log_{10} Q$$

### Step 4 — Apply to the H₂/O₂ Cell
For the canonical hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell half-cell reaction:

$$H_{2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} O_{2} \;\rightarrow\; H_{2}O$$

The reversible voltage becomes:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\Big(P_{H_{2}} \, P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}\Big) \quad \text{(gaseous product)}$$

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\Big(P_{H_{2}} \, P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}} \big/ P_{H_{2}O}\Big) \quad \text{(vapour product, no liquid)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-winning tip:** When the product is *liquid water* (which is the reference state for E°_rev), the activity of H₂O is unity and therefore **does not appear** in the Nernst equation. Examiners test this distinction repeatedly.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G = -nFE_{rev}$ | Maximum electrical work from cell reaction | J/mol (n = mol e⁻ per mol fuel) |
| $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}_{rev}$ | Same, at standard state (25 °C, 1 atm) | J/mol |
| $E_{rev} = E^{0}_{rev} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst equation (natural log form) | V |
| $E_{rev} = E^{0}_{rev} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} Q$ | Nernst equation (log₁₀ form) | V |
| $E^{0}_{rev}(\,H_{2}/O_{2},\,l) = 1.229\ \text{V}$ | Std. EMF with liquid water product | At 25 °C, 1 atm |
| $E^{0}_{rev}(\,H_{2}/O_{2},\,g) = 1.185\ \text{V}$ | Std. EMF with vapour product | At 25 °C, 1 atm |
| $E_{rev} = 1.229 - 0.000845\,(T - 298.15)$ | Empirical T-dependence (l) | T in K, E in V |
| $\dfrac{RT}{nF} = 0.0257\ \text{V}$ (n=1) | Thermal voltage factor at 25 °C | V |
| $\dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln 10 = 0.02958\ \text{V}$ | Base-10 form factor (n=2) | V at 298.15 K |
| $\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \dfrac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency | Dimensionless, $\vert\eta_{rev}\vert \leq 1$ |
| $\Delta S^{0} = -163.3\ \text{J/mol·K}$ (H₂+O₂, l) | Std. entropy change | Negative ⇒ cell cools on discharge |
| $\Delta H^{0} = -285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$ (H₂O l) | Std. enthalpy of combustion of H₂ | Higher heating value (HHV) |
| $\Delta H^{0} = -241.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$ (H₂O g) | Std. enthalpy (vapour product) | Lower heating value (LHV) |

---

## 2.3 Why the Nernst Equation Matters in Engineering

The Nernst equation is the **engineering ruler** used to:
- **Design fuel cell stacks**: it sets the open-circuit voltage (OCV) target before any irreversibilities are subtracted.
- **Diagnose operating conditions**: deviations between measured OCV and predicted E_rev reveal **fuel crossover, internal currents, or reactant starvation**.
- **Select operating pressure**: increasing reactant partial pressures raises E_rev logarithmically (used in pressurised systems and aerospace fuel cells).
- **Choose operating temperature**: temperature effects are subtle because both E°_rev and the (RT/nF) factor shift simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ✏️ Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrices

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Nernst Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise — From Thermodynamics to Voltage

Consider a general electrochemical reaction:

$$\sum_i \nu_i\, M_i^{\,z_i} \;\rightarrow\; \text{products} \;+\; n\,e^{-}$$

The **electrical work** obtainable from transferring `n` moles of electrons across a potential difference `E` is:

$$W_{elec} \;=\; n\,F\,E$$

For a **reversible** (lossless) cell, the second law demands that this electrical work equal the negative of the Gibbs free energy change:

$$n\,F\,E_{rev} \;=\; -\Delta G$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,E_{rev} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta G}{n\,F}\,}$$

This is **Equation 1** — the bridge between thermodynamics and electrochemistry.

### 3.1.2 Expressing ΔG at Non-Standard Conditions

By the definition of the **reaction quotient** Q, the change in Gibbs free energy at arbitrary state is:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta G^{0} \;+\; R\,T\,\ln Q$$

This is **Equation 2**, where:

$$Q \;=\; \prod_j a_{j}^{\nu_{j}} \;\;/\;\; \prod_i a_{i}^{\nu_{i}}$$

(`a` denotes activities of products over reactants.)

### 3.1.3 Substituting Equation 2 into Equation 1

Insert Equation 2 into Equation 1:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{n\,F}\,\big[\Delta G^{0} + R\,T\,\ln Q\big]$$

$$E_{rev} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{n\,F} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q$$

Since $E^{0}_{rev} = -\Delta G^{0} / (nF)$ (the standard form of Equation 1):

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q$$

This is the **Nernst Equation**. ∎

### 3.1.4 Specialisation to the Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell

For the reaction $H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}$:

- Number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂: `n = 2`
- For **liquid water** product, the activity of pure liquid H₂O = 1.
- For ideal gases, activity = partial pressure (in atm).

Hence:

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{a_{H_{2}O}}{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}$$

For liquid product (a_H₂O = 1):

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\bigg(\dfrac{1}{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}\bigg)$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\big(P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}\big)\,}$$

For gaseous water product (a_H₂O = P_H₂O):

$$\boxed{\,E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\bigg(\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}{P_{H_{2}O}}\bigg)\,}$$

### 3.1.5 Numerical Evaluation at 25 °C (298.15 K)

Plugging in constants at standard temperature:

$$\dfrac{R\,T}{2F} \;=\; \dfrac{(8.314)(298.15)}{(2)(96485)}$$

$$\dfrac{R\,T}{2F} \;=\; \dfrac{2478.82}{192970} \;\approx\; 0.01284\ \text{V}$$

Converting to log₁₀ (multiply by 2.303):

$$\dfrac{2.303\,R\,T}{2F} \;\approx\; 0.02958\ \text{V}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{rev} \;\approx\; 1.229 \;+\; 0.02958\,\log_{10}\!\big(P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}\big) \quad \text{(at 25 °C, liquid H₂O)}$$

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C with P_H₂ = 1.5 atm, P_O₂ = 0.21 atm, and liquid water product. Calculate the reversible cell voltage using the Nernst equation.

**Solution:**

- T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 K
- E°_rev at 80 °C using the empirical temperature correction:

$$E^{0}_{rev}(353.15) \;=\; 1.229 \;-\; 0.000845\,(353.15 - 298.15)$$

$$E^{0}_{rev}(353.15) \;=\; 1.229 \;-\; 0.000845 \times 55 \;=\; 1.229 - 0.0465 \;=\; 1.1825\ \text{V}$$

- Compute the Nernst correction factor:

$$\dfrac{R\,T}{2F} \;=\; \dfrac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2 \times 96485} \;=\; \dfrac{2936.30}{192970} \;\approx\; 0.01522\ \text{V}$$

- Reaction quotient term:

$$P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}} \;=\; (1.5)(0.21)^{0.5} \;=\; (1.5)(0.4583) \;\approx\; 0.6874$$

- Apply Nernst:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; 1.1825 \;+\; (0.01522)\,\ln(0.6874)$$

$$E_{rev} \;=\; 1.1825 \;+\; (0.01522)(-0.3747)$$

$$E_{rev} \;=\; 1.1825 \;-\; 0.00570 \;\approx\; 1.1768\ \text{V}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Because partial pressures are *below* standard (1 atm), the reversible voltage *drops slightly* below E°_rev. Conversely, pressurising the reactants would *raise* E_rev logarithmically.

### 3.1.7 Derivation of the Temperature-Dependent E°_rev

Starting from $\Delta G^{0}(T) = \Delta H^{0}(T) - T\Delta S^{0}(T)$ and using Kirchhoff's relations (assuming constant ΔC_p over the temperature range):

$$\Delta H^{0}(T) \;=\; \Delta H^{0}_{298} \;+\; \Delta C_{p}\,(T - 298)$$

$$\Delta S^{0}(T) \;=\; \Delta S^{0}_{298} \;+\; \Delta C_{p}\,\ln\!\Big(\dfrac{T}{298}\Big)$$

Substituting and dividing by −nF gives the rigorous thermal model:

$$E^{0}_{rev}(T) \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta H^{0}_{298}}{n\,F} \;+\; T\,\dfrac{\Delta S^{0}_{298}}{n\,F} \;-\; \dfrac{\Delta C_{p}\,T}{n\,F}\!\left[1 - \ln\!\Big(\dfrac{T}{298}\Big)\right]$$

For the H₂/O₂/liquid system (ΔC_p ≈ 75 J/mol·K, n = 2), this simplifies to the *empirical correlation* often quoted in KTU textbooks:

$$E^{0}_{rev}(T) \;\approx\; 1.4823 \;-\; 0.000845\,T \;+\; 0.0000431\,T\,\ln T \quad (\text{T in K})$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📐 Structural Diagrams & Functional Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — From Thermodynamics to Nernst Voltage

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **derivation pathway** linking the macroscopic thermodynamic state functions (ΔG, ΔH, ΔS) to the measurable fuel cell voltage via the Nernst equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactant Species H2 and O2] --> B[Electrochemical Reaction H2 + 0.5 O2 to H2O]
    B --> C[Compute Gibbs Free Energy Change deltaG]
    D[Standard State 25 C and 1 atm] --> E[Standard Free Energy deltaG0 equals minus 237.2 kJ per mol]
    C --> F[Apply deltaG equals deltaG0 plus RT ln Q]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Nernst Equation Erev equals E0rev minus RT over nF times ln Q]
    G --> H[Substitute partial pressures P_H2 and P_O2]
    H --> I[Compute Numerical Erev in Volts]
    I --> J{Operating Conditions}
    J --> K[Low Pressure: Erev drops below E0rev]
    J --> L[High Pressure: Erev rises above E0rev]
    J --> M[Elevated T: E0rev decreases due to negative entropy change]
    K --> N[Final Reversible Voltage Output]
    L --> N
    M --> N

    classDef thermoNode fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mathNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class C,E,F,G,I thermoNode
    class A,B,D,H,J,K,L,M mathNode
    class N outputNode
```

## 4.2 Schematic Diagram — Partial-Pressure Influence on E_rev

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Low P_H2 and P_O2] -->|Logarithmic drop| R1[Erev less than E0rev]
    P2[Standard 1 atm each] -->|Reference| R2[Erev equals E0rev equals 1.229 V]
    P3[High P_H2 and P_O2] -->|Logarithmic rise| R3[Erev greater than E0rev]

    R1 --> OUT[Open Circuit Voltage Determination]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT

    classDef lowNode fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stdNode fill:#fffde7,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef highNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class P1,R1 lowNode
    class P2,R2 stdNode
    class P3,R3 highNode
    class OUT outputNode
```

## 4.3 Architecture Summary — Why Each Block Matters

| Block | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Reactant Identification | Defines `Q` and the value of `n` (electrons transferred) |
| Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG) | Sets the *upper bound* of extractable electrical work |
| Nernst Correction Term | Quantifies voltage deviation from standard conditions |
| Pressure Influence | Drives the case for pressurised or hybrid fuel cell systems |
| Temperature Influence | Governs thermal-management strategy and stack design |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Nernst equation for a fuel cell. Why is it called the reversible voltage?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nernst equation gives the maximum theoretical EMF (electromotive force) of a fuel cell as a function of temperature, pressure, and reactant/product concentrations:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;-\; \dfrac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q$$

It is called the **reversible voltage** because it represents the cell voltage under **infinitesimally reversible** (lossless, equilibrium) conditions, where the cell operates so slowly that no entropy is produced beyond the unavoidable entropy change of the reaction itself. No irreversible losses (activation, ohmic, concentration) are present, so this voltage is the **theoretical maximum** a fuel cell can deliver under given operating conditions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write down the Nernst equation for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell when (i) the product water is in liquid state and (ii) the product water is in vapour state. State the corresponding standard EMF values at 25 °C.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Liquid water product:**

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\big(P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}\big), \quad E^{0}_{rev} = 1.229\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Vapour water product:**

$$E_{rev} \;=\; E^{0}_{rev} \;+\; \dfrac{R\,T}{2F}\,\ln\!\bigg(\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}{P_{H_{2}O}}\bigg), \quad E^{0}_{rev} = 1.185\ \text{V}$$

The difference arises because when water remains as vapour, some of the Gibbs free energy is consumed in the phase change (vaporisation), reducing the available electrical work.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are two complete alternate-choice questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from the relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work, derive the Nernst equation for an electrochemical cell. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell operating at 65 °C with P_H₂ = 1.2 atm, P_O₂ = 0.5 atm and liquid water as product, calculate the reversible cell voltage using the Nernst equation. Use the empirical relation E°_rev(T) = 1.229 − 0.000845 (T − 298.15). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Maximum electrical work from cell = nFE

**Step 2:** At equilibrium: W_max = −ΔG → ΔG = −nFE_rev

**Step 3:** At non-standard conditions: ΔG = ΔG⁰ + RT ln Q

**Step 4:** Substitute and divide by −nF:

$$E_{rev} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q = E^{0}_{rev} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

**Step 5:** Convert to log₁₀: $E_{rev} = E^{0}_{rev} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} Q$

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| E_rev | Reversible cell voltage (V) |
| E⁰_rev | Standard reversible EMF at 25 °C, 1 atm (V) |
| R | Universal gas constant = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| T | Absolute temperature (K) |
| n | Number of electrons transferred |
| F | Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol |
| Q | Reaction quotient (product activities / reactant activities) |

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute E°_rev at 65 °C:**

$$E^{0}_{rev}(338.15\,\text{K}) = 1.229 - 0.000845(338.15 - 298.15)$$

$$= 1.229 - 0.000845 \times 40 = 1.229 - 0.0338 = 1.1952\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute RT/(2F) at 65 °C:**

$$\dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{(8.314)(338.15)}{2(96485)} = \dfrac{2811.05}{192970} = 0.01457\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm argument:**

$$P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}} = (1.2)(0.5)^{0.5} = (1.2)(0.7071) = 0.8485$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Nernst equation:**

$$E_{rev} = 1.1952 + (0.01457)\,\ln(0.8485)$$

$$= 1.1952 + (0.01457)(-0.1644) = 1.1952 - 0.002395 \approx 1.1928\ \text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** **E_rev ≈ 1.193 V**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **reversible voltage (E_rev)** in a fuel cell. Discuss how it depends on **(i)** temperature and **(ii)** partial pressures of reactants. Use the hydrogen–oxygen cell as the illustrative example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell operates at 90 °C with P_H₂ = 2.0 atm and P_O₂ = 1.0 atm. The product is water vapour at 1.0 atm. Calculate the reversible voltage of the cell. Given: for the H₂/O₂ system with vapour product, E°_rev (25 °C) = 1.185 V; use the temperature-correction relation: E°_rev(T) = E°_rev(25 °C) − 0.000845 (T − 298.15). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Concept of E_rev:**
The reversible voltage E_rev is the theoretical maximum EMF of a fuel cell, achievable only under thermodynamically reversible (lossless) operation. It equals the ratio of the decrease in Gibbs free energy to the electrical charge transferred.

**(i) Temperature Dependence:**
The temperature dependence arises through two mechanisms:
- The standard reversible EMF E⁰_rev **decreases** with temperature because the entropy change ΔS⁰ of the H₂/O₂ reaction is **negative** (the reaction produces fewer gas molecules than it consumes).
- The Nernst term (RT/nF) **increases** linearly with temperature, amplifying any logarithmic deviation from standard partial pressures.

Empirically: $E^{0}_{rev}(T) = 1.229 - 0.000845(T - 298.15)$ for liquid water product.

**(ii) Partial Pressure Dependence:**
The reversible voltage increases **logarithmically** with reactant partial pressures:

$$E_{rev} \propto \ln(P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}})$$

Doubling the partial pressures only produces a few tens of millivolts of gain — the logarithmic dependence means the engineering returns of pressurisation diminish quickly. Liquid water does not appear in the equation (its activity = 1), but water vapour does.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert temperature:**

$$T = 90 + 273.15 = 363.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature-corrected E⁰_rev:**

$$E^{0}_{rev}(363.15) = 1.185 - 0.000845(363.15 - 298.15)$$

$$= 1.185 - 0.000845 \times 65 = 1.185 - 0.05493 = 1.1301\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute RT/(2F):**

$$\dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{(8.314)(363.15)}{(2)(96485)} = \dfrac{3019.35}{192970} = 0.01565\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Nernst argument (vapour product):**

$$\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}}{P_{H_{2}O}} = \dfrac{(2.0)(1.0)^{0.5}}{1.0} = 2.0$$

**Step 5 — Apply Nernst equation:**

$$E_{rev} = 1.1301 + (0.01565)\,\ln(2.0)$$

$$= 1.1301 + (0.01565)(0.6931) = 1.1301 + 0.01085 \approx 1.1410\ \text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** **E_rev ≈ 1.141 V**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in the log term:** Many students write the Nernst equation with a *plus* sign before the log term for the H₂/O₂ cell but a *minus* sign for other cells. Always check the *direction* of Q: if reactants are in the denominator of Q, the log becomes positive when partial pressures exceed 1 atm.
> 2. **Confusing E⁰_rev values:** Mixing 1.229 V (liquid water) and 1.185 V (vapour water) is one of the most common mistakes. The product state **must match** the chosen E⁰_rev.
> 3. **Unit of partial pressure:** If partial pressures are entered in **bar** instead of **atm**, the numerical result is wrong because the reference state of unit activity is 1 atm.
> 4. **Forgetting the temperature correction:** Using 1.229 V at 80 °C without correction costs full marks on computational questions.
> 5. **Mis-application of n:** Always use n = 2 for the H₂/O₂ cell (not 4 or 1). For direct-methanol or other fuels, n differs.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps:** In 7-mark derivations, merely writing the final Nernst equation without showing the ΔG → ΔG⁰ + RT ln Q substitution chain attracts only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Reversible voltage** = theoretical *maximum* EMF, achievable only under thermodynamically lossless conditions.
- ✅ **Nernst equation (general):** $E_{rev} = E^{0}_{rev} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$
- ✅ **H₂/O₂ cell (liquid water):** $E_{rev} = E^{0}_{rev} + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\big(P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{\tfrac{1}{2}}\big)$, with $E^{0}_{rev} = 1.229\ \text{V}$ at 25 °C.
- ✅ **H₂/O₂ cell (vapour water):** $E^{0}_{rev} = 1.185\ \text{V}$ at 25 °C; water-vapour pressure appears in denominator of log argument.
- ✅ **RT/(2F)** at 25 °C ≈ **0.01284 V** (natural log) or **0.02958 V** (log₁₀).
- ✅ **E_rev decreases** with temperature for the H₂/O₂ cell (ΔS < 0).
- ✅ **E_rev increases logarithmically** with reactant partial pressures.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic origin:** ΔG = −nFE_rev, derived from ΔG = ΔG⁰ + RT ln Q.
- ✅ **Standard values to memorise:** ΔG⁰ = −237.2 kJ/mol (H₂O liquid), ΔH⁰ = −285.8 kJ/mol, ΔS⁰ = −163.3 J/mol·K, n = 2.
- ✅ **KTU board expects:** clear derivation, correct substitution of numerical constants, proper distinction between liquid/vapour product states, and explicit statement of units.
- ✅ **Reversible efficiency:** $\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \approx 0.83$ for H₂ (HHV) at 25 °C — sets the upper bound of fuel cell electrical efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage and P, T and Concentration Dependence - Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage and P, T and Concentration Dependence - Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Reversible (Nernst) Cell Voltage

The **Nernst Equation** is the thermodynamic cornerstone of fuel cell analysis. It gives the **maximum possible (reversible) voltage** that a fuel cell can deliver under a given set of operating conditions (temperature, pressure, and reactant concentration), assuming the cell operates infinitely close to equilibrium with no current flowing.

For the generic half-cell reaction:

$$\text{Ox} + n\,e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

the Nernst equation is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** (activities of products raised to their stoichiometric coefficients divided by those of the reactants).

For the canonical Hydrogen–Oxygen reaction in a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC):

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

the Nernst equation assumes two well-known forms:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right) \quad \text{(liquid water product)}$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right) \quad \text{(gaseous water product)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel cell's open-circuit voltage (OCV) is **NOT** constant — it is a function of three primary operating variables. You will be tested on how to compute this dependence and physically interpret it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dam Analogy"

Imagine a hydroelectric dam. The **water level** behind the dam is the *chemical potential* (concentration/pressure) of the fuel, and the **atmospheric pressure** below the dam is the *chemical potential* of the oxidant and product. The **voltage** of the fuel cell is analogous to the **height of the water column**:

- **Higher reactant pressure** (H₂ or O₂) → *taller water column* → **higher voltage**.
- **Higher temperature** → *warmer water, slightly more chaotic flow* → **lower voltage** (for liquid water product).
- **Lower reactant concentration** (e.g., air instead of pure O₂) → *shorter water column* → **lower voltage**.

This single mental picture will guide you through every Nernst-based example in this module.

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used Throughout

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Standard Temperature | $T^{\circ}$ | **298.15** | K |
| Standard Pressure | $P^{\circ}$ | **1.00** | atm |
| Standard Voltage (H₂/O₂, liquid H₂O) | $E^{\circ}$ | **1.229** | V |
| Number of electrons transferred | $n$ | **2** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** To go from atm to Pa, multiply by **101,325**. To go from atm to bar, multiply by **1.01325**. KTU problems typically use atm, but always read the units in the question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Voltage Depends on P, T, and Concentration — The Thermodynamic Origin

The reversible voltage $E$ is directly linked to the **Gibbs free energy** of the cell reaction:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

A change in operating conditions alters $\Delta G$, and therefore $E$. The three fundamental thermodynamic identities that govern these dependencies are:

1. **Temperature dependence** (Gibbs–Helmholtz):
$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\Delta S \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}}$$

2. **Pressure dependence** (Maxwell relation from $G$):
$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \Delta V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\frac{\Delta V}{nF}}$$

3. **Composition dependence** (chemical potential identity):
$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{a_{\text{products}}^{\nu_p}}{a_{\text{reactants}}^{\nu_r}}\right)}$$

where $a_i$ is the **activity** of species $i$ (≈ partial pressure in atm for ideal gases, ≈ molar concentration in mol/L for dilute solutions).

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation of Each Dependence

- **Pressure Dependence:** Increasing the partial pressure of reactants (H₂, O₂) **shifts the equilibrium toward products**, raising the cell voltage. The logarithmic relationship is sub-linear — doubling the pressure does *not* double the voltage.
- **Temperature Dependence:** The sign of $\partial E/\partial T$ is governed by $\Delta S$. For H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l), $\Delta S < 0$, so **voltage decreases with temperature**. For the gaseous product case, the drop is milder.
- **Concentration Dependence:** Lower reactant activity means lower chemical potential and hence a **lower driving force** for the reaction. This is why air-fed cells (21% O₂) deliver lower OCV than pure-O₂ cells.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas in this table — at least one will appear in every Part B question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | General Nernst equation | V |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right)$ | PEMFC, liquid H₂O product | V |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$ | SOFC / PAFC, gaseous H₂O product | V |
| 4 | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(C_{\text{H}_2}\,C_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right)$ | Dissolved reactants (e.g., DMFC) | V |
| 5 | $E^{\circ}(T) = E^{\circ}(298) + (T-298)\!\left(\frac{\partial E^{\circ}}{\partial T}\right)$ | $E^{\circ}$ at non-standard T | V |
| 6 | $\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$ | Slope of E–T curve | V K⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\frac{\Delta V}{nF}$ | Slope of E–P curve | V Pa⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | Link between E° and ΔG° | J mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $E^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{T\,\Delta S^{\circ}}{nF}$ | $E^{\circ}$ via $\Delta H^{\circ}, \Delta S^{\circ}$ | V |
| 10 | $P_i = y_i\,P_{\text{total}}$ | Partial pressure of gas $i$ | atm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch out!** $\partial E/\partial T$ is **per Kelvin**, not per degree Celsius. The Nernst equation requires **absolute temperature** in Kelvin — a 1 °C error in conversion = 274 K error!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel cell stack design:** Nernst voltage sets the **upper bound** on stack output. Engineers use the P, T, C dependencies to pick optimal operating points (e.g., 80 °C, 3 atm for automotive PEMFC).
- **Air vs. pure O₂ trade-off:** Replacing pure O₂ with air dilutes $P_{\text{O}_2}$ by a factor of ~5, costing ~17 mV of reversible voltage — a critical design parameter.
- **Pressurization cost:** Doubling system pressure improves OCV logarithmically, but adds parasitic compressor load. Optimization yields a "sweet spot."
- **Temperature control:** SOFCs run hot (600–1000 °C) primarily for electrode kinetics; the Nernst penalty at high T is acceptable because $E^{\circ}$ itself rises when water exits as vapor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Gibbs Free Energy to the Nernst Equation

We start from the fundamental relation between Gibbs free energy and cell potential:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For a reaction with non-standard activities, $\Delta G$ is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$

Equating the two and dividing by $-nF$:

$$E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

Since $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/nF$:

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q}$$

This is the **Nernst equation** in its most general form.

For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction, $Q = 1/(P_{\text{H}_2}P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$ (activity of liquid water is unity), so:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right)$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pressure Dependence

**Problem:** A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 3$ atm and $P_{\text{O}_2} = 5$ atm. Liquid water is produced. Calculate the Nernst voltage. (Take $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V and $\Delta S^{\circ} = -163.2$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹; assume $E^{\circ}$ at 80 °C is 1.183 V.)

**Step 1 — Identify the reaction and Nernst form:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = 2$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Convert temperature:**

$$T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Nernst term constant:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{2(96485)} = \frac{2936.4}{192970} = 0.01522 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithmic argument:**

$$P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2} = (3)(5)^{0.5} = (3)(2.2361) = 6.7082$$

**Step 5 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(6.7082) = 1.9026$$

**Step 6 — Combine all terms:**

$$E = 1.183 + (0.01522)(1.9026) = 1.183 + 0.02896$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.212 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Operating at 3 atm H₂ and 5 atm O₂ gives a Nernst voltage of about **1.21 V** — slightly higher than the 25 °C/1 atm value of 1.229 V? No — it is **lower**, because the temperature penalty (1.183 V < 1.229 V) outweighs the modest pressure gain (29 mV). The cell *wants* to be cool and pressurized.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Temperature Dependence

**Problem:** A SOFC operates at 1000 K with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1$ atm, and $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1$ atm. Compute the Nernst voltage and the temperature coefficient $\partial E/\partial T$ at this point. (Use $\Delta S^{\circ} = -44.4$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ for gaseous H₂O; assume $E^{\circ}(1000\text{ K}) = 1.06$ V.)

**Step 1 — Identify the form of the Nernst equation (gaseous product):**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic argument:**

$$\frac{(1)(1)^{0.5}}{1} = 1.0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln(1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the Nernst correction is zero:**

$$E_{\text{Nernst}} = E^{\circ}(1000\text{ K}) = 1.06 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the temperature coefficient:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{nF} = \frac{-44.4}{2 \times 96485} = -2.30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V K}^{-1}$$

This means for a 10 K rise in temperature, the reversible voltage **decreases by 2.3 mV** (with pressure and composition held fixed).

**Step 5 — Cross-check by finite-difference at 1010 K:**

If we treat the partial pressures as unchanged, $E^{\circ}(1010)$ would rise by $(10)(-2.3\times 10^{-4}) = -0.0023$ V, giving $E^{\circ}(1010) = 1.058$ V. This is consistent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The temperature coefficient is **negative** when liquid water is the product (-0.846 mV K⁻¹) and **less negative** when gaseous water is the product (-0.23 mV K⁻¹). Examiners love asking "why is the SOFC voltage drop less severe?" — answer: because gaseous H₂O has higher entropy than liquid H₂O, so $|\Delta S|$ is smaller.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Concentration Dependence

**Problem:** A direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) operates at 60 °C with the following dissolved reactant concentrations at the catalyst surface: $[\text{CH}_3\text{OH}] = 0.5$ M, $[\text{O}_2] = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ M. The reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (n=6)$$

Take $E^{\circ} = 1.21$ V for this reaction. Calculate the Nernst voltage using concentration-based activities. ($T = 333.15$ K.)

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation in concentration form:**

For ideal dilute solutions, $a_i \approx C_i$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{[\text{CO}_2][\text{H}_2\text{O}]^2}{[\text{CH}_3\text{OH}][\text{O}_2]^{3/2}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Assume activities of pure products are unity** (CO₂ leaves as gas, H₂O activity ≈ 1 in dilute solution):

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{6F}\ln\!\left([\text{CH}_3\text{OH}][\text{O}_2]^{3/2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Nernst term constant:**

$$\frac{RT}{6F} = \frac{(8.314)(333.15)}{6(96485)} = \frac{2770.2}{578910} = 4.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the argument of the logarithm:**

$$[\text{CH}_3\text{OH}][\text{O}_2]^{3/2} = (0.5)(1.2\times 10^{-3})^{1.5}$$

Computing the power:

$$(1.2\times 10^{-3})^{1.5} = (1.2)^{1.5} \times (10^{-3})^{1.5} = 1.3145 \times 10^{-4.5} = 1.3145 \times 3.1623\times 10^{-5} = 4.157\times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$(0.5)(4.157\times 10^{-5}) = 2.078\times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural log:**

$$\ln(2.078\times 10^{-5}) = \ln(2.078) + \ln(10^{-5}) = 0.731 - 11.513 = -10.782$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Nernst correction:**

$$\frac{RT}{6F}\ln(\cdot) = (4.785\times 10^{-3})(-10.782) = -0.0516 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Final Nernst voltage:**

$$E = 1.21 + (-0.0516) = 1.158 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.158 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dissolved O₂ concentration is **extremely low** in aqueous electrolytes (≈ 1 mM) — a major kinetic bottleneck for DMFCs and microbial fuel cells. Designers use catalyst microporosity and gas-diffusion electrodes to maximize local [O₂].

### 3.5 Combined-Parameter Example (Capstone)

**Problem:** A PEMFC runs at 75 °C with total pressure 2.5 atm, fed with **pure H₂ and air** (21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume) at the anode and cathode respectively. Calculate the Nernst voltage.

**Step 1 — Partial pressures:**

$$P_{\text{H}_2} = 2.5 \text{ atm} \quad(\text{pure H}_2)$$
$$P_{\text{O}_2} = (0.21)(2.5) = 0.525 \text{ atm} \quad(\text{air})$$

**Step 2 — Standard voltage at 75 °C** (interpolating between 1.183 V at 80 °C and 1.229 V at 25 °C):

At 75 °C = 348.15 K:
$$E^{\circ}(348) \approx 1.183 + (80-75)(0.000846) = 1.183 + 0.0042 = 1.1872 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Nernst term:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(348.15)}{2(96485)} = 0.01500 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Log argument:**

$$P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2} = (2.5)(0.525)^{0.5} = (2.5)(0.7246) = 1.8115$$

**Step 5 — Ln and final E:**

$$\ln(1.8115) = 0.5940$$
$$E = 1.1872 + (0.01500)(0.5940) = 1.1872 + 0.00891$$
$$\boxed{E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.196 \text{ V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 1.196 V is the **reversible** OCV. Once current is drawn, the actual cell voltage drops to perhaps 0.6–0.7 V due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials — the *real* engineering challenge of fuel cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Cell Voltage Equation

The following flowchart shows how the **operating variables (P, T, C)** feed into the Nernst equation to yield the reversible cell voltage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OPERATING CONDITIONS] --> B[PRESSURE P]
    A --> C[TEMPERATURE T]
    A --> D[CONCENTRATION C]
    B --> E[PARTIAL PRESSURE Pi equals yi times P total]
    C --> F[STANDARD VOLTAGE E0 at temperature T]
    D --> G[ACTIVITY ai approximates Pi or Ci]
    E --> H[NERNST EQUATION E equals E0 plus RT over nF times ln of product of activities]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[REVERSIBLE VOLTAGE E NERNST]
    I --> J[ACTUAL CELL VOLTAGE V equals E minus activation minus ohmic minus concentration]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — From Thermodynamic Inputs to Cell Voltage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Partial Pressures P_H2 and P_O2] --> NERNST
    T[Operating Temperature T in K] --> NERNST
    C[Reactant Activities a_H2 and a_O2] --> NERNST
    NERNST[NERNST MODULE] --> OUT1[E NERNST in Volts]
    OUT1 --> SUB[Subtract Overpotentials]
    ACT[Activation Loss] --> SUB
    OHM[Ohmic Loss] --> SUB
    CONC[Concentration Loss] --> SUB
    SUB --> OUT2[V CELL Actual Operating Voltage]
```

### 4.3 Voltage Sensitivity Map — Direction of Change

| Increase In | Effect on E | Magnitude (PEMFC, 80 C) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{\text{H}_2}$ (partial) | **Increase** | $\approx +13$ mV per atm at 1 atm |
| $P_{\text{O}_2}$ (partial) | **Increase** | $\approx +6.5$ mV per atm at 1 atm |
| Total pressure (stoichiometric) | **Increase** (log) | $+44$ mV at 1 to 3 atm |
| Temperature (liquid H₂O) | **Decrease** | $-0.85$ mV per K |
| Temperature (gaseous H₂O, SOFC) | **Decrease** (mild) | $-0.23$ mV per K |
| Reactant concentration | **Increase** (log) | $\approx +13$ mV per decade (H₂) |
| Product concentration | **Decrease** | $\approx -13$ mV per decade (H₂O) |

> [!NOTE]
> The "$\approx +13$ mV per atm" rule is a useful **KTU board-exam shortcut** at 25 °C — it is exactly the Nernst term $RT/(2F) = 0.01284$ V divided by 1 atm.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
Define the Nernst equation for a fuel cell. State the standard conditions under which $E^{\circ}$ is defined.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Nernst equation expresses the **reversible cell voltage** $E$ of an electrochemical cell in terms of the **standard electrode potential** $E^{\circ}$ and the **reaction quotient** $Q$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For a fuel cell, $E^{\circ}$ is defined at **standard temperature (25 °C / 298.15 K)**, **standard pressure (1 atm / 101.325 kPa)**, and **unit activities** of all reactants and products. For the H₂/O₂ fuel cell, $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V (liquid water product) or $E^{\circ} = 1.185$ V (gaseous water product). *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — standard conditions + values)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain qualitatively how the reversible cell voltage of a hydrogen fuel cell varies with **(a)** operating pressure and **(b)** operating temperature.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**(a) Pressure:** Increasing the **partial pressure of reactants (H₂, O₂)** increases the cell voltage logarithmically, following the Nernst term $(RT/nF)\ln(P_{\text{H}_2}P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$. The effect is **sub-linear** — doubling pressure does not double the voltage. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) Temperature:** The temperature coefficient is $\partial E/\partial T = \Delta S/(nF)$. For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction, $\Delta S = -163.2$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, which is **negative**, so the **reversible voltage decreases with temperature** at approximately $-0.85$ mV K⁻¹. For gaseous water product, the drop is milder ($\approx -0.23$ mV K⁻¹). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (A)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, S1 Pattern]**
A proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) operates at **70 °C** with the following conditions:

- Anode: Pure H₂ at $P_{\text{H}_2} = 2.5$ atm
- Cathode: Air (21% O₂, 79% N₂) at total pressure 2.5 atm
- Product: Liquid water

Given: $E^{\circ}(70\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 1.189$ V, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹.

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ reaction with liquid water product. Starting from $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$, show all intermediate steps and arrive at the working formula.

**(b)** [7 marks] Calculate the **reversible open-circuit voltage** under the given operating conditions. Comment on the sign and magnitude of the deviation from $E^{\circ}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Nernst equation [7 marks]:**

Start with the fundamental thermodynamic identity linking Gibbs free energy and cell voltage [1 mark]:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For a non-standard reaction, the change in Gibbs free energy is related to the standard change by [1 mark]:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$

Equate and divide by $-nF$ [1 mark]:

$$E = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

By definition, $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/nF$ [1 mark]:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l), the reaction quotient is $Q = a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}/(a_{\text{H}_2}\,a_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$. The activity of pure liquid water is **unity** [1 mark], and for ideal gases, $a_i = P_i/P^{\circ}$ with $P^{\circ} = 1$ atm. So:

$$Q = \frac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}$$

Substituting [1 mark]:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}\right) = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}\right)$$

This is the **working Nernst equation** for the PEMFC. [1 mark for the final boxed form]

**(b) Calculation of the reversible OCV [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Identify partial pressures. Air is 21% O₂ by volume [1 mark]:

$$P_{\text{O}_2} = (0.21)(2.5) = 0.525 \text{ atm}, \quad P_{\text{H}_2} = 2.5 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2:** Convert temperature to Kelvin [0.5 mark]:

$$T = 70 + 273.15 = 343.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Nernst constant [1 mark]:

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(343.15)}{2(96485)} = \frac{2853.1}{192970} = 0.01478 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the argument of the logarithm [1 mark]:

$$P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2} = (2.5)(0.525)^{0.5} = (2.5)(0.7246) = 1.8115$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\ln(1.8115)$ [0.5 mark]:

$$\ln(1.8115) = 0.5940$$

**Step 6:** Apply the Nernst equation [1.5 marks]:

$$E = 1.189 + (0.01478)(0.5940) = 1.189 + 0.00878 = 1.198 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Comment [1.5 marks]:**
The reversible OCV is **1.198 V**, which is **lower** than $E^{\circ}(25\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 1.229$ V. The pressure gain (≈ 9 mV) from running at 2.5 atm is **insufficient to offset** the temperature penalty (40 mV drop from 25 °C to 70 °C). The net deviation from the room-temperature $E^{\circ}$ is $-31$ mV, dominated by the negative $\partial E/\partial T$. In practice, additional irreversibilities (activation, ohmic, concentration) further reduce the operating cell voltage to 0.6–0.8 V.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{rev}} = 1.198 \text{ V}}$$

---

**Q3. (B) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, S1 Pattern]**
A solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) operates at **900 °C** with the following inlet compositions:

- Anode: $P_{\text{H}_2} = 0.8$ atm, $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.2$ atm
- Cathode: $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21$ atm (air at 1 atm total)

Given: $E^{\circ}(900\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 0.95$ V, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹.

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** [7 marks] State and justify the correct form of the Nernst equation for a SOFC. Why is the water product term included in this case but not in a low-temperature PEMFC?

**(b)** [7 marks] Calculate the **reversible cell voltage** at 900 °C and determine the percentage contribution of the Nernst correction term to the total voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Selection and justification of the Nernst form [7 marks]:**

In a SOFC, the operating temperature (600–1000 °C) is well above the boiling point of water. Therefore, water is produced in the **gaseous phase** [1 mark]. The reaction is:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$

The reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}$$

and the activity of gaseous water is **NOT unity** (it depends on its partial pressure) [1 mark]. The Nernst equation becomes:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln Q = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right) \quad [2 \text{ marks}]$$

In a low-temperature PEMFC (≤ 100 °C), the product water is **liquid**, and its activity is conventionally taken as **unity** (it is a pure condensed phase in its standard state). This drops the $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ term from the equation [2 marks]. Physically, liquid water leaves the cell as a separate phase and its chemical potential does not depend on how much is being produced.

[1 mark for clarity and rigor]

**(b) Numerical calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin [0.5 mark]:

$$T = 900 + 273.15 = 1173.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Nernst constant at 1173 K [1 mark]:

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(1173.15)}{2(96485)} = \frac{9752.4}{192970} = 0.05054 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the argument of the logarithm [1 mark]:

$$\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{\,1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} = \frac{(0.8)(0.21)^{0.5}}{0.2} = \frac{(0.8)(0.4583)}{0.2} = \frac{0.3666}{0.2} = 1.833$$

**Step 4:** Compute the natural logarithm [0.5 mark]:

$$\ln(1.833) = 0.6061$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the Nernst correction term [1 mark]:

$$\Delta E_{\text{Nernst}} = (0.05054)(0.6061) = 0.03064 \text{ V} \approx 30.6 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the full Nernst equation [1 mark]:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \Delta E_{\text{Nernst}} = 0.95 + 0.0306 = 0.981 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Percentage contribution [2 marks]:**

$$\% \text{ contribution} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{Nernst}}}{E}\times 100 = \frac{0.0306}{0.981}\times 100 = 3.12\%$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Nernst}} = 0.981 \text{ V}, \quad \text{Nernst correction} = 3.12\% \text{ of total voltage}}$$

> **Comment:** The Nernst correction is small (~3%) but **vital** for accurate voltage prediction. At higher current densities, this small advantage in OCV translates to a measurable improvement in stack power output.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (per KTU 2018–2024 valuation trends):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** — this single error wipes out 50% of the calculation marks. *Always* write $T = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$.
> 2. **Using $E^{\circ}(25\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 1.229$ V for a problem that specifies a different temperature** — you must use the $E^{\circ}$ appropriate to the given temperature, or correct it using the temperature coefficient formula.
> 3. **Confusing partial vs. total pressure** — when the problem says "fed with air at 3 atm," the O₂ partial pressure is $0.21 \times 3 = 0.63$ atm, not 3 atm. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 4. **Omitting the $\frac{1}{2}$ exponent on $P_{\text{O}_2}$** — this comes directly from the stoichiometric coefficient in the reaction.
> 5. **Forgetting the gaseous-water term in SOFC problems** — when the product is gaseous H₂O, you MUST include $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ in the denominator. SOFC problems almost always test this.
> 6. **Sign error in the logarithm** — the standard form is $E = E^{\circ} - (RT/nF)\ln Q$. If you write $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/nF)\ln Q$ instead, every subsequent sign is wrong.
> 7. **Rounding too aggressively** — carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step to avoid accumulated error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **Nernst equation** gives the *reversible* (thermodynamic maximum) cell voltage under non-standard conditions. It is **not** the operating voltage — actual voltage is lower by the three overpotentials.
- [x] For the **H₂/O₂ reaction**, the standard Nernst form for **liquid water** is $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2})$ and for **gaseous water** is $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$.
- [x] **Standard values** to memorize: $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V (liq), $1.185$ V (gas), $n = 2$, $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2$ kJ mol⁻¹, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8$ kJ mol⁻¹, $\Delta S^{\circ} = -163.2$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ (liq) or $-44.4$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ (gas).
- [x] **Pressure** ↑ → voltage ↑ (logarithmic, sub-linear).
- [x] **Temperature** ↑ → voltage ↓ (because $\Delta S < 0$ for the H₂/O₂ reaction).
- [x] **Concentration** ↑ → voltage ↑ (logarithmic; same Nernst form but with $C_i$ replacing $P_i$ for dissolved species).
- [x] **Nernst term constant at 25 °C** is $RT/(2F) = 0.02569$ V; at 80 °C it is $0.03022$ V — the cell becomes *more sensitive* to pressure/concentration changes at higher $T$.
- [x] **Temperature coefficient** for liquid H₂O product: $-0.85$ mV K⁻¹; for gaseous H₂O product: $-0.23$ mV K⁻¹.
- [x] **Always** use absolute temperature (K), partial pressures (atm), and check whether product water is liquid or gas before writing the Nernst equation.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark questions** typically test: (1) derivation of the Nernst equation from Gibbs energy, (2) one numerical calculation combining P and T effects, and (3) physical interpretation/justification. Practice all three.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Faraday's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Faraday's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** are the two fundamental quantitative relationships governing the stoichiometric link between the **quantity of electric charge** passing through an electrochemical cell (such as a fuel cell or electrolyzer) and the **mass of substance** consumed or produced at the electrodes. They form the foundational mass–charge balance for every electrochemical energy conversion device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's First Law (1833):** The mass of a substance liberated (or consumed) at an electrode is **directly proportional** to the total electric charge (Q) passed through the electrolyte.
>
> **Faraday's Second Law (1833):** When the **same quantity of charge** is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated at the respective electrodes are **directly proportional to their chemical equivalent weights** (E = M/n).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a water-filling station where each "electron" is a **measured cup of water** (1 cup = 1 Faraday = 96,485 C). To bake one "loaf" (1 mole of product) of bread, you need a *fixed recipe* of cups:

- For **bread type A** (e.g., Cu from Cu²⁺), the recipe needs **2 cups** (n = 2).
- For **bread type B** (e.g., Al from Al³⁺), the recipe needs **3 cups** (n = 3).
- For **bread type C** (H₂ gas in a fuel cell), the recipe needs **2 cups** (n = 2) for each loaf of water produced.

If the baker (current) pours cups at a fixed rate, then:
- **More cups poured → more loaves baked** (First Law).
- **Different breads need different "cup-per-loaf" ratios** (Second Law).

This is precisely why a fuel cell consuming **1 mole of H₂** (2.016 g) liberates **2 moles of electrons (2 × 96,485 C)** — the charge is *stochiometrically locked* to the chemistry.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Faraday Constant, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (often rounded to 96,500 C mol⁻¹ in KTU numerical problems).
> - **Avogadro's Number, N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**.
> - **Elementary charge, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**, with F = N_A × e.
> - **Equivalent weight, E = Atomic/Molar mass (M) ÷ valence (n).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Faraday's First Law — The Why and How:**

1. **Charge as a "countable commodity":** Electric current (I) is the *rate* of charge flow (dQ/dt). Therefore, charge passed in time *t* is **Q = I × t** (Coulombs).
2. **Charge per mole of electrons:** By definition of the Faraday constant, exactly **F coulombs** are required to liberate **1 mole of a univalent substance** (n = 1).
3. **For n-valent species:** To liberate 1 mole requires **n × F** coulombs, since each atom/ion accepts or donates *n* electrons.
4. **Linear proportionality:** Doubling the charge doubles the moles reacted → doubling the mass transformed. Hence, **m ∝ Q**, leading to the first law.

**Faraday's Second Law — The Why and How:**

1. **Same charge, different substances:** If charge Q flows through two cells in series, both cells receive *identical* Q.
2. **Moles liberated differ by valence:** Cell 1 liberates m₁ = (Q × M₁)/(n₁ × F); Cell 2 liberates m₂ = (Q × M₂)/(n₂ × F).
3. **Ratio of masses equals ratio of equivalent weights:**
   $$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$
4. This demonstrates that the *effective reactive mass* per unit charge is governed by the equivalent weight, not the molar mass alone.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge from current and time | C (Coulomb) |
| 2 | $F = N_A \cdot e$ | Faraday constant definition | C mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $F = 96{,}485$ | Numerical value (use 96,500 if permitted) | C mol⁻¹ |
| 4 | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | First law: mass from electrochemical equivalent | g |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Electrochemical equivalent | g C⁻¹ |
| 6 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Combined first-law working equation | g |
| 7 | $n_{moles} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Moles of substance reacted | mol |
| 8 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}$ | Second law (mass ratio) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot F}$ | Molar flow rate of H₂ consumed (n = 2) | mol s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Faraday / Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 11 | $V_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot R \cdot T \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F \cdot P}$ | Volumetric H₂ flow (ideal gas) | m³ s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a H₂/O₂ fuel cell:** n = 2 for H₂ (H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻) and n = 4 for O₂ (O₂ + 4e⁻ → 2O²⁻). Always specify per electrode when working these problems.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Fuel Cell Sizing:** Determines the **minimum hydrogen flow rate** required to sustain a desired current output — critical for designing anode hydrogen supply systems in PEMFC and AFC stacks.
- **Electrolyzer Design:** Predicts the **H₂ production rate** (kg/day) for a given current input in PEM/alkaline electrolysis plants.
- **Battery State-of-Charge:** Coulomb counting for Li-ion and lead-acid batteries relies on the same mass–charge proportionality.
- **Electroplating & Electrowinning:** Determines deposition thickness in metal-finishing industries and the throughput of copper, zinc, and aluminum refineries.
- **Corrosion Engineering:** Faraday's law quantifies the **metal loss rate** (mm/year) under galvanic or stray-current attack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — First Law: m = (M · I · t) / (n · F)

**Starting premise:** Let *N* be the number of ions of valence *n* deposited at an electrode. Each ion accepts (or donates) exactly *n* electrons.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total electrons transferred: } & N_e = N \cdot n \\
\text{Total charge passed: } & Q = N_e \cdot e = N \cdot n \cdot e \\
\text{But } e \cdot N_A = F, \text{ so } e = \dfrac{F}{N_A} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad Q = \dfrac{N \cdot n \cdot F}{N_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of substance: } n_{mol} = \dfrac{N}{N_A} = \dfrac{Q}{n \cdot F} \quad &\text{(logical substitution)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of substance: } m = n_{mol} \cdot M = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \quad &\text{(multiplying by molar mass M)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } Q = I \cdot t, \text{ substitute: } m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \quad &\text{(final first-law equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The mass of substance liberated is linearly proportional to current, time, and molar mass; inversely proportional to valence and the Faraday constant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Molar Hydrogen Consumption Rate in a Fuel Cell

**Reaction at the anode of a PEMFC:**

$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

**Step 1:** From the stoichiometry, 1 mole of H₂ produces 2 moles of electrons → **n = 2**.

**Step 2:** The number of moles of H₂ consumed per second (molar flow rate) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I}{2 \cdot F} \quad &\text{(mol s}^{-1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass flow rate using M(H₂) = 2.016 g mol⁻¹:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot 2.016}{2 \cdot 96{,}485} \quad &\text{(g s}^{-1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical check for I = 100 A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{100}{2 \cdot 96{,}485} = 5.183 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol s}^{-1} \quad &\text{(hydrogen feed rate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5.183 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.016 = 1.045 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g s}^{-1} \approx 3.76 \text{ kg h}^{-1} \quad &\text{(mass feed rate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Volumetric Hydrogen Flow (Ideal Gas Form)

**Starting from the ideal gas law, PV = nRT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \dfrac{n R T}{P} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot R T}{P} \cdot \Delta t \quad &\text{(volume in time }\Delta t\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot R T}{n \cdot F \cdot P} = \dfrac{I \cdot R T}{2 \cdot F \cdot P} \quad &\text{(volumetric flow rate, m}^3\text{ s}^{-1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101,325 Pa), with I = 100 A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{100 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 101{,}325} = 1.161 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 \text{ s}^{-1} \quad &\text{(≈ 11.61 mL s}^{-1}\text{ or ≈ 41.8 L h}^{-1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Faraday (Coulombic) Efficiency

**Definition:** The ratio of *actual* current (or product) to the *theoretical* current (or product) predicted by Faraday's law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\% \quad &\text{(dimensionless, expressed in \%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key engineering insight:** η_F < 100 % arises from **parasitic side reactions** (e.g., H₂ crossover, methanol crossover in DMFC, corrosion side-reactions). The complement (1 − η_F) represents *wasted* fuel — a critical parameter in fuel-cell stack economics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fuel-Cell Block Diagram Showing Faraday's Law in Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Storage Cylinder] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Anode Flow Field]
    C --> D[Anode Catalyst Layer - HOR]
    D --> E[PEM Membrane - H ion transport]
    E --> F[Cathode Catalyst Layer - ORR]
    F --> G[Cathode Flow Field]
    G --> H[Water and Heat Exporter]
    I[Air Blower] --> G
    J[External Load - Electrical] --> F
    D --> J

    K[Faraday Law Engine] --> L1[Theoretical H2 flow equals I divided by 2F]
    K --> L2[Molar consumption = moles of H2 per second]
    K --> L3[Mass consumption = molar flow times molar mass of H2]
    K --> L4[Faraday Efficiency equals Actual over Theoretical times 100]

    D -.Monitors.-> K
    J -.Current I.-> K
```

### 4.2 Mass–Charge Stoichiometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Charge Q in Coulombs] --> B[Divided by Faraday Constant F]
    B --> C[Moles of Electrons n subscript e]
    C --> D[Divided by valence n]
    D --> E[Moles of Substance Reacted]
    E --> F[Multiplied by Molar Mass M]
    F --> G[Mass m in grams]
    E --> H[Multiplied by Molar Volume at STP]
    H --> I[Gas Volume V]
    G --> J[Actual Mass measured]
    J --> K[Compare with Theoretical]
    K --> L[Faraday Efficiency percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams above map the **information flow** between the electrochemical hardware (anode, membrane, cathode) and the **mathematical Faraday engine** that governs stoichiometry. They are *block-level functional architecture* schematics — not physical drawings — designed to map each engineering component to its corresponding Faraday-law equation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis. Mention the significance of the Faraday constant.

**Model Answer:**

- **Faraday's First Law:** The mass *m* of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of charge *Q* passed through the electrolyte: **m ∝ Q** ⟹ **m = (M · Q) / (n · F)**.
- **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of charge is passed through different electrolytes connected in series, the masses of the substances liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights (E = M/n)**.
- **Faraday constant (F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹):** Represents the charge carried by one mole of electrons; it is the **fundamental conversion factor** between the electrical domain (coulombs) and the chemical domain (moles of substance).

*(Self-assessment: 3/3 marks — definition 1 + equation 1 + significance 1.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** A fuel cell operates at 50 A for 2 hours. Calculate the mass of hydrogen consumed and the volume of water produced at STP. (Given: M_H₂ = 2 g mol⁻¹, n = 2, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹, molar volume = 22.4 L at STP.)

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \cdot t = 50 \times 2 \times 3600 = 360{,}000 \text{ C} \\
n_{H_2} &= \dfrac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{360{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}500} = 1.865 \text{ mol} \\
m_{H_2} &= 1.865 \times 2 = 3.73 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 1 mol H₂ produces 1 mol H₂O:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{H_2O} &= 1.865 \times 22.4 = 41.78 \text{ L (as vapor at STP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Self-assessment: 3/3 marks — charge calculation 1 + H₂ moles/mass 1 + water volume 1.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression **m = (M · I · t) / (n · F)** for the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode, starting from the definition of electric charge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEM fuel cell stack delivers a steady current of **250 A** at an average cell voltage of 0.65 V. If the **Faradaic efficiency is 92 %**, calculate:  
(i) the mass of H₂ consumed per hour, and  
(ii) the actual volume of H₂ supplied at 30 °C and 1.2 atm. (7 marks)  
*(Given: M_H₂ = 2.016 g mol⁻¹, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Express charge in terms of ions: Let N ions of valence n deposit. Total electrons transferred = N · n; total charge Q = N · n · e.

Step 2 — Relate to moles using Avogadro's number: n_mol = N / N_A ⟹ N = n_mol · N_A.

Step 3 — Substitute: Q = (n_mol · N_A) · n · e = n_mol · n · (N_A · e) = n_mol · n · F.

Step 4 — Solve for n_mol: n_mol = Q / (n · F).

Step 5 — Multiply by molar mass M: **m = n_mol · M = (M · Q) / (n · F)**.

Step 6 — Substitute Q = I · t: **m = (M · I · t) / (n · F)**. ∎

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of H₂ consumed per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= I \cdot t = 250 \times 3600 = 900{,}000 \text{ C (per hour)} \\
n_{H_2, theoretical} &= \dfrac{900{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 4.664 \text{ mol} \\
m_{H_2, theoretical} &= 4.664 \times 2.016 = 9.402 \text{ g h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Accounting for 92 % Faradaic efficiency (i.e., 8 % is "wasted" via crossover/side-reactions, so more fuel is consumed in practice):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2, actual} = \dfrac{m_{H_2, theoretical}}{\eta_F} = \dfrac{9.402}{0.92} = 10.22 \text{ g h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual volume at 30 °C, 1.2 atm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{n R T}{P} = \dfrac{4.664 \times 8.314 \times 303.15}{1.2 \times 101{,}325} \\
V &= \dfrac{11{,}757.6}{121{,}590} = 9.67 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (theoretical)} \\
V_{actual} &= \dfrac{9.67 \times 10^{-2}}{0.92} = 0.1051 \text{ m}^3 \text{ h}^{-1} \approx 105.1 \text{ L h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** State and explain Faraday's Second Law. Two electrolytic cells containing **CuSO₄** and **AgNO₃** are connected in series. The same charge liberates **2.5 g of Cu** at the first cathode. Calculate the mass of Ag deposited at the second cathode. (7 marks)  
*(Given: M_Cu = 63.5 g mol⁻¹, M_Ag = 108 g mol⁻¹, n_Cu²⁺ = 2, n_Ag⁺ = 1.)*

**(b)** Define **Faraday efficiency**. A laboratory fuel cell consumes **0.5 g of H₂ per hour** while delivering a steady current of **6.5 A**. Calculate:  
(i) the theoretical H₂ consumption rate, and  
(ii) the Faradaic efficiency. (7 marks)  
*(Given: M_H₂ = 2 g mol⁻¹, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹.)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Second Law + numerical (7 marks):**

**Statement:** When the same charge is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights: m₁/m₂ = E₁/E₂.

For Cu²⁺ → Cu: E_Cu = 63.5 / 2 = 31.75.  
For Ag⁺ → Ag: E_Ag = 108 / 1 = 108.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{m_{Cu}}{m_{Ag}} = \dfrac{E_{Cu}}{E_{Ag}} = \dfrac{31.75}{108} \\
m_{Ag} = m_{Cu} \times \dfrac{108}{31.75} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{108}{31.75} = 8.504 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: m_Ag ≈ 8.50 g**

---

**(b) Faraday efficiency + numerical (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Faraday (Coulombic) efficiency is the ratio of the actual charge (or mass) consumed in the *desired* electrochemical reaction to the theoretical charge (or mass) that should have been consumed based on Faraday's law, expressed as a percentage.

**(i) Theoretical H₂ consumption rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \cdot t = 6.5 \times 3600 = 23{,}400 \text{ C h}^{-1} \\
n_{H_2, theoretical} &= \dfrac{23{,}400}{2 \times 96{,}500} = 0.1212 \text{ mol h}^{-1} \\
m_{H_2, theoretical} &= 0.1212 \times 2 = 0.2425 \text{ g h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Faradaic efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_F = \dfrac{m_{theoretical}}{m_{actual}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.2425}{0.5} \times 100\% = 48.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 48.5 % of the hydrogen fed to the cell actually contributes to current generation — the rest is lost (crossover, parasitic reactions, incomplete electrochemical conversion). This is unacceptably low for commercial stacks (typical η_F > 95 %), indicating a faulty membrane or catalyst poisoning in this hypothetical cell.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps in Faraday's Law problems (from KTU 2024 valuation keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting to use n = 2 for H₂** (or n = 4 for O₂) — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Q:** Current must be in **amperes** and time in **seconds** before substituting into Q = I·t. Mixing hours directly yields wrong answers.
> 3. **Ideal gas law trap:** Students frequently use 22.4 L at non-STP conditions. Use V = nRT/P whenever T or P differs from 273.15 K / 1 atm.
> 4. **Confusing M with E:** Faraday's Second Law requires **equivalent weight (M/n)**, not just atomic/molar mass. Marks lost on the "ratio" step.
> 5. **Faradaic efficiency direction:** Some students calculate η_F as actual/theoretical when the *true* definition depends on which quantity is "expected." Memorize: **η_F = (Useful output) / (Theoretical maximum) × 100 %**.
> 6. **Not mentioning units** in the final numerical answer — KTU evaluators reserve **0.5 mark** for unit clarity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Faraday's First Law:** m = (M · I · t) / (n · F) — links mass to charge linearly.
- **Faraday's Second Law:** m₁ / m₂ = E₁ / E₂ — links masses of different substances to their equivalent weights for the same charge.
- **Faraday constant F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** — the single most important number in electrochemistry.
- **For H₂ in fuel cells: n = 2** (anode); for O₂: **n = 4** (cathode).
- **Theoretical H₂ flow** (mol s⁻¹) = I / (2F); **mass flow** = that × M_H₂.
- **Volumetric H₂ flow** at non-STP: V̇ = (I · R · T) / (2 · F · P).
- **Faraday efficiency η_F** < 100 % due to crossover, side reactions, and parasitic losses.
- **Always state n explicitly** when applying the law — examiners *look* for it.
- **Check units:** I in A, t in s, F in C mol⁻¹, M in g mol⁻¹ → m in grams.
- **For PEMFC design:** η_F is the link between *fuel cost* and *electrical output* — directly impacts Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE).
- **The Second Law** is rarely used in fuel-cell problems (single-substance systems) but frequently appears in metal-electrowinning and battery problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency: Thermodynamic, Voltage, and Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Efficiency: Thermodynamic, Voltage, and Fuel # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Three Pillars of Fuel Cell Efficiency In fuel cell engineering, the overall system efficiency is **not** a single number. It is a *product* of three distinct, multiplicative losses that occur at different layers of the electrochemical process. Every commercial fuel cell system must report and optimize all three to claim "high efficiency." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A fuel cell's performance is characterized by three independent efficiencies: > 1. **Thermodynamic Efficiency** ($\eta_{th}$) — *the maximum theoretically possible efficiency dictated by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.* > 2. **Voltage Efficiency** ($\eta_{V}$) — *the actual electrochemical irreversibility losses (activation, ohmic, concentration).* > 3. **Fuel Utilization Efficiency** ($\eta_{f}$) — *how completely the input fuel is electrochemically oxidized.* ## 1.2 The Master Equation The **Overall System Efficiency** is given by the canonical product: $$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{V} \times \eta_{f}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Multiplicative?** Because each efficiency is a *fraction of the previous ideal stage* that survives. If thermodynamics allows 83% to begin, voltage losses recover only 70% of that, and fuel utilization recovers 95% of what remains — the surviving useful energy is the product. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel Cascade Imagine three water wheels stacked along a single river: - **Wheel 1 (Thermodynamic):** A *frictionless*, ideal water wheel. No matter how perfectly you build it, gravity and the height of the fall physically cap how much energy you can extract. You cannot exceed the thermodynamic limit — this is **$\eta_{th}$**. - **Wheel 2 (Voltage):** A *real* wheel with friction, splashing, and bearing losses. It captures only a fraction of the energy the ideal wheel received. This fraction is **$\eta_{V}$**. - **Wheel 3 (Fuel):** A sieve at the river's mouth. Some water slips past un-used. The fraction actually turned into work is **$\eta_{f}$**. The total energy delivered to the mill is the product of all three surviving fractions. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C/mol}$ (**bold** as a physical constant). - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}$. - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂:** $\Delta H = -285.8 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ at 25°C. - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** $\Delta H = -241.8 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ at 25°C. - **Standard reversible cell voltage (E°):** **1.229 V** for H₂/O₂ at 25°C (HHV basis). - **Thermal Neutral Voltage (E_{th}):** **1.481 V** for HHV; **1.253 V** for LHV. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layer 1 — Thermodynamic Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) This efficiency answers: *"What is the absolute maximum energy recoverable from the H₂–O₂ reaction, ignoring all device imperfections?"* There are two sub-flavors, both **exam-favorites** in KTU: ### (a) Enthalpy-Based (HHV / LHV) Thermodynamic Efficiency This compares the *useful electrical work* to the *total chemical enthalpy* released by combustion. By the First Law, the maximum work is the **Gibbs Free Energy** change $\Delta G$, not the enthalpy $\Delta H$: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$ For the H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions: - Using **HHV**: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{-237.2}{-285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\%$ - Using **LHV**: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{-228.6}{-241.8} = 0.945 = 94.5\%$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** A student who uses HHV throughout gets 83% efficiency. A student who uses LHV gets 94.5%. The choice of reference (HHV vs. LHV) is a 7-mark discussion point in KTU Module 1. ### (b) Voltage-Based Thermodynamic Efficiency (Thermal Neutral Voltage Approach) Instead of dividing energies, we divide voltages. The **Thermal Neutral Voltage** $E_{th}$ is the voltage at which *all* the enthalpy of the reaction would be converted to electrical work with **zero heat generation**: $$E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF}$$ For H₂/O₂: $E_{th} = \dfrac{285.8 \times 10^3}{2 \times 96485} = 1.481 \text{ V}$ (HHV). The thermodynamic efficiency is then: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{E_{rev}}{E_{th}} = \frac{1.229}{1.481} = 0.830$$ This is mathematically equivalent to $\Delta G / \Delta H$ — it is just the voltage-domain statement of the same law. ## 2.2 Layer 2 — Voltage Efficiency ($\eta_{V}$) Voltage efficiency is purely an *electrochemical device* metric. It compares the **actual operating voltage** of the cell to the **reversible (Nernst) voltage**: $$\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$$ The gap $(E_{rev} - V_{cell})$ is the **polarization loss** $\Delta V$, which is the sum of three overpotentials: $$\Delta V = \eta_{act} + \eta_{ohmic} + \eta_{conc}$$ | Overpotential | Symbol | Physical Cause | Dominant Region | |---------------|--------|----------------|-----------------| | Activation loss | $\eta_{act}$ | Sluggish electrode kinetics | Low current | | Ohmic loss | $\eta_{ohmic}$ | Ionic + electronic resistance | Mid current | | Concentration loss | $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass-transport starvation | High current | > [!NOTE] > Voltage efficiency typically ranges from **50% to 80%** for practical PEMFC stacks — this is the largest single source of loss in a well-designed cell. ## 2.3 Layer 3 — Fuel Utilization Efficiency ($\eta_{f}$) A fuel cell rarely converts 100% of the inlet fuel. Some H₂ escapes un-reacted through the anode exhaust, or is deliberately bled out (for the air-side to remove nitrogen crossover contaminants). Fuel utilization is: $$\eta_{f} = \frac{\text{mass of fuel actually reacted}}{\text{mass of fuel supplied}}$$ For pure H₂ feeds, $\eta_{f} \approx 0.95 - 0.99$. For reformed hydrocarbon feeds, $\eta_{f}$ can drop to **0.70 - 0.85** because the reformer is never perfectly selective. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------------| | 1 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{V} \cdot \eta_{f}$ | dimensionless | $0.40 - 0.60$ (typical stack) | | 2 | Thermodynamic (HHV) | $\eta_{th} = \Delta G \vert \Delta H$ | dimensionless | $0.83$ for H₂/O₂ | | 3 | Thermodynamic (LHV) | $\eta_{th} = \Delta G \vert \Delta H$ | dimensionless | $0.945$ for H₂/O₂ | | 4 | Thermal neutral voltage | $E_{th} = \Delta H / (nF)$ | Volts | $1.481$ V (HHV), $1.253$ V (LHV) | | 5 | Voltage efficiency | $\eta_{V} = V_{cell} \mid E_{rev}$ | dimensionless | $0.50 - 0.80$ | | 6 | Nernst voltage | $E = E^\circ + (RT/nF)\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}/P_{H_2O})$ | Volts | $1.229$ V at std. cond. | | 7 | Fuel utilization | $\eta_{f} = \dot{n}_{reacted} \mid \dot{n}_{supplied}$ | dimensionless | $0.70 - 0.99$ | | 8 | Current density relation | $i = n F \dot{n}_{fuel} / A$ | A/m² | derived from Faraday | | 9 | Actual cell voltage | $V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Volts | $0.6 - 0.8$ V typical | | 10 | Stack efficiency | $\eta_{stack} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{V} \cdot \eta_{f}$ | dimensionless | same as system level | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Modern Toyota Mirai and Hyundai NEXO PEMFC vehicles target an overall stack efficiency of ~$55-60\%$ (HHV). Conventional gasoline engines are capped near $25-30\%$ by the Carnot limit — fuel cells win precisely because $\eta_{th}$ is *not* Carnot-bound. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From First Law to Thermal Neutral Voltage We start from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a constant-pressure, constant-temperature fuel cell process. The total enthalpy change of the reaction is split into two parts: $$\Delta H = \Delta G + T \Delta S$$ The term $T \Delta S$ is the *unavoidable* heat that must be rejected to the surroundings (entropic tax).
