**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam 2023]**

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

In a mixed conductor, three species — ions, electrons, and holes — carry charge simultaneously. Each species contributes a partial conductivity $\sigma_k = n_k z_k e \mu_k$. By the principle of superposition:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_i + \sigma_e + \sigma_h$$

The ionic transference number is the fraction of current carried by ions:

$$t_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{total}}, \quad t_e = \frac{\sigma_e}{\sigma_{total}}, \quad t_h = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_{total}}$$

$$\sum t_k = 1$$

Wagner used this framework to analyze materials like Ag$_2$S, transition metal oxides (e.g., CoO, NiO), and perovskite cathodes. The transference number is experimentally measurable via the Hebb–Wagner polarization method or the EMF technique.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Total conductivity:**

$$\sigma_{tot} = 0.05 + 0.95 + 0.10 = 1.10 \text{ S/cm}$$

**(ii) Transference numbers:**

$$t_i = \frac{0.05}{1.10} = 0.0455$$

$$t_e = \frac{0.95}{1.10} = 0.8636$$

$$t_h = \frac{0.10}{1.10} = 0.0909$$

**Verification:** $0.0455 + 0.8636 + 0.0909 = 1.000$ ✓

**(iii) Suitability comment:**

Since $t_i = 0.0455 < 0.01$ is *not* satisfied (electrolyte requirement is $t_i > 0.99$), the material is **NOT suitable as an electrolyte** — it would cause severe internal short-circuiting.

Since $t_e + t_h = 0.9545$ is very high (close to 1) AND it has appreciable ionic conductivity ($t_i \approx 0.05$), this is an **ideal MIEC electrode material** — it can conduct both electrons (to current collector) and ions (to extend the reaction zone beyond the triple-phase boundary). This matches the behavior of state-of-the-art SOFC cathodes like LSCF or BSCF.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing S/cm with S/m (factor of 100) is the #1 mark-deducting error. Always declare units before substitution.
> 2. **Charge value:** For oxide-ion conduction, the charge is $q = 2e$, NOT $e$. Many students incorrectly use $q = e$.
> 3. **Transference number:** The sum must equal **exactly 1**; rounding errors should not make it 1.001 or 0.999 in the final line.
> 4. **Arrhenius form:** KTU expects the modified form $\sigma T = A \exp(-E_a/kT)$ for solids, not the simple $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a/kT)$ — read the question carefully.
> 5. **MIEC vs Electrolyte:** Many students confuse MIEC electrodes with electrolytes. Remember — electrolytes want $t_i \approx 1$; electrodes want $t_e \approx 1$ but often accept $t_i > 0$ if it extends TPB.
> 6. **Hopping vs band:** Don't write "electrons hop" for metals — they move via band conduction. Use "hopping" specifically for ions and polarons.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ionic conductivity** governs electrolyte performance; **electronic conductivity** governs electrode performance.
- The **Nernst–Einstein equation** $\sigma_i = n z^2 e^2 D / (kT)$ is the fundamental link between diffusion and conduction.
- Conductivity follows **Arrhenius behavior**: $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a/kT)$ — always plot $\ln(\sigma T)$ vs $1/T$ for $E_a$.
- A good electrolyte has $t_i \geq 0.99$; a good electronic conductor has $t_i \leq 0.01$.
- **MIECs** (LSCF, BSCF, Ni-YSZ cermet) carry both ions and electrons — extend the reaction zone beyond TPBs.
- Typical solid electrolyte $\sigma_i$ ranges: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ S/cm.
- Conductivity is measured experimentally via **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)**.
- **Common electrolytes and their ionic conductors:**
  - Nafion® (PEMFC) — $H^+$ conduction via Grotthuss + vehicle mechanism
  - YSZ (SOFC) — $O^{2-}$ via vacancy hopping
  - GDC, LSGM — $O^{2-}$ at lower temperatures (IT-SOFC)
  - Phosphoric acid (PAFC) — $H^+$
  - KOH (AFC) — $OH^-$
  - Molten carbonate (MCFC) — $CO_3^{2-}$
- **Ohmic loss** in cell: $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot L / \sigma_i$ — directly tied to electrolyte thickness and conductivity.
- **Haven ratio** $H_R = D_{tracer}/D_{\sigma}$ accounts for correlation in ion hopping; usually $H_R < 1$.
- **Polaron hopping** dominates in many perovskite cathodes at intermediate temperatures.
- **Faraday constant** $F = 96485$ C/mol is sometimes needed in place of $N_A \cdot e$ for derivations involving mole-based quantities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ionic Conduction in Different FC Electrolytes: Aqueous, Polymeric, and Ceramic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ionic Conduction in Different FC Electrolytes: Aqueous, Polymeric, and Ceramic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ionic conduction in a fuel cell electrolyte** refers to the selective, non-electronic transport of charge through the electrolyte medium via the migration of ions (H⁺, OH⁻, O²⁻, or CO₃²⁻) from one electrode to the other, completing the internal electrical circuit while preventing direct fuel-oxidant mixing. The electrolyte is the *ionic highway* of the fuel cell, and its conductivity, transport number, and chemical stability dictate the operational temperature, power density, and durability of the entire stack.

The three major families of electrolytes covered in this module are:

1. **Aqueous Electrolytes** — Liquid-phase ion transport (Alkaline AFC, Phosphoric Acid PAFC, Molten Carbonate MCFC).
2. **Polymeric Electrolytes** — Solid-phase proton transport in hydrated ion-exchange membranes (PEMFC — Nafion, SPEEK, PBI).
3. **Ceramic Electrolytes** — Solid-phase oxygen-ion transport in crystalline oxide lattices (SOFC — YSZ, GDC, LSGM, CGO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *The electrolyte in a fuel cell is an electronic insulator but an ionic conductor. Its ionic transference number $t_i$ must approach unity ($t_i \rightarrow 1$), while its electronic transference number $t_e \rightarrow 0$ to maximize Faradaic efficiency and minimize internal short-circuit losses.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **one-way bus corridor** in a busy city. The buses (ions) can travel only along a designated lane (the electrolyte). Cars (electrons) are *not allowed* on this lane — they must take a different, longer route (the external circuit, where they do useful work like lighting a bulb). The "bus lane" must be:
- **Selective:** Only the right kind of ion may board.
- **Fast:** Ions must move with minimal traffic jams (low resistance = high conductivity).
- **Stable:** The lane must not collapse in rain, heat, or vibration.

Now, the three electrolyte families are three different *road-building philosophies*:
- **Aqueous:** Pave a road with *liquid water* (cheap, but freezes and boils).
- **Polymeric:** Lay down a *flexible plastic sponge* soaked with water (lightweight, but dries out at high temperature).
- **Ceramic:** Cast a *solid concrete tile* with oxygen tunnels baked in (heavy and brittle, but works at fiery temperatures).

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol / Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant **F** | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| Boltzmann constant **k_B** | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J K⁻¹** | Thermal energy per particle |
| Gas constant **R** | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | Macroscopic thermal energy |
| Typical σ (Aqueous, 25 °C) | **0.1 – 1 S cm⁻¹** | Excellent room-T conduction |
| Typical σ (Nafion, hydrated) | **0.01 – 0.1 S cm⁻¹** | Highly humidity dependent |
| Typical σ (YSZ, 1000 °C) | **0.01 – 0.1 S cm⁻¹** | Needs T > 700 °C to activate |
| Operating T (AFC) | **50 – 200 °C** | Aqueous KOH |
| Operating T (PAFC) | **150 – 220 °C** | Concentrated H₃PO₄ |
| Operating T (MCFC) | **600 – 700 °C** | Molten Li/K carbonate |
| Operating T (PEMFC) | **60 – 100 °C** | Hydrated Nafion |
| Operating T (SOFC) | **600 – 1000 °C** | YSZ / GDC / LSGM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):** Aqueous systems work *cold*, polymeric systems work *warm*, ceramic systems work *hot*. The conductivity of any electrolyte must exceed **0.01 S cm⁻¹** to be considered practically useful in a fuel cell.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Framework of Ionic Conduction

Regardless of the medium, ionic conduction is governed by three fundamental parameters:

- **Charge carrier concentration** ($n_i$, mol cm⁻³) — how many ions are available.
- **Charge of the carrier** ($z_i \cdot e$) — how much charge each carries.
- **Mobility** ($\mu_i$, cm² V⁻¹ s⁻¹) — how fast the carrier drifts under an electric field.

**Step-by-step logic of conduction:**

1. A chemical potential gradient (or electrical field $\vec{E}$) drives the mobile species.
2. The ion jumps from one equilibrium site to a neighbouring vacant/available site.
3. The jump rate depends on temperature via thermal activation (Boltzmann statistics).
4. Macroscopically, this is expressed as a current density $\vec{J}_i = \sigma_i \vec{E}$.

---

### 2.2 Aqueous Electrolytes — Mechanism

#### 2.2.1 Alkaline (AFC) — KOH (typically 30–40 wt%)
- **Mobile ion:** OH⁻ (hydroxide)
- **Electrode reactions:**
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^-$
- **Conduction mechanism:** Vehicular — bulk migration of OH⁻ solvated by water shells (Grotthuss contribution is minor in concentrated KOH).
- **Conductivity:** $\sigma_{\text{KOH}} \approx 0.6\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$ at 25 °C for 30 wt%.

#### 2.2.2 Phosphoric Acid (PAFC) — H₃PO₄ (~100 wt%)
- **Mobile ion:** H⁺ (proton), conducted via *Grotthuss mechanism* through H-bonded H₃PO₄ network.
- **Self-ionization:** $2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_4\text{PO}_4^+ + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$
- **Operating T:** 150–220 °C; above 200 °C the acid dehydrates, reducing conductivity sharply.

#### 2.2.3 Molten Carbonate (MCFC) — Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ eutectic (62/38 mol%)
- **Mobile ion:** CO₃²⁻
- **Electrode reactions:**
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$
  - Anode: $\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$
- **Conduction:** Purely vehicular in the molten state; requires CO₂ in cathode feed.

---

### 2.3 Polymeric Electrolytes — Mechanism (PEMFC)

**Material:** Nafion™ — a perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) membrane with the repeat unit:

$$\text{--}(\text{CF}_2\text{--CF}_2)_n\text{--CF}_2\text{--CF}(\text{CF}_3)\text{--O--CF}_2\text{--CF}_2\text{--SO}_3\text{H}\text{--}$$

- **Mobile ion:** H⁺ (proton)
- **Two co-existing mechanisms:**

**Mechanism A — Grotthuss (Structural Diffusion):**
Proton hops from one water molecule / sulfonic acid site to the next via H-bond rearrangement. *No net mass transport of water.*

**Mechanism B — Vehicular (Einstein Diffusion):**
Proton rides as H₃O⁺ (or larger hydronium clusters) through the aqueous nanochannels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite:** For Nafion, *Grotthuss* dominates at low hydration (λ < 5), while *vehicular* dominates at high hydration (λ > 12), where $\lambda = n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} / n_{\text{SO}_3\text{H}}$ is the hydration number.

- **Conductivity at λ = 12, 25 °C:** $\sigma \approx 0.08\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$.
- **Failure mode above 100 °C:** Membrane dries; water clusters collapse; conductivity drops by an order of magnitude.

---

### 2.4 Ceramic Electrolytes — Mechanism (SOFC)

**Material:** Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) — $\text{ZrO}_2$ doped with 8 mol% $\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3$.

- **Mobile ion:** O²⁻ (oxide ion)
- **Defect reaction (Kröger–Vink notation):**
$$\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{ZrO}_2} 2\text{Y}_{\text{Zr}}' + 3\text{O}_\text{O}^\times + \text{V}_\text{O}^{\bullet\bullet}$$

In words: each Y₂O₃ substitutes two Zr⁴⁺ sites (creating two Y′_Zr) and **introduces one oxygen vacancy** V_O^{••} to maintain charge neutrality. **These vacancies are the charge carriers.**

- **Hopping mechanism:** Adjacent O²⁻ ions *swap* with the vacancy; the apparent motion of the vacancy is equivalent to O²⁻ transport in the opposite direction.
- **Why high T?** Hopping is thermally activated; below ~600 °C, $\sigma_{\text{YSZ}}$ is too low.
- **Conductivity at 1000 °C:** $\sigma \approx 0.1\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$.

**Alternatives to YSZ:**
- **GDC** (Gd-doped Ceria, $\text{Gd}_{0.1}\text{Ce}_{0.9}\text{O}_{1.95}$) — higher conductivity at 500–700 °C *intermediate-temperature* SOFC.
- **LSGM** ($\text{La}_{0.9}\text{Sr}_{0.1}\text{Ga}_{0.8}\text{Mg}_{0.2}\text{O}_{3-\delta}$) — perovskite, very high O²⁻ conductivity.
- **Protonic ceramics** (BZY — $\text{BaZr}_{0.8}\text{Y}_{0.2}\text{O}_{3-\delta}$) — conduct H⁺ at 400–700 °C.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All symbols in SI units unless stated.** Use $\vert\ \vert$ rendered as `\vert` so as not to break table syntax.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \sum_i n_i \vert z_i \vert F \mu_i$ | General ionic conductivity (Nernst–Einstein form) | Total conductivity is the *sum* over all species $i$ |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{n z^2 F^2 D}{RT}$ | Nernst–Einstein relation linking $\sigma$ to diffusivity $D$ | Most useful derivation on the board |
| 3 | $\sigma T = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Arrhenius law for activated hopping | Plot $\ln(\sigma T)$ vs. $1/T$ → slope $= -E_a / k_B$ |
| 4 | $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial x} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i c_i}{RT}\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ | Nernst–Planck equation (drift + diffusion) | Full ion-flux equation |
| 5 | $t_i = \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\text{total}}}$ | Transference number | $t_i + t_e = 1$; for good electrolyte $t_i \approx 1$ |
| 6 | $R_{\text{ohmic}} = \dfrac{L}{\sigma A}$ | Area-specific resistance (ASR) of electrolyte | $L$ = thickness, $A$ = area |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{n_{\text{SO}_3\text{H}}}$ | Nafion hydration number | Typical operating $\lambda \approx 12$ |
| 8 | $D_{\text{O}^{2-}} = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Oxygen-ion diffusivity in YSZ | Used to derive #3 |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = i \cdot R_{\text{ohmic}}$ | Ohmic overpotential loss | Direct application in polarization curves |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\text{Nafion}} = (0.005139 \lambda - 0.00326)\ \exp\!\left[1268\left(\dfrac{1}{303} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Springer-style empirical model (S cm⁻¹) | Used in numerical PEMFC modelling |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AFC (alkaline):** Used in *Apollo* and *Space Shuttle* programs; simplicity, but CO₂ intolerance is a major drawback.
- **PAFC:** The most mature fuel cell technology for stationary combined heat-and-power (CHP) — commercial units by ONSI/UTC, Toshiba.
- **MCFC:** 250 kW – 3 MW stationary plants by FuelCell Energy (USA), Ansaldo (Italy).
- **PEMFC:** Dominant in *transportation* (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) due to quick start-up and high power density.
- **SOFC:** Bloom Energy (USA) 200 kW – MW stationary systems; residential micro-CHP units by Solidpower, Convion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** *The choice of electrolyte family dictates the operating temperature, the fuel flexibility, the catalyst choice, and the stack architecture. There is no universal best electrolyte — only the right electrolyte for the application.*

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst–Einstein Relation

**Goal:** Derive $\sigma = \dfrac{n z^2 F^2 D}{RT}$ starting from a microscopic hopping model.

**Step 1 — Microscopic drift velocity.** An ion of charge $q = zF$ in an electric field $E$ experiences a force $F_{\text{elec}} = zFE$. The ion drifts through the medium with velocity $v_d$ proportional to $E$:

$$v_d = \mu E$$

where $\mu$ is the **ionic mobility** (m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Current density.** If there are $n$ charge carriers per unit volume (mol m⁻³), the current density is:

$$J = n \cdot q \cdot v_d = n \, zF \, \mu E$$

Comparing with Ohm's law $J = \sigma E$:

$$\sigma = n z F \mu$$

**Step 3 — Relate mobility to diffusivity (Einstein relation).** From statistical mechanics, the thermal energy of a particle is related to its mobility and diffusion coefficient:

$$\mu = \dfrac{D}{k_B T}$$

where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

**Step 4 — Substitute into conductivity.** For molar concentration (mol m⁻³), we must multiply by Avogadro's number $N_A$ (so that $n$ becomes moles). With $R = N_A k_B$:

$$\sigma = n z F \cdot \dfrac{D}{k_B T} = \dfrac{n z F D}{k_B T}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $N_A$ to convert to molar basis ($n$ is per mole now in mol m⁻³; or we use SI for mol m⁻³):

$$\sigma = \dfrac{n z F D}{k_B T} = \dfrac{n z F \cdot N_A D}{R T}$$

**Final Nernst–Einstein Equation (molar form):**

$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{n z^2 F^2 D}{R T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relation is the *bridge* between the diffusive transport ($D$) and the electrical transport ($\sigma$). It is the single most derived equation in KTU Module 3 papers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Law for Activated Hopping

**Goal:** Show why $\sigma T = A \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ in YSZ and Nafion.

**Step 1 — Rate of successful hops.** In a crystal lattice, an ion must overcome an energy barrier $E_a$ to jump to a neighbouring site. The probability of a successful hop per unit time is given by the Boltzmann factor:

$$\nu(T) = \nu_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $\nu_0$ is the *attempt frequency* (Debye frequency, $\sim 10^{13}$ Hz).

**Step 2 — Diffusivity is proportional to jump rate.** The tracer diffusivity $D$ in a cubic lattice of spacing $a$ and vacancy concentration $x_v$ is:

$$D = \gamma \, a^2 \, \nu_0 \, x_v \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $\gamma$ is a geometric factor (1/6 for a cubic lattice).

**Step 3 — Substitute into Nernst–Einstein.**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{n z^2 F^2}{R T} \cdot \gamma \, a^2 \, \nu_0 \, x_v \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\sigma T = \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{n z^2 F^2 \gamma a^2 \nu_0 x_v}{R}\right)}_{A} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma T = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Linear form for plotting.** Take natural log:

$$\ln(\sigma T) = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$$

A plot of $\ln(\sigma T)$ versus $1/T$ gives a **straight line of slope** $-E_a / k_B$. This is the universal method to extract the *activation energy* experimentally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** If the problem gives $\sigma$ at two temperatures and asks for $E_a$, use the *two-point* form:
> $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_2 T_2}{\sigma_1 T_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$
> This appears in 14-mark problems almost every year.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vacancy Concentration in YSZ

**Goal:** Show that 8 mol% Y₂O₃–ZrO₂ creates a *fixed* oxygen-vacancy concentration.

**Step 1 — Pure ZrO₂ stoichiometry.** Pure zirconia has charge-balanced Zr⁴⁺ and O²⁻ ions. Substitution of Y³⁺ on a Zr⁴⁺ site must be charge-compensated.

**Step 2 — Kröger–Vink notation.** In this notation: subscripts denote *sites*, superscripts denote *effective charge* (· = +1, ′ = −1, × = neutral).

$$\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{ZrO}_2} 2\text{Y}_{\text{Zr}}' + 3\text{O}_\text{O}^\times + \text{V}_\text{O}^{\bullet\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Charge balance.** Two Y' give total charge $-2$. One V_O^{••} gives total charge $+2$. Neutral. ✓

**Step 4 — Vacancy concentration.** For $y$ mol fraction of Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂, the oxygen-vacancy fraction is:

$$[\text{V}_\text{O}^{\bullet\bullet}] = y$$

For $y = 0.08$ (i.e., 8 mol% Y₂O₃, written 8YSZ):

$$[\text{V}_\text{O}^{\bullet\bullet}] = 0.08 \quad \text{(i.e., 8 % of oxygen sites are vacant)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vacancy concentration is *fixed* by the dopant level and is *independent of temperature and $p_{\text{O}_2}$* over a wide range. This is why YSZ conductivity is so reproducible and reliable.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydration Number and Conductivity in Nafion

**Goal:** Link the *physical* state of water in the membrane (λ) to the *electrical* property σ.

**Step 1 — Definition.** $\lambda = n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} / n_{\text{SO}_3\text{H}}$. Each sulfonic acid group can absorb a cluster of water molecules.

**Step 2 — Volume of the membrane.** A fully hydrated Nafion 117 membrane absorbs $\lambda \approx 22$ water molecules per SO₃H group; this corresponds to ~34 wt% water.

**Step 3 — Microstructure.** At low λ, water is tightly bound to SO₃⁻ groups. At λ > 5, water clusters form *nanochannels* ~2.5 nm in diameter. At λ > 12, channels percolate and provide continuous proton pathways.

**Step 4 — Empirical model (Springer, 1991):**

$$\sigma(\lambda, T) = (0.005139 \lambda - 0.00326)\ \exp\!\left[1268 \left(\dfrac{1}{303} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$$

Plug in $\lambda = 12$, $T = 353$ K (80 °C):

$$\sigma = (0.005139 \times 12 - 0.00326)\ \exp\!\left[1268\left(\dfrac{1}{303} - \dfrac{1}{353}\right)\right]$$

$$= (0.06167 - 0.00326)\ \exp\!\left[1268 \times (0.003300 - 0.002833)\right]$$

$$= 0.05841 \times \exp(0.5925) = 0.05841 \times 1.8085 = 0.1057\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{Nafion}}(\lambda=12,\,80°C) \approx 0.106\ \text{S cm}^{-1}}$$

This value matches well with experimental data and explains why PEMFCs operate at λ ≈ 12–14 for peak performance.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Activation Energy of YSZ

**Given:** YSZ conductivity $\sigma_1 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 800\ \text{K}$ and $\sigma_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 1000\ \text{K}$. Find $E_a$.

**Step 1 — Apply two-point Arrhenius form:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_2 T_2}{\sigma_1 T_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute left-hand side:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_2 T_2}{\sigma_1 T_1} = \dfrac{(5.0 \times 10^{-3})(1000)}{(1.0 \times 10^{-4})(800)} = \dfrac{5.0}{0.08} = 62.5$$

$$\ln(62.5) = 4.135$$

**Step 3 — Compute right-hand side factor:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{1000} - \dfrac{1}{800} = 0.001 - 0.00125 = -0.00025\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$:**

$$4.135 = -\dfrac{E_a}{8.314}\times(-0.00025)$$

$$E_a = \dfrac{4.135 \times 8.314}{0.00025} = \dfrac{34.36}{0.00025} = 137{,}440\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 137.4\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

This is consistent with the **literature value for YSZ (~100–120 kJ mol⁻¹)**, confirming the validity of the Arrhenius model.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Aqueous vs Polymeric vs Ceramic

| Parameter | Aqueous (AFC/PAFC/MCFC) | Polymeric (PEMFC) | Ceramic (SOFC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State of electrolyte** | Liquid (alkaline/acid/molten salt) | Solid hydrated membrane | Solid crystalline oxide |
| **Mobile ion** | OH⁻, H⁺, or CO₃²⁻ | H⁺ (proton) | O²⁻ (oxide ion) |
| **Primary mechanism** | Vehicular | Grotthuss + Vehicular | Vacancy hopping |
| **Operating T** | 50–700 °C | 60–100 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| **σ at operating T** | 0.1–1 S cm⁻¹ | 0.05–0.1 S cm⁻¹ | 0.01–0.1 S cm⁻¹ |
| **E_a (typical)** | 5–15 kJ mol⁻¹ | 10–20 kJ mol⁻¹ | 80–120 kJ mol⁻¹ |
| **Water management** | Must avoid flooding/drying | Critical — must stay hydrated | Not applicable (anhydrous) |
| **CO₂ tolerance** | AFC: intolerant; PAFC: tolerant | Tolerant (CO₂ ok) | Tolerant |
| **Mechanical** | Liquid containment issues | Flexible, robust | Brittle, fragile |
| **Cost** | Low (AFC), medium (MCFC) | High (Nafion ~$500/m²) | Medium–High |
| **Example materials** | KOH, H₃PO₄, Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | Nafion, SPEEK, PBI | YSZ, GDC, LSGM, BZY |
