# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Define the "hydraulic diameter" of a non-circular flow channel. Why is it used in fuel cell flow-channel analysis instead of the actual geometric diameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hydraulic diameter** D_h of a non-circular duct is defined as four times the ratio of the cross-sectional area to the wetted perimeter:

$$D_h = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w}$$

It is used in fuel-cell flow-channel analysis because **(i)** the Navier–Stokes equations for non-circular ducts reduce to the same dimensionless form as for circular pipes when expressed in terms of D_h, and **(ii)** it allows the use of universal correlations like f_D–Re charts and the Darcy–Weisbach equation. For a square channel of side a: D_h = a. For a wide, shallow channel (a ≫ b): D_h → 2b.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip (½ mark):** Always state the formula **and** give at least one numerical example (square or rectangle) for full marks.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Distinguish between Darcy's law and the Forchheimer equation. In which flow regime is each applicable for a fuel cell gas diffusion layer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Darcy's Law | Forchheimer Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | $-dP/dx = (\mu/k) v$ | $-dP/dx = (\mu/k) v + \beta \rho v^2$ |
| **Physical basis** | Viscous (linear) | Viscous + inertial (non-linear) |
| **Regime** | Low pore Re (Re_pore ≲ 1) | Moderate–high pore Re (Re_pore > 1) |
| **Validity** | Creeping flow | Wide range (including Darcy regime) |
| **Fuel cell use** | Low current density, dilute GDL | High current density, compressed GDL |

**Darcy's law** is the low-velocity limit; **Forchheimer** adds a quadratic (inertial) term to capture the deviation as pore Reynolds number increases. In a fuel cell, the appropriate choice depends on operating current density and GDL compression.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **A PEM fuel cell has a single serpentine flow field on the cathode side. The channel has a square cross-section of side a = 1 mm, total length L = 2 m (including all bends), and carries dry air at T = 353 K and P_in = 1.5 atm (absolute). The average mass flow rate of air is ṁ = 5 × 10⁻⁵ kg/s. The dynamic viscosity of air at 353 K is μ = 2.15 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. The air density at inlet is ρ_in = 1.65 kg/m³.**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **Reynolds number** in the channel, the **friction factor** (assuming fully-developed laminar flow in the square cross-section), and the **pressure drop** along the channel.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** If the gas diffusion layer (GDL) immediately under the channel has permeability k = 8 × 10⁻¹² m², thickness L_GDL = 250 μm, and Forchheimer coefficient β = 1.2 × 10⁵ m⁻¹, and if the average through-plane velocity into the GDL is v_GDL = 0.04 m/s, estimate the **through-plane GDL pressure drop** using the Forchheimer equation. Discuss whether Darcy's law alone would be adequate.

---

### **Solution B1(A):**

**Step 1: Cross-section and hydraulic diameter**
For a square channel, a = 1 mm = 1 × 10⁻³ m. The hydraulic diameter is:
$$D_h = a = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the mean velocity**

Mass flow rate is ρ·v·A_c. For square A_c = a² = 1 × 10⁻⁶ m². Hence:
$$v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \, A_c} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{(1.65)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = 30.3 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excessively high velocity!** Real PEMFC channels typically operate at v = 5 – 20 m/s. The KTU problem may use idealized numbers; proceed with the calculation as stated.

**Step 3: Reynolds number**
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu} = \frac{(1.65)(30.3)(1 \times 10^{-3})}{2.15 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5.00 \times 10^{-2}}{2.15 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2326$$

This is **above the laminar-to-turbulent transition** (Re_crit ≈ 2300 for square ducts). For an *exam-safe* approach, treat it as laminar using the f_D·Re = 56.92 correlation; in practice, a transition correction may be needed.

**Step 4: Friction factor (laminar, square channel)**
$$f_D = \frac{56.92}{\text{Re}} = \frac{56.92}{2326} = 0.02447$$

**Step 5: Pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach)**
$$\Delta P = f_D \frac{L}{D_h} \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = (0.02447) \frac{2}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \frac{(1.65)(30.3)^2}{2}$$

Compute:
- v² = 918.1 m²/s²
- (ρ v²)/2 = (1.65)(918.1)/2 = 757.4 Pa
- L/D_h = 2/(1×10⁻³) = 2000
- ΔP = 0.02447 × 2000 × 757.4 = **37,070 Pa ≈ 37.1 kPa**

**Answer (a):**
- Re ≈ 2326
- f_D ≈ 0.0245
- **ΔP_channel ≈ 37.1 kPa**

---

### **Solution B1(B):**

**Step 1: Forchheimer equation in 1-D, through-plane**

$$-\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\mu}{k} v + \beta \rho v^2$$

**Step 2: Compute viscous term**
$$\frac{\mu}{k} = \frac{2.15 \times 10^{-5}}{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.6875 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa·s/m}^3$$

$$\text{Viscous term} = (2.6875 \times 10^{6})(0.04) = 1.075 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa/m} = 107{,}500 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute inertial term**

Use ρ at GDL conditions ≈ 1.65 kg/m³ (assume similar to channel):
$$\beta \rho = (1.2 \times 10^5)(1.65) = 1.98 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa·s}^2/\text{m}^4$$

$$\text{Inertial term} = (1.98 \times 10^5)(0.04)^2 = (1.98 \times 10^5)(0.0016) = 316.8 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by L_GDL = 250 × 10⁻⁶ m**

$$\Delta P_{\text{viscous}} = (1.075 \times 10^5)(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 26.9 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{inertial}} = (316.8)(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.079 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{GDL}} = 26.9 + 0.079 \approx 27.0 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5: Compare to Darcy-only**

Darcy alone would predict: ΔP_Darcy = 26.9 Pa. Forchheimer predicts 27.0 Pa. **Difference = 0.3%, negligible here.**

**Step 6: Discussion**

At v_GDL = 0.04 m/s, the pore Reynolds number is:
$$\text{Re}_{\text{pore}} = \frac{\rho v_{\text{pore}} \, d_p}{\mu} = \frac{\rho v_{\text{Darcy}} \sqrt{k}}{\mu \, \varepsilon} \approx \frac{(1.65)(0.04)(\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-12}})}{(2.15 \times 10^{-5})(0.7)}$$

$$= \frac{(1.65)(0.04)(2.83 \times 10^{-6})}{(1.505 \times 10^{-5})} = \frac{1.87 \times 10^{-7}}{1.505 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.012$$

Re_pore ≪ 1 → flow is firmly in the **Darcy regime**. Inertial effects are **negligible** (0.3% contribution). **Darcy's law is adequate** at this low velocity.

> [!WARNING]
> **For higher current densities**, the through-plane velocity scales linearly with j. At j = 2 A/cm² (5× the current of j = 0.4 A/cm² assumed), v = 0.2 m/s, and the inertial term scales as v² → 25× larger → Forchheimer contribution becomes ~7% of total. **At very high j (> 3 A/cm²), Forchheimer becomes essential.** The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to mention this scaling behaviour.

**Answer (b):**
- ΔP_GDL ≈ 27.0 Pa (Forchheimer)
- ΔP_GDL ≈ 26.9 Pa (Darcy only)
- **Darcy's law is adequate at this low Re_pore**, but Forchheimer must be used at high current density.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice B — Alternative to A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **A PEM fuel cell has a cathode flow field comprising N = 20 parallel straight channels of width w = 1 mm, depth d = 1 mm, and length L = 0.3 m. Humidified air at T = 343 K, P_in = 1.2 atm enters the channels. The mass flow rate of air is ṁ_total = 4 × 10⁻⁵ kg/s. The cathode is operated at stoichiometry ξ_air = 2.0 at a current I = 50 A. The viscosity of humid air is μ = 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, density ρ = 1.4 kg/m³. Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol.**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **mean velocity** in each channel, the **Reynolds number**, the **friction factor** (assume fully developed laminar flow in a square cross-section), and the **pressure drop** along the channel length.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** If the cell current is to be increased to I = 80 A (with stoichiometry held at ξ = 2.0), determine the **new mean velocity**, the **new pressure drop**, and comment on whether the **flow regime** has changed. Discuss the implications for pumping power and reactant distribution.

---

### **Solution B1-B(A):**

**Step 1: Per-channel mass flow rate**
With 20 equal parallel channels:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{per channel}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{20} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Mean velocity per channel**

A_c = w·d = 1 mm × 1 mm = 1 × 10⁻⁶ m².
$$v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho A_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(1.4)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = 1.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic diameter and Reynolds number**

Square channel: D_h = 1 mm = 1 × 10⁻³ m.
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{(1.4)(1.43)(1 \times 10^{-3})}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-3}}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 95.3$$

Laminar (Re < 2300) ✓

**Step 4: Friction factor (laminar, square)**
$$f_D = \frac{56.92}{\text{Re}} = \frac{56.92}{95.3} = 0.597$$

**Step 5: Pressure drop**
$$\Delta P = f_D \frac{L}{D_h} \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = (0.597) \frac{0.3}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \frac{(1.4)(1.43)^2}{2}$$

- v² = 2.04 m²/s²
- ρ v²/2 = (1.4)(2.04)/2 = 1.43 Pa
- L/D_h = 300
- ΔP = 0.597 × 300 × 1.43 = **256 Pa**

**Answer (a):**
- v ≈ 1.43 m/s
- Re ≈ 95
- f_D ≈ 0.597
- **ΔP ≈ 256 Pa**

---

### **Solution B1-B(B):**

**Step 1: New mass flow rate**

Moles of O₂ consumed per second at I = 80 A:
$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{80}{4 \times 96485} = 2.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}$$

At ξ = 2.0, supplied O₂ moles = 2 × 2.07 × 10⁻⁴ = 4.14 × 10⁻⁴ mol/s.
Air is 21% O₂, 79% N₂ by mole → supplied air = 4.14×10⁻⁴ / 0.21 = 1.97 × 10⁻³ mol/s.
At T = 343 K, P = 1.2 atm = 121,590 Pa, mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = \dot{n} \cdot M_{\text{avg}} = (1.97 \times 10^{-3})(0.029) = 5.72 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

(Assuming M_avg for air ≈ 0.029 kg/mol.)

**Step 2: Per-channel mass flow and velocity**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{per channel}} = \frac{5.72 \times 10^{-5}}{20} = 2.86 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$
$$v_{\text{new}} = \frac{2.86 \times 10^{-6}}{(1.4)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = 2.04 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: New Reynolds number**
$$\text{Re}_{\text{new}} = \frac{(1.4)(2.04)(1 \times 10^{-3})}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 136$$

**Step 4: New friction factor**
$$f_{D,\text{new}} = \frac{56.92}{136} = 0.418$$

**Step 5: New pressure drop**
$$\Delta P_{\text{new}} = (0.418)(300) \frac{(1.4)(2.04)^2}{2} = (125.4)(2.91) = 365 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6: Discussion**

- Velocity increased by factor 1.43 (= 80/50 × correction for stoich = 1.6 × 0.9 ≈ 1.43)
- Pressure drop increased by factor 365/256 = 1.43 (linear in v at fixed Re·f_D constant for laminar)
- Re increased to 136, still well below transition (2300)
- Pumping power scales as ΔP × Q̇ ∝ v² × v = v³ → 2.92× at higher current
- **Implication:** Parallel channels provide low ΔP and good distribution; however, parallel designs risk **flow maldistribution** (some channels receiving more flow than others due to manufacturing tolerances or partial blockage). Serpentine designs avoid this at the cost of higher ΔP.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Students often confuse **Re·f_D = const** (laminar) with **f_D = const** (turbulent). In laminar flow, f_D decreases linearly with v; in turbulent flow, f_D is approximately constant (Blasius: f_D = 0.316/Re^0.25). The pressure drop scales differently! Always check the regime first.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students LOSE marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that hydraulic diameter is for non-circular ducts.** A 1 mark penalty is common if you write "D_h = 2 mm" without showing the calculation.
> 2. **Mixing up Darcy's law (porous) with Darcy–Weisbach (channels).** They are *different* equations with *different* friction-factor conventions. Always state the regime (channel or porous) before writing an equation.
> 3. **Treating the GDL as a channel.** The GDL is a *porous medium*. You cannot use Navier–Stokes. Use Darcy/Forchheimer.
> 4. **Ignoring compressibility.** In a serpentine channel with significant ΔP, use the **P²** formulation for ideal gas. Linear ΔP is a *small-ΔP* approximation.
> 5. **Ignoring the cross-section aspect ratio.** The constant f_D·Re = 56.92 is for a *square* channel. For aspect ratios other than 1, the constant changes (use the table from Section 2.1.3). A common 1–2 mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Convective Transport**

**🔑 Core Concepts (memorize these first):**

- [ ] **Three transport domains**: manifold → channel → GDL → catalyst layer
- [ ] **Hydraulic diameter**: D_h = 4A_c/P_w (square: D_h = a)
- [ ] **Reynolds number** for channel: Re = ρvD_h/μ
- [ ] **Darcy–Weisbach**: ΔP = f_D(L/D_h)(ρv²/2)
- [ ] **Laminar square channel**: f_D·Re = **56.92**
- [ ] **Laminar circular pipe**: f_D·Re = **64** (comparison)
- [ ] **Darcy's law**: v = −(k/μ)∇P (porous, low Re)
- [ ] **Forchheimer equation**: −∇P = (μ/k)v + βρv² (porous, high Re)
- [ ] **Continuity**: ∇·(ρv) = 0 (steady, no source)
- [ ] **Mass flow rate**: ṁ = ρvA_c
- [ ] **Stoichiometry**: ξ = ṅ_supplied / ṅ_consumed
- [ ] **Faraday's law**: I = 2F·ṅ_H₂ = 4F·ṅ_O₂

**🔑 Channel Configurations (know the trade-offs):**

- [ ] **Straight/Parallel**: lowest ΔP, poor uniformity
- [ ] **Single Serpentine**: highest ΔP, excellent water removal
- [ ] **Multi-pass Serpentine**: medium ΔP, good uniformity
- [ ] **Interdigitated**: forced through-plane flow, best mass transfer
- [ ] **Pin/Mesh**: complex 3-D, R&D stage
- [ ] **Porous insert (metal foam)**: emerging technology

**🔑 Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**

- [ ] Inlet: prescribed v_in or ṁ_in
- [ ] Outlet: P_out or ∂P/∂x = 0
- [ ] Wall: v_wall = 0 (no-slip)
- [ ] Channel/GDL interface: continuity of P and mass flux
- [ ] GDL/CL interface: flux = j/nF (Faraday consumption)

**🔑 Design Numbers to Remember:**

- [ ] Typical D_h (channel) = 0.5 – 2 mm
- [ ] Typical L_GDL = 100 – 400 μm
- [ ] Typical GDL porosity ε = 0.6 – 0.8
- [ ] Typical GDL permeability k = 1 × 10⁻¹¹ m²
- [ ] Typical ξ_air = 2 – 3, ξ_H₂ = 1.1 – 1.3
- [ ] Typical channel Re = 50 – 500 (laminar)
- [ ] Typical ΔP_channel = 5 – 50 kPa (serpentine)

**🔑 Equations to Derive (in order of exam frequency):**

1. Hydraulic diameter for rectangle/square
2. Pressure drop in a square channel (Darcy–Weisbach)
3. P(x) along a channel with reactant consumption
4. GDL pressure drop with Forchheimer
5. Through-plane Darcy velocity → pore velocity conversion

**🔑 Conceptual Distinctions to State Clearly:**

- Channel flow vs. Porous-media flow (governing equation)
- Darcy's law vs. Forchheimer (regime)
- Friction factor f_D vs. Forchheimer coefficient β
- Superficial velocity vs. interstitial (pore) velocity
- Mass flow rate conservation vs. momentum conservation

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 scheme advice:** In long-answer (14-mark) questions, always structure your solution as: (1) state the physical domain, (2) write the governing equation explicitly, (3) apply boundary conditions, (4) solve step by step with units, (5) discuss design implications. The KTU examiner's answer key for this module follows this template closely. Marks are distributed roughly: 2 for setup, 3 for governing equation, 4 for algebra/numbers, 3 for interpretation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sizing of a Fuel Cell Stack** is the systematic engineering design process that determines the optimal configuration of a fuel cell system—including the **number of individual cells (N)**, the **active electrode area (A_cell)**, and the **operational parameters (current density, voltage, temperature, pressure)**—required to meet a specified power demand while satisfying efficiency, thermal, and economic constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Sizing refers to the calculation methodology for determining the geometric and electrical parameters of a fuel cell stack such that it delivers a target electrical power output ($P_{req}$) under specified operating conditions, while accounting for polarization losses, parasitic loads, and Balance of Plant (BOP) requirements.

The fundamental sizing problem can be formally stated as:

$$\text{Given: } P_{req}, V_{bus}, \eta_{sys}, T_{op}, P_{op} \longrightarrow \text{Find: } N, A_{cell}, \dot{n}_{H_2}, \dot{n}_{O_2}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Fuel Cell Stack like a Battery Tower Built with LEGO Bricks:**
> 
> Imagine you need to light up a small village that requires **220V** to power household appliances. You have small batteries that each provide only **1.5V** (similar to a single fuel cell producing ~0.6–0.7V). To get 220V, you must **stack** these batteries in **series** (this is analogous to placing cells in a fuel cell stack). 
> 
> Now, if the village also needs to run power-hungry equipment (like a water pump), you need **more current**, which means you need **larger battery plates** (analogous to a larger **active electrode area**). 
> 
> So sizing = **"How many cells do I stack (N)?"** AND **"How big should each cell be (A_cell)?"**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics form the backbone of fuel cell stack sizing:

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Lower Heating Value of Hydrogen (LHV):** $\Delta H_{LHV} = 241.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (or 33.33 kWh/kg)
- **Higher Heating Value of Hydrogen (HHV):** $\Delta H_{HHV} = 285.84 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (or 39.4 kWh/kg)
- **Standard Cell Voltage (E⁰):** $1.229 \text{ V}$ at 25°C, 1 atm
- **Air Composition (by volume):** 21% O₂, 79% N₂
- **Typical Operating Cell Voltage Range:** $0.5 \text{ V} - 0.75 \text{ V}$

## 1.4 Why Stack Sizing Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:**
> 1. **Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO):** Stack sized for ~100-150 kW with ~300-400 cells.
> 2. **Stationary Power (Bloom Energy, Doosan):** 50 kW–MW scale with hundreds of cells.
> 3. **Portable/Military:** 50 W–1 kW micro-stacks with tens of cells.
> 4. **Aerospace (NASA):** High specific power stacks with optimized A_cell.

A poorly sized stack leads to:
- **Over-sizing** → Wasted capital cost, parasitic losses, mass penalty
- **Under-sizing** → Insufficient power, thermal runaway, accelerated degradation

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inputs and Outputs of the Sizing Problem

### 2.1.1 Design Inputs (Specifications from Customer/System Designer)

The sizing engineer receives a set of boundary conditions that define the design space:

| **Input Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Typical Range / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Required Net Power Output | $P_{net}$ | 1 W to 1 MW |
| System DC Bus Voltage | $V_{bus}$ | 12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 300–400 V (auto) |
| Operating Temperature | $T_{op}$ | 60°C – 800°C (depending on type) |
| Operating Pressure | $P_{op}$ | 1 atm – 5 atm |
| Fuel Type & Purity | — | Pure H₂, reformate H₂ |
| Oxidant Type | — | Air or pure O₂ |
| Expected System Efficiency | $\eta_{sys}$ | 40% – 60% |
| Lifetime Requirement | — | 5,000 – 40,000 hours |

### 2.1.2 Design Outputs (What the Sizing Process Must Determine)

| **Output Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Cells | $N$ | Cells connected in series |
| Active Electrode Area | $A_{cell}$ | Geometric area (cm²) per cell |
| Stack Voltage | $V_{stack}$ | $N \times V_{cell}$ |
| Stack Current | $I_{stack}$ | Total current produced |
| H₂ Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_{H_2}$ | kg/s or kg/h |
| Air/O₂ Flow Rate | $\dot{V}_{air}$ | SLPM or m³/h |
| Coolant Flow Rate | $\dot{V}_{cool}$ | L/min |
| Stack Mass & Volume | $M_{stack}, V_{stack}$ | kg and m³ |

## 2.2 The Sizing Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Engineering Procedure)

### Step 1: Establish the Gross Power Requirement

The system must overcome both the **net useful power** AND the **parasitic power** consumed by auxiliary components (BOP).

$$P_{gross} = \frac{P_{net}}{\eta_{BOP}} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

Where $\eta_{BOP}$ accounts for compressor, pump, fan, controller, and inverter losses (typically **0.85–0.95**).

### Step 2: Determine the Operating Cell Voltage

The cell voltage is **not** the open-circuit voltage (1.229 V). It is the actual working voltage under load, determined by the polarization curve:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

Typical design operating point: $V_{cell} = 0.6 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}$ (PEMFC).

### Step 3: Select the Design Current Density

Current density ($j$) is selected based on the application and cooling capacity:

| **Application** | **Typical j (A/cm²)** |
|---|---|
| Low-power portable | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| Automotive | 0.8 – 1.5 |
| Stationary | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| Aerospace (high power density) | 1.5 – 3.0 |

### Step 4: Calculate the Active Area per Cell

$$A_{cell} = \frac{P_{gross}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j} \quad \text{[cm}^2\text{]}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Number of Cells

$$N = \frac{V_{stack,target}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{V_{bus}/\eta_{DC-DC}}{V_{cell}}$$

$N$ is rounded up to the nearest integer (you cannot have fractional cells).

### Step 6: Calculate the Stack Current

$$I_{stack} = j \times A_{cell} \quad \text{[Amperes]}$$

### Step 7: Determine Fuel and Oxidant Flow Rates (Stoichiometry)

Using Faraday's law of electrolysis:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,consumed} = \frac{I_{stack} \cdot N}{2F} \quad \text{[mol/s]}$$

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,consumed} = \frac{I_{stack} \cdot N}{4F} \quad \text{[mol/s]}$$

Actual supply rates account for stoichiometry (typically $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.1$–$1.2$ and $\lambda_{air} = 2.0$–$3.0$):

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,supply} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2,consumed}$$

### Step 8: Thermal Sizing

The heat to be removed is the difference between the chemical energy input and the electrical output:

$$Q_{heat} = P_{gross} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{cell}} - 1\right) \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Must Memorize All of These:**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{gross} = P_{net}/\eta_{BOP}$ | Gross power to overcome parasitics | W |
| 2 | $V_{cell} = E_0 - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Working cell voltage | V |
| 3 | $A_{cell} = P_{gross}/(N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j)$ | Active electrode area | cm² |
| 4 | $N = V_{stack}/V_{cell}$ | Number of cells (integer) | — |
| 5 | $I = j \cdot A_{cell}$ | Stack current | A |
| 6 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage | V |
| 7 | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}$ | Stack power | W |
| 8 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot I/(2F)$ | Hydrogen supply rate | mol/s |
| 9 | $\dot{n}_{O_2} = \lambda_{O_2} \cdot I/(4F)$ | Oxygen supply rate | mol/s |
| 10 | $\dot{n}_{air} = \lambda_{air} \cdot I/(4F \cdot y_{O_2})$ | Air supply rate | mol/s |
| 11 | $\eta_{cell} = V_{cell}/E_0$ | Cell electrical efficiency | — |
| 12 | $Q_{heat} = P_{gross}(1/\eta_{cell} - 1)$ | Heat to remove | W |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = (P_{stack}/\eta_{cell})/\text{LHV}$ | H₂ mass flow | kg/s |
| 14 | $P_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}$ | Chemical power input | W |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sizing methodology is the **backbone of fuel cell system design** in:

- **Automotive:** Determining if a stack fits in the vehicle floor and delivers 100+ kW.
- **Backup Power:** Ensuring uninterrupted 50 kW for telecom towers.
- **Aircraft Auxiliary Power Units (APU):** Minimizing mass while maximizing power.
- **Spacecraft:** NASA's high-pressure H₂/O₂ stacks for the Space Shuttle and Apollo.
- **Submarines:** Air-independent propulsion (AIP) using PEMFC stacks.

The sizing output feeds directly into:
1. **Mechanical design** (cell frames, end plates, compression)
2. **Thermal design** (cooling plates, radiators)
3. **Fluid distribution** (manifolds, humidifiers)
4. **Cost estimation** (materials, BOP components)
- **Control strategy** (load following, start-up/shut-down protocols)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Stack Power Equation

Starting from the most fundamental relationships, we can derive the master power equation for a fuel cell stack.

### Step 1: Single-Cell Power

The power produced by a single fuel cell is the product of its operating voltage and the current flowing through it:

$$P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I_{cell}$$

The current in a cell is governed by the current density and the active area:

$$I_{cell} = j \cdot A_{cell}$$

Substituting this into the cell power equation:

$$P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}$$

### Step 2: Stack-Level Power (N Cells in Series)

When $N$ identical cells are connected in **series**, the stack voltage adds up while the current remains the same:

$$V_{stack} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} V_{cell,k} = N \cdot V_{cell}$$

$$I_{stack} = I_{cell} = j \cdot A_{cell}$$

Therefore, the **total stack power** is:

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack} = (N \cdot V_{cell}) \cdot (j \cdot A_{cell})$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

This is the **master sizing equation**. From it, we can solve for any unknown when the other three are known:

$$A_{cell} = \frac{P_{stack}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j}, \quad N = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}}, \quad j = \frac{P_{stack}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot A_{cell}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Active Area When Stack Voltage is the Constraint

Sometimes, the design driver is the **DC bus voltage** of the application (e.g., a 300 V traction bus for an EV).

### Step 1: Number of Cells from Voltage Constraint

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{V_{bus,DC}}{\eta_{DC-DC} \cdot V_{cell}} \right\rceil$$

The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures $N$ is an integer (you can't build 287.3 cells!).

### Step 2: Active Area from Power and the Computed N

$$A_{cell} = \frac{P_{stack}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j}$$

### Step 3: Stack Current

$$I_{stack} = j \cdot A_{cell}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrogen Consumption Using Faraday's Law

### Step 1: Electrochemical Half-Reaction

At the anode of a hydrogen fuel cell:

$$H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

This means **1 mole of H₂** liberates **2 moles of electrons**.

### Step 2: Charge Carried Per Mole of H₂

The total charge per mole of hydrogen, using Faraday's constant $F$:

$$Q_{per\ mol\ H_2} = 2 \cdot F = 2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \text{ C/mol}$$

### Step 3: Moles of H₂ Per Second (Stoichiometric)

For a stack delivering current $I_{stack}$:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,stoich} = \frac{I_{stack}}{2F} \quad \text{[mol/s]}$$

### Step 4: Actual Supply Rate with Stoichiometric Ratio

The actual fuel supplied must be higher than the stoichiometric rate to account for incomplete utilization and dynamic response:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,supply} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{2F}$$

Where the typical stoichiometry $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.1$ to $1.2$ (i.e., 10–20% excess).

### Step 5: Mass Flow Rate Conversion

Using the molar mass of hydrogen $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ g/mol}$:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2,supply} \cdot M_{H_2} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot \frac{I_{stack} \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Air Flow Rate (Oxygen from Atmospheric Air)

### Step 1: Oxygen Required (Stoichiometric)

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{I_{stack}}{4F} \quad \text{[mol/s]}$$

### Step 2: Air Required

Since air contains only **21% oxygen by volume (mole fraction)**, the required air flow is:

$$\dot{n}_{air,stoich} = \frac{\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{I_{stack}}{4F \cdot 0.21}$$

### Step 3: Actual Air Supply Rate

$$\dot{n}_{air,supply} = \lambda_{air} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{4F \cdot 0.21}$$

Where $\lambda_{air} = 2.0$ to $3.0$ (air stoichiometry is much higher than fuel because N₂ dilutes the O₂).

### Step 4: Volumetric Air Flow in SLPM (Standard Liters Per Minute)

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \dot{n}_{air,supply} \cdot 22.4 \times 60 \quad \text{[SLPM]}$$

Where 22.4 L/mol is the molar volume at STP.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cell Efficiency and Heat Generation

### Step 1: Thermodynamic Maximum Voltage

The maximum possible voltage is the **Higher Heating Value equivalent**:

$$E_{HHV} = \frac{\Delta H_{HHV}}{2F} = \frac{285{,}840 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

The **Lower Heating Value equivalent** (water leaves as vapor):

$$E_{LHV} = \frac{\Delta H_{LHV}}{2F} = \frac{241{,}830 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} = 1.253 \text{ V}$$

The **reversible (Nernst) voltage** at standard conditions is $1.229$ V, which lies between these.

### Step 2: Cell Electrical Efficiency

The efficiency of a single cell is the ratio of the actual operating voltage to the thermodynamic ideal:

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{LHV}} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.253}$$

For $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V: $\eta_{cell} = 0.7/1.253 = 55.9\%$.

### Step 3: Heat Generation Per Cell

By the first law of thermodynamics, the energy not converted to electricity is released as heat:

$$Q_{cell} = P_{chem,in} - P_{elec,out} = (1 - \eta_{cell}) \cdot P_{chem,in}$$

For the entire stack:

$$Q_{stack} = (1 - \eta_{cell}) \cdot P_{gross} = P_{gross} \cdot \left(\frac{1.253}{V_{cell}} - 1\right) \quad \text{[W]}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a PEMFC stack to power a small forklift.
> **Specifications:**
> - Net power required: $P_{net} = 10$ kW
> - DC bus voltage: $V_{bus} = 48$ V
> - Operating cell voltage: $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V
> - Current density: $j = 0.5$ A/cm²
> - BOP efficiency: $\eta_{BOP} = 0.90$
> - H₂ stoichiometry: $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.15$
> - Air stoichiometry: $\lambda_{air} = 2.5$

### Solution:

**Step 1: Gross Power**

$$P_{gross} = \frac{P_{net}}{\eta_{BOP}} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.90} = 11{,}111.11 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Number of Cells**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{48}{0.65} \right\rceil = \lceil 73.85 \rceil = 74 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3: Active Area per Cell**

$$A_{cell} = \frac{P_{gross}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j} = \frac{11{,}111.11}{74 \times 0.65 \times 0.5}$$

$$A_{cell} = \frac{11{,}111.11}{24.05} = 462.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4: Stack Current**

$$I_{stack} = j \cdot A_{cell} = 0.5 \times 462.0 = 231.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: H₂ Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{2F} = 1.15 \times \frac{231.0}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.377 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.377 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 = 2.776 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s} = 0.010 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6: Air Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \lambda_{air} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{4F \cdot 0.21} = 2.5 \times \frac{231.0}{4 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.21}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = 2.5 \times \frac{231.0}{81{,}047.4} = 7.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 7.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4 \times 60 = 9.58 \text{ SLPM}$$

**Step 7: Heat Generation**

$$Q_{stack} = P_{gross} \cdot \left(\frac{1.253}{0.65} - 1\right) = 11{,}111.11 \times 0.928 = 10{,}307 \text{ W} \approx 10.3 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary:** A 74-cell stack, each with 462 cm² active area, drawing 231 A, consuming 0.01 kg/h of H₂ and 9.58 SLPM of air, while generating 10.3 kW of heat that must be removed by the cooling system.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stack Sizing

The following block diagram illustrates the complete **input-process-output architecture** of a fuel cell stack sizing calculation, from initial specifications to final design parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Power Demand Pnet] --> B[Define Design Inputs]
    B --> B1[Operating Temperature Top]
    B --> B2[Operating Pressure Pop]
    B --> B3[Bus Voltage Vbus]
    B --> B4[Efficiency Target etasys]
    C[Select Operating Point] --> C1[Cell Voltage Vcell]
    C --> C2[Current Density j]
    D[Gross Power Module] --> D1[Pgross = Pnet divided by etasys BOP]
    E[Cell Count Calculator] --> E1[N = Vbus divided by Vcell rounded up]
    F[Active Area Module] --> F1[Acell = Pgross divided by N times Vcell times j]
    G[Current Module] --> G1[Istack = j times Acell]
    H[Fuel Supply Module] --> H1[nH2 = lambda H2 times I divided by 2F]
    I[Air Supply Module] --> I1[nair = lambda air times I divided by 4F times 0.21]
    J[Thermal Module] --> J1[Qheat = Pgross times 1 divided by etacell minus 1]
    K[Mechanical Module] --> K1[Stack Mass and Volume Estimation]
    L[Final Stack Design Output] --> L1[Stack Voltage Vstack]
    L --> L2[Stack Current Istk]
    L --> L3[Fuel Consumption mH2]
    L --> L4[Air Flow Vair]
    L --> L5[Heat Load Qheat]
    L --> L6[Mass and Volume]
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> F1
    G1 --> H1
    G1 --> I1
    G1 --> J1
    F1 --> K1
    K1 --> L
    E1 --> L
    H1 --> L
    I1 --> L
    J1 --> L
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Voltage Cascade in a Fuel Cell Stack

This diagram shows how individual cell voltages add up to the stack voltage, and how the current path remains common throughout the series connection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STACK[FUEL CELL STACK N CELLS IN SERIES]
        C1[CELL 1\nV1 = 0.65V] --> C2[CELL 2\nV2 = 0.65V]
        C2 --> C3[CELL 3\nV3 = 0.65V]
        C3 --> C4[CELL N\nVN = 0.65V]
        C4 --> DOT[... continues ...]
        DOT --> CN[CELL N\nVN = 0.65V]
    end
    H2IN[Hydrogen Inlet Manifold] --> STACK
    AIRIN[Air Inlet Manifold] --> STACK
    COOL[Coolant Channel] --> STACK
    STACK --> VOUT[Stack Voltage Vstack = N times Vcell]
    STACK --> IOUT[Stack Current Istk = constant j times Acell]
    VOUT --> LOAD[External DC Load or DC-DC Converter]
    IOUT --> LOAD
    STACK --> H2OUT[Unreacted H2 and Product Water Out]
    STACK --> AIROUT[Cathode Exhaust Air and Water Vapor Out]
    STACK --> QOUT[Heat Qheat to Cooling System]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Design Decision Tree for Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Sizing Process]) --> Q1{Is Power Rating\nGT 100 kW?}
    Q1 -- YES --> P_HIGH[Select High Power Density Stack\nj gt 1.0 A per cm squared]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is Voltage\nConstraint Driven?}
    Q2 -- YES --> V_PATH[Compute N from Vbus divided by Vcell]
    Q2 -- NO --> P_PATH[Compute N from Power Density Target]
    P_HIGH --> A_CALC[Calculate Acell from Master Equation]
    V_PATH --> A_CALC
    P_PATH --> A_CALC
    A_CALC --> CHECK{Acell within\nManufacturable Range\n50 to 1000 cm squared?}
    CHECK -- NO --> ITER[Adjust j or Vcell and Recompute]
    ITER --> A_CALC
    CHECK -- YES --> FLOW[Calculate Fuel and Air Flow]
    FLOW --> HEAT[Calculate Heat Load]
    HEAT --> COOL_CHECK{Cooling\nFeasible?}
    COOL_CHECK -- NO --> ITER2[Reduce j to Lower Heat]
    ITER2 --> A_CALC
    COOL_CHECK -- YES --> FINAL([Final Design Parameters])
    FINAL --> OUTPUT[Output N, Acell, Istk, Vstk, mH2, Vair, Qheat]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sizing of a Fuel Cell Stack. List the major inputs and outputs.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Sizing of a fuel cell stack is the engineering design process that determines the number of cells, active area, and operating parameters required to deliver a specified electrical power output.

**Major Inputs:**
- Net power requirement ($P_{net}$)
- DC bus voltage ($V_{bus}$)
- Operating temperature and pressure
- Cell operating voltage ($V_{cell}$)
- Current density ($j$)
- BOP efficiency ($\eta_{BOP}$)
- Stoichiometric ratios ($\lambda_{H_2}, \lambda_{air}$)

**Major Outputs:**
- Number of cells ($N$)
- Active electrode area per cell ($A_{cell}$)
- Stack voltage ($V_{stack}$)
- Stack current ($I_{stack}$)
- H₂ and air flow rates
- Heat to be removed ($Q_{heat}$)

---

### Question 2: What is the role of stoichiometric ratio in fuel cell stack sizing?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The stoichiometric ratio $\lambda$ is the ratio of the actual supply rate of a reactant to its stoichiometric (theoretical) consumption rate. For hydrogen, $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.1 - 1.2$, while for air, $\lambda_{air} = 2.0 - 3.0$.

**Role in Sizing:**
1. It determines the actual fuel/oxidant flow rates that the BOP must deliver.
2. It accounts for incomplete reactant utilization, dead-end anode purges, and dynamic load transients.
3. It directly affects compressor/pump sizing, parasitic power, and system efficiency.
4. A higher $\lambda$ improves performance stability but reduces overall efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Design Sizing of a 5 kW PEMFC Stack

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**A 5 kW PEMFC stack is to be designed for a backup power application. Given the following specifications, determine:**
- **(a)** The number of cells, active area, and stack current **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The hydrogen and air consumption rates, and the heat load to be removed **(7 marks)**

**Specifications:**
- Net power: $P_{net} = 5$ kW
- DC bus voltage: $V_{bus} = 24$ V
- Cell operating voltage: $V_{cell} = 0.70$ V
- Current density: $j = 0.4$ A/cm²
- BOP efficiency: $\eta_{BOP} = 0.92$
- H₂ stoichiometry: $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.20$
- Air stoichiometry: $\lambda_{air} = 2.5$
- Molar mass of H₂: $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Calculate Gross Power**

$$P_{gross} = \frac{P_{net}}{\eta_{BOP}} = \frac{5000}{0.92} = 5434.78 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Number of Cells**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{0.70} \right\rceil = \lceil 34.29 \rceil = 35 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Active Area per Cell**

$$A_{cell} = \frac{P_{gross}}{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j} = \frac{5434.78}{35 \times 0.70 \times 0.4}$$

$$A_{cell} = \frac{5434.78}{9.8} = 554.57 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate Stack Current**

$$I_{stack} = j \cdot A_{cell} = 0.4 \times 554.57 = 221.83 \text{ A}$$

**Verification:**

$$P_{stack} = 35 \times 0.70 \times 221.83 = 5434.8 \text{ W} \approx P_{gross} \checkmark$$

**Result (a):** $N = 35$ cells, $A_{cell} = 554.57$ cm², $I_{stack} = 221.83$ A.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Hydrogen Molar Flow Rate**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \lambda_{H_2} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{2F} = 1.20 \times \frac{221.83}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = 1.20 \times 1.1495 \times 10^{-3} = 1.379 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 2: Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times M_{H_2} = 1.379 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 = 2.78 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

Converting to kg/h: $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.78 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 / 1000 = 0.010$ kg/h

**Step 3: Air Molar Flow Rate**

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \lambda_{air} \cdot \frac{I_{stack}}{4F \cdot 0.21} = 2.5 \times \frac{221.83}{4 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.21}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = 2.5 \times \frac{221.83}{81{,}047.4} = 6.84 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 4: Volumetric Air Flow in SLPM**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 6.84 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.4 \times 60 = 9.19 \text{ SLPM}$$

**Step 5: Heat Load**

$$Q_{stack} = P_{gross} \left(\frac{1.253}{0.70} - 1\right) = 5434.78 \times 0.79 = 4293.5 \text{ W} \approx 4.29 \text{ kW}$$

**Result (b):** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 0.010$ kg/h, $\dot{V}_{air} = 9.19$ SLPM, $Q_{stack} = 4.29$ kW.

---

### Question B: Comparative Sizing Analysis at Two Current Densities

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**A 50 kW fuel cell system is required for an electric bus. Perform the following:**
- **(a)** Determine the number of cells and active area if the design current density is $j_1 = 0.6$ A/cm², given $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V, $V_{bus} = 300$ V, and BOP efficiency $\eta_{BOP} = 0.90$ **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** If a higher power density design is required, the current density is increased to $j_2 = 1.0$ A/cm². Recalculate the active area and comment on the trade-offs **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Gross Power**

$$P_{gross} = \frac{P_{net}}{\eta_{BOP}} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.90} = 55{,}555.56 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Number of Cells**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{V_{bus}}{V_{cell}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{300}{0.65} \right\rceil = \lceil 461.54 \rceil = 462 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3: Active Area**

$$A_{cell,1} = \frac{55{,}555.56}{462 \times 0.65 \times 0.6} = \frac{55{,}555.56}{180.18} = 308.31 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4: Stack Current**

$$I_{stack,1} = 0.6 \times 308.31 = 185.0 \text{ A}$$

**Result (a):** $N = 462$, $A_{cell,1} = 308.31$ cm², $I_{stack,1} = 185.0$ A.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Recalculate Active Area**

$$A_{cell,2} = \frac{55{,}555.56}{462 \times 0.65 \times 1.0} = \frac{55{,}555.56}{300.3} = 185.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2: Stack Current at Higher j**

$$I_{stack,2} = 1.0 \times 185.0 = 185.0 \text{ A}$$

Wait — let me re-examine: the stack current actually depends on the product $j \cdot A_{cell}$. With higher $j$ and lower $A_{cell}$:

$$I_{stack} = j \cdot A_{cell} = 1.0 \times 185.0 = 185.0 \text{ A}$$

The current is the same because we kept the power constant.

**Step 3: Trade-off Analysis**

| **Parameter** | **Design 1 (j=0.6)** | **Design 2 (j=1.0)** |
|---|---|---|
| Active Area | 308.31 cm² (Larger) | 185.0 cm² (Smaller) |
| Stack Volume | Higher | Lower (~40% reduction) |
| Stack Mass | Higher | Lower |
| Cell Efficiency | Higher (less polarization) | Lower (more losses) |
| Heat Density | Lower | Higher (~67% more W/cm²) |
| Cooling Complexity | Easier | Harder (more thermal mgmt) |
| Cost of MEA | Higher (larger area) | Lower (smaller area) |

**Comment:** Increasing current density reduces the active area and stack volume/mass, which is desirable for vehicle applications. However, this comes at the cost of reduced cell efficiency (more activation and ohmic losses), higher heat flux requiring more sophisticated cooling, and accelerated degradation of the membrane electrode assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to round up N:** Writing $N = 73.85$ instead of $N = 74$ will cost full marks on the cell count.
> 2. **Using OCV (1.229 V) instead of operating voltage:** Always use the design $V_{cell}$ (e.g., 0.65 V) as specified in the problem, NOT the theoretical maximum.
> 3. **Confusing stoich ratio direction:** $\lambda$ is supply/consumed, so supply is always GREATER than consumed. Watch the inequality.
> 4. **Forgetting air's 21% O₂ content:** If the question asks for air flow, divide by 0.21.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $A_{cell}$ must be in cm² when $j$ is in A/cm². Mixing units causes silent errors.
> 6. **Heat calculation sign error:** Heat is $P_{gross} \times (1/\eta - 1)$, NOT $P_{gross} \times (1 - \eta)$.
> 7. **Not adding BOP losses:** Using $P_{net}$ directly instead of $P_{gross}$ under-specifies the stack.
> 8. **No thermal verification:** KTU 2024 examiners often ask follow-ups like "Is this heat manageable?" Always include a brief thermal feasibility check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Know Before Exam:**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- **Sizing = finding N, A_cell, j, V_cell that yield P_required**
- **Master equation:** $P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}$ (always start here!)
- **Series connection** → voltages add, current stays constant
- **Parallel connection** → currents add, voltage stays constant (used in some advanced designs)

### 🔑 Key Numbers to Memorize
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- Molar volume at STP: 22.4 L/mol
- Air O₂ mole fraction: 0.21
- H₂ LHV: 241.83 kJ/mol (or 33.33 kWh/kg)
- LHV-based thermodynamic voltage: 1.253 V
- Standard Nernst voltage: 1.229 V

### ⚙️ Typical Design Values
- PEMFC operating voltage: 0.6 – 0.75 V
- PEMFC current density: 0.4 – 1.5 A/cm²
- $\lambda_{H_2} = 1.1 - 1.2$
- $\lambda_{air} = 2.0 - 3.0$
- BOP efficiency: 0.85 – 0.95

### 📋 Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Using $V_{cell} = 1.229$ V (this is OCV, not operating voltage)
- ❌ Forgetting to apply ceiling function to N
- ❌ Mixing up gross vs. net power
- ❌ Confusing H₂ stoichiometry with air stoichiometry
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mol/s to SLPM for air

### 🧠 Design Trade-off Rules
- **Higher j** → smaller A_cell, smaller stack, but lower efficiency
- **Higher V_cell** → higher efficiency, lower current, but lower power density
- **More cells (N)** → higher stack voltage, but more parasitic losses
- **Higher temperature** → faster kinetics, but material degradation
- **Higher pressure** → higher Nernst voltage, but compressor parasitic power

### 📐 Order of Operations (Always Follow This)
1. **Compute $P_{gross}$** from $P_{net}$ and $\eta_{BOP}$
2. **Compute N** from $V_{bus}$ and $V_{cell}$ (round up)
3. **Compute $A_{cell}$** from master equation
4. **Compute $I_{stack}$** from $j \cdot A_{cell}$
5. **Compute fuel/air flows** with stoichiometry
6. **Compute heat load** for thermal design
7. **Verify feasibility** (cooling, volume, mass)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Advice for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> When a numerical problem appears, ALWAYS write the master equation $P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot j \cdot A_{cell}$ first, then identify which two variables are given and which two you need to find. This single habit will earn you 70% of the marks automatically. Then proceed systematically — KTU examiners reward clear, logical step-by-step solutions over shortcut mental math.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stack Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell Stack Configuration** refers to the systematic arrangement, interconnection, and mechanical assembly of multiple individual fuel cells (unit cells) into a single integrated unit to deliver practically useful voltage, current, and power output. A typical Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) unit cell produces only **0.6 V to 0.8 V** under load; therefore, cells must be connected in **electrical series** via **bipolar plates** to achieve commercially viable voltages (typically **12 V, 24 V, 48 V, or 200 V–400 V** for automotive and stationary applications).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECHT634)**, stack configuration encompasses:
- The **mechanical stack-up** of Membrane Electrode Assemblies (MEA), bipolar plates, gaskets, and end plates
- The **electrical interconnection topology** (series, parallel, or hybrid)
- The **fluidic manifold design** (external, internal, or hybrid) for reactant distribution
- The **thermal and compression management** strategy
- The **sealing and assembly methodology** ensuring long-term durability

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Sandwich" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a fuel cell stack like a multi-storied apartment building connected to a single power grid.** Each floor (unit cell) generates a small amount of electricity on its own — useless for the city. But when you stack 50–100 floors together, connect them in series (electrically like ladders going floor-to-floor), and supply each floor with its own fuel (hydrogen) and air (oxygen), the building becomes a power plant. The "bipolar plates" are the reinforced concrete floors that carry electricity from one apartment to the next AND distribute water pipes and gas pipes simultaneously. Without the floors (bipolar plates), you cannot stack apartments; without sealing the gaps, the gas leaks. This is exactly how a fuel cell stack works.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a Stack?

| Parameter | Single Cell | Stack (N cells in series) |
|---|---|---|
| Open Circuit Voltage | **0.6 V – 0.8 V** | N × 0.6 V to 0.8 V |
| Practical Voltage | ~0.6 V under load | N × 0.6 V |
| Power Output | ~0.5 W/cm² | N × area × power density |
| Application | Laboratory only | EVs, backup power, grid |

A standard PEMFC system in a Toyota Mirai uses approximately **370 cells in series** to deliver ~250 kW at 650 V. This illustrates the engineering necessity of stacking.

## 1.4 Key Components at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **The seven primary components of a fuel cell stack are:**
> 1. **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** — the heart where the electrochemical reaction occurs
> 2. **Bipolar Plates (BPP)** — dual-function structural and current-conducting plates with flow fields
> 3. **Gaskets** — prevent gas and coolant leakage
> 4. **Current Collectors** — at stack extremities only
> 5. **End Plates** — distribute compressive load
> 6. **Tie Rods / Bolts** — apply uniform compression
> 7. **Manifolds** — feed/remove reactants and products
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Principle

A single PEMFC unit cell consists of an anode, cathode, and proton-exchange membrane. The half-reactions are:

$$\text{Anode:} \quad H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode:} \quad \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$

$$\text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The **Nernst open-circuit voltage** for a single cell is:

$$E_{Nernst} = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15) + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

Where:
- **T** = Cell temperature (K)
- **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **P** = Partial pressures (atm)

## 2.2 Stack Voltage Theory

For an **ideal stack of N cells in series**:

$$V_{stack,ideal} = N \cdot V_{cell}$$

For a **real stack**, the actual voltage is reduced by irreversible losses (activation, ohmic, concentration):

$$V_{stack} = N \cdot \left(E_{Nernst} - V_{act} - V_{ohmic} - V_{conc}\right)$$

### 2.2.1 Loss Breakdown (Polarization Components)

| Loss Term | Symbol | Physical Origin | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation Loss | $\eta_{act}$ | Sluggish electrode kinetics | 0.05 V – 0.15 V |
| Ohmic Loss | $\eta_{ohmic}$ | Ionic + electronic resistance | 0.01 V – 0.05 V |
| Concentration Loss | $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass transport limitations | 0.01 V – 0.10 V |
| **Total Loss** | $\Sigma\eta$ | All irreversibilities | **0.07 V – 0.30 V** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the must-memorize equations for the KTU 2024 ESE on Stack Configuration.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell}$ | Ideal stack voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Real cell voltage | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Tafel activation loss | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot (R_{ionic} + R_{electronic})$ | Ohmic polarization | V |
| 5 | $\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration polarization | V |
| 6 | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}$ | Stack power output | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $P_{density} = \frac{P_{stack}}{A_{cell} \cdot N}$ | Power per unit active area | W/cm² |
| 8 | $I_{stack} = i \cdot A_{cell}$ | Stack current | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | $\eta_{voltage} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}}$ | Voltage efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{Faradaic} = \frac{i}{i_{total}}$ | Faradaic efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{thermal} = \frac{T_{cell} - T_{ambient}}{T_{cell}}$ | Carnot-related | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $A_{stack} = N \cdot A_{cell}$ | Total active area | cm² |
| 13 | $V_{stack} = N \cdot E_0 - I \cdot R_{total}$ | Linearized stack model | V |
| 14 | $R_{total} = N \cdot R_{cell} + R_{interconnect}$ | Total stack resistance | Ω |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE**: In the table above, all absolute values and pipes have been written using standard text representation to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In your exam, write formulas clearly with proper LaTeX when used inside display equations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of stack configuration directly determine:
- **Automotive fuel cells** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) — 300–400 cells, 650 V
- **Stationary power** (Bloom Energy SOFC) — hundreds of cells, 50–250 kW
- **Portable power** (military, drones) — 10–50 cells, 12–48 V
- **Backup power** (telecom towers) — 20–80 cells, 24–48 V

A poorly designed stack configuration causes:
- **Voltage non-uniformity** → cell reversal → permanent damage
- **Thermal runaway** → membrane dehydration → failure
- **Reactant starvation** → carbon corrosion in catalyst
- **Mechanical fatigue** → seal leakage → safety hazard

This is why stack configuration is a **core Module 4 topic** in PECHT634.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Power Output and Efficiency

### Step 1: Define the operating point

Consider a PEMFC stack with **N = 50 cells** connected in series. Each cell has an active area of **A = 200 cm²**. The stack operates at a current density of **i = 0.6 A/cm²**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the operating current of one cell:**

$$I_{cell} = i \cdot A_{cell} = 0.6 \, \frac{A}{cm^2} \times 200 \, cm^2 = 120 \, A$$

**Explanation:** Current density × active area = total current. This is the current drawn from a single cell.

---

### Step 2: Calculate the operating current of the entire stack

Since all cells are in **series**, the same current flows through every cell. Therefore:

$$I_{stack} = I_{cell} = 120 \, A$$

**Explanation:** In a series connection, current is conserved. The current through the stack equals the current through any individual cell.

---

### Step 3: Calculate the cell voltage at this current density

Assume the cell voltage at i = 0.6 A/cm² is **V_cell = 0.65 V** (a typical operating point from the polarization curve).

**Step 3a — Cell voltage determination:**
The student should consult the polarization curve for the specific MEA. For Nafion-based MEAs with Pt loading of 0.4 mg/cm², V_cell = 0.65 V at 0.6 A/cm² is a reasonable assumption.

---

### Step 4: Calculate the ideal stack voltage

$$V_{stack,ideal} = N \cdot V_{cell} = 50 \times 0.65 \, V = 32.5 \, V$$

**Explanation:** In series, voltages add. The total open-circuit stack voltage is 50 times that of a single cell.

---

### Step 5: Calculate the stack power output

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack} = 32.5 \, V \times 120 \, A = 3900 \, W = 3.9 \, kW$$

**Explanation:** Electrical power is voltage multiplied by current. This represents the **net DC electrical output** of the stack at the chosen operating point.

---

### Step 6: Calculate the power density (volumetric figure of merit)

Assume the active area per cell is 200 cm² and the cell pitch (thickness per cell including BPP) is **d_cell = 4 mm = 0.4 cm**.

$$V_{stack,volume} = N \cdot A_{cell} \cdot d_{cell} = 50 \times 200 \times 0.4 = 4000 \, cm^3 = 4 \, L$$

$$\rho_{power,volume} = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{stack,volume}} = \frac{3900 \, W}{4 \, L} = 975 \, W/L$$

**Explanation:** This is the **volumetric power density** — critical for automotive applications where space is at a premium. Modern automotive stacks target > 3 kW/L.

---

### Step 7: Calculate the gravimetric (mass) power density

Assume each cell mass (MEA + 2 BPPs + gaskets) is **m_cell = 150 g**. End plates, tie rods, manifolds add another **m_end = 10 kg**.

$$m_{stack} = N \cdot m_{cell} + m_{end} = 50 \times 0.15 \, kg + 10 \, kg = 17.5 \, kg$$

$$\rho_{power,mass} = \frac{P_{stack}}{m_{stack}} = \frac{3900 \, W}{17.5 \, kg} \approx 223 \, W/kg$$

**Explanation:** The **gravimetric power density** is critical for aerospace and portable applications. DOE 2025 targets exceed 900 W/kg for transportation stacks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Uniformity and Cell-to-Cell Variation

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is a KTU-favorite question.** A stack with non-uniform cell voltages suffers the **"weakest cell" problem**.

### Step 1: Define the cell voltage distribution

Let the individual cell voltages be: V₁, V₂, V₃, ..., V_N. The stack voltage is:

$$V_{stack} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$$

### Step 2: Define the mean cell voltage

$$\bar{V} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = \frac{V_{stack}}{N}$$

### Step 3: Define the cell-to-cell standard deviation

$$\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(V_i - \bar{V})^2}$$

### Step 4: Compute the coefficient of variation

$$CV_V = \frac{\sigma_V}{\bar{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial acceptance criterion:** For a healthy PEMFC stack, **CV_V must be less than 5%**. A CV_V > 10% indicates significant non-uniformity — often caused by:
> - Uneven reactant distribution
> - Temperature gradients
> - Compression non-uniformity
> - Manufacturing tolerances in MEAs

### Step 5: Numerical example

A 10-cell stack has cell voltages: 0.65, 0.66, 0.64, 0.65, 0.67, 0.63, 0.66, 0.65, 0.64, 0.65 V.

**Compute the mean:**

$$\bar{V} = \frac{0.65+0.66+0.64+0.65+0.67+0.63+0.66+0.65+0.64+0.65}{10} = 6.50/10 = 0.650 \, V$$

**Compute deviations squared:**

| Cell | V_i (V) | (V_i − 0.650) | (V_i − 0.650)² |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.65 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 2 | 0.66 | 0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 3 | 0.64 | −0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 4 | 0.65 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 5 | 0.67 | 0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 6 | 0.63 | −0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 7 | 0.66 | 0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 8 | 0.65 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 9 | 0.64 | −0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 10 | 0.65 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **0.0012** |

**Compute standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{0.0012}{10}} = \sqrt{0.00012} \approx 0.01095 \, V$$

**Compute coefficient of variation:**

$$CV_V = \frac{0.01095}{0.650} \times 100\% \approx 1.685\%$$

**Conclusion:** CV_V = 1.685% < 5%, so the stack is operating healthily.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stack Compression Force and Contact Resistance

The **optimal compression pressure** on the MEA is critical. Too little → high contact resistance; too much → membrane damage.

### Step 1: Contact resistance model

The contact resistance at the BPP–GDL interface is empirically:

$$R_{contact} = \frac{R_0}{P_{comp}^{\beta}}$$

Where:
- **R_0** = Base contact resistance (Ω·cm²) at unit pressure
- **P_comp** = Compression pressure (MPa)
- **β** = Empirical exponent (~0.5 for typical carbon papers)

### Step 2: Total stack resistance

$$R_{stack} = N \cdot (R_{mem} + R_{GDL,anode} + R_{GDL,cathode} + 2R_{contact,BPP} + R_{bipolar,plate})$$

### Step 3: Optimum compression point

The optimum is found by minimizing the sum of ohmic loss and mechanical damage:

$$\frac{d}{dP_{comp}}\left(I^2 R_{stack} + k_{damage} \cdot P_{comp}^2\right) = 0$$

Solving:

$$P_{comp,opt} \approx \left(\frac{I^2 \beta R_0}{2 k_{damage}}\right)^{1/(\beta + 2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical industrial value:** P_comp,opt = **0.5 MPa to 1.5 MPa** for PEMFC stacks. SOFC stacks require much higher compression (2–5 MPa) due to ceramic brittleness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fuel Cell Stack Architecture (Top-to-Bottom View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1[End Plate Anode Side] --> A2[Current Collector Anode]
    A2 --> A3[Bipolar Plate 1 Anode Flow Field]
    A3 --> A4[MEA Cell 1 Membrane Electrode Assembly]
    A4 --> A5[Bipolar Plate 2 Cathode Flow Field]
    A5 --> A6[MEA Cell 2]
    A6 --> A7[Bipolar Plate 3]
    A7 --> A8[MEA Cell N]
    A8 --> A9[Bipolar Plate N Cathode]
    A9 --> A10[Current Collector Cathode]
    A10 --> A11[End Plate Cathode Side]
    A11 --> A12[Tie Rods Compression Bolts]
    A12 --> A13[Manifold Hydrogen Inlet]
    A12 --> A14[Manifold Air Inlet]
    A12 --> A15[Manifold Coolant Inlet]
    A12 --> A16[Manifold Product Water Outlet]

    B1[Electrical Series Connection] -.-> A3
    B1 -.-> A5
    B1 -.-> A7
    B1 -.-> A9

    C1[Fluidic Parallel Distribution] -.-> A13
    C1 -.-> A14
    C1 -.-> A15
    C1 -.-> A16
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stack Configuration Topologies

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Series Stack Configuration] --> T1a[Voltage Adds: N times Vcell]
    T1 --> T1b[Current Constant: Icell]
    T1 --> T1c[Application: High Voltage Low Current]

    T2[Parallel Stack Configuration] --> T2a[Current Adds: N times Icell]
    T2 --> T2b[Voltage Constant: Vcell]
    T2 --> T2c[Application: Low Voltage High Current]

    T3[Series Parallel Hybrid Stack] --> T3a[Multiple Strings in Parallel]
    T3 --> T3b[Each String Has Ns Cells in Series]
    T3 --> T3c[Application: Megawatt Stationary Power]

    T4[Segmented Stack Architecture] --> T4a[Independent Cell Monitoring]
    T4 --> T4b[Cell Voltage Telemetry]
    T4 --> T4c[Application: R and D Prototypes]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Manifold Configuration Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
```
