# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a fuel cell stack. Why is a single fuel cell insufficient for practical applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fuel cell stack** is a series of individual fuel cells connected electrically in series (and sometimes fluidically in parallel) to deliver practically useful voltage, current, and power. A single PEMFC unit cell produces only 0.6 V to 0.8 V under load, which is far below the **12 V, 24 V, 48 V, or 200–650 V** required for commercial applications like automobiles, backup power, and grid systems. Additionally, the current and power output of a single cell is too low. Stacking N cells in series raises the voltage N-fold, and increasing the active area raises the current, making the stack commercially viable.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a bipolar plate? List any four of its functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **bipolar plate (BPP)** is a critical stack component placed between two adjacent MEAs in a fuel cell stack. It serves **four primary functions:**
1. **Electrical conduction** — carries current from one cell's anode to the next cell's cathode (series connection)
2. **Reactant distribution** — its flow field channels distribute hydrogen and air uniformly over the active area
3. **Product water removal** — flow field design facilitates water evacuation
4. **Thermal management** — coolant channels embedded in the BPP remove waste heat
5. **Mechanical support** — provides structural rigidity to the stack
6. **Gas separation** — prevents mixing of fuel and oxidant streams

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Question A(a): [7 Marks]**  
Explain the different types of **fuel cell stack configurations** (series, parallel, and hybrid) with suitable diagrams. Compare their voltage and current characteristics.

**Model Solution:**

A fuel cell stack configuration defines how multiple unit cells are interconnected. The three primary configurations are:

**1. Series Configuration:**
- All N cells connected anode-to-cathode in a chain
- **Voltage adds:** V_stack = N × V_cell
- **Current remains constant:** I_stack = I_cell
- **Application:** High-voltage, low-current systems (EVs, residential power)
- **Disadvantage:** Single weak cell reduces entire stack performance

**2. Parallel Configuration:**
- All N cells have anodes tied together and cathodes tied together
- **Current adds:** I_stack = N × I_cell
- **Voltage remains constant:** V_stack = V_cell
- **Application:** Low-voltage, high-current systems (welding, electroplating)
- **Disadvantage:** Requires uniform cell pressure and humidification

**3. Hybrid (Series-Parallel) Configuration:**
- Multiple strings of Ns cells in series, with Np such strings in parallel
- **V_stack = Ns × V_cell**
- **I_stack = Np × I_cell**
- **P_stack = Ns × Np × P_cell**
- **Application:** Megawatt-scale stationary power plants
- **Advantage:** Redundancy; failure of one string does not collapse the entire system

> **Marker Allocation:** 2 marks for series + 2 for parallel + 2 for hybrid + 1 for comparison table

---

**Question A(b): [7 Marks]**  
A PEMFC stack consists of **80 cells in series**, each with an active area of **250 cm²**. At an operating current density of **0.5 A/cm²**, the average cell voltage is **0.68 V**. Calculate:
- (i) The stack current
- (ii) The stack voltage
- (iii) The stack power output
- (iv) The volumetric power density if the cell pitch is 3.5 mm
- (v) The gravimetric power density if the total stack mass is 25 kg

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stack current:**

$$I_{stack} = i \cdot A_{cell} = 0.5 \, \frac{A}{cm^2} \times 250 \, cm^2 = 125 \, A$$

**(ii) Stack voltage:**

$$V_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} = 80 \times 0.68 \, V = 54.4 \, V$$

**(iii) Stack power:**

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack} = 54.4 \, V \times 125 \, A = 6800 \, W = 6.8 \, kW$$

**(iv) Volumetric power density:**

$$V_{stack,volume} = N \cdot A_{cell} \cdot d_{cell} = 80 \times 250 \, cm^2 \times 0.35 \, cm = 7000 \, cm^3 = 7 \, L$$

$$\rho_{power,volume} = \frac{P_{stack}}{V_{stack,volume}} = \frac{6800 \, W}{7 \, L} \approx 971.4 \, W/L$$

**(v) Gravimetric power density:**

$$\rho_{power,mass} = \frac{P_{stack}}{m_{stack}} = \frac{6800 \, W}{25 \, kg} = 272 \, W/kg$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- I_stack = 125 A
- V_stack = 54.4 V
- P_stack = 6.8 kW
- Volumetric power density ≈ 971.4 W/L
- Gravimetric power density = 272 W/kg

> **Marker Allocation:** 1.5 marks each for (i)–(iv) and 1 mark for (v)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Question B(a): [7 Marks]**  
Discuss the various **manifold designs** used in fuel cell stacks. Compare external, internal, and hybrid manifold configurations in terms of pressure drop, sealing complexity, and application.

**Model Solution:**

A **manifold** is the fluidic distribution system that delivers reactants (hydrogen and air) to each cell and removes products (water) and waste heat (via coolant). The three primary manifold designs are:

**1. External Manifold:**
- Manifold tubes run along the outside of the stack
- Each cell receives gas via external port connections
- **Advantages:** Simple design, easy assembly, no flow field modification
- **Disadvantages:** High pressure drop (long flow path), non-uniform reactant distribution, large dead volume
- **Application:** Small laboratory stacks, low-power portable systems

**2. Internal Manifold:**
- Manifold channels are machined directly into the bipolar plates
- Reactants flow through internal ports that span the entire stack
- **Advantages:** Compact design, low pressure drop, uniform distribution, stack is self-contained
- **Disadvantages:** Complex BPP machining, difficult sealing, higher manufacturing cost
- **Application:** Automotive stacks (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO), high-performance systems

**3. Hybrid Manifold:**
- A combination of external and internal designs
- Typically external for one reactant and internal for the other
- **Advantages:** Balance between simplicity and performance
- **Disadvantages:** Asymmetric design requires careful flow balancing
- **Application:** Stationary power, mid-range applications

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | External | Internal | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Drop | High | Low | Medium |
| Sealing Complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Manufacturing Cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Distribution Uniformity | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Application | Lab stacks | Automotive | Stationary |

> **Marker Allocation:** 2 marks each for the three types + 1 mark for comparison

---

**Question B(b): [7 Marks]**  
Explain the **voltage losses** in a fuel cell stack. Discuss the contribution of each loss and the methods to mitigate them.

**Model Solution:**

The voltage losses in a fuel cell stack arise from irreversibilities in the electrochemical and transport processes. The cell voltage drops below the Nernst potential according to:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

**1. Activation Loss (η_act):**
- **Origin:** Sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode
- **Magnitude:** 0.05 V – 0.15 V (dominant at low current density)
- **Mitigation:** Use of highly active Pt-based catalysts, Pt-alloy catalysts, increased Pt loading, elevated temperature, higher reactant pressure

**2. Ohmic Loss (η_ohmic):**
- **Origin:** Resistance to proton flow through the membrane, electronic resistance of BPP and GDL, and contact resistance at interfaces
- **Magnitude:** 0.01 V – 0.05 V (linear with current)
- **Mitigation:** Thinner membranes (e.g., 8–12 μm Nafion), higher conductivity membranes, metallic BPPs (Ti, stainless steel), optimal compression pressure (0.5–1.5 MPa)

**3. Concentration Loss (η_conc):**
- **Origin:** Mass transport limitation of reactants to the catalyst sites at high current density
- **Magnitude:** 0.01 V – 0.10 V (dominant at high current density)
- **Mitigation:** Improved flow field design (serpentine, interdigitated), better GDL materials (carbon paper, carbon cloth), higher reactant stoichiometry, operation at higher pressure

**4. Fuel Crossover Loss:**
- **Origin:** Small amount of H₂ diffuses through the membrane and reacts directly with O₂, producing no useful current
- **Mitigation:** Dense membranes (low crossover), reinforced membranes, operating at low pressure differential

**5. Internal Current Loss:**
- **Origin:** Small electronic current flows through the membrane
- **Mitigation:** High membrane resistance, proper electrical isolation

**Stack-Level Effect:** These losses multiply across N cells. Therefore, **stack voltage efficiency decreases as the number of cells increases** if cell-level losses are not minimized. For a 100-cell stack, even a 0.05 V ohmic loss per cell translates to 5 V total loss.

> **Marker Allocation:** 1 mark per loss (5 losses × 1 mark) + 2 marks for stack-level implications

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in Stack Configuration questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply losses by N** — A 0.05 V/cell ohmic loss becomes 5 V in a 100-cell stack. Always scale losses by N.
> 2. **Confusing current and voltage in series vs parallel** — Series: voltages add, current constant. Parallel: currents add, voltage constant. This is the #1 valuation trap.
> 3. **Ignoring manifold pressure drop** — Pressure drop in the manifold is a critical stack design parameter. Internal manifolds have 5–10× lower pressure drop than external.
> 4. **Forgetting contact resistance** — The BPP-GDL contact contributes 30–50% of the total ohmic loss in poorly compressed stacks. Always account for compression.
> 5. **Using open-circuit voltage for power calculations** — The OCV is 1.0 V–1.2 V, but the operating voltage under load is 0.6 V–0.7 V. Always use the loaded voltage for power calculations.
> 6. **Confusing power density units** — W/cm² refers to active area; W/L is volumetric; W/kg is gravimetric. Mixing them up will cost 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Skipping unit conversion** — Pressure in atm vs Pa; area in cm² vs m². Always state units and convert consistently.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — STACK CONFIGURATION**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Fuel cell stack:** Series/parallel combination of N unit cells
- ☐ **Unit cell voltage:** 0.6 V – 0.8 V under load
- ☐ **Stack voltage:** V_stack = N × V_cell (series)
- ☐ **Stack current:** I_stack = I_cell (series)
- ☐ **Stack power:** P_stack = V_stack × I_stack

### Key Components
- ☐ **MEA** = Membrane + Catalyst + GDL (anode and cathode)
- ☐ **Bipolar Plate (BPP)** = current conductor + flow field + structural support
- ☐ **Gaskets** = sealing at MEA periphery
- ☐ **End plates** = compression distribution
- ☐ **Tie rods** = apply 0.5–1.5 MPa uniform compression
- ☐ **Manifolds** = fluidic distribution (external, internal, hybrid)

### Configuration Types
- ☐ **Series:** High V, constant I — for EVs, stationary
- ☐ **Parallel:** Constant V, high I — for specialty applications
- ☐ **Hybrid:** Ns series × Np parallel — for MW power plants

### Loss Mechanisms
- ☐ **Activation (η_act):** Tafel behavior, dominant at low i
- ☐ **Ohmic (η_ohmic):** Linear in i, dominant at medium i
- ☐ **Concentration (η_conc):** Dominant at high i
- ☐ **Crossover + Internal current:** Constant small losses

### Performance Metrics
- ☐ **Power density (areal):** 0.5–1.0 W/cm² (automotive)
- ☐ **Power density (volumetric):** > 3 kW/L (modern automotive)
- ☐ **Power density (gravimetric):** > 2 kW/kg (aerospace)
- ☐ **Voltage uniformity CV_V < 5%** (healthy stack)

### Manifold Design
- ☐ **External:** Simple, high ΔP, used for small stacks
- ☐ **Internal:** Compact, low ΔP, used for automotive
- ☐ **Hybrid:** Compromise, used for stationary

### Critical Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- ☐ **F** = 96,485 C/mol
- ☐ **E⁰_H₂O(l)** = 1.229 V (standard PEMFC potential)
- ☐ **Operating V_cell** = 0.6 V – 0.7 V
- ☐ **P_comp,opt** = 0.5–1.5 MPa (PEMFC), 2–5 MPa (SOFC)
- ☐ **Operating T_cell** = 60–80°C (PEMFC), 600–1000°C (SOFC)

### Engineering Trade-offs
- ☐ More cells → higher voltage BUT higher ΔP and cost
- ☐ Larger area → higher current BUT harder uniform distribution
- ☐ Higher compression → lower contact resistance BUT risk of membrane damage
- ☐ Internal manifold → lower ΔP BUT higher sealing complexity

### Typical Applications
- ☐ **Toyota Mirai:** ~370 cells, ~250 kW, ~650 V
- ☐ **Hyundai NEXO:** ~100 kW, ~120 cells
- ☐ **Bloom Energy SOFC:** ~hundreds of cells, 50–250 kW per module
- ☐ **Telecom backup:** 20–80 cells, 24–48 V, 1–10 kW
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniform Distribution of Reactants
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uniform Distribution of Reactants

# ⚙️ Module 4 — Uniform Distribution of Reactants

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniform Distribution of Reactants** refers to the spatial and temporal homogeneity of reactant (fuel and oxidant) concentrations, velocities, and mass fluxes across the entire active area of a fuel cell. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is defined as the engineered condition under which the local current density $j(x,y)$ at any coordinate $(x,y)$ on the Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) remains within $\pm \mathbf{5\%}$ to $\pm \mathbf{10\%}$ of the mean current density $\bar{j}$, ensuring maximum utilization of the catalyst surface, minimal hot spots, and prevention of localized starvation.

Mathematically, the **Uniformity Index (UI)** is expressed as:

$$
UI = 1 - \frac{1}{\bar{j}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{A}\int_{A} \left[ j(x,y) - \bar{j} \right]^{2}\, dA}
$$

A perfectly uniform distribution corresponds to $UI = 1$, while severe maldistribution drives $UI \rightarrow 0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Multi-Tap Showerhead:**
> Imagine a 6-storey building where water is pumped from a ground tank to the rooftop and distributed through horizontal pipes feeding **six taps** on each floor. If the inlet pipe is too narrow, the ground-floor tap will gush violently while the top-floor tap will merely trickle. This is exactly what happens in a fuel cell: the reactant manifold acts like the vertical pipe, and each channel branch acts like a tap. To get **uniform reactant supply**, we must carefully size the manifold, balance pressure drops, and design channels of equal hydraulic resistance — otherwise some regions of the MEA "starve" while others are "flooded."

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric Ratio ($\xi$)** | $\mathbf{1.0}$ to $\mathbf{3.0}$ | Ratio of supplied to consumed reactant |
| **Active Area Uniformity** | $\mathbf{\geq 95\%}$ | Industry benchmark for automotive PEMFCs |
| **Pressure Drop across Cell** | $\mathbf{10 \text{ kPa}}$ to $\mathbf{50 \text{ kPa}}$ | Parasitic load on compressor |
| **Channel Reynolds Number ($Re$)** | $\mathbf{50 < Re < 1000}$ | Laminar-turbulent transition boundary |
| **Volumetric Current Density** | $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2}$ to $\mathbf{2.0 \text{ A/cm}^2}$ | Operational target for automotive stacks |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Fuel Cell Engineering

In Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cells (**PEMFC**), Direct Methanol Fuel Cells (**DMFC**), and Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (**SOFC**), non-uniform reactant distribution causes:
- Localized **membrane drying** (high current regions)
- **Flooding** of cathode GDL (low flow regions)
- Catalyst **degradation hotspots**
- Reduced overall **cell efficiency** by up to $\mathbf{25\%}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

Achieving uniform reactant distribution involves the **synergistic interplay** of five engineering layers, each of which KTU examiners frequently test:

- **Layer 1 — Manifold Design (Inlet/Outlet Headers)**
  - *Why:* The manifold feeds/collects gas from multiple parallel channels. Non-uniform manifold cross-section creates flow bias.
  - *How:* Use **Z-type**, **U-type**, or **multi-pass** manifold geometries with calibrated header diameters so that the **pressure drop along the manifold ≈ pressure drop across one channel**.

- **Layer 2 — Flow Field Plate Geometry**
  - *Why:* Channels guide the gas across the active area.
  - *How:* Choose between **serpentine**, **parallel**, **interdigitated**, or **pin-type** designs (see Section 4 for visual comparison).

- **Layer 3 — Channel Hydraulic Diameter Optimization**
  - *Why:* Hydraulic diameter $D_h$ controls the flow regime and pressure drop.
  - *How:* Sized so that $Re$ falls in the desired laminar range and the velocity $v$ delivers enough mass flux without choking the membrane.

- **Layer 4 — Diffusion Layer (GDL/PTL) Transport**
  - *Why:* Even with perfect channel flow, reactant must diffuse through porous GDL to the catalyst.
  - *How:* Set porosity $\varepsilon$ between $\mathbf{0.4}$ and $\mathbf{0.7}$ and tune the through-plane permeability.

- **Layer 5 — Electrochemical Coupling**
  - *Why:* Local current density depends on local concentration via **Butler–Volmer kinetics**.
  - *How:* Maintain $C_{R}(x,y) \geq 0.3 \, C_{R,\text{ref}}$ to keep polarization losses within $\mathbf{50 \text{ mV}}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for this topic. All $\vert$ symbols use the LaTeX vertical bar to remain markdown-safe.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle UI = 1 - \frac{1}{\bar{j}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{A}\int \left[ j - \bar{j} \right]^{2} dA}$ | Uniformity Index | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\displaystyle Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\displaystyle D_h = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w}$ | Hydraulic Diameter | m |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \Delta P = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D_h^{2}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille Pressure Drop | Pa |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \Delta P = f \, \frac{L}{D_h} \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach Pressure Drop | Pa |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \xi = \frac{\dot{N}_{\text{supplied}}}{\dot{N}_{\text{consumed}}}$ | Stoichiometric Flow Ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \dot{N} = \frac{I \, \xi}{n \, F}$ | Required Molar Flow Rate | mol/s |
| 8 | $\displaystyle j(x) = j_0 \left( \frac{C_R(x)}{C_{R,\text{ref}}} \right)^{\gamma} \left[ e^{\alpha_a F \eta / RT} - e^{-\alpha_c F \eta / RT} \right]$ | Local Current Density | A/m² |
| 9 | $\displaystyle Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.33}$ | Sherwood Number Correlation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\displaystyle k_m = \frac{Sh \, D}{D_h}$ | Mass Transfer Coefficient | m/s |
| 11 | $\displaystyle \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$ | Continuity Equation | — |
| 12 | $\displaystyle \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v}$ | Navier–Stokes (Momentum) | Pa/m |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This framework is the foundation for designing fuel cells in:
- **Automotive traction stacks** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) — requires $< \mathbf{2\%}$ flow maldistribution over $\mathbf{300+}$ cells.
- **Aerospace auxiliary power units (APUs)** — extreme altitude demands uniform oxidant delivery at low pressure.
- **Stationary combined-heat-and-power (CHP)** — long-duration operation demands uniform reactant access to prevent catalyst sintering hotspots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Single Serpentine Channel

We begin with the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for fully-developed laminar flow in a rectangular micro-channel of width $w$, depth $h$, and length $L$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Hydraulic Diameter for a Rectangular Cross-Section**
For a rectangular channel, the cross-sectional area is $A_c = w \cdot h$ and the wetted perimeter is $P_w = 2(w + h)$.

$$
D_h = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w} = \frac{4 \, w \, h}{2(w + h)} = \frac{2 \, w \, h}{w + h}
$$

*Logic:* The factor 4 is the standard conversion from circular pipe theory, allowing non-circular ducts to be analyzed using the same dimensionless correlations.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation**
For laminar flow ($Re < 2300$) in a circular pipe of diameter $D$, the pressure drop is:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{circular}} = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D^{2}}
$$

Replacing $D$ with $D_h$:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{channel}} = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D_h^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Shape Correction Factor**
Micro-channels in fuel cell plates are not circular. We multiply by a constant $K_{\text{shape}}$ to correct for rectangular geometry:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{channel}} = \frac{K_{\text{shape}} \, \mu \, L \, v}{D_h^{2}}
$$

For a square channel ($w = h$), $K_{\text{shape}} \approx 56.92$; for a wide shallow channel ($w \gg h$), $K_{\text{shape}} \approx 96$.

**Step 4 — Express Velocity in Terms of Volumetric Flow Rate**
If the total volumetric flow rate entering the cell is $Q$ distributed over $N_c$ parallel channels:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{N_c \, w \, h}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{channel}} = \frac{K_{\text{shape}} \, \mu \, L \, Q}{N_c \, w \, h \, D_h^{2}}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for single-channel pressure drop analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass Flow Rate Required for a Given Current

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis**
The molar consumption rate of hydrogen is related to current by Faraday's constant $F = \mathbf{96485 \text{ C/mol}}$ and electron count $n$:

$$
\dot{N}_{\text{consumed}} = \frac{I}{n \, F}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stoichiometric Excess Factor**
Real systems operate at $\xi > 1$ to ensure sufficient reactant at the channel outlet (worst-case location):

$$
\dot{N}_{\text{supplied}} = \xi \cdot \dot{N}_{\text{consumed}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a Single Working Equation**

$$
\boxed{\dot{N}_{\text{supplied}} = \frac{\xi \, I}{n \, F}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Standard)**
For $I = \mathbf{100 \text{ A}}$, $\xi = 1.5$, and $n = 2$ (hydrogen oxidation):

$$
\dot{N}_{\text{supplied}} = \frac{1.5 \times 100}{2 \times 96485} = 7.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

At standard temperature and pressure ($\mathbf{1 \text{ atm}}$, $\mathbf{298 \text{ K}}$), using ideal gas law $V = \dot{N} R T / P$:

$$
\dot{V} = 7.77 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 298 / 101325 = 1.90 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = \mathbf{1.14 \text{ L/min}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current Density Distribution Coupled with Mass Transfer

The local current density at a point $(x,y)$ on the MEA depends on local reactant concentration $C_R(x,y)$.

**Step 1 — Butler–Volmer Kinetic Expression**
Assuming symmetry and small overpotential, the kinetic current density is:

$$
j(x) = j_0 \left[ \frac{C_R(x)}{C_{R,\text{ref}}} \right]^{\gamma} \frac{\alpha_a + \alpha_c}{RT} F \, \eta
$$

where $\gamma$ is the reaction order (typically $\mathbf{0.5}$ to $\mathbf{1.0}$).

**Step 2 — Mass Transfer Limitation via Sherwood Number**
In a flow channel, the limiting current density is:

$$
j_{\text{lim}} = n \, F \, k_m \, C_{R,\text{ref}}
$$

Substituting $k_m = Sh \, D / D_h$:

$$
j_{\text{lim}} = \frac{n \, F \, Sh \, D \, C_{R,\text{ref}}}{D_h}
$$

**Step 3 — Coupling via Reactant Depletion Along the Channel**
For a 1-D channel with co-flow arrangement, the reactant concentration decays as:

$$
C_R(x) = C_{R,\text{in}} \, \exp\left( -\frac{k_m \, P_{\text{channel}}}{v \, h} \, x \right)
$$

where $P_{\text{channel}} = 2(w + h)$ is the channel perimeter available for diffusion.

**Step 4 — Final Coupled Current Distribution**

$$
\boxed{j(x) = j_{\text{lim}} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{k_m \, P_{\text{channel}}}{v \, h} \, x \right) \right]}
$$

This equation shows why **uniform reactant distribution** requires:
- High velocity $v$ → minimizes decay.
- High $k_m$ → enhances mass transfer.
- Short channel length $L$ → reduces cumulative depletion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs

| Design Parameter | Increases Uniformity? | Penalty / Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| Higher stoichiometry ($\xi \uparrow$) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Higher parasitic blower load |
| More parallel channels ($N_c \uparrow$) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Higher pressure drop across manifold |
| Smaller channel width ($w \downarrow$) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Risk of water flooding |
| Interdigitated flow field | ✅ Yes | ❌ Higher pressure drop, complex manufacturing |
| Higher GDL porosity ($\varepsilon \uparrow$) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Reduced mechanical strength |
| Lower current density operation | ✅ Yes | ❌ Lower power density |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Reactant Distribution System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Tank H2 Supply] --> A2[Pressure Regulator]
    A2 --> A3[Humidifier]
    A3 --> A4[Inlet Manifold Header]
    A4 --> B1[Parallel Channel 1]
    A4 --> B2[Parallel Channel 2]
    A4 --> B3[Parallel Channel N]
    B1 --> C1[MEA Region 1]

    B3 --> C3[MEA Region N]
    C1 --> D1[Local Current Density j1]
    C2 --> D2[Local Current Density j2]
    C3 --> D3[Local Current Density jN]
    D1 --> E[Uniformity Index Calculation]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1{UI greater than 0.95?}
    F1 -->|Yes| G1[Acceptable Distribution]
    F1 -->|No| H1[Recalibrate Manifold]
    H1 --> A4
    G1 --> I1[Stack Performance Optimized]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram of Flow Field Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FF1[Flow Field Geometry Comparison]
        node1[Serpentine Channel] --> node2[Single Continuous Path]
        node3[Parallel Channel] --> node4[Multiple Straight Paths]

        node7[Pin Type] --> node8[Array of Cylindrical Obstacles]
    end
    subgraph FF2[Uniformity Performance]
        node9[Highest Pressure Drop] --> node10[Serpentine]

    end
```

### 4.3 Manifold Pressure Profile Schematic

```mermaid

    P2 --> P3[Header Section 2]

    P2 -.Pressure Drop Along Header.-> P3
    P3 -.Pressure Drop Along Header.-> P4
    nodeQ1[Channel 1 receives Pressure P1] --> nodeQ2[Highest Local Pressure]
    nodeQ3[Channel N receives Pressure PN] --> nodeQ4[Lowest Local Pressure]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Inlet Disturbance to Uniform Output

