**(a)** A 50-cell PEM fuel cell stack operates at a current of 250 A and 0.65 V per cell. The H₂ supply stoichiometry is 1.15 and the air supply stoichiometry is 2.0. Assuming pure H₂ and air (21% O₂) feeds, standard conditions, and LHV for H₂ = 241.83 kJ/mol:

- (i) Calculate the H₂ and air volumetric flow rates at STP. (4 marks)
- (ii) Compute the stack efficiency (LHV basis) and the gross electrical power output. (3 marks)

**(b)** Design the thermal management and water recovery subsystem for the above stack:
- (iii) Calculate the heat generation rate in kW that must be removed by the cooling system. (3 marks)
- (iv) Calculate the rate of water production at the cathode in kg/h and discuss whether a closed-loop humidification is feasible. (4 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a)(i) H₂ and air volumetric flow rates:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 250 \text{ A}, \quad n = 2, \quad F = 96485 \text{ C/mol} \\
\dot{n}_{H_2,\,stoich} &= \dfrac{I}{2F} = \dfrac{250}{2 \cdot 96485} = 1.296 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s} \\
\dot{n}_{H_2,\,actual} &= \zeta_{H_2} \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2,\,stoich} = 1.15 \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.490 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to volumetric flow at STP using $V_m = 22.414 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/mol:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{H_2} &= 1.490 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 22.414 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 3.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 2.00 \text{ L/min at STP}
\end{aligned}$$

For air:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{air,\,stoich} &= \dfrac{I}{4F \cdot 0.2095} = \dfrac{250}{4 \cdot 96485 \cdot 0.2095} \\
&= 3.092 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s} \\
\dot{n}_{air,\,actual} &= 2.0 \cdot 3.092 \times 10^{-3} = 6.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s} \\
\dot{V}_{air} &= 6.184 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 22.414 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.386 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 8.32 \text{ L/min at STP}
\end{aligned}$$

**(a)(ii) Stack efficiency and power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{stack} &= N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 50 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 250 = 8125 \text{ W} = 8.125 \text{ kW} \\
\eta_{stack,\,LHV} &= \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{LHV}/nF \cdot F} = \dfrac{0.65 \cdot 2F}{241830} \\
&= \dfrac{0.65 \cdot 2 \cdot 96485}{241830} = \dfrac{125430.5}{241830} \\
&\approx 0.5186 = 51.86\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(b)(iii) Heat generation rate:**

Using $\Delta H_{LHV} = 241.83$ kJ/mol:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} &= \dot{n}_{H_2,\,actual} \cdot \Delta H - P_{stack} \\
&= 1.490 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 241830 - 8125 \\
&= 360.33 - 8125
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — that gives a negative value, which is incorrect. **Correction:** heat generation should be computed using the **stoichiometric** H₂ consumption (chemical energy input), not the supplied amount (which is the same since the unreacted H₂ is recirculated/purged):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} &= \dot{n}_{H_2,\,stoich} \cdot \Delta H - P_{stack} \\
&= 1.296 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 241830 - 8125 \\
&= 313.41 - 8125
\end{aligned}$$

This is still negative. **Correction:** The correct formula uses HHV (1.481 V equivalent) since that's the reference:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} &= I \cdot (1.481 - N \cdot V_{cell}/N) \quad \text{[per cell basis, simpler]} \\
&= I \cdot (1.481 - V_{cell}) \\
&= 250 \cdot (1.481 - 0.65) \\
&= 250 \cdot 0.831 = 207.75 \text{ W per cell} \\
\dot{Q}_{stack} &= 50 \cdot 207.75 = 10387.5 \text{ W} \approx 10.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the heat load on the cooling system. **(Final answer: ~10.4 kW)**

**(b)(iv) Water production rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{H_2O} &= \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2O}}{2F} = \dfrac{250 \cdot 18}{2 \cdot 96485} \\
&= \dfrac{4500}{192970} = 2.332 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \\
&= 83.95 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Closed-loop feasibility:** With ~84 kg/h of product water and typical humidifier demand of ~0.5–1.0 kg/h per kW of stack power, the produced water far exceeds humidification needs. A **closed-loop water recovery system** is highly feasible and economically justified; surplus water can be drained or used for external purposes (e.g., cooling make-up).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Pure H₂/O₂ System and Nernst Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Question]**

**(a)** A 100-cell PEM fuel cell operates on pure H₂ and pure O₂ at 1.5 bar (absolute) and 75 °C. The partial pressures at the electrode inlets are: $p_{H_2} = 1.4$ bar, $p_{O_2} = 1.4$ bar. Given $T = 348.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $n = 2$:

- (i) Compute the **Nernst open-circuit voltage** per cell. (4 marks)
- (ii) The cell shows activation overpotential $\eta_{act} = 0.05$ V, ohmic overpotential $\eta_{ohm} = 0.08$ V, and concentration overpotential $\eta_{conc} = 0.03$ V at a current density of 0.5 A/cm². Calculate the operating cell voltage and the stack voltage. (3 marks)

**(b)** Compare air-fed and pure-O₂ operation:
- (iii) Quantify the **Nernst voltage penalty** when switching from pure O₂ to air (21%) at the same total pressure of 1.5 bar. (3 marks)
- (iv) Discuss three practical implications of this penalty on system design. (4 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a)(i) Nernst voltage (H₂/O₂ cell):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{Nernst} &= E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

At $T = 348.15$ K, $E^0 = 1.229$ V (use the simplified form for 1 atm; for partial-pressure correction, we use the ratio):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{H_2} &= 1.4 \text{ bar} = 1.382 \text{ atm} \\
p_{O_2} &= 1.4 \text{ bar} = 1.382 \text{ atm} \\
\dfrac{RT}{nF} &= \dfrac{8.314 \cdot 348.15}{2 \cdot 96485} = 0.01500 \text{ V} \\
\ln(1.382 \cdot 1.382^{1/2}) &= \ln(1.382^{1.5}) = 1.5 \cdot \ln(1.382) \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 0.3234 = 0.4851 \\
E_{Nernst} &= 1.229 + 0.01500 \cdot 0.4851 \\
&= 1.229 + 0.00728 = 1.2363 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**(a)(ii) Operating cell voltage and stack voltage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc} \\
&= 1.2363 - 0.05 - 0.08 - 0.03 \\
&= 1.0763 \text{ V} \\
V_{stack} &= N \cdot E_{cell} = 100 \cdot 1.0763 = 107.63 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b)(iii) Nernst penalty for air vs. pure O₂:**

The ratio of $p_{O_2,\,air}$ to $p_{O_2,\,pure}$ at the same total pressure:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{O_2,\,pure} &= 1.4 \text{ bar} \\
p_{O_2,\,air} &= 0.2095 \cdot 1.5 = 0.3143 \text{ bar} \\
\Delta E_{Nernst} &= \dfrac{RT}{nF} \cdot \ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2,\,air}}{p_{O_2,\,pure}}\right)^{1/2}\right] \\
&= 0.01500 \cdot \ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{0.3143}{1.4}\right)^{1/2}\right] \\
&= 0.01500 \cdot \ln(0.4735) \\
&= 0.01500 \cdot (-0.7478) \\
&= -0.01122 \text{ V} \approx -11.2 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Penalty: ~11 mV per cell** (at higher current densities, this penalty effectively increases due to mass-transport effects).

**(b)(iv) Practical implications:**

1. **Increased Stack Size** — To compensate for the ~11 mV penalty per cell, additional cells (or larger active area) are needed for the same power, increasing stack cost and volume by ~1.5–3% (1.3 marks).
2. **Lower Peak Efficiency** — A 11 mV drop on a 0.7 V operating cell reduces efficiency by $11/700 \approx 1.6\%$; in HHV terms, this is ~1 percentage point of system efficiency (1.3 marks).
3. **Need for Air Pressurization or Stoichiometry Excess** — To recover the lost O₂ partial pressure, designers increase cathode pressure (parasitic compressor load) or use higher stoichiometry (more air flow → more water-management burden and blower power). This reduces overall system efficiency by 3–5% (1.4 marks).

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Questions**
> 1. **Stoichiometry Factor Confusion** — Students often forget to apply ζ (and confuse ζ_{H₂} with ζ_{air}). Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 2. **Air Composition Error** — Using 0.21 instead of 0.2095 (or 1/4.76 instead of 1/0.2095) in molar air flow. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **HHV vs. LHV** — Use LHV (1.229 V) for fuel cell theoretical voltage; HHV (1.481 V) for thermal/heat calculations. Confusing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Heat-Generation Sign Error** — Some students compute $\dot{Q} = V \cdot I - \dot{n}\Delta H$ and get negative values. The correct expression is $\dot{Q} = \dot{n}\Delta H - V_{stack}I$ (or use the voltage-deficit method: $\dot{Q} = I \cdot (1.481 - V_{cell})$ per cell). Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 5. **BoP Component Justification** — Just naming "compressor, humidifier, radiator" without stating **why** each is needed loses 0.5–1 mark per component. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the *function*, not just the *name*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Stoichiometric core reaction:** 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O; n = 2 electrons per H₂; O₂ need is 1 mol per 2 mol H₂.
- [ ] **Air contains 21% O₂** (or 0.2095), so 1 mol O₂ needs 4.76 mol air.
- [ ] **Faraday's Law**: $\dot{n} = I/(nF)$ is the *minimum* H₂ flow; multiply by ζ to get actual flow.
- [ ] **LHV-based ideal voltage** = 1.229 V; **HHV-based** = 1.481 V.
- [ ] **Six BoP subsystems** must all be designed: H₂ supply, O₂/air supply, humidification, thermal, water, power/control.
- [ ] **PEMFC operating temperature** = 60–80 °C (low-temp PEM); SOFC = 650–850 °C.
- [ ] **Air stoichiometry** ζ ≈ 2.0 (higher than H₂ ζ ≈ 1.1) — to prevent flooding and ensure uniform O₂ distribution.
- [ ] **Heat generated** ≈ 40–50% of HHV input energy; must be actively removed.
- [ ] **Nernst penalty** for air vs. pure O₂ is ~10–15 mV at moderate pressures; grows with current density.
- [ ] **System efficiency** = η_{stack} × η_{BoP}; typical PEMFC system efficiency is 40–60% (LHV, peak).
- [ ] **BoP parasitic losses** typically 5–15% of stack output, dominated by compressor/blower power.
- [ ] **Humidification** is critical: too dry → membrane conductivity loss; too wet → electrode flooding.
- [ ] **Coolant** must be deionized (conductivity < 5 μS/cm) to prevent internal short-circuiting.
- [ ] **Water recovery** in a closed-loop design can supply 100% of humidification demand.
- [ ] **Cryogenic H₂/O₂ systems** (space, military) eliminate compressor and humidification, but add storage/boil-off complexity.
- [ ] **Toyota Mirai-class automotive** BoP cost ≈ 50% of total system cost; power density dominated by BoP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen-Air Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen-Air Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Hydrogen-Air System** in a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) is defined as the integrated **Balance of Plant (BOP)** assembly that manages the storage, conditioning, metering, and delivery of **hydrogen fuel** and **air (oxidant)** to the Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA), while simultaneously handling the by-products (water and heat) generated by the electrochemical reaction:

$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The Hydrogen-Air System is **not** just the supply lines — it is a *closed-loop* engineering subsystem that ensures the MEA always receives reactants at the correct **pressure**, **temperature**, **humidity**, and **stoichiometric ratio** to sustain efficient electrochemical conversion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lungs of a Fuel Cell"

Imagine the PEMFC stack as a **living human body**:
- **Hydrogen tank & regulator** = the **stomach** (stores and digests fuel slowly).
- **Air compressor/blower** = the **lungs** (inhales oxygen from the atmosphere).
- **Humidifier** = the **saliva glands** (moistens the air before it enters).
- **MEA** = the **heart** (where oxygen and food meet and energy is pumped out).
- **Coolant loop & radiator** = the **sweating skin** (removes excess heat).
- **Water separator & condenser** = the **kidneys** (expels liquid waste).
- **DC-DC converter** = the **nervous system** (regulates the power output).

Just as a human cannot run on an empty stomach or with blocked lungs, a fuel cell cannot perform without a precisely engineered Hydrogen-Air System. The system must be **responsive** (rapid start-up), **stable** (constant pressure), and **clean** (no contaminants poisoning the MEA).

### 1.3 Key System Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Standard Values to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - Standard temperature: **T₀ = 298.15 K (25 °C)**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Faraday's constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**
> - Air molar composition: **O₂ = 20.95 % (~21 %)**, **N₂ = 78.09 %**, **Ar = 0.93 %**
> - Molar mass of dry air: **M_air = 28.97 g/mol**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂: **ΔH_LHV = 241.83 kJ/mol** (water as vapor)
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂: **ΔH_HHV = 285.84 kJ/mol** (water as liquid)
> - Enthalpy of vaporization of water at 25 °C: **ΔH_vap = 44.01 kJ/mol**
> - Stoichiometric H₂ : O₂ ratio: **2 : 1 (molar)** → **1 : 0.5**

### 1.4 Primary Subsystems (Top-Level Classification)

| Subsystem | Core Function | Key Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrogen Subsystem** | Store, regulate, and meter H₂ flow | Tank, regulator, solenoid valve, mass-flow controller |
| **Air Subsystem** | Filter, compress, and meter air flow | Filter, compressor/blower, MFC, back-pressure valve |
| **Humidification Subsystem** | Maintain membrane conductivity | Bubbler, membrane humidifier, enthalpy wheel |
| **Thermal Management Subsystem** | Remove waste heat (40–60 % of LHV) | Cooling plates, radiator, coolant pump |
| **Water Management Subsystem** | Prevent flooding & drying | Separator, condenser, water trap |
| **Power Conditioning Unit (PCU)** | Convert DC to usable DC/AC | DC-DC converter, inverter |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrogen Subsystem — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The hydrogen side must deliver fuel at **controlled pressure** (typically 1.2–3.0 bar gauge for low-pressure PEMFC) and **stoichiometric flow** (λ_H₂ ≥ 1.0; practically 1.05–1.5 to avoid dead-ended starvation).

**Step 1 — Storage:** Hydrogen is stored either as:
- **Compressed gas (CGH₂)** at 350 bar or 700 bar (most common in light-duty vehicles).
- **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂)** at 20 K (cryogenic; long-haul trucks, aerospace).
- **Metal hydrides** (solid-state; low-pressure stationary backups).

**Step 2 — Pressure Regulation:** A two-stage regulator drops tank pressure (350–700 bar) → intermediate (10–15 bar) → anode inlet (1.2–3.0 bar).

**Step 3 — Flow Metering:** A **thermal mass-flow controller (MFC)** uses a bypass tube with heater and temperature sensors. The mass flow is derived from the temperature differential ΔT.

**Step 4 — Recirculation & Purge:** In dead-ended anode mode, nitrogen and water vapor accumulate in the anode, diluting the fuel. A **purge valve** (typically solenoid) periodically vents the anode stream, and a **recirculation pump/ejector** returns unreacted H₂ to improve utilization (typical H₂ utilization 90–99 %).

**Why each step matters:**
- Storage determines **energy density** (Wh/kg, Wh/L).
- Regulation prevents **MEA mechanical damage** from pressure spikes.
- Metering ensures **Faradaic efficiency**.
- Recirculation boosts **system efficiency** and reduces **venting losses**.

### 2.2 Air Subsystem — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Air is supplied at a **stoichiometric excess ratio (λ_air)** of **1.5–3.0** to:
1. Provide adequate oxygen partial pressure.
2. Sweep out product water from the cathode.
3. Avoid concentration polarization (mass-transport losses).

**Step 1 — Filtration:** A HEPA + activated-carbon filter removes particulates, hydrocarbons, and SO₂ (poisoning agents). The inlet must be **SOx-free, NOx-free, and CO-free** because Pt catalysts are highly sensitive.

**Step 2 — Compression:** A **compressor** (for pressurized systems > 2 bar) or **blower** (for atmospheric/low-pressure systems) raises air pressure. Compressor power is parasitic and reduces net system efficiency.

**Step 3 — Humidification:** Air must be humidified to **30–100 % RH** at stack temperature to maintain membrane hydration (proton conductivity requires water). Methods:
- **Bubbler humidifier** (simplest, low-cost).
- **Membrane humidifier** (uses Nafion tube-in-shell; more controllable).
- **Enthalpy wheel** (industrial scale).

**Step 4 — Flow Control & Back-Pressure:** A back-pressure valve at the cathode exit regulates stack pressure, balancing performance against parasitic load.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Stoichiometric Excess

If λ_air = 1.0, the entire air stream would be consumed → no convective water removal → **cathode flooding** → concentration losses → failure. Excess air (λ ≈ 2.0) sweeps liquid water out of the Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL) pores via shear drag, maintaining dry, open diffusion pathways.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for Hydrogen-Air System problems. Memorize the units and the assumptions.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot \lambda_{H_2}}{2F}$ | Hydrogen molar flow rate (per stack) | mol/s |
| 2 | $\dot{n}_{O_2,\,req} = \dfrac{I}{4F}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ demand (Faraday) | mol/s |
| 3 | $\dot{n}_{air} = \dfrac{\lambda_{air} \cdot I}{4F \cdot y_{O_2}}$ | Total air molar flow | mol/s |
| 4 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{air} \cdot R \cdot T_{air}}{P_{air}}$ | Volumetric air flow (ideal gas) | m³/s |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{n}_{air} \cdot M_{air}$ | Mass flow of air | kg/s |
| 6 | $E_{Nernst} = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Reversible cell voltage | V |
| 7 | $E^\circ_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229\,\text{V}$ (LHV) or 1.481 V (HHV) | Standard potential at 25 °C | V |
| 8 | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{\mu_{F}\cdot\Delta H_{LHV}}{\Delta H_{HHV}} \approx 83\,\%$ | Electrochemical utilization of LHV | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{cell}\cdot\eta_{BOP}\cdot\eta_{PCU}$ | Total system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{parasitic} = P_{comp} + P_{pump} + P_{fan} + P_{control}$ | Parasitic power loss | W |
| 11 | $Q_{gen} = I\bigl(E_{th}-V_{cell}\bigr)$ (per cell) | Heat generation rate | W |
| 12 | $\dot{Q}_{remove} = \dot{m}_{cool}\cdot c_{p,cool}\cdot\Delta T_{cool}$ | Heat removal by coolant | W |
| 13 | $\dot{V}_{H_2O} = \dfrac{I}{2F}\cdot M_{H_2O}$ | Product water generation | m³/s (or kg/s) |
| 14 | $\Delta V_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ (Tafel) | Activation overpotential | V |
| 15 | $\Delta V_{ohm} = i\cdot R_{ASR}$ (ASR = Area-Specific Resistance) | Ohmic overpotential | V |
| 16 | $\Delta V_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1-\dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential | V |
| 17 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \Delta V_{act} - \Delta V_{ohm} - \Delta V_{conc}$ | Cell polarization equation | V |
| 18 | $U_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2,\,consumed}}{\dot{n}_{H_2,\,supplied}} \times 100\%$ | Hydrogen utilization | % |
| 19 | $\rho_{H_2,\,700bar} \approx 40\,\text{g/L}$ | CGH₂ density at 700 bar | g/L |
| 20 | $E_{storage} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}\cdot\Delta H_{LHV}}{M_{H_2}}$ | Stored chemical energy | J |

> **Notation key:** *I* = stack current (A); *F* = 96,485 C/mol; *y_O₂* = 0.2095; *λ* = stoichiometric ratio; *μ_F* = fuel utilization; *α* = charge-transfer coefficient; *i₀* = exchange current density; *i_L* = limiting current density; *R_ASR* = area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO):** Hydrogen stored at 70 MPa; λ_air ≈ 2.0; air supplied by a screw-type centrifugal compressor driven by a high-speed electric motor.
- **Stationary (Bloom Energy, 200 kW SOFC):** Uses air at low pressure; integrates heat-recovery steam generation.
- **Aerospace (NASA, Boeing):** Cryogenic LH₂ + LOX feed; water is a drinking-water by-product.
- **Backup power (telecom towers):** Metal-hydride storage for silent, emission-free, indoor-safe operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a PEMFC Stack

**Given:** A PEMFC stack delivers $I = 240\,\text{A}$ of current, operating at $T = 353\,\text{K}$ ($80\,^\circ\text{C}$) and $P = 1.5$ bar (absolute), with air stoichiometric ratio $\lambda_{air} = 2.0$.

**Find:** (a) Molar flow rate of O₂ required by the electrochemical reaction. (b) Total molar flow rate of air. (c) Volumetric flow rate of air at stack inlet.

**Derivation — Part (a): O₂ demand from Faraday's Law**

The half-cell cathode reaction is:
$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Four electrons are transferred per mole of O₂. By Faraday's Law, the molar consumption rate of O₂ is:

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{n\,F}$$

where $n = 4$ electrons per O₂ molecule.

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{240\,\text{A}}{4 \times 96{,}485\,\text{C/mol}}$$

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{240}{385{,}940} = 6.218 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol/s}$$

**Conversion logic:** The numerator is current (coulombs per second); the denominator is the charge per mole of O₂ (coulombs per mole). The result is in mol/s.

**Derivation — Part (b): Total air molar flow**

Air contains $y_{O_2} = 0.2095$ mole fraction of oxygen. To supply the required O₂ at stoichiometric excess $\lambda_{air} = 2.0$:

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\lambda_{air}\cdot \dot{n}_{O_2}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{2.0 \times 6.218 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2095}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{1.2436 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2095} = 5.937 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/s}$$

**Derivation — Part (c): Volumetric air flow at stack inlet**

Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$:

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{\dot{n}_{air}\cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{5.937 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.314 \times 353}{1.5 \times 101{,}325\,\text{Pa}}$$

**Numerator:**
$$5.937 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.314 = 4.936 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$4.936 \times 10^{-2} \times 353 = 17.42$$

**Denominator:**
$$1.5 \times 101{,}325 = 151{,}987.5\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{17.42}{151{,}987.5} = 1.146 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{air} \approx 0.1146\,\text{L/s} = 6.876\,\text{L/min} = 412.6\,\text{L/h}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Voltage for Hydrogen-Air Cell (with Humid Air)

**Given:** A hydrogen-air PEMFC operates at $T = 353\,\text{K}$, $P = 1.5\,\text{bar}$. Anode: pure H₂ at 1.4 bar. Cathode: air at 1.4 bar with 50 % relative humidity. Find the open-circuit voltage $E_{Nernst}$.

**Step 1 — Determine partial pressures (Dalton's Law):**

The Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ reaction:
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Water vapor partial pressure at 353 K:**

From steam tables (or Antoine equation), saturation pressure of water at 80 °C is $P_{sat,H_2O} = 0.4736\,\text{bar}$. With 50 % RH:
$$P_{H_2O} = 0.50 \times 0.4736 = 0.2368\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Dry-air partial pressure on cathode side:**

$$P_{dry\,air} = P_{cathode} - P_{H_2O} = 1.4 - 0.2368 = 1.1632\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen partial pressure:**

$$P_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \times P_{dry\,air} = 0.2095 \times 1.1632 = 0.2437\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 5 — Plug into Nernst equation:**

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}\ln\!\left(\frac{1.4 \times 0.2437^{1/2}}{0.2368}\right)$$

**Calculate thermal voltage term:**
$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{192{,}970} = 0.01521\,\text{V}$$

**Calculate the log argument:**
$$\sqrt{0.2437} = 0.4937$$
$$1.4 \times 0.4937 = 0.6912$$
$$\frac{0.6912}{0.2368} = 2.919$$
$$\ln(2.919) = 1.0715$$

**Final E:**
$$E = 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 1.0715 = 1.229 + 0.0163$$

$$\boxed{E_{Nernst} \approx 1.245\,\text{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice the OCV (1.245 V) is *higher* than the standard 1.229 V because the partial pressure of water vapor (denominator) is *lower* than 1 bar, increasing the natural-log term. Pressurization and dryness of the cathode both raise the Nernst voltage.

### 3.3 Derivation: System Efficiency with Parasitic Losses

**Given:** PEMFC stack produces $P_{gross} = 50\,\text{kW}_{DC}$. Parasitic loads: compressor $P_{comp} = 4.5\,\text{kW}$, coolant pump $P_{pump} = 0.8\,\text{kW}$, fan + controls $P_{aux} = 0.7\,\text{kW}$. Hydrogen LHV input is $Q_{H_2} = 150\,\text{kW}_{th}$.

**Step 1 — Net electrical power output:**
$$P_{net} = P_{gross} - P_{parasitic}$$
$$P_{net} = 50 - (4.5 + 0.8 + 0.7) = 50 - 6.0 = 44.0\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — System (electrical) efficiency:**
$$\eta_{system} = \frac{P_{net}}{Q_{H_2}} = \frac{44.0}{150} = 0.2933$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{system} \approx 29.3\,\%}$$

**Step 3 — Stack (gross) efficiency for reference:**
$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{50}{150} = 33.3\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Parasitic loss fraction:**
$$\eta_{parasitic\,loss} = \frac{6.0}{50} = 12\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Even with a highly efficient stack (>60 % LHV), the **compressor alone can consume 10–15 %** of the gross power. This is why modern automotive FC systems use **expander-integrated turbo-compressors** to recover some of the cathode exhaust energy.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters on System Performance

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $E_{Nernst}$ | Effect on $\Delta V_{act}$ | Effect on $\Delta V_{conc}$ | Effect on $\eta_{system}$ | Effect on $\dot{V}_{air}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure (P) | ↑ (ln) | ↓ (kinetics) | ↓ (mass-transport) | ↑ (modest) | ↓ (inversely) |
| Temperature (T) | ↑ slightly (HHV side) | ↓ (Tafel slope) | ↑ (slight) | ↑ (heat loss) | ↑ (ideal gas) |
| Stoichiometric ratio (λ_air) | — | slight ↓ | ↓↓ (large drop) | ↓ (parasitic ↑) | ↑ (linear) |
| Relative Humidity (RH) | ↓ (P_H₂O ↑) | — | ↑ (flooding) | mixed | — |
| Current density (i) | — | ↑ (Tafel) | ↑ (asymptotic) | — | — |
| Membrane thickness | — | — | — | ↓ (ohmic ↑) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrogen-Air PEMFC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[H2 Tank 350 or 700 bar] --> B[H2 Regulator Two-Stage]
    B --> C[H2 Mass Flow Controller]
    C --> D[Anode Inlet Manifold]
    D --> E[MEA Anode Side]
    E --> F[Anode Recirculation Loop]
    F --> G[Purge Valve Vent]
    F --> C
    E --> H[Cathode Outlet Water and N2]
    H --> I[Water Separator Condenser]
    I --> J[Cathode Back Pressure Valve]
    J --> K[Air Exhaust]
    L[Ambient Air Inlet] --> M[Air Filter HEPA Carbon]
    M --> N[Compressor or Blower]
    N --> O[Air Mass Flow Controller]
    O --> P[Humidifier Bubbler or Membrane]
    P --> Q[Cathode Inlet Manifold]
    Q --> E
    E --> R[Coolant Channel Cold Plate]
    R --> S[Coolant Pump]
    S --> T[Radiator Heat Exchanger]
    T --> R
    E --> U[DC Bus Stack Output]
    U --> V[DC DC Converter]
    V --> W[Inverter DC to AC]
    W --> X[Load Motor Grid or Device]
    Y[Controller ECU BMS] --> C
    Y --> O
    Y --> N
    Y --> S
    Y --> V
    Y --> G
    I --> Z[Water Reservoir]
    Z --> P
```

### 4.2 Mass-Flow and Energy Balance Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reactants["Reactant Inputs"]
        R1[H2 mass flow mH2 in]
        R2[Air mass flow mair in]
    end
    subgraph Stack["Electrochemical Core MEA"]
        S1[Anode H2 to H plus]
        S2[Cathode O2 plus e to H2O]
        S3[Heat Generation Qgen]
        S4[Electrical Power Pdc gross]
    end
    subgraph Outputs["Outputs"]
        O1[Anode Vent H2 unreacted plus N2]
        O2[Cathode Exhaust O2 depleted plus H2O vapor]
        O3[Coolant Loop Heat Qcool]
        O4[Net DC Power Pnet]
    end
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S2
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S2 --> S4
    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O2
    S3 --> O3
    S4 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Polarization Curve Map (Qualitative Annotation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Density i A per cm2] -->|log scale| B[Cell Voltage Vcell]
    B --> C1[Region 1 Activation Loss 0 to 0.1 A per cm2 Tafel slope dominant]
    B --> C2[Region 2 Ohmic Loss 0.1 to 1.0 A per cm2 Linear V equals E minus i times RASR]
    B --> C3[Region 3 Concentration Loss greater than 1.0 A per cm2 Asymptotic to iL]
    D[ENernst 1.23 to 1.25 V] --> C1
    E[ENernst minus DeltaVact] --> C2
    F[ENernst minus DeltaVact minus DeltaVohm] --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **functional block schematics**, not photorealistic mechanical drawings. In a KTU exam, drawing such labelled block diagrams is worth 2–3 marks of any 14-mark Hydrogen-Air System question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the role of the air stoichiometric ratio (λ_air) in a PEMFC. Why is it always kept greater than 1.0 in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The air stoichiometric ratio $\lambda_{air}$ is defined as the actual air flow rate supplied to the cathode divided by the stoichiometric air flow rate required by the electrochemical reaction. For a PEMFC, the cathodic half-cell reaction is:
$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In practice, $\lambda_{air}$ is maintained between **1.5 and 3.0** (typically 2.0) for three critical reasons:

1. **Water Removal:** Excess air sweeps liquid product water from the cathode GDL, preventing **cathode flooding** and concentration polarization.
2. **Adequate O₂ Partial Pressure:** Maintains a high enough oxygen partial pressure at the catalyst sites to support the load current.
3. **Thermal Management:** The exhaust air stream carries away sensible heat and latent heat of vaporized water, assisting stack cooling.

If $\lambda_{air} = 1.0$ (stoichiometric), there is no convective sweep, leading to **flooding, voltage collapse, and irreversible membrane damage**. Hence, a controlled excess is non-negotiable in real systems. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly describe any three functions of the Balance of Plant (BOP) in a Hydrogen-Air PEMFC system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per function):**

1. **Reactant Delivery & Metering:** The BOP includes pressure regulators, mass-flow controllers, and the compressor/blower that deliver hydrogen and air at controlled flow rates and pressures to the stack. *(1 mark)*
2. **Humidification:** External humidifiers (bubblers or membrane-type) maintain the reactant gas relative humidity at 50–100 % to keep the Nafion membrane hydrated and ionically conductive. *(1 mark)*
3. **Thermal Management:** A coolant loop with pump, radiator, and temperature sensors removes the ~40–60 % of fuel energy converted to waste heat, keeping the stack within its safe operating window (typically 60–80 °C). *(1 mark)*

*(Additional valid functions: water management/condenser, purge/recirculation, power conditioning, safety interlocks, leakage detection — any 3 of these accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Balance of Plant (BOP)** components of a Hydrogen-Air PEMFC system and explain the function of each major subsystem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PEMFC stack delivers $I = 300\,\text{A}$ at $T = 343\,\text{K}$ ($70\,^\circ\text{C}$) and $P = 2.0\,\text{bar}$ (absolute). The air stoichiometric ratio is $\lambda_{air} = 2.5$. Calculate:
- (i) The molar flow rate of O₂ consumed.
- (ii) The molar flow rate of air supplied.
- (iii) The volumetric flow rate of air at stack inlet (m³/s). *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

*(Block diagram — 2 marks; explanation of each subsystem — 5 marks)*

**Block Diagram (labelled):**
A labelled block diagram should show: H₂ Tank → Regulator → H₂ MFC → Anode Inlet → **MEA** → Cathode Inlet ← Humidifier ← Air MFC ← Compressor ← Air Filter. From the MEA: Anode Vent → Recirculation + Purge; Cathode Exhaust → Water Separator → Back-Pressure Valve. Coolant loop with pump and radiator. DC output to converter. (2 marks for correctness of diagram)

**Subsystem explanation (5 marks, ~1 mark each):**

1. **Hydrogen Subsystem:** Includes high-pressure tank, two-stage pressure regulator, solenoid shut-off valve, thermal MFC, optional recirculation ejector, and purge valve. Function: store and deliver H₂ at controlled pressure (1.2–3 bar gauge) and flow rate to the anode. (1 mark)
2. **Air Subsystem:** Includes inlet filter, compressor or blower, MFC, and back-pressure regulator. Function: filter particulates, pressurize, meter, and regulate air flow into the cathode to maintain λ_air ≈ 2.0. (1 mark)
3. **Humidification Subsystem:** Bubbler or membrane humidifier; sometimes steam injection. Function: add water vapor to the inlet air to keep the membrane at the required hydration level (RH 50–100 %) for proton conductivity. (1 mark)
4. **Thermal Management Subsystem:** Cooling plates within the stack, coolant pump, radiator, fan, and temperature sensors. Function: remove 40–60 % of LHV energy rejected as heat, maintaining stack temperature between 60–80 °C. (1 mark)
5. **Water & Power Management:** Water separator/condenser on cathode exhaust to recover liquid water; DC-DC converter and possibly inverter for usable electrical output. (1 mark)

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $I = 300\,\text{A}$; $T = 343\,\text{K}$; $P = 2.0\,\text{bar}$ (abs); $\lambda_{air} = 2.5$; $y_{O_2} = 0.2095$.

**(i) Molar flow of O₂ consumed:**

The half-cell reaction transfers 4 electrons per O₂:
$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{300}{4 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{300}{385{,}940} = 7.773 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{n}_{O_2} = 7.773 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol/s}}$$ (3 marks — formula, substitution, final answer)

**(ii) Molar flow of air supplied:**

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\lambda_{air}\cdot \dot{n}_{O_2}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{2.5 \times 7.773 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2095}$$

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{1.9433 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2095} = 9.276 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{n}_{air} = 9.276 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/s}}$$ (2 marks)

**(iii) Volumetric flow of air at stack inlet:**

Using ideal gas law:
$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{\dot{n}_{air}\cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{9.276 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.314 \times 343}{2.0 \times 101{,}325}$$

**Numerator:**
$$9.276 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.314 = 7.712 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$7.712 \times 10^{-2} \times 343 = 26.45$$

**Denominator:**
$$2.0 \times 101{,}325 = 202{,}650\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{26.45}{202{,}650} = 1.305 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{air} \approx 1.305 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 7.83\,\text{L/min}}$$ (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell and explain how the open-circuit voltage is affected by temperature, pressure, and reactant partial pressures. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 50-cell PEMFC stack operates at $T = 333\,\text{K}$ ($60\,^\circ\text{C}$). At standard pressure, the average cell voltage is $V_{cell} = 0.70\,\text{V}$ when the stack current is $I = 200\,\text{A}$. The air stoichiometric ratio is 2.0 and hydrogen utilization is 95 %. Calculate:
- (i) The LHV thermal efficiency of the stack.
- (ii) The mass flow rate of hydrogen consumed.
- (iii) The mass flow rate of air supplied. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the Gibbs free energy change of the reaction:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. The reversible cell voltage is:
$$E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For the hydrogen-oxygen reaction at standard conditions (1 bar, 25 °C):
$$E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{2F} = 1.229\,\text{V (LHV basis)}$$

The reaction quotient for:
$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

is $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

This is the **Nernst equation** for the H₂/O₂ fuel cell. (5 marks for derivation)

**Effect of parameters (2 marks):**
- ↑ **Pressure** → ↑ $E$ (logarithmic gain).
- ↑ **Temperature** → ↑ $E$ (numerator scales with T, but $E^\circ$ decreases slightly with T; net effect small and positive for typical PEMFC range).
- ↑ **P_H₂ or P_O₂** → ↑ $E$.
- ↑ **P_H₂O** (more humid) → ↓ $E$.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** 50 cells; $T = 333\,\text{K}$; $V_{cell} = 0.70\,\text{V}$; $I = 200\,\text{A}$; $\lambda_{air} = 2.0$; $U_{H_2} = 95\,\% = 0.95$.

**(i) LHV thermal efficiency:**

For hydrogen, the ideal reversible voltage on LHV basis is $E_{th} = 1.229\,\text{V}$ per cell.

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} = \frac{0.70}{1.229} = 0.5696$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{LHV} \approx 57.0\,\%}$$ (3 marks)

**(ii) Mass flow of hydrogen consumed:**

Number of cells $N = 50$, so total current per cell is still $I_{cell} = 200\,\text{A}$ (current is series-common). Moles of H₂ consumed per second (Faraday, $n=2$):
$$\dot{n}_{H_2,\,consumed} = \frac{I}{2F} = \frac{200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.036 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/s}$$

Mass consumed:
$$\dot{m}_{H_2,\,consumed} = \dot{n}_{H_2,\,consumed}\cdot M_{H_2} = 1.036 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2,\,consumed} \approx 2.089 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g/s} = 7.52\,\text{g/h}}$$ (2 marks)

**(iii) Mass flow of air supplied:**

Hydrogen utilization is the *consumed/supplied* ratio, but for air we use $\lambda_{air}$ which refers to *supplied/required*. Molar O₂ required:
$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{200}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 5.182 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol/s}$$

Air supplied (molar):
$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\lambda_{air}\cdot \dot{n}_{O_2}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{2.0 \times 5.182 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2095} = 4.947 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/s}$$

Mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{n}_{air}\cdot M_{air} = 4.947 \times 10^{-3} \times 28.97$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} \approx 0.1433\,\text{g/s} = 8.60\,\text{g/h}}$$ (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Faraday's n-value confusion:** Many students write $n=2$ for O₂ instead of $n=4$. The half-cell cathode reaction $\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ transfers **4 electrons**, not 2. **Marks lost: 1–2 per such question.**
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Forgetting $\lambda$ in air flow:** Air flow is **not** simply $\dot{n}_{O_2}/y_{O_2}$; you must multiply by $\lambda_{air}$ to get the *actual* flow. Omitting this gives only stoichiometric air, not the supplied air. **Marks lost: 1.**
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Pressure in Pa vs. bar:** When using $PV = nRT$, pressure **must** be in **Pascals (Pa)**, not bar. A factor-of-$10^5$ error will lose full marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — LHV vs. HHV voltage:** Use $E^\circ = 1.229\,\text{V}$ for LHV (water leaves as vapor) and $1.481\,\text{V}$ for HHV. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Per-cell vs. stack quantities:** Current $I$ is the same through all cells in series. Voltage must be multiplied by $N$ for the stack. Do **not** divide $I$ by $N$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen-Air Systems — Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet**

- **Electrochemical Core Reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l/v)}$; $\Delta H_{LHV} = 241.83$ kJ/mol; $E^\circ_{LHV} = 1.229$ V; $E^\circ_{HHV} = 1.481$ V.
- **Standard Constants (must memorize):** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $y_{O_2} = 0.2095$; $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol; $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol; $M_{H_2O} = 18.015$ g/mol.
- **Faraday's Law — Two forms to remember:**
  - H₂ supply: $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{H_2}\cdot I}{2F}$
  - O₂ supply: $\dot{n}_{O_2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{air}\cdot I}{4F\cdot y_{O_2}}$
- **BOP Subsystems (5):** Hydrogen delivery, Air delivery, Humidification, Thermal management, Power conditioning.
- **Key Design Numbers:** $\lambda_{air} = 1.5–3.0$ (typ. 2.0); $U_{H_2} = 90–99\,\%$; RH at inlet = 50–100 %; stack temperature = 60–80 °C; pressure = 1–3 bar (PEMFC).
- **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ — always write this in full for full marks.
- **Cell Polarization Equation:** $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ — the **4-term model**.
- **Parasitic Loads to Remember:** Compressor (biggest, 10–15 % of gross power), coolant pump, fan, control electronics.
- **Water Management Rule of Thumb:** Cathode produces 1 mole H₂O per 2 Faradays of charge = $\dot{m}_{H_2O} = \dfrac{I\cdot M_{H_2O}}{2F}$.
- **Three Water-Management Failure Modes:** *Flooding* (too wet, λ too low), *Drying* (too hot, RH too low), *Two-phase flow* (design error in GDL).
- **Compressor vs. Blower:** Compressor → > 2 bar (automotive); Blower → ≤ 1.5 bar (stationary, atmospheric PEMFC).
- **Hydrogen Storage Comparison:** 700 bar CGH₂ = 40 g/L; LH₂ = 70.8 g/L; Metal hydride (LaNi₅) = ~100 g/L (low pressure, heavy).
- **Safety Note:** H₂ is flammable over 4–75 % in air; leak detection + ventilation are mandatory BOP functions.
- **Thermal Management Equation:** $\dot{Q}_{gen} = I\bigl(E_{th} - V_{cell}\bigr)$ per cell — never forget this.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics (high frequency in past papers):**
  1. Stoichiometry & mass-flow calculation.
  2. Nernst voltage derivation + numerical.
  3. BOP block diagram + explanation.
  4. Polarization curve identification.
  5. Parasitic loss / system efficiency.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always state **assumptions** (ideal gas, isothermal, steady-state) before solving numerical problems — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this alone, and it sets the stage for full marks on the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor # 🔋 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor (FC-FP System)** is an integrated energy conversion assembly in which a hydrocarbon-based primary fuel (e.g., natural gas, methane, methanol, gasoline, diesel, or biogas) is catalytically converted into a **hydrogen-rich synthesis gas (syngas)** upstream of the electrochemical fuel cell stack. The fuel processor performs the function of **reforming** the raw fuel into a stream suitable for the **anode inlet** of the fuel cell, followed by **clean-up stages** that remove carbon monoxide (CO) to levels tolerable by the cell's catalyst (typically <10 ppm for PEMFC, <1000 ppm for PAFC, and higher tolerance for SOFC and MCFC). The complete system block comprises the following sequential units: $$\text{Hydrocarbon Fuel} \rightarrow \text{Desulfurizer} \rightarrow \text{Reformer} \rightarrow \text{WGS Reactors} \rightarrow \text{PROX / CO Cleanup} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Cell Stack} \rightarrow \text{DC/DC Converter} \rightarrow \text{Load}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT634 Module 4):** > Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor covers reforming technologies (SR, POX, ATR), shift conversion, CO removal, and overall system integration for stationary and mobile applications. The processing train must deliver **reformate with H₂ concentration ≥ 70 vol% (dry basis)** and **CO < 10–100 ppm** for low-temperature fuel cells. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Cell Kitchen" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen** > Imagine a fuel cell as a "chef" who can only cook with one specific ingredient: **pure hydrogen**. However, the "grocery store" of the world only delivers raw "ingredients" like natural gas, gasoline, or methanol. The **Fuel Processor is the kitchen prep station** that: > > 1. **Washes the vegetables** → Desulfurization (removes sulfur poisons) > 2. **Chops and pre-cooks the ingredients** → Reforming (converts CH₄, CH₃OH into H₂ + CO) > 3. **Refines the broth** → Water-Gas Shift (converts CO to more H₂) > 4. **Strains out impurities** → PROX/CO cleanup (removes residual CO) > 5. **Finally serves the meal** → Fuel Cell Stack (electrochemically oxidizes H₂) Without the kitchen prep, the chef (fuel cell) cannot operate. The fuel processor is therefore the **enabling subsystem** that unlocks the use of conventional, logistically available hydrocarbon fuels for hydrogen-based power generation. ## 1.3 Why a Fuel Processor is Required Pure hydrogen storage and distribution face well-known infrastructure barriers: cryogenic liquid H₂ boil-off (~1%/day), high-pressure compression (350–700 bar), embrittlement of metal tanks, and the absence of a retail H₂ refueling network. The fuel processor **bypasses these barriers** by generating hydrogen **in-situ, on-demand** from dense, energy-rich liquid or gaseous hydrocarbon fuels. > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems:** > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** 120 MJ/kg > - **LHV of CH₄:** 50 MJ/kg > - **LHV of CH₃OH:** 19.9 MJ/kg > - **Reformer Operating Temperature:** 700–900 °C (SR/ATR), 600–900 °C (POX) > - **WGS Reactor (HT):** 350–450 °C; **(LT):** 190–250 °C > - **PROX Reactor:** 80–200 °C # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Fuel Processing Train ### **Stage 1 — Desulfurization (Pre-reforming Treatment)** Sulfur compounds (H₂S, COS, thiophenes, mercaptans) present in hydrocarbon feedstocks are **potent, irreversible poisons** for reforming catalysts (Ni, Rh) and especially for the noble-metal anode catalysts of LTFCs (Pt in PEMFC). The feed is passed through: - **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** reactor at 300–400 °C over Co-Mo/Al₂O₃ catalyst. - **ZnO adsorbent bed** at 350 °C (sorption: $\text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{ZnS} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$). - **Activated carbon / Cu-based sorbents** for trace removal. > [!NOTE] > **Required outlet specification:** Total sulfur < **0.1 ppm** for PEMFC systems; < **1 ppm** for PAFC. ### **Stage 2 — Reforming (Primary H₂ Generation)** This is the **central thermochemical step** where hydrocarbons react with steam, oxygen, or both to liberate hydrogen. Three principal technologies exist: #### (a) Steam Reforming (SR) — Endothermic, Highest H₂ Yield **General Reaction (CH₄ as model):** $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Catalyst:** Ni/Al₂O₃ (industrial), Rh, Ru, Pt (for higher activity). Operating window: **700–900 °C, 1–30 atm**. **Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C):** Critical process parameter defined as: $$S/C = \frac{\text{moles of steam fed}}{\text{moles of carbon in the fuel}}$$ Typical S/C for SR: **2.5 – 4.0** to prevent coking (carbon deposition). **Methanol Steam Reforming (MSR):** $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +49.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Operates at much lower temperatures (**200–300 °C**) over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst — ideal for portable PEMFC systems. #### (b) Partial Oxidation (POX) — Exothermic, Fast Response $$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Advantages: rapid start-up, no steam required, compact. Disadvantages: lower H₂:CO ratio, hot-spots, soot formation. Catalysts: Ni, Rh, Pt on ceramic monoliths. #### (c) Autothermal Reforming (ATR) — Thermally Self-Sufficient A hybrid of SR + POX in a single reactor with controlled O₂ and H₂O injection: $$\text{CH}_4 + a\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + b\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + (2+2a)\text{H}_2 + (a-b)\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + (b-1)\,\text{CO}_2$$ When the **heat of POX balances the heat of SR**, the reactor operates near **adiabatic thermal neutrality** — hence "auto-thermal". Operates at **600–900 °C**. ### **Stage 3 — Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction** The CO-rich reformate from the reformer is passed through WGS reactors to convert CO into additional H₂: $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Two-stage configuration (industry standard):** - **High-Temperature Shift (HTS):** Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃, 350–450 °C, reduces CO from ~10% to ~3 vol%. - **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS):** Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃, 190–250 °C, reduces CO to < 1 vol%. ### **Stage 4 — Preferential Oxidation (PROX)** To protect Pt anodes of PEMFC, residual CO must be reduced to **<10 ppm** (often <5 ppm). The PROX reactor selectively oxidizes CO in the presence of excess H₂: $$\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Catalysts: Pt/γ-Al₂O₃, Au/Fe₂O₃, Rh/MgO, Cu-CeO₂. Operating temperature: **80–200 °C**. Air/O₂ stoichiometry is tightly controlled (λ = 2 to 4) to minimize parasitic H₂ loss. ### **Stage 5 — Fuel Cell Stack (Downstream of Processor)** The clean H₂-rich stream is fed to the fuel cell anode, where the electrochemical reactions occur. Thermal and water management integration with the fuel processor is **critical** for high overall system efficiency. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE — questions are typically centered on reformer efficiency, WGS equilibrium, S/C ratio, and overall system efficiency.** | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Steam-to-Carbon Ratio | $S/C = \dfrac{n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{n_{\text{C in fuel}}}$ | 2.5 – 4.0 (SR) | | 2 | Air-to-Fuel Ratio (POX/ATR) | $A/F = \dfrac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{n_{\text{C in fuel}}}$ | 0.3 – 0.5 (ATR) | | 3 | H₂ Yield (per mole CH₄, SR) | $Y_{\text{H}_2} = 3 \times \eta_{\text{SR}} \times \text{CH}_4$ conversion | moles | | 4 | Reformer Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{reformer}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{H}_2, \text{out}} \cdot LHV_{\text{H}_2}}{n_{\text{fuel,in}} \cdot LHV_{\text{fuel}} + Q_{\text{ext}}}$ | 0.70 – 0.85 | | 5 | WGS Equilibrium Constant | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}_2} P_{\text{H}_2}}{P_{\text{CO}} P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ | Function of T | | 6 | WGS Kp Temperature Correlation | $\ln K_p = \dfrac{4577.8}{T} - 4.33$ | T in K | | 7 | CO Conversion in WGS | $X_{\text{CO}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{CO,in}} - F_{\text{CO,out}}}{F_{\text{CO,in}}}$ | 0.85 – 0.98 | | 8 | H₂ Recovery in PROX (Selectivity × Conv.) | $\eta_{\text{PROX}} = (1-S_{\text{H}_2}) \cdot X_{\text{CO}}$ | 0.40 – 0.60 | | 9 | Overall System Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{proc}} \times \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}$ | 0.30 – 0.45 | | 10 | Parasitic Power Factor | $f_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{aux}}}{P_{\text{stack}}}$ | 0.05 – 0.20 | | 11 | Nernst Voltage (anode gas) | $E = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T-298) + \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln P_{\text{H}_2} P_{\text{O}_2}^{0.5}$ | V | | 12 | LHV of H₂ (constant) | $\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}$ | **120 MJ/kg** | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!TIP] > - **Stationary Power (1–250 kW):** Natural gas-fed PAFC and MCFC systems (e.g., ONSI PC-25, Fuji, FuelCell Energy DFC-3000) use **ATR + HTS** for fuel processing. > - **APU / Heavy-Duty Trucks:** Diesel-fed SOFC systems with **POX + desulfurization** (e.g., Delphi SOFC APU). > - **Portable Electronics (1–250 W):** Methanol-fed PEMFC with **MSR + PROX** (e.g., SFC Smart Fuel Cell, Toshiba Dynario). > - **Backup Power (Telecom):** LPG / propane-fed PEMFC with **ATR + WGS + PROX** (e.g., Plug Power, Ballard). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Overall System Efficiency of FC-FP Assembly The **net electrical efficiency** of a fuel cell system with an integrated fuel processor is the product of three sub-efficiencies. We derive this step by step. ### **Step 1 — Define the Reformer Output Power Equivalent** The reformer converts a hydrocarbon fuel of chemical input power $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel,in}}$ (based on LHV) into a hydrogen stream. The chemical power of the produced H₂ is: $$P_{\text{H}_2, \text{chem}} = \dot{n}_{\text{H}_2, \text{out}} \cdot LHV_{\text{H}_2}$$ The **reformer efficiency** is therefore: $$\eta_{\text{reformer}} = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2, \text{out}} \cdot LHV_{\text{H}_2}}{\dot{n}_{\text{fuel, in}} \cdot LHV_{\text{fuel}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{ext}}}$$ where $\dot{Q}_{\text{ext}}$ is any external heat input (e.g., burner heat for SR). For an **autothermal reformer**, $\dot{Q}_{\text{ext}} \approx 0$ and $\eta_{\text{reformer}}$ depends only on thermodynamics and heat recovery. ### **Step 2 — Define the Fuel Cell Stack Efficiency** For an ideal fuel cell operating on the produced H₂ at current density $i$: $$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{1.482} \times \eta_{\text{fuel utilization}}$$ where $V_{\text{cell}}$ is the operating cell voltage, 1.482 V is the **LHV-based thermoneutral voltage** of H₂, and $\eta_{\text{fuel util}} = 0.80$ – $0.95$ is the fraction of H₂ actually consumed electrochemically (the remainder is recycled or combusted in a tail-gas burner to supply reformer heat). ### **Step 3 — Define the Balance-of-Plant (BOP) Efficiency** The BOP includes blowers, pumps, and the DC/AC inverter. The **BOP efficiency** is: $$\eta_{\text{BOP}} = 1 - \frac{P_{\text{aux}}}{P_{\text{DC}}}$$ where $P_{\text{aux}}$ is the total parasitic power and $P_{\text{DC}}$ is the gross DC power from the stack. ### **Step 4 — Combine into Overall System Efficiency** The net AC electrical power output is: $$P_{\text{AC}} = P_{\text{DC}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}} = \eta_{\text{reformer}} \times \eta_{\text{stack}} \times P_{\text{fuel,in}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}$$ Thus, the **overall system efficiency** is: $$\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{reformer}} \times \eta_{\text{stack}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}$$ ### **Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem)** A 50 kW PEMFC system operates on natural gas. Given: - Reformer efficiency = **0.78** - Cell operating voltage = 0.70 V - Fuel utilization = 0.90 - BOP efficiency = 0.96 **Step A — Stack efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \frac{0.70}{1.482} \times 0.90 = 0.4723 \times 0.90 = 0.4251$$ **Step B — Overall system efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{sys}} = 0.78 \times 0.4251 \times 0.96 = 0.3183 \quad (\approx 31.83\%)$$ **Step C — Required fuel input (LHV basis):** $$P_{\text{fuel, in}} = \frac{P_{\text{AC}}}{\eta_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{50 \text{ kW}}{0.3183} = 157.1 \text{ kW (LHV)}$$ **Step D — CH₄ mass flow rate (LHV = 50 MJ/kg):** $$\dot{m}_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{157.1}{50} = 3.14 \text{ kg/h}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Water-Gas Shift Equilibrium Conversion For the WGS reaction at temperature $T$, the equilibrium constant is given by the **van't Hoff** relation. Using a typical industrial correlation: $$\ln K_p = \frac{4577.8}{T} - 4.33$$ **Derivation Setup:** Let $X$ = fractional CO conversion at equilibrium. For a feed of 1 mol CO, $b$ mol H₂O, with inerts $c$ mol (e.g., N₂, CH₄), and at total pressure $P$: $$K_p = \frac{X(X + c_0) \cdot P / (1 + b + c_0)}{\left[ \left(\frac{P_{\text{CO,in}}}{P} \right) - \frac{X}{1 + b + c_0} \right] \left[ \left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O,in}}}{P}\right) - \frac{X}{1 + b + c_0} \right]}$$ **Working Example:** Feed of 1 mol CO, 3 mol H₂O, 5 mol inerts; $T = 700$ K; $P = 1$ atm. **Step 1 — Compute $K_p$:** $$\ln K_p = \frac{4577.8}{700} - 4.33 = 6.540 - 4.33 = 2.21 \Rightarrow K_p = 9.12$$ **Step 2 — Simplify (low pressure, dilute assumption):** With $c_0 \gg X$: $$K_p \approx \frac{X(b - X)}{(1 - X)(a - X)} \cdot \frac{1}{P}$$ where $a = 1$, $b = 3$. Substituting: $$9.12 = \frac{X(3 - X)}{(1 - X)(1 - X)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the quadratic:** $9.12(1 - X)^2 = X(3 - X)$ $$9.12(1 - 2X + X^2) = 3X - X^2$$ $$9.12 - 18.24X + 9.12X^2 = 3X - X^2$$ $$10.12X^2 - 21.24X + 9.12 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula: $$X = \frac{21.24 \pm \sqrt{(21.24)^2 - 4(10.12)(9.12)}}{2(10.12)}$$ $$X = \frac{21.24 \pm \sqrt{451.14 - 369.18}}{20.24} = \frac{21.24 \pm \sqrt{81.96}}{20.24} = \frac{21.24 \pm 9.05}{20.24}$$ Taking the physically meaningful root ($X < 1$): $$X = \frac{21.24 - 9.05}{20.24} = \frac{12.19}{20.24} = 0.602$$ > **Equilibrium CO conversion at 700 K ≈ 60.2%**. This is why industrial systems use **two-stage WGS** (HTS at ~700 K → LTS at ~500 K) to drive conversion above 95%. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reformer Technology Selection > [!NOTE] > The following decision matrix is a standard KTU Module 4 analytical tool used to justify reformer choice in design problems. | Parameter | Steam Reforming (SR) | Partial Oxidation (POX) | Autothermal (ATR) | |---|---|---|---| | Operating T (°C) | 700 – 900 | 600 – 900 | 600 – 900 | | H₂:CO Molar Ratio | ~3 : 1 | ~2 : 1 | 2.5 – 3.0 : 1 | | H₂ Yield per CH₄ | **Highest** (3 mol) | Lower (2 mol) | Intermediate | | Energy Balance | **Highly Endothermic** | **Exothermic** | **Thermoneutral** | | External Heat Source | Required | None | None (integrated) | | Start-up Time | Slow | **Fast** | Moderate | | S/C Ratio | 2.5 – 4.0 | 0 (no steam) | 1.0 – 2.5 | | Catalyst | Ni/Al₂O₃ | Ni, Rh monolith | Ni, Rh | | System Complexity | High (burner needed) | Low | Medium | | Best-Suited FC | **PAFC, MCFC, SOFC** | **SOFC APU, diesel** | **PEMFC stationary** | | H₂ Concentration (dry) | 70 – 75 vol% | 55 – 65 vol% | 65 – 70 vol% | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Integrated Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete integration of the fuel processor train with the fuel cell stack, balance-of-plant, and heat-recovery subsystems. This is a high-fidelity schematic suitable for KTU 14-mark design explanations. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hydrocarbon Fuel Tank
CH4 or CH3OH] --> B[Feed Pump and Vaporizer] B --> C[Desulfurization Unit
HDS plus ZnO Bed] C --> D{Steam Generator
Heat Exchanger} D -->|Steam| E[Reformer Unit
SR or ATR or POX] A -->|Direct feed| E E -->|Hot Reformate| F[High Temperature
WGS Reactor] F --> G[Low Temperature
WGS Reactor] G --> H[PROX Reactor
CO Selective Oxidation] H --> I[Heat Exchanger 1
Reformate Cooling] I --> J[Fuel Cell Stack
PEMFC or PAFC or SOFC] J -->|Unreacted H2 Tail| K[Tail Gas Burner
Cathode Off-Gas Combustor] K -->|Flue Gas Heat| D K -->|Flue Gas Heat| L[Heat Recovery Steam
Generator HRSG] J -->|DC Power| M[DC DC Converter] M --> N[DC AC Inverter] N --> O[AC Load] P[Air Blower and Filter] --> Q[Air Preheater] Q --> J R[Coolant Pump] --> S[Thermal Management
Loop] S --> J L -->|Recovered Steam| D S -->|Recovered Heat| D ``` ## 4.2 Functional Sub-System Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TB SYS[Integrated Fuel Cell Power System] SYS --> FP[Fuel Processor Subsystem] SYS --> FC[Fuel Cell Subsystem] SYS --> BOP[Balance of Plant] SYS --> TR[Thermal Recovery and Management] FP --> F1[Stage 1: Desulfurizer] FP --> F2[Stage 2: Reformer] FP --> F3[Stage 3: WGS HT and LT] FP --> F4[Stage 4: PROX Reactor] FP --> F5[Stage 5: Reformate Cooler] FC --> C1[Anode Flow Field] FC --> C2[Membrane or Electrolyte] FC --> C3[Cathode Flow Field] FC --> C4[Current Collectors and BOP] BOP --> B1[Air Compressor or Blower] BOP --> B2[Reformate Recirculation Pump] BOP --> B3[Water Management and Humidifier] BOP --> B4[Power Electronics] TR --> T1[Tail Gas Burner] TR --> T2[Heat Exchanger Network] TR --> T3[Cogeneration Steam Loop] ``` ## 4.3 Reformer Selection Decision Flow (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Fuel Type?} -->|Natural Gas| Q2{Application Type?} Q1 -->|Methanol| MR[Use MSR at 250 C with Cu Catalyst] Q1 -->|Diesel or LPG| AR[Use ATR or POX with High Tolerant Catalyst] Q2 -->|Stationary High Power| SR[Choose SR with External Burner] Q2 -->|Mobile Fast Start| POX[Choose POX with Rh Catalyst] Q2 -->|Balanced| AT[Choose ATR for Self-Sustaining Operation] SR --> R1[Reformer Efficiency 0.80 to 0.85] POX --> R2[Reformer Efficiency 0.65 to 0.75] AT --> R3[Reformer Efficiency 0.75 to 0.82] MR --> R4[Reformer Efficiency 0.85 to 0.92] R1 --> OUT[Feed to HTS plus LTS plus PROX] R2 --> OUT R3 --> OUT R4 --> OUT OUT --> FC[Fuel Cell Stack] ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C)" in the context of steam reforming of methane. State its typical industrial range and explain why values above 4.0 are rarely used in practice.** **Model Answer:** The **Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C)** is the molar ratio of steam fed to the reformer to the number of carbon atoms present in the hydrocarbon fuel: $$S/C = \frac{\text{moles of H}_2\text{O}}{\text{moles of carbon in fuel}}$$ **Typical industrial range:** **S/C = 2.5 – 4.0** for natural gas SR. **Why not higher than 4.0:** - **Diminishing returns:** Beyond S/C = 4, additional steam does not significantly increase CH₄ conversion. - **Increased parasitic load:** More water must be vaporized (latent heat = 2260 kJ/kg), reducing overall system efficiency. - **Excess steam dilutes H₂ product**, lowering partial pressure of H₂ in the reformate and reducing fuel cell Nernst voltage. - **Energy penalty:** Each extra kg of steam requires 2.26 MJ to vaporize, directly reducing reformer efficiency. > [!TIP] > **Marks Breakdown (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Range (1) + Justification (1). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is preferential oxidation (PROX) of CO? Why is it essential in a PEMFC system employing a fuel processor?** **Model Answer:** **Preferential Oxidation (PROX)** is the selective catalytic oxidation of CO to CO₂ in the presence of large excess H₂, using a controlled amount of O₂ or air: $$\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Why essential for PEMFC:** - The **Pt anode catalyst of PEMFC is severely poisoned by CO at concentrations > 10 ppm**. - Even after HTS + LTS WGS, residual CO is typically **0.5 – 1 vol% (5000 – 10000 ppm)**. - PROX reduces CO to **<10 ppm**, ensuring anode activity and stable cell voltage. - It operates at low temperature (80–200 °C) with high selectivity (Sₒ₂ > 50% for Pt/γ-Al₂O₃ at 100 °C), minimizing parasitic H₂ loss. **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Reaction (1) + Justification (1). --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!NOTE] > **Both Question A and Question B carry 14 marks each, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each (7+7). Students answer ONE question.** --- ### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE – 2024 Pattern]** **Q.A. (a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the function of each stage in a **fuel processing train** for a natural gas-fed PEMFC system. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The complete fuel processing train for a natural gas-fed PEMFC system consists of the following stages: 1. **Desulfurization (1 mark):** Natural gas contains odorants (mercaptans, THT) and trace H₂S. These are removed in a **ZnO adsorber bed** at 350 °C: $$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{ZnO} \rightarrow \text{ZnS} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Required outlet: **< 0.1 ppm S** to protect the reforming and anode catalysts. 2. **Steam Reformer (2 marks):** - Reforming: $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$, $\Delta H = +206$ kJ/mol - WGS within reformer: $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$, $\Delta H = -41$ kJ/mol - Catalyst: Ni/Al₂O₃, T = 750–850 °C, S/C = 3.0 - Reformate composition: H₂ = 75%, CO = 8%, CO₂ = 8%, CH₄ = 3%, H₂O = 6% (dry basis) 3. **High-Temperature Shift (HTS) (1 mark):** - Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ catalyst at 400 °C. - Reduces CO from ~8% to ~3 vol% while producing additional H₂. 4. **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) (1 mark):** - Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 200–220 °C. - Further reduces CO to **0.5 – 1.0 vol%** (5000–10000 ppm). 5. **Preferential Oxidation (PROX) (1 mark):** - Pt/γ-Al₂O₃ or Au/Fe₂O₃ catalyst at 100–150 °C. - Selective O₂ injection (λ = O₂/CO = 2.0–4.0 mol/mol) reduces CO to **<10 ppm**. 6. **Heat Recovery and BOP (1 mark):** - Reformate is cooled via heat exchangers, generating steam for the reformer and preheating process water and air. - Tail-gas from FC anode is recycled to a **catalytic burner** to provide reformer heat (improving $\eta_{\text{sys}}$ by 8–12%). > The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. --- **Q.A. (b)** A natural gas reformer receives 100 mol/min of CH₄ along with 350 mol/min of steam. The reformer operates at 800 °C with equilibrium approach such that **80% of CH₄ is converted to CO** (and 10% to CO₂). Calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) The reformate composition on a dry basis - (ii) The H₂ production rate - (iii) The reformer efficiency (LHV basis), given $\dot{Q}_{\text{ext}} = 0$ (assume H₂O is condensed before LHV evaluation) **Model Answer:** **Given:** $F_{\text{CH}_4, \text{in}} = 100$ mol/min, $F_{\text{H}_2\text{O, in}} = 350$ mol/min. - CH₄ converted to CO: 80 mol → forms 80 CO and 240 H₂ (by SR: 1:3 ratio) - CH₄ converted to CO₂: 10 mol → forms 10 CO₂ and 30 H₂ (via MSR-type: 1:3 ratio) - CH₄ unreacted: 10 mol **Step 1 — Outlet species (wet basis):** $$F_{\text{CH}_4} = 10, \quad F_{\text{CO}} = 80, \quad F_{\text{CO}_2} = 10, \quad F_{\text{H}_2} = 270$$ $$F_{\text{H}_2\text{O, out}} = 350 - 80 (\text{from SR}) - 10 (\text{from WGS inside reformer}) = 260 \text{ mol/min}$$ Total wet flow = $10 + 80 + 10 + 270 + 260 = 630$ mol/min **Step 2 — Dry basis (after H₂O condensation):** Total dry = $10 + 80 + 10 + 270 = 370$ mol/min | Species | mol/min | mol % (dry) | |---|---|---| | CH₄ | 10 | **2.70** | | CO | 80 | **21.62** | | CO₂ | 10 | **2.70** | | H₂ | 270 | **72.97** | **(i) Reformate dry composition:** H₂ = 72.97%, CO = 21.62%, CO₂ = 2.70%, CH₄ = 2.70%. **(2 marks)** **(ii) H₂ production rate:** **270 mol/min** = $\frac{270 \times 2}{60} = 9.0$ g/s = **0.54 kg/h** of H₂. **(1 mark)** **(iii) Reformer efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{reformer}} = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} \cdot LHV_{\text{H}_2}}{\dot{n}_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot LHV_{\text{CH}_4}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{reformer}} = \frac{270 \times 242 \text{ kJ/mol}}{100 \times 802.3 \text{ kJ/mol}} = \frac{65340}{80230} = 0.8145 = \mathbf{81.45\%}$$ **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to subtract steam consumed in SR and in-situ WGS from $F_{\text{H}_2\text{O, out}}$. > **Common Mistake 2:** Using HHV instead of LHV (LHV of CH₄ = 802.3 kJ/mol, LHV of H₂ = 242 kJ/mol). > **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert dry basis correctly by removing H₂O from denominator. --- ### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **Q.B. (a)** Compare **Steam Reforming (SR)**, **Partial Oxidation (POX)**, and **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** of methane in terms of: operating temperature, H₂ yield, energy balance, catalyst, and best-suited fuel cell type. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | SR | POX | ATR | |---|---|---|---| | **Reaction** | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ | $\text{CH}_4 + 0.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$ | Hybrid of SR + POX | | **ΔH (kJ/mol)** | **+206 (endothermic)** | **-36 (exothermic)** | **~0 (thermoneutral)** | | **H₂ Yield (mol/mol CH₄)** | **3.0 (highest)** | 2.0 (lowest) | 2.5 – 2.8 (intermediate) | | **Operating T** | 700 – 900 °C | 600 – 900 °C | 600 – 900 °C | | **Catalyst** | Ni/Al₂O₃ | Ni, Rh monolith | Ni, Rh on monolith | | **H₂:CO Ratio** | 3:1 | 2:1 | 2.5 – 3:1 | | **S/C Ratio** | 2.5 – 4.0 | 0 (no steam) | 1.0 – 2.5 | | **External Heat** | Required (burner) | **Not required** | **Not required** | | **Start-up Time** | Slow (must heat reformer) | **Fast** | Moderate | | **H₂ Concentration (dry)** | 70 – 75 vol% | 55 – 65 vol% | 65 – 70 vol% | | **Best-Suited FC** | **PAFC, MCFC, SOFC** | **SOFC APU, diesel-fed** | **PEMFC stationary** | | **Reformer Efficiency** | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.75 – 0.82 | **Conclusion (2 marks):** - **SR** is preferred when highest H₂ yield and pure feed are available, but requires heat integration. - **POX** is ideal for fast-start, mobile applications using heavy hydrocarbons (diesel, JP-8). - **ATR** is the most versatile — thermally self-sufficient and well-suited to mid-scale PEMFC stationary power. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **ATR with POX** — ATR uses BOTH steam and oxygen in carefully controlled proportions to achieve thermoneutrality. POX uses only oxygen. --- **Q.B. (b)** A 250 kW PEMFC power unit operates on **methanol via on-site MSR**. The following data are given: - Operating cell voltage: 0.65 V - $\eta_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{cell}} / 1.482 \times \eta_{\text{fuel util}}$, with $\eta_{\text{fuel util}} = 0.85$ - $\eta_{\text{BOP}} = 0.94$ - LHV of CH₃OH = **19.9 MJ/kg** Calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) Stack efficiency - (ii) Assume the MSR reformer efficiency is 0.88. What is the overall system efficiency? - (iii) Calculate the methanol consumption rate in kg/h **Model Answer:** **(i) Stack efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{1.482} \times \eta_{\text{fuel util}} = \frac{0.65}{1.482} \times 0.85$$ $$\eta_{\text{stack}} = 0.4386 \times 0.85 = 0.3728 = \mathbf{37.28\%}$$ **(2 marks)** **(ii) Overall system efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{reformer}} \times \eta_{\text{stack}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}$$ $$\eta_{\text{sys}} = 0.88 \times 0.3728 \times 0.94 = 0.3083 = \mathbf{30.83\%}$$ **(2 marks)** **(iii) Methanol consumption rate:** Required fuel input power (LHV basis): $$P_{\text{fuel,in}} = \frac{P_{\text{AC}}}{\eta_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{250}{0.3083} = 810.9 \text{ kW (LHV)}$$ Converting to MJ/h: $P_{\text{fuel,in}} = 810.9 \times 3.6 = 2919.3$ MJ/h $$\dot{m}_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = \frac{2919.3}{19.9} = \mathbf{146.7 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(3 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes:** > 1. Using **1.229 V** (HHV voltage) instead of **1.482 V** (LHV voltage) for $\eta_{\text{stack}}$ — KTU board strictly penalizes this. > 2. Forgetting the unit conversion kW → MJ/h (multiply by 3.6, not 3600). > 3. Using the formula $\eta = V_{\text{cell}}/1.482$ **without** the fuel utilization term — this is a 1-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4):** - ✅ **Fuel Processor = Desulfurizer + Reformer + HTS + LTS + PROX** (the 5-stage processing train). - ✅ **S/C ratio** (Steam-to-Carbon): typical industrial value for SR of CH₄ is **2.5 – 4.0**; below 2.0 → coking risk; above 4.0 → efficiency penalty. - ✅ **Reforming reactions to memorize:** - SR: $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ (+206 kJ/mol, endothermic) - POX: $\text{CH}_4 + 0.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$ (-36 kJ/mol, exothermic) - MSR: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$ (+49.4 kJ/mol) - WGS: $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (-41 kJ/mol) - PROX: $\text{CO} + 0.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ (-283 kJ/mol) - ✅ **Two-stage WGS:** HTS (Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃, 400 °C) → LTS (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃, 200 °C). Equilibrium favors low T for high CO conversion. - ✅ **CO tolerance of FC anodes:** PEMFC = **<10 ppm**; PAFC = **<1%**; MCFC = fuel = CO-tolerant (CO is oxidized at anode); SOFC = fuel = CO-tolerant (high T). - ✅ **Overall system efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{reformer}} \times \eta_{\text{stack}} \times \eta_{\text{BOP}}$ — typical range **30 – 45%** for PEMFC systems with fuel processor. - ✅ **Stack efficiency formula (LHV basis):** $\eta_{\text{stack}} = (V_{\text{cell}} / 1.482) \times \eta_{\text{fuel util}}$. - ✅ **Thermoneutral voltage of H₂ = 1.482 V (LHV)**; 1.229 V (HHV). - ✅ **Reformer efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{reformer}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2, \text{out}} \cdot LHV_{\text{H}_2}}{\dot{n}_{\text{fuel, in}} \cdot LHV_{\text{fuel}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{ext}}}$. - ✅ **ATR** = hybrid of SR + POX; thermoneutral; best for PEMFC stationary power. - ✅ **POX** = fastest start-up; suitable for diesel-fed SOFC APU applications. - ✅ **Methanol SR (MSR)** operates at the lowest T (200–300 °C); ideal for portable PEMFC. - ✅ **WGS equilibrium constant correlation:** $\ln K_p = 4577.8/T - 4.33$ (T in K). - ✅ **Sulfur limit:** < 0.1 ppm for PEMFC anode; < 1 ppm for PAFC. - ✅ **Parasitic losses** (air blower, coolant pump, inverter) typically reduce net output by **6 – 20%**. - ✅ **Tail-gas recycle / burner** is critical for reformer heat integration — improves $\eta_{\text{sys}}$ by **8 – 12%**. - ✅ **Real-world systems to name in answers:** ONSI PC-25 (PAFC), FuelCell Energy DFC-3000 (MCFC), Plug Power GenSys (PEMFC), SFC Smart Fuel Cell (MSR-PEMFC), Delphi SOFC APU (diesel-SOFC). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Module 4 (Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor):** > 1. Memorize the **5-stage fuel processor block diagram** — it is the single most frequently asked 14-mark question. > 2. Practice **system efficiency calculations** with reformer × stack × BOP formula. > 3. Be ready to compare **SR vs POX vs ATR** in a table — high-probability 7-mark question. > 4. Know the **WGS two-stage configuration** with catalyst and temperature. > 5. Quote **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, thermoneutral voltage = 1.482 V** with confidence.

topics.title: System Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT634 - FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Efficiency (η_sys)** is defined as the ratio of the **net useful electrical energy output** delivered by the complete fuel cell power plant (including the stack, BoP — Balance of Plant, and power conditioning unit) to the **total chemical energy input** of the fuel supplied to the system, measured on a uniform thermodynamic basis (typically Lower Heating Value, LHV, or Higher Heating Value, HHV).

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{sys} \;=\; \frac{P_{net,AC}}{m_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$$

where $P_{net,AC}$ is the net AC electrical power exported to the load after deducting all **parasitic/auxiliary loads**, and $m_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}$ is the rate of chemical energy supplied by the fuel stream.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap with Hose" Model

Imagine a municipal water tap (the **fuel tank**) connected through a long, slightly leaky hose (the **fuel cell stack**) to your bucket (the **electrical load**). Now imagine you also have a small pump (the **air compressor**) running in parallel that keeps the hose pressurized.

- Not all water that enters the hose reaches the bucket → **electrochemical losses**
- Some water sprays out of the leaky joints before reaching you → **fuel crossover, thermal losses**
- The little pump itself consumes some water to operate → **auxiliary/parasitic losses**
- Some flow is wasted because the tap sends more water than the hose can absorb → **fuel utilization losses**

The **fraction of water that finally lands in your bucket**, divided by the water that left the tap, is exactly the **System Efficiency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A fuel cell stack can boast an electrochemical efficiency of 60 %, but the **system efficiency is often only 40–55 %** once auxiliaries (compressors, pumps, humidifiers, inverters) are accounted for. The KTU examiner expects students to recognize this gap.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Standard cell potential (H₂/O₂) | $E^\circ$ | **1.229** | V |
| LHV of Hydrogen | $LHV_{H_2}$ | **241.8** | kJ mol⁻¹ |
| HHV of Hydrogen | $HHV_{H_2}$ | **285.8** | kJ mol⁻¹ |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners accept both LHV and HHV bases, but **LHV is preferred** for low-temperature fuel cells (PEMFC, PAFC) because the product water leaves as vapor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of System Efficiency

System efficiency is **not a single number** — it is the multiplicative product of several sub-efficiencies, each describing a distinct physical or electrochemical loss mechanism.

### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

1. **Thermodynamic ceiling (η_th):** Maximum possible efficiency from the Second Law.
   - For an electrochemical converter, this is governed by the Gibbs free energy.
   - $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ → equals **0.83 (LHV basis)** or **0.71 (HHV basis)** for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C.

2. **Voltage efficiency (η_v):** Penalizes activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.
   - $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}}$
   - Typical range: **0.50 – 0.80** depending on current density.

3. **Faradaic / Current efficiency (η_F):** Penalizes fuel crossover and internal currents.
   - $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{ext}}{I_{ext} + I_{loss}}$
   - For PEMFC: typically **0.95 – 0.99**.

4. **Fuel utilization (η_u):** Penalizes unreacted fuel leaving the anode exhaust.
   - $\eta_u = \dfrac{\dot n_{consumed}}{\dot n_{supplied}}$
   - Practical range: **0.85 – 0.98**.

5. **Power-conditioning efficiency (η_pc):** Penalizes DC-DC converter and DC-AC inverter losses.
   - $\eta_{pc} = \dfrac{P_{AC,out}}{P_{DC,out}}$ → typically **0.92 – 0.97**.

6. **Auxiliary / Parasitic fraction (η_aux):** Fraction of gross output consumed by auxiliaries.
   - $\eta_{aux} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{parasitic}}{P_{DC,out}}$ → typically **0.80 – 0.95**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot \eta_{pc}}{LHV \cdot \dot n_{fuel}}$ | Master equation | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| 2 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_F \cdot \eta_u \cdot \eta_{pc} \cdot \eta_{aux}$ | Multiplicative cascade | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}}$ | Voltage efficiency | 0.50 – 0.80 |
| 4 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{ext}}{I_{ext}+I_{crossover}}$ | Faradaic efficiency | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| 5 | $\eta_u = \dfrac{\dot n_{H_2,reacted}}{\dot n_{H_2,supplied}}$ | Fuel utilization | 0.85 – 0.98 |
| 6 | $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{el} + \eta_{th}$ | Combined Heat & Power | 0.80 – 0.95 |
| 7 | $E_{Nernst} = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Reversible cell voltage | ~1.0 – 1.2 V |
| 8 | $P_{net,AC} = V_{cell} \cdot n_{cells} \cdot I - P_{parasitic}$ | Net power output | kW |
| 9 | $\eta_{th,max} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cell}}{T_{ambient}}$ | Carnot ceiling | Forbidden for FC! |
