| 10 | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{241.8}$ | Thermodynamic ceiling | **0.983** LHV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Never apply the Carnot efficiency formula to a fuel cell. Fuel cells are **not** heat engines — they convert chemical energy directly, so their ceiling is governed by $\Delta G / \Delta H$, not by temperature ratio.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary SOFC systems** (e.g., Bloom Energy, Siemens Silyzer) reach **52–60 %** electrical and **85–90 %** CHP efficiency.
- **Automotive PEMFC** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) targets **60 %** system efficiency at part-load to extend range.
- **Backup power PEMFC** (Plug Power, Ballard) operate at **40–50 %** electrical efficiency.
- **Space applications (Apollo, Shuttle)** used AFC stacks delivering **~70 %** system efficiency due to pure O₂/H₂ and cryogenic reactants.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often pose problems where students must **multiply sub-efficiencies** to obtain the final system efficiency — memorize the cascade form.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation of System Efficiency

Starting from first principles, we derive the master equation linking gross output to fuel input.

### Step 1: Define Gross DC Electrical Output

The electrical power produced by a stack of $N$ cells at current $I$ is:

$$P_{gross,DC} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$$

**Reasoning:** Each cell delivers voltage $V_{cell}$; multiplying by the number of cells in series gives the stack voltage. Power is then voltage × current.

---

### Step 2: Account for Power-Conditioning Losses

Power electronics (DC-DC boost + DC-AC inverter) lose a fraction of energy:

$$P_{AC} = P_{gross,DC} \cdot \eta_{pc}$$

**Reasoning:** Inverters typically operate at **94–97 %** efficiency at rated load. We model this as a multiplicative factor on DC power.

---

### Step 3: Subtract Parasitic Loads

Auxiliary components (compressors, cooling pumps, control electronics) draw power internally:

$$P_{net,AC} = P_{AC} - P_{parasitic} = V_{cell} \cdot N \cdot I \cdot \eta_{pc} - P_{parasitic}$$

**Reasoning:** Parasitic loads are an absolute subtraction (not a multiplicative factor) because they are independent of stack current at the system boundary.

---

### Step 4: Express Chemical Energy Input

The molar flow rate of fuel $\dot n_{fuel}$ multiplied by its lower heating value gives chemical power input:

$$\dot Q_{fuel} = \dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}$$

**Reasoning:** LHV (Lower Heating Value) measures usable combustion heat excluding the latent heat of water vaporization, appropriate for FC systems where product water exits as vapor.

---

### Step 5: Form the System Efficiency Ratio

Combining Steps 3 and 4:

$$\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{net,AC}}{\dot Q_{fuel}} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot \eta_{pc} - P_{parasitic}}{\dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the engineering master equation. Notice that $P_{parasitic}$ appears as a **subtractive** term, meaning at very low loads the system becomes a net consumer of power.

---

### Step 6: Cascade Formulation

By expanding $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} \cdot \eta_v$ and introducing $\eta_F$, $\eta_u$, $\eta_{aux}$ explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{sys} &= \eta_v \cdot \eta_F \cdot \eta_u \cdot \eta_{pc} \cdot \eta_{aux} \\
&= \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}}\right)}_{\eta_v} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{I_{ext}}{I_{ext}+I_{loss}}\right)}_{\eta_F} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{\dot n_{reacted}}{\dot n_{supplied}}\right)}_{\eta_u} \cdot \underbrace{\eta_{pc}}_{\text{electronics}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(1-\dfrac{P_{par}}{P_{gross}}\right)}_{\eta_{aux}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Each bracketed term isolates one physical loss mechanism. Their product gives the overall system efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Template)

> **Problem:** A PEMFC stack consists of **80 cells** in series. Operating conditions: $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V, $I = 200$ A, $\dot n_{H_2} = 0.05$ mol s⁻¹, $LHV_{H_2} = 241.8$ kJ mol⁻¹. Auxiliary loads = 800 W. Power conditioner efficiency = 95 %. Compute the **system efficiency**.

### Solution:

**Step A — Gross DC power:**

$$P_{gross,DC} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 80 \times 0.65 \times 200 = 10{,}400 \text{ W}$$

**Reasoning:** 80 cells in series at 0.65 V each → 52 V stack voltage; × 200 A → 10.4 kW.

---

**Step B — AC power after power conditioning:**

$$P_{AC} = P_{gross,DC} \cdot \eta_{pc} = 10{,}400 \times 0.95 = 9{,}880 \text{ W}$$

**Reasoning:** Inverter efficiency of 95 % is applied multiplicatively.

---

**Step C — Net AC power (subtract parasitic):**

$$P_{net,AC} = 9{,}880 - 800 = 9{,}080 \text{ W}$$

**Reasoning:** Auxiliary loads (pumps, fans, controls) consume 800 W internally.

---

**Step D — Chemical input power:**

$$\dot Q_{fuel} = \dot n_{H_2} \cdot LHV = 0.05 \times 241{,}800 = 12{,}090 \text{ W}$$

**Reasoning:** Molar flow converted to J s⁻¹ using LHV in J mol⁻¹.

---

**Step E — System efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{9{,}080}{12{,}090} = 0.7510 \approx \mathbf{75.1\%}$$

**Reasoning:** A high system efficiency is achieved because parasitic load is small relative to gross output, indicating a well-sized stack.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question supplies $E_{Nernst}$ instead of $V_{cell}$, compute $\eta_v$ first using $\eta_v = V_{cell}/E_{Nernst}$, then multiply through the cascade. The cascade form **simplifies marking** because the examiner can verify each factor separately.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Flow Block Diagram of a Fuel Cell Power Plant

The diagram below maps every subsystem, its input/output energy streams, and where losses occur.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank H2 LHV Chemical Energy] --> B[Reformer / Humidifier]
    B --> C[FC Stack Electrochemical Conversion]
    C --> D[DC-DC Converter]
    D --> E[DC-AC Inverter]
    E --> F[AC Load Net Useful Output]
    C --> G[Cooling Loop Thermal Losses]
    B --> H[Anode Tail Gas Unreacted Fuel]
    I[Air Blower / Compressor] --> C
    J[Parasitic Power] --> I
    J --> D
    J --> E
    style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style F fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46
    style C fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a
```

**Reading Guide:**

| Node | Represents | Efficiency Node |
|---|---|---|
| A | Chemical energy inlet | $\eta_u$ (fuel utilization) |
| B | Fuel processing | $\eta_{reformer}$ |
| C | Electrochemical conversion | $\eta_v \cdot \eta_F$ |
| D | DC-DC conversion | $\eta_{DC-DC}$ |
| E | DC-AC inversion | $\eta_{inv}$ |
| F | AC load (output) | $\eta_{sys}$ |
| G, H | Thermal & exhaust losses | Energy lost |

---

## 4.2 Sankey-Style Loss Allocation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[100% Chemical Energy Input LHV Basis] --> EN[Energy to Stack eta_th = 83%]
    EN --> EL[Voltage Losses eta_v]
    EL --> CL[Faradaic / Crossover Losses eta_F]
    CL --> FU[Fuel Utilization Loss eta_u]
    FU --> PC[Power Conditioning Loss eta_pc]
    PC --> AX[Auxiliary / Parasitic Loss eta_aux]
    AX --> OUT[Net Useful AC Output eta_sys = 40-55%]
    EN -.Heat Loss.-> TH[Waste Heat 30-50%]
    FU -.Unreacted H2.-> EX[Exhaust 2-15%]
    style IN fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style OUT fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46
    style TH fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style EX fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
```

**Interpretation:** Starting from 100 % chemical input, energy is depleted sequentially through six multiplicative loss stages. The remaining fraction is $\eta_{sys}$. For a well-designed PEMFC system, this final fraction is typically **45–55 %**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define system efficiency of a fuel cell power plant. Why is it always lower than the stack's electrochemical efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**
System efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ is the ratio of **net AC electrical power delivered to the load** to the **LHV-based chemical energy input** of the fuel supplied to the plant.

It is lower than the electrochemical (stack) efficiency because the system includes parasitic loads such as **air compressors, coolant pumps, control electronics, and power-conditioning losses** in DC-DC and DC-AC converters. The stack efficiency considers only the electrochemical conversion, whereas system efficiency includes **all auxiliary overheads**.

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \cdot \eta_{aux} \cdot \eta_{pc} \leq \eta_{stack}
$$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the five major sub-efficiencies that multiply to give the overall fuel cell system efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Voltage efficiency** $\eta_v = V_{cell}/E_{Nernst}$
2. **Faradaic / Current efficiency** $\eta_F = I_{ext}/(I_{ext}+I_{loss})$
3. **Fuel utilization efficiency** $\eta_u = \dot n_{reacted}/\dot n_{supplied}$
4. **Power-conditioning efficiency** $\eta_{pc}$
5. **Auxiliary / Parasitic efficiency** $\eta_{aux}$

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_F \cdot \eta_u \cdot \eta_{pc} \cdot \eta_{aux}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the master expression for the system efficiency of a fuel cell power plant, clearly identifying each loss term and explaining its physical origin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50-cell SOFC stack operates at $V_{cell} = 0.78$ V, $I = 250$ A, $\eta_u = 90\%$, $\eta_{pc} = 94\%$, and $P_{parasitic} = 1.2$ kW. The fuel supply rate is $\dot n_{CH_4} = 0.04$ mol s⁻¹ with $LHV_{CH_4} = 802.3$ kJ mol⁻¹. Calculate the **system electrical efficiency** and the **combined heat-and-power (CHP) efficiency** if the recoverable waste heat is 6 kW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from first principles:

**Step 1:** Gross DC power: $P_{DC} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$

**Step 2:** After power conditioning: $P_{AC} = P_{DC} \cdot \eta_{pc}$

**Step 3:** Net useful output: $P_{net} = P_{AC} - P_{parasitic}$

**Step 4:** Chemical input: $\dot Q = \dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV$

**Step 5:** Forming the ratio:

$$
\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot \eta_{pc} - P_{par}}{\dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV}
$$

**Step 6:** Cascade form:

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_F \cdot \eta_u \cdot \eta_{pc} \cdot \eta_{aux}
$$

Loss origins: $\eta_v$ → activation/ohmic/concentration overpotentials; $\eta_F$ → fuel crossover; $\eta_u$ → anode exhaust; $\eta_{pc}$ → inverter/converter; $\eta_{aux}$ → parasitic loads. **(Full 7 marks awarded only if all five loss origins are stated.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Gross DC power:**

$$P_{DC} = 50 \times 0.78 \times 250 = 9{,}750 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — After power conditioning:**

$$P_{AC} = 9{,}750 \times 0.94 = 9{,}165 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Net electrical output:**

$$P_{net} = 9{,}165 - 1{,}200 = 7{,}965 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Chemical input:**

$$\dot Q = 0.04 \times 802{,}300 = 32{,}092 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — System electrical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{el} = \dfrac{7{,}965}{32{,}092} = 0.2482 \approx \mathbf{24.82\%}$$

**Step 6 — CHP efficiency:**

$$\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{7{,}965 + 6{,}000}{32{,}092} = \dfrac{13{,}965}{32{,}092} = 0.4352 \approx \mathbf{43.52\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **subtract $P_{parasitic}$** before computing $\eta_{sys}$ — this loses 2 marks.
> 2. CHP efficiency requires **adding recovered heat to the numerator** — not subtracting. Common error.
> 3. CHP efficiency **cannot exceed 100 %** by thermodynamic laws (Second Law). If your answer does, recheck units of $LHV$.
> 4. SOFC electrical efficiency in practice is 50–60 % — if your answer is far below 25 %, verify the fuel flow rate (it may be in SLPM, requiring conversion to mol s⁻¹).

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches why CHP (combined heat and power) operation raises the **effective system efficiency** of a fuel cell plant above 80 %, even when electrical efficiency alone is only 50 %. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC stack delivers 60 V at 300 A to a DC bus. The inverter operates at 96 % efficiency. Auxiliaries consume 1.5 kW. Hydrogen is supplied at 0.08 mol s⁻¹ (LHV = 241.8 kJ mol⁻¹). Determine the **voltage efficiency** if $E_{Nernst} = 1.18$ V, and the **overall system efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Conceptual Answer (7 marks):**

A fuel cell rejects ~40–50 % of its input energy as **low-grade waste heat** at temperatures of 60–90 °C (PEMFC) or 700–900 °C (SOFC). This heat is normally discarded.

In CHP mode:
- The **electrical efficiency** $\eta_{el}$ is reported on its own (~50 %).
- The **thermal efficiency** $\eta_{th}$ captures recovered heat (~35–40 %).
- The **CHP efficiency** $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{el} + \eta_{th}$ can reach **85–95 %**.

For SOFC, the high-grade heat (>700 °C) can drive a **bottoming cycle** or be used for **absorption chilling**, lifting total efficiency further. For PEMFC, low-grade heat is best suited to **domestic hot water** or **space heating**.

Hence even a modest electrical efficiency becomes commercially attractive when waste heat is valorized — this is the principal selling point of stationary fuel cell systems.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Per-cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = \dfrac{60}{N}$$

We need $N$. Given $E_{Nernst} = 1.18$ V per cell, and assuming a 60-cell stack (typical for 60 V output):

$$V_{cell} = \dfrac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ V}$$

> *(If $N$ is not stated explicitly, examiners accept the assumption $N=60$ since stack voltage = 60 V.)*

**Step 2 — Voltage efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{Nernst}} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.18} = 0.8475 \approx \mathbf{84.75\%}$$

**Step 3 — Gross DC power:**

$$P_{DC} = 60 \times 300 = 18{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Net AC output:**

$$P_{net} = 18{,}000 \times 0.96 - 1{,}500 = 17{,}280 - 1{,}500 = 15{,}780 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Chemical input:**

$$\dot Q = 0.08 \times 241{,}800 = 19{,}344 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Overall system efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{15{,}780}{19{,}344} = 0.8158 \approx \mathbf{81.58\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. $\eta_v > 1$ is **physically impossible** — if your calculation gives $> 100\%$, you inverted the ratio.
> 2. A $\eta_{sys}$ above 100 % is also impossible (First Law violation).
> 3. Do **not** mix HHV and LHV — recheck which value the problem supplies.
> 4. Always express auxiliary loads in the **same unit** as gross power (kW here).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Efficiency — Key Takeaways (Last-Minute Revision)**

- ☐ **Definition:** $\eta_{sys} = P_{net,AC} / (\dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV)$
- ☐ **Cascade:** $\eta_{sys} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_F \cdot \eta_u \cdot \eta_{pc} \cdot \eta_{aux}$ — **memorize this order**
- ☐ **Voltage efficiency:** $\eta_v = V_{cell}/E_{Nernst}$ — penalizes activation + ohmic + concentration losses
- ☐ **Faradaic efficiency:** $\eta_F \approx 0.97$ for PEMFC; reflects crossover
- ☐ **Fuel utilization:** Industrial systems target 85–98 %
- ☐ **Power conditioning:** DC-DC + DC-AC inverters at 92–97 %
- ☐ **Parasitic losses:** Subtracted, not multiplied (absolute subtraction)
- ☐ **CHP:** $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{el} + \eta_{th}$ can exceed 85 %
- ☐ **Typical values:** PEMFC ~50 %, SOFC ~55–60 % electrical; CHP ~85–95 %
- ☐ **Never apply Carnot** — fuel cells are not heat engines
- ☐ **LHV preferred** for low-T FCs (PEMFC, PAFC); HHV for high-T FCs (SOFC, MCFC)
- ☐ **Master equation:** $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot \eta_{pc} - P_{par}}{\dot n_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$
- ☐ **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹ — remember for related derivations
